In a 6-neuron layer with dropout rate p = 0.5, each neuron has a 50% chance of being zeroed out on each forward pass. Active neurons pass values through; dropped neurons output 0. Every training step sees a different random subset. Dropout rate p = 0.5 means drop 50%; p = 0.1 means drop 10%.
summary: Dropout randomly zeroes out neurons during training so each forward pass sees a different random subnetwork.

text: Why dropout works: forced redundancy. No single neuron can become a 'lone specialist' that the network depends on entirely. Every neuron must be useful even when its neighbors are missing. Without dropout: neuron 3 memorizes a specific pattern; if neuron 3 fails, the network breaks. Knowledge is fragile and concentrated. With dropout: all neurons learn overlapping representations; any 3 of 6 can carry the signal. Knowledge is distributed and robust. Analogy: a team of 6 people where any 3 might be absent on any given day - everyone must learn everyone else's job. The team becomes resilient with no single point of failure.
summary: Dropout works by forcing redundancy so no single neuron becomes critical, distributing knowledge across overlapping representations.

text: The actual math of dropout. Step 1: input vector, e.g., [0.8, 0.3, 0.5, 0.2, 0.7, 0.4]. Step 2: random Bernoulli mask with p = 0.5, e.g., [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]. Step 3: multiply element-wise to get [0.8, 0, 0.5, 0, 0.7, 0]. Step 4: scale by 1/(1 - p) = 1/0.5 = 2 to get [1.6, 0, 1.0, 0, 1.4, 0]. Why scale? Without scaling, the expected output is only 0.5 × original (half the neurons are zero). Scaling by 1/(1 - p) restores the expected value to match the original, so the network sees the same magnitude at training and inference. This is called inverted dropout.
summary: Inverted dropout multiplies activations by a random 0/1 Bernoulli mask then scales surviving values by 1/(1-p) to preserve expected magnitude.

text: At inference, dropout is completely turned off. When the model is deployed and making predictions, all neurons are active, no mask is applied, no scaling is needed. All 6 of 6 neurons remain ACTIVE. The scaling during training (multiplying by 1/(1 - p)) already compensated for the missing neurons. So at inference, using all neurons with their original weights produces the correct expected output. This is called 'inverted dropout.'
summary: At inference, dropout is disabled and all neurons stay active because training-time scaling already compensated for the dropped units.

text: Where dropout lives in a transformer block. Vertical flow: Input → Multi-Head Attention → DROPOUT (p = 0.1) → Add & Norm → Feed-Forward Network → DROPOUT (p = 0.1) → Add & Norm. Dropout is applied at two key locations inside each transformer block: after attention and after FFN, each with p = 0.1. Typical dropout rate in transformers: p = 0.1 (drop only 10%). This is much lower than the 0.5 used in earlier networks because transformers are already heavily regularized by their architecture.
summary: In a Transformer block, dropout with p=0.1 is applied after attention and after the FFN, just before each Add & Norm.

text: Momentum formula diagram: m_t = β₁ · m_(t-1) + (1 - β₁) · g_t. Annotations label m as 'momentum', β₁ shown as 0.9, and g_t as 'gradient'. Shows the exponential moving average of gradients with β₁=0.9 weighting history at 90% and the new gradient at 10%.
summary: Diagram showing the momentum formula with annotations for the 0.9 history weight and current gradient.

text: Adaptive learning rate diagram: v_t = β₂ · v_(t-1) + (1 - β₂) · g_t². Annotations label v as 'velocity', β₂ shown as 0.999, and g_t² as 'squared grad'. Tracks average squared gradient magnitude per parameter so dividing by √v scales each parameter's step adaptively.
summary: Diagram showing the second-moment (velocity) formula with annotations for β₂=0.999 and squared gradient.

text: Bias correction formulas diagram: m-hat_t = m_t / (1 - β₁^t) and v-hat_t = v_t / (1 - β₂^t), labeled 'corrected momentum' and 'corrected velocity'. Compensates for zero-initialized running averages so early-step estimates are scaled up correctly.
summary: Diagram showing the m-hat and v-hat bias-correction formulas with their corrected-momentum and corrected-velocity labels.

text: Full Adam update rule diagram: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε), with labels 'new weight', 'old weight', and 'learning rate'. Combines momentum direction, adaptive √v scaling, bias correction, and the epsilon stabilizer into a single per-parameter update.
summary: Diagram of the complete Adam update equation with labeled new weight, old weight, and learning rate components.

text: AdamW update rule diagram: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · (m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε) + λ · θ_t). Labels separate 'Adam step (unchanged)' from 'decay (separate)'. Shows decoupled weight decay applied directly to θ instead of being mixed into the gradient.
summary: Diagram of the AdamW update with decoupled weight decay shown as a separate λ·θ term added to the Adam step.

text: This chapter builds Adam (and its variant AdamW) from scratch as the real optimizer used in modern deep learning. Plain SGD applies one learning rate to all parameters, but real networks have parameters with gradient magnitudes that differ by orders of magnitude (e.g., 170,000x). Adam fixes this with three pieces: (1) Momentum m = β₁·m + (1-β₁)·g (β₁=0.9) smooths noisy gradients via an exponential moving average. (2) Adaptive learning rates via v = β₂·v + (1-β₂)·g² (β₂=0.999) where dividing by √v gives each parameter its own effective step size. (3) Bias correction m-hat = m/(1-β₁ᵗ), v-hat = v/(1-β₂ᵗ) compensates for zero-initialized averages in early steps. Full update: θ = θ - α · m-hat / (√v-hat + ε), with α=0.001 and ε=1e-8. AdamW decouples weight decay (+ λ·θ applied outside the Adam step) for clean, uniform regularization. AdamW with β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-8, λ in [0.01, 0.1] is the default optimizer for GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Gemini, and Claude.
summary: Chapter-level summary of how Adam combines momentum, adaptive rates, and bias correction, and how AdamW improves it with decoupled weight decay.

text: Plain SGD uses one learning rate for every parameter, but neural networks have millions of parameters with very different gradient scales. Parameter A (an embedding weight) sees huge gradients because many tokens flow through it: step = 0.001 x -50.0 = 0.05, which can overshoot the minimum. Parameter B (a deep layer bias) gets tiny gradients because the signal dilutes through many layers: step = 0.001 x -0.0003 = 0.0000003, barely any learning. Parameter A's step is 170,000x bigger than B's. Lowering lr to fix A starves B; raising it to fix B explodes A. SGD has no way to treat each parameter differently. Adam fixes this with two ideas: momentum (smoothing noisy gradients) and adaptive rates (per-parameter effective learning rate).
summary: Explains why a single SGD learning rate fails when parameters have wildly different gradient scales, motivating Adam.

text: Idea 1 - Momentum. Instead of using only the current gradient, keep a running average m (the first moment). Formula: m_t = β₁ · m_(t-1) + (1 - β₁) · g_t. Default β₁ = 0.9 means 90% of momentum comes from history, 10% from the current gradient. Think of a heavy ball rolling downhill - it does not change direction instantly. Concrete walkthrough across 4 gradient steps: t=1 g=+0.8 -> 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x 0.8 = +0.080. t=2 g=+0.6 -> 0.9 x 0.08 + 0.1 x 0.6 = +0.132. t=3 g=-0.9 -> 0.9 x 0.132 + 0.1 x (-0.9) = +0.029. t=4 g=+0.7 -> 0.9 x 0.029 + 0.1 x 0.7 = +0.096. At step 3 the gradient flips to -0.9 but momentum barely moves, then recovers by step 4. Exponential moving average: gradient from 1 step ago weighted 0.9, 2 steps ago 0.81, 10 steps ago 0.35 - recent gradients matter most, old ones fade exponentially.
summary: Defines Adam's momentum term as an exponential moving average of gradients with β₁=0.9 and walks through 4 numerical steps.

text: Idea 2 - Adaptive Learning Rates. Track squared gradient magnitude per parameter, called v (the second moment). Formula: v_t = β₂ · v_(t-1) + (1 - β₂) · g_t². Default β₂ = 0.999. In the final update we divide by √v. Parameter A: typical gradient 50, v tracks 50² = 2500, divide by √2500 = 50, effective rate lr / 50 - big gradients get tamed automatically. Parameter B: typical gradient 0.01, v tracks 0.01² = 0.0001, divide by √0.0001 = 0.01, effective rate lr / 0.01 - small gradients get boosted automatically. Parameter A's effective learning rate is 5,000x smaller than Parameter B's. The same global learning rate α works for both - √v handles per-parameter scaling.
summary: Introduces Adam's second moment v as the per-parameter squared-gradient EMA that creates adaptive learning rates by dividing by √v.

text: Bias Correction - Fixing the Cold Start. Both m and v are initialized to zero, so early running averages are biased toward zero. At step 1 with β₁=0.9, m₁ = 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x g₁ = 0.1 x g₁ - only 10% of the true gradient, our estimate is 10x too small. Correction formulas: m-hat_t = m_t / (1 - β₁^t), v-hat_t = v_t / (1 - β₂^t). How much it changes: at t=1, 1 - β₁¹ = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1, m-hat = m₁ / 0.1 = 10x boost - massive correction because m only captured 10% of the true value. At t=1000, 1 - 0.9¹⁰⁰⁰ ≈ 1 - 0 = 1.0, m-hat = m / 1.0 = no change - correction vanishes. Same logic for v-hat with β₂=0.999: at t=1 correction is 1,000x; by t=5000 it is gone. Bias correction is only significant during the first few hundred steps.
summary: Explains why m and v need bias correction in early steps and shows the dividing-by-(1-β^t) formula collapses to no-op late in training.

text: The Full Adam Update Rule: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε). Symbols: θ is the parameter value (single weight or bias); α is learning rate (typically 0.001); m-hat is bias-corrected momentum - which direction to go; v-hat is bias-corrected velocity - how much to scale the step; ε is a tiny constant 1e-8 to prevent division by zero. Full numerical walkthrough at t=1 with θ=0.500, g₁=0.4, α=0.001: Step 1 m₁ = 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x 0.4 = 0.04. Step 2 v₁ = 0.999 x 0 + 0.001 x 0.4² = 0.001 x 0.16 = 0.00016. Step 3 m-hat = 0.04 / (1 - 0.9) = 0.04 / 0.1 = 0.4. Step 4 v-hat = 0.00016 / (1 - 0.999) = 0.00016 / 0.001 = 0.16. Step 5 update = 0.001 x 0.4 / (√0.16 + 1e-8) = 0.001 x 0.4 / 0.4 = 0.001. θ₂ = 0.500 - 0.001 = 0.499. At t=1 with bias correction, the effective step is almost exactly α regardless of gradient magnitude - the step size is bounded near α while direction comes from momentum.
summary: Combines momentum, velocity, and bias correction into the full Adam update formula and walks through every arithmetic step for one parameter.

text: AdamW - Weight Decay Done Right. Weight decay is regularization that shrinks all weights slightly each step. Original Adam mixed weight decay into the gradient (L2 regularization), but Adam divides by √v, which includes the decay term. Parameters with large v barely feel decay; parameters with small v feel too much - decay is not applied equally. AdamW formula: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · (m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε) + λ · θ_t). L2 in Adam: decay added to gradient then divided by √v - parameters with large v feel almost no decay - strength varies per parameter, unintended. AdamW: decay applied directly to θ, outside Adam - every parameter gets the same decay rate λ - clean separation, uniform as intended. GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Gemini, Claude all use AdamW with β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-8, λ between 0.01 and 0.1. The 2017 paper by Loshchilov and Hutter showed decoupled weight decay improves generalization. Complete recipe: 1) compute gradient g; 2) m = β₁·m + (1-β₁)·g; 3) v = β₂·v + (1-β₂)·g²; 4) m-hat = m/(1-β₁ᵗ), v-hat = v/(1-β₂ᵗ); 5) θ = θ - α·(m-hat/(√v-hat+ε) + λ·θ).
summary: Contrasts L2-in-Adam against AdamW's decoupled weight decay and gives the canonical hyperparameters and 5-step recipe used by modern transformers.

text: Line graph showing learning rate increasing linearly from zero during the warmup phase over 2000 training steps. Y-axis is LR, x-axis is Step. Points: (0,0), (400,0.0002), (800,0.0004), (1200,0.0006), (1600,0.0008), (2000,0.001). Title: Linear Warmup (2000 steps). Color: blue.
summary: Linear warmup graph: LR rises from 0 to 0.001 across 2000 steps.

text: Line graph showing learning rate with linear warmup to a peak then smooth cosine decay to zero, illustrating the complete LR schedule. Annotation marks the Peak. Points show ramp from 0 to 0.001 by step 2000, then cosine decay through 0.000927 (step 3000), 0.000691 (step 5000), 0.0005 (step 6000), 0.000146 (step 8000), down to 0 at step 10000. Color: green. Title: Warmup + Cosine Decay.
summary: Combined warmup + cosine decay graph showing LR ramp to 0.001 peak at step 2000, then smooth cosine descent to 0 by step 10000.

text: Learning Rate Warmup & Decay: a constant learning rate fails because optimal LR changes during training. Solution: schedule the LR. First, warmup linearly from 0 to a peak value over ~2000 steps so noisy initial gradients don't destabilize training. Then apply cosine decay using lr_min + 0.5*(lr_max-lr_min)*(1+cos(pi*step/total)) to smoothly reduce LR to the minimum. GPT-3 used this exact pattern: 2000-step warmup to peak 6e-4, cosine decay to 6e-5 (10% of peak) over 300B tokens. Cosine beats linear because it spends more time at useful intermediate rates.
summary: LR scheduling combines linear warmup and cosine decay; modern LLMs like GPT-3 use this to stabilize training and converge well.

text: Why a constant learning rate fails: no single learning rate works well for the entire training process. Too high and you diverge. Too low and you never converge. Concrete examples: lr = 0.01 causes loss to explode to NaN after a few steps. lr = 0.0001 makes training crawl, still not converged after 100K steps. lr = 0.001 works OK but still not optimal for the whole run. The best learning rate changes over the course of training. Early on you need small steps. In the middle, larger steps. Near the end, small steps again.
summary: Constant learning rate fails because optimal LR changes during training - early needs small, middle needs larger, end needs small.

text: Warmup - start tiny, ramp up. At step 1, weights are random garbage. Gradients are noisy and unreliable. A large learning rate plus bad gradients equals immediate divergence. Linear warmup over 2000 steps: step 0 has lr = 0 (no updates yet), step 1000 has lr = 0.0005 (halfway to peak), step 2000 has lr = 0.001 (peak reached). Small LR lets the model find its footing before taking bigger steps. The gradients stabilize as the weights move toward a reasonable region of the loss landscape.
summary: Warmup linearly ramps learning rate from zero to peak over the first ~2000 steps so noisy initial gradients don't blow up training.

text: Cosine decay - smooth descent. After warmup, smoothly decrease the LR following a cosine curve. The formula: lr = lr_min + 0.5 x (lr_max - lr_min) x (1 + cos(pi x step / total)). Warmup phase steps 0-2000: linear ramp from 0 to peak. Decay phase steps 2000+: cosine curve gently reduces to minimum. Combined warmup + cosine decay schedule peaks at 0.001 around step 2000 then decays smoothly to zero by step 10000.
summary: After warmup peak, cosine decay smoothly reduces LR following lr_min + 0.5*(lr_max-lr_min)*(1+cos(pi*step/total)).

text: Real-world schedule: GPT-3. Actual schedule used to train GPT-3 (175 billion parameters): Warmup over 375M tokens (~2,000 steps). Peak LR = 6 x 10^-4 (0.0006). Decay over 300B tokens using cosine schedule. Min LR = 6 x 10^-5 (10% of peak). Why cosine and not linear decay? Cosine spends more time at useful learning rates (the middle range) and approaches zero very gradually. Linear decay spends equal time at every LR, wasting steps at near-zero rates where no meaningful learning occurs.
summary: GPT-3 used 2000-step warmup to peak LR 6e-4, then cosine decay to 6e-5 over 300B tokens; cosine beats linear by spending more time at useful rates.

text: Diagram - All Weights = 0: The Symmetry Problem. Shows an input box with [1.0, 0.5] connected to three neurons n1, n2, n3, each with w=[0,0]. All three neurons output 0.0. Arrows lead from all three identical outputs to the label 'All identical!'. Bottom caption: 'Same weights, same outputs, same gradients, stuck forever'. The diagram visualizes why zero initialization makes every neuron in a layer compute the exact same value, leaving the network unable to differentiate neurons during training.
summary: Diagram illustrating the symmetry problem - three neurons with all-zero weights produce identical outputs and the network is stuck forever.

text: Weight Initialization - How Random? Weights cannot start at zero (symmetry problem - all neurons identical), too small (signal vanishes through layers), or too large (signal explodes to NaN). The solution is careful scaled randomness. Xavier/Glorot uses std = sqrt(2 / (fan_in + fan_out)) to preserve variance across layers. He initialization multiplies by sqrt(2) to compensate for ReLU killing half the activations: std = sqrt(2 / fan_in). Modern networks use He for ReLU; transformers often scale by 1/sqrt(depth) for residual connections. Good initialization keeps signal variance near 1.0 through many layers, preventing both vanishing and exploding signals.
summary: Chapter overview: weight initialization must avoid zero (symmetry), too-small (vanishing), and too-large (exploding) - Xavier and He scale randomness mathematically to keep variance stable.

text: Why not start weights at zero? If all weights start at zero, every neuron in a layer computes the exact same output. All gradients are identical. No neuron ever becomes different from any other. The network is stuck forever. This is the symmetry problem: every neuron is a clone of every other. With input [1.0, 0.5] and w=[0,0] for neurons n1, n2, n3 - all outputs equal 0.0, all identical, same gradients. No matter how long you train, they will never differentiate.
summary: Initializing all weights to zero causes the symmetry problem where every neuron stays identical and the network cannot learn.

text: Too small initialization: if weights start with very small random values (std = 0.001), numbers shrink toward zero at every layer. Each layer multiplies the previous output by tiny weights, so values get exponentially smaller. Input [1.0, 0.5] with magnitude 1.0. After Layer 1: [0.002, -0.001] magnitude 0.002. After Layer 3: [~0.000004, ~0.000002]. After Layer 5: [~0, ~0]. By layer 5-10, every value is effectively zero. Gradients also vanish - the network cannot learn. The network is dead.
summary: Initializing weights too small causes signals and gradients to shrink exponentially through layers, making the network unable to learn.

text: Too large initialization: if weights start with large random values (std = 5.0), numbers grow uncontrollably at every layer. Input [1.0, 0.5]. After Layer 1: [8.5, -12.3]. After Layer 3: [~450, ~-820]. After Layer 5: [~45000, ~-89000]. The values overflow, activations saturate, gradients explode, and training produces NaN within a few steps. The network is unstable from the very first forward pass.
summary: Initializing weights too large causes activations to grow exponentially, producing NaN and unstable training within a few steps.

text: Xavier/Glorot initialization: scale random weights so the variance of the output matches the variance of the input. The key: divide by the square root of the number of inputs (fan_in). Formula: std = sqrt(2 / (fan_in + fan_out)). Example for a 512 to 512 layer: std = sqrt(2 / (512 + 512)) = sqrt(2 / 1024) = sqrt(0.00195) = 0.044. With this scale, signal variance stays approximately 1.0 through many layers: Layer 1 ~1.0, Layer 3 ~0.98, Layer 5 ~0.95, Layer 10 ~0.90. Stable signal - no vanishing, no exploding. Named after Xavier Glorot, who proved this mathematically in 2010.
summary: Xavier/Glorot initialization uses std = sqrt(2 / (fan_in + fan_out)) to keep signal variance stable across layers.

text: He initialization: designed for ReLU. ReLU sets all negative values to zero, killing roughly half the neurons' outputs. This halves the variance at each layer. To compensate, multiply Xavier's scale by sqrt(2). Formula: std = sqrt(2 / fan_in). Example for 512 inputs: std = sqrt(2 / 512) = sqrt(0.00391) = 0.0625. Compare Xavier 0.044 - He is about 42% larger to compensate for ReLU's halving. Xavier + ReLU: variance shrinks (L1=1.0, L5=0.5, L10=0.25). He + ReLU: variance stable (L1=1.0, L5=0.98, L10=0.95). Named after Kaiming He (2015). Most modern networks with ReLU use He initialization. Transformers often use a variant scaled by 1/sqrt(depth) for residual connections.
summary: He initialization uses std = sqrt(2 / fan_in) to compensate for ReLU killing half the activations, keeping variance stable in ReLU networks.

text: Tokenization converts text into numeric IDs that a neural network can consume. Three strategies exist: character-level (too granular), word-level (huge vocab and out-of-vocabulary failures), and sub-word like BPE (the sweet spot at ~50K tokens). BPE starts with characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build its vocabulary. Real GPT models tokenize 'The cat sat on the mat last week' into IDs like [464, 3797, 2906, 319, 262, 4416, 1297, 1285], where each ID indexes a 768-dim learned embedding fed into the model.
summary: Chapter-level overview of tokenization strategies, BPE training, and how token IDs feed into embeddings.

text: Computers only understand numbers. Text is useless to an AI - no matter how eloquent. The first step is to convert 'I love cats' into [0, 1, 2]. This is Tokenization: slicing text into chunks and assigning each a number from a vocabulary.
summary: Tokenization converts text into numeric IDs because models only operate on numbers.

text: Three tokenization strategies exist. Character-level: 'love' -> ['l','o','v','e'] -> [108, 111, 118, 101]. Pros: works for any word, new letters rare. Cons: way too granular, sequences become huge. Word-level: 'unhappy' -> ['unhappy'] -> [5942]. Pros: human intuitive. Cons: 50K+ vocab needed, new words crash the model. Sub-word (BPE): 'unhappy' -> ['un','##happy'] -> [100, 6420]. Pros: sweet spot, ~50K tokens covers everything. Cons: need to learn splits first.
summary: Compares character, word, and sub-word tokenization strategies with tradeoffs.

text: Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) - the gold standard. BPE learns which character pairs appear together most. It starts with characters, then merges the most common pairs repeatedly. Step 0: split 'I love cats' into characters [I, _, l, o, v, e, _, c, a, t, s]. Step 1: count adjacent pairs: l+o=1, o+v=1, v+e=1, c+a=1, a+t=1, t+s=1. Step 2: merge most common pair (all tied at 1, pick l+o -> lo). Step 3: repeat merging. Recount pairs. lo+v is highest. Merge -> lov. Then lov+e -> love. Then c+a+t+s -> cats. Final tokens with IDs: I=0, space=1, love=2, space=1, cats=3. The sequence [0, 1, 2, 1, 3] is what gets fed to the model.
summary: Walks through BPE step by step on 'I love cats', merging frequent pairs into tokens with IDs.

text: Real example: GPT tokenizer. Modern tokenizers like GPT-2 use learned vocabularies of ~50,000 tokens. The sentence 'The cat sat on the mat last week' becomes: The=464, cat=3797, sat=2906, on=319, the=262, mat=4416, last=1297, week=1285. Sequence: [464, 3797, 2906, 319, 262, 4416, 1297, 1285]. Each token ID points to a learned embedding (a 768-dimensional vector in GPT-3). The model never sees text - only numbers.
summary: Real GPT tokenizer mapping a sentence to token IDs that index 768-dim learned embeddings.

text: Self-supervised learning is how GPT pretrains: it learns from raw text without labels by predicting the next token. One sentence becomes many (input, target) training pairs. The model outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary, cross-entropy loss measures the gap (Loss = -log(P_correct)), and gradient descent adjusts weights across billions of examples. Through next-token prediction alone, the model learns grammar, facts, reasoning, and style.
summary: This chapter overview: GPT pretrains via self-supervised next-token prediction with cross-entropy loss on billions of free training examples.

text: Self-Supervised Learning is the trick behind GPT. GPT learns from raw text with NO labels. It doesn't need humans to annotate. Instead, it creates its own training signal by predicting the next token. One simple rule: given all the words so far, predict what comes next.
summary: Introduces self-supervised learning where GPT generates its own training signal by predicting the next token from raw text without human labels.

text: Example: 'The cat sat on the mat'. One sentence becomes FIVE training examples. Each one says: given these words, the next word is... Input 'The' -> target 'cat' [3797]. Input 'The cat' -> target 'sat' [2906]. Input 'The cat sat' -> target 'on' [319]. Input 'The cat sat on' -> target 'the' [262]. Input 'The cat sat on the' -> target 'mat' [4416]. From a tiny 8-word sentence we get 5 (input, target) pairs. From 1 billion words we get 1 billion training examples. For FREE.
summary: Shows how a single sentence is split into multiple (input, target) training pairs, demonstrating why self-supervised learning produces unlimited training data for free.
terms: the cat sat on the mat

text: Inside the model: raw prediction. When we give the model 'The cat sat on', it outputs probabilities for the next token. Distribution: 'the' = 0.35 (correct, 35%), 'cat' = 0.15, 'mat' = 0.12, 'on' = 0.10, all others = 0.28. The model predicted 'the' with 35% confidence. The correct answer is 'the'. Good! But not perfect - 35% is not 100%.
summary: Demonstrates the model output as a probability distribution over the vocabulary with a worked example showing the correct answer received only 35% confidence.

text: Loss: how wrong was the prediction? We use cross-entropy loss to measure the gap. For this example: Loss = -log(0.35) = 1.05. Higher probability -> lower loss. If we predicted 1.0 (100%), loss = 0. If 0.1 (10%), loss approximately 2.3. Examples: P=1.0 -> L=0.0 (perfect). P=0.5 -> L=0.69 (so-so). P=0.35 -> L=1.05 (our example). P=0.1 -> L=2.3 (bad).
summary: Introduces cross-entropy loss formula Loss = -log(P_correct) with worked numerical examples showing how loss grows as confidence drops.

text: Why self-supervised works. The model learns to predict next words by trial and error on BILLIONS of examples. Through this alone, it learns: Grammar - predicting next word requires understanding subject-verb agreement and tenses. Facts - to predict 'The capital of India is ___', it must memorize that Delhi is the answer. Reasoning - to predict the output of a math problem, it must perform the computation internally. Style transfer - after SFT the model uses this learned knowledge to follow human instructions.
summary: Explains why next-token prediction alone teaches the model grammar, facts, reasoning, and style by forcing internal understanding to make accurate predictions.

text: During training: gradient descent. After each batch of examples, the model computes loss, then uses backprop to adjust weights to reduce future loss. Iteration 1: avg loss on batch = 4.2 -> adjust weights. Iteration 2: avg loss = 3.8 -> adjust weights. Iteration 3: avg loss = 3.1 -> adjust weights. ... After 1 trillion tokens: avg loss on batch = 0.8. The model gets better at predicting. This is 'pretraining' - GPT-3 trained for 300+ billion tokens.
summary: Shows the iterative gradient descent training loop where average batch loss steadily decreases from 4.2 down to 0.8 over a trillion tokens of pretraining.

text: Cross-entropy loss formula rendered as mathematical notation: Loss = -log_e(P_correct). Each piece is annotated: 'Loss' on the left equals; the negative sign flips the log; log_e is natural log; P_correct (with 'correct' subscript) is the probability the model assigned to the actual next word from the training data.
summary: Diagram showing the cross-entropy loss formula with each symbol labeled.

text: Plotted negative log probability curve: y-axis is Loss (-log P) from 0 to ~4.6, x-axis is probability assigned to correct word from 0.0 to 1.0. The curve drops steeply near P=0 and flattens toward P=1. Annotated points: P=0.95 confident & right (loss ~0.05, green); P=0.50 coin flip (loss ~0.69, yellow); P=0.10 wrong guess (loss ~2.30, orange); P=0.01 very wrong (loss ~4.60, red). The shape demonstrates the exponential penalty for low confidence and the tiny penalty for high confidence.
summary: Diagram of the -log(P) loss curve with four annotated points spanning very wrong to confident & right.

text: Cross-entropy loss is how every LLM grades its own predictions. The formula Loss = -log_e(P_correct) takes the probability the model assigned to the actually correct next word (which comes from the training data, not the formula) and flips its log. The -log curve is steep at low probability (massive penalty for being confidently wrong) and flat near 1 (tiny penalty for being right). Cross-entropy is preferred over MSE because it punishes confident mistakes properly. During training, per-example losses are averaged across a batch to give one number that drives weight updates.
summary: Chapter recap of the cross-entropy loss formula, its shape, why it beats MSE, and how batch averaging plugs into training.

text: How do we grade the model? The model predicts probabilities for every word in the vocabulary. We need to turn that into one number that says how good the predictions are. A loss function is a single score that tells us how wrong the model was. Lower loss equals better predictions. The loss function used by every LLM is called cross-entropy loss.
summary: Introduces the need for a single loss number to grade an LLM's vocabulary-wide probability output, motivating cross-entropy loss.

text: The formula: Loss = -log_e(P_correct). In plain English: take the natural log of how confident the model was in the right answer, then flip the sign. P_correct is the probability the model assigned to the actual next word; the negative sign flips it. The shape of -log(P): on a graph of loss vs probability, the curve is steep on the left (low probability gives massive penalty) and flat on the right (high probability gives tiny penalty). Annotated points: P=0.95 (confident & right) gives loss ~0.05; P=0.50 (coin flip) gives ~0.69; P=0.10 (wrong guess) gives ~2.30; P=0.01 (very wrong) gives ~4.60. Where does 'correct' come from? Not from inside the formula - from the training data. During training, the model sees real text like 'The cat sat on the mat'. We already know the next word is 'mat' because it is right there in the sentence. The model outputs 50,000 probabilities (one per word). We reach into that list, pull out the one probability assigned to 'mat', and plug THAT into the formula. Worked example: model output {the: 2%, cat: 5%, mat: 62%, dog: 1%, ...}. Correct answer (from training data): 'mat'. So P_correct = P('mat') = 0.62. Loss = -ln(0.62) = 0.48. The 'comparison' between actual and expected is implicit: we only look at the probability the model gave to the known correct word. There is no subtraction. The entire comparison is: how confident were you in the right answer? Tied to the LLM context: model gives 'mat' P=0.95 -> Loss 0.05, almost zero, barely any weight adjustment. Model gives 'mat' P=0.50 -> Loss 0.69, weights nudged. Model gives 'mat' P=0.01 -> Loss 4.60, massive correction. The model gets punished harshly for being confidently wrong, but barely punished when already doing well.
summary: Presents Loss = -log(P_correct), shows the -log curve's shape, and explains that the correct word comes from training data with a worked 'mat' example.

text: Why -log? The intuition. Imagine you're a weather forecaster. You say '70% chance of rain'. If it rains, you did decent (70% is not 100%, but ok). If it doesn't rain, you were wrong (you said 70%). Loss measures your mistake: Loss = -log(your prediction for what actually happened). Examples: You said 'It WILL rain (P=0.95)', actual IT DID RAIN, loss = 0.05, grade A+. You said 'Maybe rain (P=0.50)', actual it rained, loss = 0.69, grade C. You said 'Probably dry (P=0.10)', actual it rained, loss = 2.30, grade F.
summary: Uses a weather forecaster analogy to give intuition for why -log of the assigned probability measures prediction error.

text: Cross-entropy formula in detail. When predicting one token from a 50K vocabulary: Loss = -log_e(P_correct), where P_correct is between 0 and 1 (it's a probability). Concrete table: P=0.99 -> L=0.01; P=0.90 -> L=0.10; P=0.50 -> L=0.69; P=0.10 -> L=2.30; P=0.01 -> L=4.60. As probability increases, loss approaches zero. As probability drops, loss explodes. This penalizes confident wrong answers.
summary: Restates the loss formula and shows a P-to-loss table illustrating how loss explodes as the assigned probability drops.

text: Why cross-entropy, not MSE? MSE (Mean Squared Error) is the default loss for predicting numbers - like predicting house prices. But language models predict probabilities over a vocabulary. MSE Loss formula: (P_pred - P_true)^2. Example: P_pred=0.1, P_true=1.0 -> (0.1-1.0)^2 = 0.81. Problem: treats small and large errors equally and doesn't penalize confidence enough. Cross-Entropy Loss formula: -log(P_correct). Example: P_pred=0.1 when true=1.0 -> -log(0.1) = 2.30. It heavily punishes confident mistakes - which is what we want.
summary: Contrasts MSE with cross-entropy and shows why cross-entropy's stronger penalty for confidently wrong probabilities makes it the right loss for LLMs.

text: Batch loss: averaging across examples. During training, we compute loss on a BATCH of examples, then take the mean. Example 1: Predict 'sat' -> model says P=0.4 -> loss = 0.92. Example 2: Predict 'on' -> model says P=0.8 -> loss = 0.22. Example 3: Predict 'mat' -> model says P=0.1 -> loss = 2.30. Batch Loss = (0.92 + 0.22 + 2.30) / 3 = 1.15. The model updates weights to reduce this batch loss. Then the next batch is loaded and loss is recomputed.
summary: Shows that during training, per-example losses are averaged into a single batch loss that drives weight updates.

text: Architectural diagram: four input nodes (one-hot [0,0,0,1] for word 'on') connect to Hidden Layer 1 (8 circles), then Hidden Layer 2 (8 circles), then Output layer with 20 circles - one per vocab word, with 'the' and 'mat' highlighted as active high-score outputs. Arrows labeled 'weights' between layers indicate every connection is a learned weight. Color-coded: orange input, purple hidden layers, green output. Demonstrates flow input → hidden → hidden → vocabulary-sized output.
summary: Visual schematic of the full forward-pass network: input one-hot, two hidden layers, 20-way output.

text: This chapter walks through a full neural network forward pass on a 20-word toy vocabulary. Steps: (1) tiny vocab grid, (2) input 'The cat sat on' predicts word 5, (3) network diagram input→2 hidden→20 outputs, (4) one output circle does dot product of incoming values and its own weights to produce a logit (e.g. 'mat'=4.40), (5) all 20 raw logits shown, (6) softmax exponentiates and normalizes them into probabilities, (7) top prediction is 'the' at 46% with cross-entropy loss 0.78, (8) the entire process reduces to multiply-and-add with learned weights.
summary: Chapter-level overview of how a neural network goes from input tokens to softmax probabilities for next-word prediction.

text: Tiny toy world: model knows only 20 words. Vocabulary: the, cat, sat, on, mat, a, dog, ran, is, was, big, small, red, blue, and, in, it, my, to, with. Each word has an ID [0]-[19]. Model never sees letters - only these numeric IDs. Forget 50,000-word real vocabularies; this 20-word example makes the full forward pass tractable.
summary: Introduces a 20-word toy vocabulary used throughout the chapter so every step can be shown end-to-end.

text: Input sentence: "The cat sat on" with token IDs [0], [1], [2], [3]. Model's job: predict word #5. The four input tokens flow into the network and the output should be the most likely next word, shown as ??? placeholder.
summary: Sets up the prediction task: given four input words, predict the fifth.

text: Neural network architecture diagram: Input layer (4 nodes for 'on' as one-hot [0,0,0,1]) → Hidden Layer 1 (8 circles) → Hidden Layer 2 (8 circles) → Output layer (20 circles, one per vocab word). Every connecting line is a learned weight. Each circle does: multiply inputs by weights, sum, apply activation. The last input word gets converted to numbers, flows through hidden layers, then the output layer has one circle for every word in the vocabulary.
summary: Shows the full neural network: input → 2 hidden layers → 20 output circles, one per vocabulary word.

text: How the 'mat' output circle computes its score (logit). Values arriving from previous layer: [2.1, -0.8, 1.5, 0.3, -1.2, 0.9, 1.7, -0.4]. Weights for 'mat' circle: [0.6, 0.3, 0.8, 0.1, -0.5, 0.2, 0.9, 0.4]. Score = (2.1 x 0.6) + (-0.8 x 0.3) + (1.5 x 0.8) + (0.3 x 0.1) + (-1.2 x -0.5) + (0.9 x 0.2) + (1.7 x 0.9) + (-0.4 x 0.4) = 1.26 + (-0.24) + 1.20 + 0.03 + 0.60 + 0.18 + 1.53 + (-0.16) = 4.40. Score for 'mat' = 4.40. Same operation runs for all 20 output circles, each with its own weights.
summary: Walks through the dot product that one output circle uses to produce a single word's score (logit).

text: Raw scores (logits) for all 20 words after input 'The cat sat on'. Sorted top to bottom: the +4.2, mat +3.8, a +2.1, in +1.6, was +1.4, cat +1.1, and +0.9, dog +0.8, to +0.7, is +0.5, sat +0.3, it +0.2, ran -0.2, on -0.5, with -0.9, big -1.5, red -1.8, small -2.1, blue -2.3, my -3.1. 'The' and 'mat' have highest scores; 'my' is deeply negative. These are raw numbers, not probabilities.
summary: Shows concrete logit values for all 20 vocab words; raw numbers ranging positive and negative.

text: Softmax conversion from logits to probabilities. Two steps: raise e (2.718) to the power of each score, then divide each by the total. Formula: probability = e^score / sum(e^score) across all 20 words. Example computations: 'the' score=+4.2 → e^4.2 ≈ 66.69, 'mat' score=+3.8 → e^3.8 ≈ 44.70, 'a' score=+2.1 → e^2.1 ≈ 8.17, etc. Sum of all e^score = EXP_SUM. Each probability = e^score / EXP_SUM. Result: every probability positive, all sum to 100%.
summary: Converts raw logits into a probability distribution using exponentiation then normalization (softmax).

text: Final probability bars for top 10 words after softmax. The model's top prediction is 'the' at 46.0%, followed by 'mat'. The result reads: 'The cat sat on the' (model picks 'the'). Actual next word in training was 'the' → loss = -log(0.460) = 0.78. Not bad, but not 100% confident. Backprop will nudge the weights to make the correct word more likely next time. Plus 10 more words below 1% each.
summary: Shows the final probability distribution and computes the cross-entropy loss for the predicted token.

text: Key insight: the entire pipeline is just multiply and add. (1) Take numbers in: word 'on' becomes [0, 0, 0, 1, ...] or an embedding. (2) Multiply by weights, add up, apply activation - each circle does this. (3) Repeat through every layer (GPT-2 has 12 layers, GPT-4 has ~100+). (4) Last layer has one circle per vocabulary word (20 for us, 50,000 for real GPT). (5) Softmax to get probabilities. The only 'smart' part is the weights - the numbers on each connection line. Training (gradient descent) adjusts those weights trillions of times until multiply-and-add produces good predictions.
summary: Wraps up the chapter: a language model is just stacked multiply-add layers; intelligence lives entirely in the learned weights.

text: Deep neural network output layer diagram. Five columns left to right: Layer 1 (cyan neurons n₁ n₂ n₃), Layer 2 (cyan neurons), dots column indicating intermediate layers, Layer N labeled '(= Hidden State)' with purple neurons h₁ h₂ h₇₆₈ inside a dashed purple box highlighting it as the hidden state, an orange Unembedding 'W' matrix box, and an Output column of green neurons labeled 'the', 'mat', '...', 'dog' with 'mat' brightly highlighted as active. Edges connect every neuron of one column to every neuron of the next (fully connected); orange edges connect the hidden state to the output through W. Bottom note: 'each layer has 768 neurons - only 3 shown for clarity'. Output column subtitle: '(50K logits)'.
summary: Network diagram showing hidden layers feeding a hidden state through the unembedding matrix W into vocabulary output neurons with 'mat' highlighted as the active prediction.

text: This chapter explains the output layer of an LLM: how a hidden state (768 numbers from the final transformer layer) becomes a probability distribution over 50,000 vocabulary words. It walks through what a hidden state is, what logits (raw scores) are, how the unembedding matrix multiplies the hidden state to produce one logit per vocabulary word via dot products, how softmax converts logits to probabilities (e^logit / sum of all e^logits), why a single linear layer suffices (hidden layers did the hard work), and the weight tying trick where the unembedding matrix is the embedding matrix transposed so similar words at input get similar logits at output.
summary: End-to-end walkthrough of the LLM output layer: hidden state, logits, unembedding matrix, softmax, parameter count, and weight tying.

text: What is a hidden state? When a neural network processes input, what it produces is a hidden state. Step 1: input sentence enters the model. Each word in 'The cat sat on the' gets converted to a vector of 768 numbers (the embedding). So 'the' becomes [-0.14, 0.62, -0.38, ...]. Step 2: hidden layers transform these vectors, one layer at a time. Layer 1 learns basic patterns: 'the' is an article, 'cat' is a noun. Layer 2 learns combinations: 'the cat' is a noun phrase. Layer 6 learns grammar: 'sat on the' expects a location next. Layer 12 (final) encodes the full understanding: what word should come next. Each layer takes in 768 numbers and outputs 768 new numbers; the vector gets refined and enriched at every step. Step 3: the final layer's output is the hidden state. After all layers process the input, the last layer outputs a vector of 768 numbers for the last token ('the'). Hidden state for 'the' (768 numbers): [-0.14, 0.62, -0.38, 1.05, -0.72, 0.91, ..., 0.21]. It is called 'hidden' because it lives inside the model between layers and you never see it. It is called a 'state' because it represents the model's current understanding. These 768 numbers encode the meaning of 'the cat sat on the', the grammar, the context, and a prediction of what word should come next. Now we have 768 numbers, but we need a word; the vocabulary has 50,000 tokens.
summary: Defines hidden state as the 768-number vector output by the final hidden layer that encodes the model's full understanding before word prediction.

text: From hidden state to scores: what is a logit? The hidden state has 768 numbers. The model needs to decide which word comes next out of 50,000 vocabulary words, so it computes a score for every single word. Higher score = more likely. Example scores for 'The cat sat on the ___': 'mat' +8.2, 'floor' +7.5, 'sofa' +6.1, 'the' +0.3, 'because' -3.7, 'photosynthesis' -8.9, plus 49,994 more. These scores are called logits. A logit is just the raw score the model gives to a word. The name comes from log-odds in mathematics, but for our purposes: logit = score. 'Logit' is the name for the value that comes out, not the layer and not the neuron; the layer is called the output projection or LM head. Positive logit (+8.2) = good fit. Near-zero logit (+0.3) = no strong opinion. Negative logit (-8.9) = terrible fit. Logits are NOT probabilities: they are raw numbers, can be -100 or +100, do not add up to 100%, and are not percentages. To turn them into probabilities we need softmax. Pipeline so far: hidden state (768 numbers) → ??? → 50,000 logits → softmax → probabilities. The missing piece is the unembedding layer.
summary: Introduces logits as raw per-word scores produced by the output projection, with positive/negative meaning fit, and notes logits are not probabilities until softmax.

text: The Unembedding Layer: from 768 numbers to 50,000 scores. There is one more layer after all the hidden layers, called the unembedding layer (or output projection). The full pipeline: Layer 1 → Layer 2 → ... → Layer N (= hidden state) → Unembedding W → Output (50K logits). Each layer has 768 neurons (only 3 shown for clarity in the diagram). The hidden state's three sample neurons are labeled h₁, h₂, h₇₆₈. The unembedding matrix W is highlighted as a separate box between Layer N and the output column showing words like 'the', 'mat', 'dog'. The hidden state (768 numbers) gets multiplied by the unembedding matrix (768 x 50,000). The result: one logit for every word in the vocabulary.
summary: Describes the unembedding layer placed after the final hidden layer that maps the 768-number hidden state to 50,000 vocabulary logits via matrix multiply.

text: How does the unembedding actually work? One dot product per word. The unembedding matrix has 50,000 rows. Each row is a 768-dimensional vector that represents one vocabulary word. To get the logit for a word, take the dot product of the hidden state with that word's row. Worked example computing the logit for 'mat': Hidden state (768 values) = [-0.14, 0.62, -0.38, 1.05, ...]. Row for 'mat' in unembedding matrix = [0.45, 0.82, -0.21, 0.67, ...]. logit = dot product = (-0.14 x 0.45) + (0.62 x 0.82) + (-0.38 x -0.21) + (1.05 x 0.67) + ... = -0.063 + 0.508 + 0.080 + 0.704 + ... (768 terms added up). logit for 'mat' = 8.2. This happens for ALL 50,000 words in parallel. Each word has its own row, its own dot product, its own logit. Words whose rows are similar to the hidden state get high scores; words whose rows point in a different direction get low scores.
summary: Shows a concrete dot-product calculation between the hidden state and the unembedding row for 'mat' producing logit 8.2, repeated for all 50,000 words.

text: The result: logits for every word in the vocabulary. After computing all 50,000 dot products for 'The cat sat on the', we get raw scores (logits): 'mat' +8.2, 'floor' +7.5, 'sofa' +6.1, 'table' +5.8, 'bed' +5.2, 'she' -1.4, 'because' -3.7, 'photosynthesis' -8.9, plus 49,992 more tokens with their own scores. These are just raw numbers - they can be negative, large, or small. They are not probabilities yet. We need softmax to convert them.
summary: Lists example logit values across plausible and implausible next-word candidates after the full unembedding multiplication.

text: Softmax: from raw logits to probability distribution. Softmax does two things: makes every number positive using e^score, then divides by the total so everything adds up to 100%. Formula: probability = e^logit / sum of ALL e^logits across 50,000 tokens. Worked example for the top 5 tokens: 'mat' logit=8.2 → e^8.2 = 3641 → 56.8%. 'floor' logit=7.5 → e^7.5 = 1808 → 28.2%. 'sofa' logit=6.1 → e^6.1 = 446 → 7.0%. 'table' logit=5.8 → e^5.8 = 330 → 5.2%. 'bed' logit=5.2 → e^5.2 = 181 → 2.8%. The model predicts 'mat' with 56.8% confidence. This is the final output - a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary. During training, we compare this to the actual next word and compute the loss.
summary: Defines softmax formula and walks through e^logit values and resulting percentages for top-5 candidates, ending with mat at 56.8%.

text: Why does one simple layer work? The hidden layers did the hard part. The output layer is just a matrix multiply - no activation function, no non-linearity. It seems too simple, but the hidden layers already did all the hard work. All hidden layers (many layers deep): job is to understand context, grammar, meaning, and encode 'what should come next' into the 768-dim hidden state. Parameter count: billions of parameters. Output layer (1 matrix): job is to translate the hidden state into a logit for each vocabulary word - just one dot product per word. Parameter count: 768 x 50K = 38.4M parameters. The output layer has less than 0.02% of the total parameters. It is tiny. All the intelligence lives in the hidden layers.
summary: Explains the output layer is a pure linear projection with ~38.4M parameters (under 0.02% of total) because the hidden layers already encode meaning.

text: The clever trick: weight tying. The embedding layer converts tokens to vectors; the embedding matrix has shape 50,000 x 768 with one 768-dim row per word. Embedding matrix (input side): shape 50,000 x 768, converts word → vector. Row for 'mat' = [0.45, 0.82, -0.21, 0.67, ...]. Unembedding matrix (output side): shape 768 x 50,000, converts hidden state → one logit per word. This is just the embedding matrix transposed. This is called weight tying. The same matrix is used for both embedding and unembedding (just transposed). The model only needs to learn one set of word vectors, not two. Why it works (visual example): the hidden state encodes 'something you sit on, on the floor'. During embedding, similar words were placed near each other in the 768-dim space: 'mat' [0.45, 0.82, -0.21, ...] similarity 92%, 'rug' [0.43, 0.79, -0.19, ...] 89%, 'carpet' [0.41, 0.80, -0.23, ...] 87%, 'table' [0.30, 0.55, 0.12, ...] 58%, 'sky' [-0.60, 0.10, 0.85, ...] 5%. The dot product between the hidden state and each word's embedding measures similarity. 'mat', 'rug', 'carpet' have embeddings pointing in a similar direction to the hidden state, so their logits are high. 'sky' points in a completely different direction, so its logit is near zero. Weight tying enforces this: if 'mat' and 'rug' were learned as similar meanings at input, they automatically get similar logits at output. The model learns one definition per word, working in both directions.
summary: Explains weight tying where the unembedding matrix is the transposed embedding matrix, halving learned word vectors and making input/output similarity consistent.

text: This chapter - Autoregressive Generation: One Token at a Time. Training and generation are two different modes. In training the model sees the whole sentence at once and predicts the next token at every position in one parallel pass. In generation (inference) the model only has the prompt and must build the answer one token at a time: run the full pipeline (hidden layers, output projection, softmax) on the current sequence, pick a token, append it, repeat. This is autoregressive generation. Sampling strategies decide how to pick: greedy (argmax), pure sampling (weighted dice roll), top-K (sample from the K highest-probability tokens). Temperature divides each logit before softmax: low T (0.2) sharpens to nearly greedy, T=1.0 is the model's natural distribution, high T (2.0) flattens the distribution for more creativity. T=0 means argmax (division by zero is undefined). It works because the model sees the full prior context at every step, training matched this exact task, and small mistakes are absorbed by the rich context. Generation stops when the model emits a special end-of-text token (e.g. <END> or <|endoftext|>) or hits a max length cap like 4096 tokens.
summary: Chapter-level summary of how trained LLMs generate text token by token via repeated forward passes, sampling strategies, and a temperature-scaled softmax until a stop token appears.

text: Two very different modes. The model has two lives. During training, it sees entire sentences and learns to predict the next word at every position. But when you actually use it (generation), it has to build the answer one word at a time. Training (learning phase): The model sees ALL words [The, cat, sat, on, the, mat] at once. At each position it predicts the next word and checks if it was right. Fast - one pass through the whole sentence. Generation (using the model): The model only has the prompt [The, cat, sat, on] plus a ??? slot. It must predict the next word, add it, then predict the next, add it... one word at a time. This is called autoregressive generation.
summary: Contrasts training (parallel next-word prediction across the whole sentence) with generation (sequential one-token-at-a-time prediction starting from the prompt).

text: Watch it build the sentence, word by word. Prompt: "The cat sat on". At each step, the full pipeline runs: tokens go in, hidden layers process them, output layer produces 50,000 probabilities via softmax, and the model picks one word. Step 1: input [The, cat, sat, on] -> hidden layers -> output layer -> softmax -> 'the' 72%, 'a' 15%, 'that' 8%. Pick 'the' and append. Step 2: input [The, cat, sat, on, the] -> 'mat' 68%, 'floor' 18%, 'sofa' 7%. Pick 'mat'. Step 3: input [The, cat, sat, on, the, mat] -> 'last' 45%, 'all' 22%, 'and' 15%. Pick 'last'. Step 4: input [The, cat, sat, on, the, mat, last] -> 'week' 91%, 'night' 5%, 'time' 2%. Pick 'week'. Final result: "The cat sat on the mat last week" - cyan tokens are the original prompt, green tokens were generated by the model one at a time.
summary: Worked four-step example showing how 'The cat sat on' is grown into 'The cat sat on the mat last week' by repeatedly running the full pipeline and appending the picked token.

text: How does the model pick a word? The model outputs a probability for every word. But how do we choose one? Three strategies. Greedy: always pick the highest probability. With probs the=72%, a=15%, that=8%, his=3%, greedy always picks 'the'. Same input always gives same output. Safe and consistent, but can get repetitive - the model might loop the same phrase. Sampling: roll the dice (weighted by probability). Imagine spinning a wheel where 'the' takes up 72% of the wheel, 'a' takes 15%, and so on. Wherever the wheel lands, that is the word we pick. More creative and diverse. But sometimes picks low-probability words that make no sense. Top-K: sample, but only from the best K options. Keep only the top 10 (or 40, or 100) most likely words; throw out the rest; then sample from those. Example: keep 'the' 72%, 'a' 15%, 'that' 8%; remove 'photosynthesis' 0.001%. The sweet spot: creative enough to surprise you, but never picks something completely absurd.
summary: Explains the three decoding strategies for choosing the next token: deterministic greedy argmax, full probability-weighted sampling, and top-K sampling that filters to the most likely K candidates before sampling.

text: Temperature: the creativity dial. Before softmax, we divide each logit by a number called temperature. This controls how 'spread out' the probabilities are. Same logits 'the'=4.2, 'a'=2.8, 'that'=1.5 give very different probabilities. Temperature = 0.2 (cold, nearly greedy): dividing by 0.2 makes logits huge (21, 14, 7.5). Softmax crushes everything onto the winner: 'the'=99%, 'a'=1%, 'that'=0%. Temperature = 1.0 (default, balanced): logits stay the same. Probabilities reflect the model's true confidence: 'the'=72%, 'a'=18%, 'that'=10%. Temperature = 2.0 (hot, creative): dividing by 2.0 makes logits small (2.1, 1.4, 0.75). Softmax spreads probability more evenly: 'the'=45%, 'a'=30%, 'that'=25%. Formula: softmax(logit / temperature). Low temperature -> winner takes all. High temperature -> more even distribution. Temperature = 0 can't go into the formula (division by zero), so in practice T=0 means argmax - just pick the highest logit, skip softmax entirely. Most APIs use T=0.2 as the lowest practical value.
summary: Defines the temperature parameter as a divisor applied to logits before softmax, with worked examples at T=0.2 (sharp), T=1.0 (default), T=2.0 (flat) and the special case T=0 = argmax.

text: Why does this work? And when does it stop? Predicting one word at a time sounds fragile, but it works because of three facts. Full context at every step: at step 4 the model does not just see 'last' - it sees ALL previous words 'The cat sat on the mat last'. Every generated word gets the benefit of the entire history. The hidden layers process all of these words together, so the model never 'forgets' what came before. Training matches generation: the model was trained on exactly this task - given the words so far, predict the next one. Generation is not a new challenge but the exact thing the model practiced trillions of times. Self-correction is built in: even if the model picks a slightly unexpected word at step 2, it adjusts at step 3. The full context includes the 'mistake', and the model learned to continue coherently from any plausible word. Errors rarely compound. When does it stop? The vocabulary includes a special stop token (like <END> or <|endoftext|>). When the model assigns the highest probability to this token, generation stops. It is a learned behavior - the model saw thousands of sentences ending during training and learned to predict the stop token at natural endpoints. Step 5 example: input 'The cat sat on the mat last week', model predicts <END> with 85% confidence -> generation complete. In practice, there is also a max length limit (like 4096 tokens). If the model has not produced a stop token by then, generation is cut off.
summary: Explains why one-token-at-a-time generation is robust (full context, training/inference parity, self-correction) and how it terminates via a learned stop token or a hard max-length cutoff.

text: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) turns a raw pretrained next-token predictor into a helpful assistant. After pretraining, the model just predicts whatever text would follow on the internet (Wikipedia answers, Reddit snark, spam, sarcasm). To fix this, humans hand-write ~100,000 example conversations in a User/Assistant format. The model trains on these conversations using the same next-token prediction objective, but loss is only computed on the assistant's response tokens, not the user's question. After training, probabilities shift dramatically (e.g., 'Photosynthesis' as the first reply word jumps from 8% to 35%, while 'What' drops from 12% to 2%). At deploy time, user input is wrapped in a hidden template (User: ... Assistant:) often using special tokens like <|im_start|>, <|im_end|>. SFT only needs ~100K examples (vs trillions for pretraining) because it does not teach knowledge - the model already knows facts, grammar, and code from pretraining. SFT only nudges surface-level behavior: 'when you see User: question, respond with helpful answer'. Uses a tiny learning rate so deep weights barely move.
summary: Chapter overview of Supervised Fine-Tuning: how ~100K labeled User/Assistant conversations turn a raw pretrained model into a helpful assistant by changing surface behavior, not knowledge.

text: The problem after pretraining: the model just predicts internet text. After pretraining, the model has read trillions of words from the internet and can predict the next word very well, but it has no idea it should be helpful. If you type 'What is photosynthesis?', the model does not think 'I should answer this question' - it thinks 'what text would typically follow this on the internet?'. Possible continuations and rough probabilities the model assigns: a Wikipedia-style answer (15%), another question like 'What about cellular respiration?' (12%), a Reddit comment 'just Google it lol' (10%), a spam link or ad (8%), a sarcastic reply (6%). The model gives probability to ALL of these because it saw all of them during pretraining. We need to change this.
summary: Pretrained models predict whatever internet text would follow, not helpful answers, so a question gets distributed across Wikipedia replies, follow-up questions, Reddit snark, and spam.

text: The fix: show the model what a good assistant says. Humans sit down and write ~100,000 example conversations. Each example uses the exact format the model will see when deployed: 'User:' followed by a question, then 'Assistant:' followed by the ideal response. Example 1 - User: 'What is photosynthesis?' Assistant: 'Photosynthesis is the process where plants convert light energy into chemical energy. It happens in two stages: light-dependent reactions in the thylakoid, and the Calvin cycle in the stroma.' Example 2 - User: 'Write a Python function to reverse a list.' Assistant: 'def reverse_list(lst):\n    return lst[::-1]'. Each example tells the model: 'When a user asks this, this is what you should say.' The model trains on these conversations instead of random internet text.
summary: SFT data is ~100K human-written User/Assistant example conversations covering questions, code, and tasks - each one a demonstration of the ideal helpful response.

text: The key trick: loss is only computed on the assistant's words, not the user's question. The model reads the full conversation and predicts the next word at every position - same as pretraining. But the loss is ONLY calculated on the assistant's response tokens. The user's question is just context (no loss). Walking through token positions: 'User:' -> 'What' (no loss, just reading), 'User: What' -> 'is' (no loss), 'User: What is' -> 'photosynthesis?' (no loss), '...photosynthesis?' -> 'Assistant:' (no loss), 'Assistant:' -> 'Photosynthesis' (LOSS - model guessed 'What' at 12%, correct was 8%), '...Photosynthesis' -> 'is' (LOSS - guessed 'is' at 25%, correct 25%), '...Photosynthesis is' -> 'the' (LOSS - guessed 'a' at 18%, correct 'the' at 15%), '...is the' -> 'process' (LOSS - guessed 'study' 11%, correct 'process' 9%). The model is never graded on predicting the user's question. It is only graded on how well it predicts the ideal assistant response. Backpropagation then nudges the weights to make the correct response words more likely.
summary: During SFT, cross-entropy loss is masked so it applies only to assistant-response tokens; user-question tokens are read for context but contribute zero gradient.

text: Before vs After SFT: what changes in the output probabilities. After 100,000 training conversations, the weights have shifted. The model now strongly favors helpful assistant text after 'Assistant:'. Same input 'What is photosynthesis?', same model architecture, but different first-word probabilities. Before SFT (predicts generic internet text): What 12%, The 10%, Photosynthesis 8%, I 6%, So 5%, It 4%. After SFT (predicts helpful assistant response): Photosynthesis 35%, The 8%, It 5%, What 2%, I 2%, So 1%. 'Photosynthesis' jumped from 8% to 35% because in the SFT data, answers to 'What is X?' almost always start with 'X is...'. The weights got nudged thousands of times in that direction. 'What' dropped from 12% to 2% because good assistants don't repeat the question back.
summary: Concrete probability shift after SFT: the answer-starting word jumps from 8% to 35% while echoing the question drops from 12% to 2%.

text:
The hidden template: what you never see. When you type a message to ChatGPT or Claude, you never see the full text that goes to the model. Behind the scenes, your message gets wrapped in a template. What you type: 'What is photosynthesis?' What actually goes to the model: 'User: What is photosynthesis?
Assistant:'. The model then starts predicting what comes after 'Assistant:' one word at a time. ChatGPT uses special tokens: <|im_start|>system
You are a helpful assistant.
<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
What is photosynthesis?
<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant
Photosynthesis is the process by which plants...
<|im_end|>. Everything up through <|im_start|>assistant is template input; the response text and final <|im_end|> are generated by the model. The model learns during SFT that after a complete answer it should output <|im_end|> to stop. This is also why system prompts work: 'You are a helpful assistant' sits before the user's message; change it to 'You are a pirate' and the model changes how it speaks.
summary: User input is wrapped in a hidden chat template using special tokens like <|im_start|>, <|im_end|> with system/user/assistant roles - the model continues after 'assistant' and emits <|im_end|> to stop.

text: Why 100K examples is enough. Pretraining needed trillions of words. SFT needs only ~100K conversations. That feels wrong - why is so little data enough? Because SFT is not teaching new knowledge. The model already knows what photosynthesis is, already knows Python, already knows grammar. All of that was learned during pretraining and is stored deep in the weights. SFT only changes the surface behavior - the pattern of 'when you see User: followed by a question, respond with a clear, helpful answer.' That is a much simpler pattern than all of human language. Comparison table: Pretraining uses trillions of words, learns all of language (grammar, facts, code, reasoning, style), and builds the entire brain from scratch. SFT uses ~100K conversations, learns one pattern (question goes in, helpful answer comes out), and only nudges the surface weights slightly. SFT uses a very small learning rate so the deep weights that store facts barely move. Only the surface-level style changes - same knowledge, expressed as a helpful conversation instead of random internet text.
summary: ~100K SFT examples suffice because they only teach surface behavior (question -> helpful answer), not knowledge - which already lives in the deep weights from pretraining; tiny learning rate keeps deep weights nearly unchanged.

text: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) aligns an SFT-trained LLM with human preferences through four stages: (1) the SFT model generates multiple candidate responses for each prompt; (2) human annotators rank pairs of responses to build a preference dataset; (3) a separate reward model is trained on those pairwise comparisons to output a single quality score for any (prompt, response); (4) PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) loops Generate → Score → Compare to SFT model via KL divergence → Nudge weights, with the objective Final = Reward - (β x KL). The KL penalty (β around 0.1 to 0.5) keeps the policy close to the SFT reference and prevents reward hacking, where the LLM finds shortcuts that fool the reward model. RLHF goes beyond SFT because it can score novel responses, optimize fuzzy human values like helpful/safe/honest, and explicitly suppress harmful outputs.
summary: RLHF aligns LLMs with human values via a 4-step pipeline: generate, rank, train reward model, then PPO with a KL penalty to keep the policy close to the SFT model.

text: RLHF: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. After SFT the model is better but not perfect. It may be verbose, unsafe, or disagreeable. RLHF is a 4-step process that uses human judgments to train a reward model, then uses that reward model to optimize the LLM for qualities humans care about.
summary: Introduces RLHF as a 4-step process that uses human judgments to train a reward model and then optimize the LLM beyond what SFT alone achieves.

text: Step 1: Generate multiple responses. For a given prompt, the SFT model generates MULTIPLE candidate responses. Temperature controls diversity. Example prompt: 'What's the best programming language for beginners?' produces ranked candidates such as: #1 'Python is best - simple syntax, huge libraries, great community' (clear & actionable), #2 'Python or Go. Python for web, Go for systems. Depends on goals' (nuanced), #3 'JavaScript. Runs everywhere, but quirky syntax can confuse newcomers' (decent but flawed reasoning), #4 'Rust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors catch bugs early' (incorrect - too hard for beginners), #5 'I am an AI and cannot learn programming' (nonsensical).
summary: Step 1 of RLHF: the SFT model generates several diverse candidate responses per prompt, controlled by temperature, ranging from clear and helpful to nonsensical.

text: Step 2: Human annotators rank responses. Humans read pairs of responses and judge: 'Which is better?' This creates a dataset of pairwise comparisons. Example A: 'Python is best - simple syntax, huge libraries, great community' vs B: 'Python or Go. Python for web, Go for systems. Depends on goals.' → Winner: B (more nuanced, acknowledges tradeoffs). Example A: same Python answer vs B: 'Rust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors catch bugs early.' → Winner: A (A is actually beginner-friendly; B is too complex). From tens of thousands of such comparisons, we build training data for a reward model.
summary: Step 2 of RLHF: humans compare pairs of responses and pick a winner, producing tens of thousands of pairwise preference judgments.

text: Step 3: Train a reward model. The reward model is a separate neural network. Input: a prompt + a complete response (as one block of text). Output: one single number (e.g. 8.5) - higher = more helpful, safer, better. It learns from the human comparisons in Step 2. Each comparison is a training example: a winner response and a loser response for the same prompt. Examples: prompt 'Best language for beginners?' winner 'Python - simple syntax, huge community, tons of tutorials' vs loser 'Rust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors...' (Python is actually beginner-friendly); prompt 'Is the earth flat?' winner 'No, the Earth is roughly spherical. Here is the evidence...' vs loser 'There are many perspectives on this topic...' (direct and accurate; does not treat misinformation as legitimate). Training rule: the winner must get a higher score than the loser. After tens of thousands of pairs, the reward model learns to detect what makes a response helpful, accurate, and safe.
summary: Step 3 of RLHF: a separate neural network is trained to map (prompt, response) to a scalar score by enforcing that the human-preferred winner scores higher than the loser.

text: After training: the reward model can score any response. Examples scored on the same prompt: 'Python - simple, huge community.' → 8.5 (high); 'Depends... Python vs Go...' → 7.2 (high); 'Rust is safest.' → 2.1 (low); 'I cannot answer.' → 1.0 (low). Now we have a machine that can grade any response automatically. This is what PPO will use in Step 4 - instead of asking a human to grade every response (too slow and expensive), we ask the reward model (instant and free).
summary: Once trained, the reward model can automatically score any new response, replacing slow expensive human grading inside the PPO training loop.

text: Step 4: PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) - how the model actually improves. PPO is the training loop that uses the reward model to make the LLM better. One training round, step by step: (1) Generate - give the model a prompt like 'Best language for beginners?' and let it write a full response (e.g. 'Python is great for beginners because of its simple syntax and large community'). (2) Score - feed prompt + response into the reward model, which outputs e.g. 8.5/10. (3) Compare - the original SFT model (before PPO training started) also looks at the same prompt; we measure how different the PPO model's response is from what the SFT model would have said. This difference is the KL divergence. If the PPO model has drifted too far from the SFT model, we penalize it. (4) Nudge - if reward was high and the model has not drifted too far, nudge weights to make this response MORE likely; if reward was low, nudge to make it LESS likely; if drift is too far from SFT even with high reward, pull the model back. This loop repeats thousands of times.
summary: Step 4 of RLHF: the PPO loop repeats Generate → Score (via reward model) → Compare to SFT via KL divergence → Nudge weights, thousands of times.

text: What is KL (Kullback-Leibler) divergence? KL divergence measures how different the PPO model's word predictions are from the original SFT model's predictions. Real comparison for the first word after 'Best language for beginners?': SFT model (original) probabilities - Python 40%, I 15%, The 12%, others 33%. PPO model (after 500 rounds) - Python 85%, I 3%, The 2%, others 10%. The SFT distribution was spread out (40, 15, 12...). The PPO distribution is concentrated (85% on one word). These look very different, so the KL divergence is high: 1.8.
summary: KL divergence quantifies how different the PPO model's next-word probability distribution is from the frozen SFT reference; concentrated PPO outputs versus spread SFT outputs give high KL.

text: The formula: Final Score = Reward - (β x KL). Reward = how good is this response (from the reward model, higher = better). KL = how far has the model drifted from the SFT model (higher = more different). β (beta) = a dial engineers set, typically 0.1 to 0.5; higher β = stricter penalty for drifting. Three worked examples with β = 0.2: (1) 'Python - simple syntax, huge community, tons of tutorials.' Reward 8.5, KL 0.02 → Final = 8.5 - (0.2 x 0.02) = 8.5 - 0.004 = 8.50. Good response, barely drifted, almost full reward kept. (2) 'Just learn Python I guess, whatever works, who cares.' Reward 3.1, KL 0.15 → Final = 3.1 - 0.03 = 3.07. Lazy response, low reward, small nudge. (3) 'PYTHON PYTHON PYTHON PYTHON BEST BEST BEST!!!' Reward 9.2, KL 24.0 → Final = 9.2 - 4.8 = 4.40. Reward hacking - high score but model went crazy; KL penalty wipes out the reward. Why KL is necessary: without it, the model finds shortcuts. The reward model is not perfect and has blind spots. The PPO model can discover responses that trick the reward model into giving high scores even though humans would hate them - repeating keywords, overconfident tone, filler the reward model likes. The KL penalty says 'you can improve, but you cannot become a completely different model.'
summary: The PPO objective Final = Reward - (β x KL) balances reward gains against drift from the SFT reference; β tunes strictness and the KL penalty is essential to prevent reward hacking.

text: Why RLHF matters: what it fixes that SFT cannot. SFT teaches the model to follow instructions by showing it good examples, but it can only learn from the exact examples humans wrote. RLHF goes further. Three fixes: (1) SFT can only copy the style of examples it saw → the reward model can score any new response the model invents, even ones no human ever wrote, so the model can discover better responses than exist in SFT data. (2) You cannot write a formula for 'helpful' → cross-entropy measures prediction accuracy, but 'helpful', 'safe', 'honest' are fuzzy human judgments; the reward model learns these from human comparisons and PPO optimizes for them. (3) SFT model can still produce harmful responses → humans rate unsafe responses low; the reward model learns to give them low scores; PPO nudges weights away from generating such content.
summary: RLHF surpasses SFT by letting the reward model score novel responses, by capturing fuzzy values like helpful/safe/honest that have no formula, and by explicitly suppressing harmful outputs.

text: This chapter introduces Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) as a simpler alternative to RLHF. RLHF needs four models (policy, reference, reward, value) and an unstable PPO RL loop. DPO skips the reward model and RL loop entirely, training directly on human preference pairs (prompt, winner, loser) with a single supervised loss: Loss = -log(sigmoid(beta x gap)), where gap = log(pi_current/pi_reference) for the preferred response minus the same for the rejected response. A frozen reference model prevents drift. Beta (typically 0.1 to 0.5) acts as a safety leash - low beta is aggressive, high beta is conservative. Worked example with P=0.045 vs 0.030 (preferred) and P=0.015 vs 0.025 (rejected) gives gap = +0.92 and loss = 0.649 at beta = 0.1. DPO achieves quality within 1-2% of RLHF using half the models and a single hyperparameter. Variants IPO (squared loss), KTO (single thumbs up/down signal), and ORPO (combined SFT + alignment) extend the approach.
summary: DPO replaces RLHF's reward-model-plus-PPO pipeline with one supervised log-sigmoid loss over preference pairs, controlled by beta.

text: RLHF aligns an LLM with human values but requires four models held in GPU memory at once: (1) Policy model - the LLM being trained, generates responses; (2) Reference model - frozen copy of the policy before RLHF, prevents drift; (3) Reward model - separate neural net that scores how good a response is; (4) Value model - another neural net that estimates future rewards for PPO. On top of the four models, RL training (PPO) is unstable: the reward model can be gamed, hyperparameters are tricky, and training often crashes. The motivating question: can we get the same alignment result with less machinery?
summary: RLHF is powerful but needs four models in GPU memory plus unstable PPO training.

text: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO, Rafailov et al., 2023) skips the reward model entirely. Insight: instead of training a separate judge to score responses and then running complex RL to optimize for those scores, directly adjust the model to prefer what humans preferred. Analogy: RLHF says hire a food critic, score every dish, then optimize cooking to maximize the critic's score. DPO says just ask diners which dish they liked better and directly learn from that preference. Pipeline comparison - RLHF: Preferences -> Train reward model -> RL loop (PPO) -> Better policy (3 training stages, 4 models). DPO: Preferences -> Better policy (1 training stage, 2 models). DPO folds the reward model and RL loop into a single supervised loss function.
summary: DPO collapses RLHF's reward-model-plus-PPO pipeline into one supervised loss using preferences directly.

text: One DPO training step uses the same human preference pairs RLHF already collects. Example - Prompt: "What's the best programming language for beginners?" Preferred (Winner): "Python - simple syntax, huge libraries, great community." Rejected (Loser): "Rust. Memory safety. Compile-time errors catch bugs early." A human said: "The Python answer is better." That single judgment is ALL DPO needs for this training step - no scores, no rankings, just "A is better than B." What DPO will do: nudge the model to assign higher probability to the Python answer and lower probability to the Rust answer, but not too much, so the model does not go off the rails.
summary: DPO needs only preference pairs: a prompt with a chosen winner and rejected loser response.

text: DPO compares the current model against a frozen reference copy (the model before DPO training) to prevent over-change. For each response we ask: how much did the model's probability change relative to the reference? Reference Model (frozen): P(Python answer) = 0.030, P(Rust answer) = 0.025. Probabilities are tiny because the model distributes across thousands of possible responses. Current Model (training): P(Python answer) = 0.045, P(Rust answer) = 0.015 - model boosted preferred, reduced rejected. Preferred log-ratio: log(0.045 / 0.030) = log(1.50) = +0.41. Positive - model boosted the preferred response by 50% relative to reference. Rejected log-ratio: log(0.015 / 0.025) = log(0.60) = -0.51. Negative - model reduced the rejected response by 40%. The gap: (+0.41) - (-0.51) = +0.92. Positive gap means the model is simultaneously boosting what humans preferred AND reducing what they rejected.
summary: DPO computes a log-probability ratio between current and reference model for both winner and loser, then takes their difference as the gap.

text: The full DPO loss formula: Loss = -log( sigmoid( beta x gap ) ), where gap = log(pi/pi_ref) for preferred - log(pi/pi_ref) for rejected. Plugging in our numbers with beta = 0.1: (1) The gap = +0.92. (2) Scale by beta: 0.1 x 0.92 = 0.092. (3) Apply sigmoid: sigmoid(0.092) = 0.523. (4) Take -log: -log(0.523) = 0.649. Final Loss = 0.649.
