Late pairs (d126, d127) rotate SLOW: low frequency, sensitive to distant position differences - 'Is this word 500 or 1000 positions away?'. The frequency formula is the same idea as sinusoidal PE: f_i = 1 / 10000^(2i/d), where i is the pair index and d is the total dimension. Pair 0 rotates fastest, pair 63 rotates slowest.
summary: RoPE applies independent 2D rotations to each (x,y) dimension pair, with frequencies decreasing geometrically from pair 0 to pair 63.

text: Why RoPE won. Comparison table - Sinusoidal vs Learned PE vs RoPE. Type: Absolute / Absolute / Relative. Applied to: Embedding (add) / Embedding (add) / Q and K only (rotate). Max length: Fixed at training / Fixed at training / Can extrapolate. Extrapolation: Fails beyond max / Fails beyond max / Works with scaling tricks. Parameters: 0 (fixed formula) / pos x dim / 0 (fixed formula). RoPE extrapolation - NTK-aware scaling: if a model trained on 4096 tokens needs to handle 16384, you can scale the rotation frequencies by a factor. The 'NTK-aware' variant adjusts different frequency bands differently - slowing down the fast-rotating pairs while keeping the slow ones nearly unchanged. This extends context length 4x or more with minimal quality loss. Models using RoPE: LLaMA, LLaMA 2, LLaMA 3, Mistral, Qwen, GPT-NeoX, PaLM. Nearly every modern open-source LLM uses RoPE. It has become the default choice for position encoding since 2022.
summary: RoPE wins because it is relative, zero-parameter, extrapolates via NTK-aware scaling, and is used in nearly every modern LLM (LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, PaLM).

text: This chapter sets up the core problem attention solves: word embeddings are static, so the same word gets the exact same vector regardless of surrounding words. The chapter uses 'bank' (river bank vs financial bank) and 'love' ('I love cats' vs 'love is blind') to show meaning depends on context. The goal of attention: take a fixed embedding like love = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1] and transform it into a NEW vector that represents the word in its specific context, by letting each word look around, find which other words are relevant, and absorb information from them.
summary: Why attention is needed: embeddings are context-free, but word meaning depends on neighbors.

text: Words alone are meaningless. Context is everything. After embedding, every instance of the same word gets the exact same vector. But the same word can mean completely different things depending on surrounding words.
summary: States the core problem: identical embeddings for the same word ignore context.

text: Two sentences with the word 'bank'. Sentence 1: 'I sat by the river bank' — context words sat, by, river → 'bank' means 'edge of a river'. Sentence 2: 'I deposited money in the bank' — context words sat, by (highlighted as deposited, money) → 'bank' means 'financial institution'. Same word 'bank', identical embedding, but the surrounding words completely change its meaning. Switchable buttons let the learner toggle between sentence 1 and sentence 2 to see how context words determine which meaning of 'bank' applies.
summary: Worked example showing 'bank' has two meanings selected entirely by surrounding context words.

text: This isn't just about 'bank' - it's about every word. The model has a big dictionary (learned during training) that maps every word to a fixed list of numbers. Take 'love': after embedding, 'love' always gets the same numbers, no matter what sentence it's in. But 'love' in 'I love cats' is different from 'love' in 'love is blind.' These numbers capture the word's meaning in isolation. It has no idea what words are around it. That's a problem - because we need context. The goal: take 'love' = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1] and transform it into a NEW list of numbers that represents 'love in the context of I and cats.'
summary: Generalizes the problem to every word and states attention's goal: transform isolated embedding into a contextual vector.

text: This is what Attention solves. It lets each word look around at the other words and ask 'which other words are relevant to me?' and then absorb context — absorbing information from the relevant ones. After attention: 'love' in 'I love cats' absorbs info from 'I' and 'cats' → now represents 'affection from me toward cats'. Same input vector, different output vector depending on context.
summary: Defines what attention does: each word looks around, picks relevant words, absorbs their info to produce a context-aware output vector.

text: This chapter — How Does a Word Look At Others? Attention mimics how humans read: when seeing 'it', your brain scans for what it refers to, focusing on nouns rather than every word equally. Attention computes a relevance score for every other word in the sentence. Worked example uses 'The cat sat because it was tired' — when 'it' is selected, 'cat' receives 55% attention (highest), so the model figures out 'it' = 'cat'. The chapter ends by motivating the dot product as the mathematical tool to measure relevance between two vectors.
summary: Attention computes a relevance score for every word, like how reading 'it' scans for the referent noun.

text: Think about how YOU read. When you see 'it' in a sentence, your brain scans for what 'it' refers to. You focus on nouns, not every word equally. Attention does the same thing — computes a relevance score for every other word.
summary: Attention works like human reading: scan all words and assign a relevance score to each.

text: Interactive demo with sentence: 'The cat sat because it was tired'. Click any word to see what it attends to. When 'it' is selected, the attention scores across all 7 words are: The 5%, cat 55%, sat 10%, because 5%, it 5%, was 10%, tired 10%. 'cat' receives the maximum 55%, highlighted yellow/orange. Other examples: 'The' attends most to 'cat' (30%); 'cat' attends most to 'sat' (35%); 'sat' attends most to 'cat' (40%); 'because' attends most to 'it' and 'tired' (25% each); 'was' attends most to 'it' (30%) and 'tired' (25%); 'tired' attends most to 'cat' (35%). When 'it' is selected → 'cat' gets 55%. The model figures out 'it' = 'cat'.
summary: Concrete example: 'it' attends 55% to 'cat' in 'The cat sat because it was tired', resolving the pronoun.
terms: The cat sat because it was tired

text: But how do we compute these scores mathematically? We need a tool for measuring 'relevance' between two vectors. Enter: the dot product.
summary: Motivates the dot product as the math tool for measuring relevance between two word vectors.

text: This chapter introduces the dot product as the mathematical tool that measures similarity between two vectors. Given vectors A and B, multiply matching pairs and sum: e.g. A=[1,2,3], B=[4,5,6] gives 1x4 + 2x5 + 3x6 = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. The resulting single number reflects directional similarity: vectors pointing the same way produce a large positive number, opposite directions produce a large negative number, and perpendicular (unrelated) vectors produce a value near zero. Attention needs this tool to score relevance between words. However, the chapter ends by warning that we cannot dot-product raw word embeddings directly: words like 'I' (pronoun) and 'love' (verb) are related yet have dissimilar raw meanings, so we must compare them on a different basis - this motivates Query, Key, and Value.
summary: Dot product multiplies pairs and sums to get one number measuring directional similarity, the math tool attention uses for relevance scoring.

text: Here's the problem: we have three lists of numbers (one per word). We need a mathematical operation that computes 'how relevant is word A to word B?' The tool is the dot product - multiply pairs, add them up. Big result means related. Small result means unrelated.
summary: Sets up the need for a math operation that scores relevance between two word vectors and names the dot product as that tool.

text: Given two vectors, multiply pairs and sum. A = [1, 2, 3], B = [4, 5, 6]. Multiply each pair: 1x4 = 4, 2x5 = 10, 3x6 = 18. Sum them up: 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. That's it - multiply pairs, add them up, get one number.
summary: Worked example computing the dot product of [1,2,3] and [4,5,6] step by step to arrive at 32.

text: What does this number mean? It measures how similar two vectors are in direction. Think of you and a friend both pointing somewhere. Same direction (-> ->): large positive value, 'these are similar!' Opposite directions (-> <-): large negative value, 'these are opposites!' Perpendicular and unrelated (-> up): close to zero, 'nothing in common.' The dot product captures directional alignment between vectors.
summary: Explains the geometric meaning of the dot product as a directional alignment score: positive=same way, negative=opposite, ~zero=perpendicular.

text: But we cannot just dot-product the raw word numbers directly. 'I' is a pronoun, 'love' is a verb - their raw meanings aren't similar, but they ARE related (I is the one who loves). We need to compare them on a different basis. This sets up why we transform embeddings into Query, Key, and Value before computing the dot product.
summary: Warns that raw embeddings can't be dot-producted directly because related words may have dissimilar raw meanings, motivating Q/K/V projections.

text: This chapter explains why raw word embeddings cannot be dot-producted directly to measure attention relevance. Words like 'I' (pronoun) and 'love' (verb) have different raw meanings so a direct dot product is low, yet they are strongly related ('I' is the one loving). The fix: compare on a different basis - what each word is LOOKING FOR vs what each word OFFERS. 'love' looks for 'who is doing the loving?' (a subject, a person), and 'I' offers 'I am a subject, a person' - HIGH MATCH. This motivates needing three separate views of each word: Query, Key, Value.
summary: Why raw embedding dot products fail and why words need separate Query/Key/Value views.

text: We can't just dot product the raw word numbers. In 'I love cats': 'I' = pronoun, 'love' = verb, 'cats' = noun. Their raw meanings aren't similar at all - the dot product of 'I' and 'love' would be low. Tags: 'I' = pronoun, self-reference; 'love' = verb, emotion. But they are related - 'I' is the one who loves! Their meanings are different, but their relationship is strong. Raw dot product can't capture this.
summary: Raw embeddings of related words like 'I' and 'love' have low dot product because their meanings differ even though their relationship is strong.

text: We need to compare on a different basis. Instead of comparing what words mean, compare what one is LOOKING FOR vs what the other OFFERS. Example: 'love' is looking for: 'who is doing the loving?' (a subject, a person). 'I' OFFERS: 'I am a subject, a person' - HIGH MATCH! Not 'what do these words mean?' but 'what is one looking for vs what does the other offer?'
summary: Reframe attention as comparing what one word is looking for against what another word offers, producing high matches like love seeking a subject and I offering a subject.

text: That's why we need three separate views of each word.
summary: Closing motivation: each word must be projected into three separate views (Q, K, V).

text: This chapter introduces the classroom analogy for Query, Key, and Value in attention. Imagine a classroom of 30 students. Student Riya has a question: 'who has yesterday's notes?' She looks around at everyone. Riya's question is the Query (what am I looking for?). Each other student's label or advertisement (e.g., Priya: 'I know the homework', Aman: 'I have yesterday's notes', Rahul: 'I know the canteen menu') is a Key (what can I be found for?). Aman matches, so he hands over the actual notes - physics equations, dates, diagrams - that real content is the Value (here's my actual info). Mapping back to attention: Query asks the question, Key advertises matchability, Value carries the real content delivered after a match.
summary: Classroom analogy maps Query to Riya's question, Key to each student's label, and Value to the actual notes handed over.

text: Imagine a classroom. 30 students. Student Riya has a question: 'who has yesterday's notes?' She looks around at everyone.
summary: Sets up the classroom scene: Riya wants yesterday's notes and scans 30 classmates.

text: Visual mapping. Riya (👩‍🎓) with her question 'Who has yesterday's notes?' is labeled Query. Three other students each have a label: Priya: 'I know the homework' - no match. Aman: 'I have yesterday's notes' - MATCH (✓). Rahul: 'I know the canteen menu' - no match. Each student's label is a Key. After the match, Aman (📦) hands over the actual notes - 'Physics equations, dates, diagrams - the real content.' That handoff is the Value.
summary: Diagram tags Riya's question as Query, each student's offering as Key, and Aman's handed-over notes as Value, with one matching student.

text: Mapping to attention. Riya's question (who has the notes?) corresponds to Query - 'what am I looking for?' (🔍). Each student's label (what they can help with) corresponds to Key - 'what can I be found for?' (🏷️). Notes handed over (the actual content) corresponds to Value - 'here's my actual info' (📦).
summary: Explicit table mapping Riya's question, student labels, and handed-over notes to Query, Key, and Value with their guiding questions.

text: This chapter - Every Word is Both Asker and Answerer. Core idea: in self-attention, every token produces a Query, a Key, and a Value simultaneously. Using the classroom analogy, Riya, Aman, and Priya each have their own question (Query), their own label of what they offer (Key), and their own actual information (Value). The same is true for words in a Transformer: in "The cat sat because it was tired", "cat" asks its own question while also advertising itself as a noun-subject and carrying its own semantic content. This dual role is what makes self-attention symmetric across tokens, and motivates the next chapter's question of why Key and Value must remain separate.
summary: Chapter-level summary: in self-attention every token simultaneously plays asker (Q), advertiser (K), and information-carrier (V).

text: Here's what confuses people: in the classroom analogy, every student is both an asker and an answerer simultaneously. Riya has a Query (her question), but she also has a Key (what she can offer others) and a Value (her actual info). Aman has a Key and Value, but also has his own Query (maybe he's looking for homework help). Each participant produces all three roles at the same time.
summary: Every student in the attention classroom plays asker and answerer simultaneously, producing Query, Key, and Value at once.

text: Every student produces all three. Riya: Query "who has notes?", Key "I know Hindi grammar", Value her Hindi grammar knowledge. Aman: Query "who can help with homework?", Key "I have yesterday's notes", Value his actual notes content. Priya: Query "who knows the schedule?", Key "I know the homework", Value her homework answers. Each row shows one person carrying a Query, a Key, and a Value at the same time.
summary: Concrete table showing Riya, Aman, and Priya each producing their own Query, Key, and Value simultaneously.

text: Same thing happens with words in a Transformer. In the sentence "The cat sat because it was tired", every word simultaneously produces a Query, Key, and Value. "cat" asks its own question while also advertising itself and carrying its own information. The classroom dynamic maps directly onto tokens: each token is asker, answerer, and information-bearer at once.
summary: Mapping the classroom analogy onto a Transformer: every word in a sentence produces its own Q, K, and V simultaneously.
terms: The cat sat because it was tired

text: Each student - and each word - simultaneously asks a question and advertises itself and carries information. But why do we need Key and Value to be separate? Why can't they be the same thing? This sets up the next chapter on why K and V must differ.
summary: Recap that every word plays asker, advertiser, and information-carrier, raising the question of why Key and Value must be separate.

text: This chapter explains why Key and Value must be separate vectors in attention. Uses a restaurant analogy: the menu description is the Key (what helps you match your craving to a dish), the actual food on the plate is the Value (what you actually consume). If Key and Value were the same, you would get a plate with the menu description printed on it - useless. Connecting back to the Transformer with 'The cat sat because it was tired': cat's Key encodes 'I'm a noun, subject, animate' which helps 'it' find cat; cat's Value carries the rich semantic content (small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated) that 'it' actually absorbs. Key is optimized for matching, Value is optimized for content delivery.
summary: Why Key and Value must be separate: Key is for matching (menu description), Value is the actual content (food on plate).

text: Think about ordering food at a restaurant. The menu description = Key: 'Paneer Tikka Masala - rich tomato-based gravy, smoky grilled paneer, mildly spiced, served with naan'. This helped you decide - yes, this matches what I'm craving. It's optimized for matching your preference (your Query) to the right dish. The actual food on your plate = Value: the smoky charred paneer cubes, the creamy gravy, the aroma, the nourishment. This is the actual content you receive. What you actually consume.
summary: Restaurant analogy: menu description is the Key (used for matching), actual food is the Value (the real content delivered).

text: If Key and Value were the same thing: the waiter brings a plate with the words 'rich tomato-based gravy, smoky grilled paneer, mildly spiced, served with naan' printed on it. That's useless! You wanted the ACTUAL FOOD, not the description.
summary: If K equaled V you'd receive the menu text printed on a plate instead of the real food - illustrating why they must differ.

text: Now connect this back to the Transformer. Take the sentence: 'The cat sat because it was tired.' The word 'it' needs to find what it refers to. It finds 'cat'. cat's Key (the menu description) encodes: 'I'm a noun, I'm a subject, I'm animate' - this is what helped 'it' find 'cat' in the first place. Optimized for matching. cat's Value (the actual food) carries: rich semantic content - small, furry, four-legged, alive, domesticated - this is the actual information that 'it' absorbs after finding the match. Key helped 'it' FIND 'cat' (menu description). Value is what 'it' actually GOT from 'cat' (the actual food).
summary: Applied to 'The cat sat because it was tired': cat's Key encodes grammatical match info; cat's Value carries the rich semantic content 'it' absorbs.

text: This chapter frames Q, K, V through a Google Search analogy. Your typed search string is the Query. Each web page's keywords/tags are the Key. Google matching the query against page keywords is the dot product. The matched page's actual content (what you read) is the Value. The chapter then unifies three analogies covering the same Q/K/V concept: Classroom (question / label / notes), Restaurant (craving / menu description / actual food), and Google (search query / page keywords / page content). Next: compute full attention with real numbers.
summary: Google Search analogy maps Q=search query, K=page keywords, dot product=matching, V=page content; unified with classroom and restaurant analogies.

text: Another way to see Q, K, V is Google Search, broken into 4 steps. Step 1: Your search query, e.g. "best pizza near me" = Query. Step 2: Each page's keywords/tags, e.g. "pizza delivery NYC", "pizza recipes" = Key. Step 3: Google matches query against keywords to find the best-matching pages = Dot Product. Step 4: The matched page's CONTENT, the actual text you read = Value. So the user's typed search string acts as the Query, page metadata/tags act as Keys, the matching score is computed by a dot product, and what you actually consume (page body content) is the Value.
summary: Step-by-step mapping of a Google search to attention: typed query=Query, page tags=Key, matching=dot product, page content=Value.

text: Three analogies, same concept. Classroom: question / label / notes (Riya's question = Query, each student's label = Key, the notes Aman hands over = Value). Restaurant: craving / menu description / actual food (your craving = Query, menu text = Key, the actual dish on the plate = Value). Google: search query / page keywords / page content (typed search = Query, page tags = Key, page text = Value). All three illustrate the same triple: something does the asking (Query), something advertises matchability (Key), something carries the actual information that gets absorbed (Value). Next step: compute full attention step by step with real numbers.
summary: Recap of three parallel analogies (classroom, restaurant, google) all mapping cleanly to Query, Key, and Value.

text: This chapter explains how Q, K, V vectors are created from a word's embedding. Each word starts as a single embedding vector (e.g., "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]). To get three different views (Query, Key, Value), the embedding is multiplied by three different W matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each W matrix is just a grid of numbers (a table/spreadsheet). Multiplying input by a table produces an output. Analogy: a photo with three filters - each filter highlights a different feature (eyes, nose, mouth). For real Transformers (GPT-style), embedding is 512 numbers, each W matrix is 512×64 = 32,768 numbers, producing 64-number Q/K/V vectors per head.
summary: How Q, K, V vectors are produced by multiplying a word's embedding through three learned weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V.

text: So far, Q, K, V are just concepts in our head. We know each word needs: Query - "what am I looking for?", Key - "what do I offer?", Value - "what info do I carry?". But each word starts as just one list of numbers - its embedding. For example: "love" = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1]. That's one list. We need three different lists from it (Query, Key, Value). How do you get three different things from one thing? You transform it three different ways. And the tool for each transformation is: a grid of numbers.
summary: Sets up the puzzle: a word starts with one embedding vector but needs three different vectors (Q, K, V), so we must transform it three ways.

text: The grid of numbers is called a W matrix (W = "weights"). It's just a table of numbers. Like a spreadsheet. Rows and columns filled with numbers. Example: a tiny W grid: 0.02, -0.15, 0.31, 0.08, 0.41, 0.07, -0.22, -0.19, -0.09, 0.33, 0.14, 0.27, 0.18, -0.05, 0.42, 0.11. When you multiply a word's embedding by this grid, you get a new, smaller list of numbers - the Query (or Key, or Value, depending on which grid). Multiply input by a table → get an output. Nothing more complicated than that.
summary: Defines a W matrix as just a table of weights; multiplying an embedding by it produces a Q, K, or V vector.

text: Think of it like a photo with three filters. You have a photo of a person and want three different views. View 1: highlight only the eyes → captures where the person is looking. View 2: highlight only the nose → captures the nose shape. View 3: highlight only the mouth → captures the expression. How do you create these views? Apply three different filters. Each filter is a grid of numbers that, when multiplied with the photo, produces a different view. That's EXACTLY what W_Q, W_K, W_V are. Word embedding: [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] ← the "photo". Grid 1 (W_Q): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what am I looking for?". Grid 2 (W_K): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what do I offer?". Grid 3 (W_V): a 4×2 grid - filter for "what info do I carry?". Multiply the same embedding by Grid 1 → Query (2-number list). By Grid 2 → Key. By Grid 3 → Value.
summary: Photo-with-three-filters analogy: each W matrix is a filter highlighting a different aspect of the same embedding to produce Q, K, or V.

text: Now let's see the actual three grids in action: same word embedding goes through three different grids - each produces a different output.
summary: Transitions to showing the three grids transforming the same embedding into three distinct outputs.

text: "love" embedding = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1, ..., 0.3] - 512 numbers representing "love". Multiply by 3 different grids. Grid 1: W_Q → produces Query. Emphasizes: "what this word NEEDS from others". Output: 64 numbers. Grid 2: W_K → produces Key. Emphasizes: "what this word can be MATCHED on". Output: 64 numbers. Grid 3: W_V → produces Value. Emphasizes: "what useful INFO this word carries". Output: 64 numbers. Each W matrix takes a 512-number embedding and outputs 64 numbers.
summary: Concrete example: 512-dim 'love' embedding flows through W_Q, W_K, W_V to produce three 64-number vectors with distinct semantic emphases.

text: Same input (embedding), three different outputs depending on which grid you use. Each grid is 512×64 = 32,768 numbers. But where do these numbers come from?
summary: Recap that one embedding plus three 512×64 grids (32,768 weights each) gives three distinct outputs, teasing the next question of where the weights come from.

text: SVG diagram showing three arrows fanning out from a 4-number embedding down to Q, K, and V. The center arrow is labeled x W_Q (blue), the middle x W_K (orange), the right x W_V (green). Each arrow leads to a separate 2-number bar: Query [0.3, 0.7], Key [0.9, 0.4], Value [0.6, -0.2]. Illustrates three parallel compressed views of the same embedding.
summary: Diagram of three projection arrows fanning from one embedding into Q, K, and V vectors via separate W matrices.

text: SVG diagram showing two arrows fanning from one 4-number embedding to two attention heads. Left arrow labeled x W_Q1 (cyan) leads to Head 1 Q1 = [0.3, 0.7]. Right arrow labeled x W_Q2 (pink) leads to Head 2 Q2 = [-0.4, 0.2]. Each head has its own W_Q matrix and produces a different 2-number Query from the same embedding.
summary: Diagram of one embedding feeding two parallel heads, each with its own W_Q producing a different 2-number Query.

text: This chapter explains why Q, K, V are smaller than the embedding. W_Q is a rectangular 4x2 (or 512x64 in real GPT) projection that down-projects the embedding into a compressed query space. The embedding itself keeps its full d_model size and continues through the network; Q, K, V are temporary lenses used only inside attention. The smaller size exists because multi-head attention splits a fixed output budget across many parallel heads: d_k = d_model / num_heads. After all heads finish, their outputs concatenate back to d_model and pass through W_O into the residual stream.
summary: Chapter overview: Q/K/V are smaller than the embedding because multi-head attention divides d_model across heads, with d_k = d_model / num_heads.

text: The puzzle: why is W_Q shaped 4x2, not 4x4? In the prior chapter W_Q was written as a 4x2 grid. If the embedding has 4 numbers, shouldn't the query also have 4 numbers? Short answer: no. Q (and K and V) are smaller than the embedding. W_Q's job is to project the 4-number embedding down to a 2-number query. The input and output sizes do not have to match. Concrete shapes: embedding [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] (4 numbers) times W_Q (4x2 matrix) = query [0.3, 0.7] (2 numbers). 4 rows = input size, 2 columns = output size.
summary: Sets up why W_Q is a rectangular 4x2 projection rather than square, mapping a 4-number embedding down to a 2-number query.

text: The embedding stays big. Q, K, V are small views. Think of the embedding as the word's full profile - it stays 4 numbers long for the entire journey through the Transformer. Q, K, V are three compressed lenses on that profile, built only for the attention step. Three different W matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V), three different 2-number views. Example: embedding [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8] becomes Query [0.3, 0.7], Key [0.9, 0.4], Value [0.6, -0.2]. The 4-number embedding itself is untouched; it continues through the rest of the network in its full original size.
summary: Explains that the embedding keeps its full size through the Transformer while Q, K, V are temporary compressed projections only used inside attention.

text: Why smaller? Multi-head attention splits the budget. The Transformer runs several heads in parallel on the same embedding. Each head gets its own W_Q, W_K, W_V and works independently, looking at the embedding through its own 2-number lens. Two heads, each with W_Q (4x2). Each produces a different 2-number Query because W_Q1 and W_Q2 learned different things. Example: Head 1 Q1 = [0.3, 0.7], Head 2 Q2 = [-0.4, 0.2]. After both heads do their attention, outputs are concatenated back to 4 numbers total (2 + 2) and fed forward. Budget intuition: fixed 4 output numbers per token. Options: one big head with 4-number Q, or two small heads each with 2-number Q. Multi-head picks the second: more heads, each specialized. Formula: d_k = d_model / num_heads.
summary: Reveals that Q/K/V are small because the embedding budget is split across multiple parallel attention heads, with d_k = d_model / num_heads.

text: Real Transformer numbers (GPT-style). Scale everything up. The chapter's 4 becomes 512. The chapter's 2 becomes 64. The chapter's 2 heads become 8. This chapter (teaching): d_model = 4 (embedding size), num_heads = 2, d_k = 4 / 2 = 2 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 4 x 2. Real GPT (typical): d_model = 512 (embedding size), num_heads = 8, d_k = 512 / 8 = 64 (Q/K/V per head), W_Q per head = 512 x 64. 8 heads x 64 numbers each = 512 numbers total, which fits exactly back into the embedding size. The budget is preserved across heads.
summary: Worked example scaling the teaching numbers up to real GPT-style values: d_model 512, 8 heads, d_k 64, W_Q 512x64.

text: Shapes recap. Embedding: d_model (4 here, 512 in real) - lives through the whole Transformer. Q, K, V (per head): d_k (2 here, 64 in real) - temporary, only inside attention. W_Q, W_K, W_V (per head): d_model x d_k (4x2 here, 512x64 in real) - projects embedding to Q / K / V. Multi-head concat + W_O: back to d_model (4 here, 512 in real) - returns to the main residual stream. Bottom line: W_Q is not square. It's a rectangular projection from embedding space into a smaller query space. The embedding keeps its original size; the query is a compressed view used only for the attention computation.
summary: Recap table of every shape in attention: embedding stays d_model, Q/K/V are d_k per head, W matrices are d_model by d_k, concat plus W_O returns to d_model.

text: This chapter — W Matrices: Learned During Training. The Q/K/V projection matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V are not designed by humans. They start as random numbers on day 1. Training repeats billions of times: model predicts next word, computes error, error flows backward through every grid entry via backpropagation, each number gets nudged a tiny amount (e.g. -0.09 → -0.07). After billions of nudges, the random noise settles into meaningful values: W_Q produces Queries ("what am I looking for?"), W_K produces Keys ("what do I offer?"), W_V produces Values ("what info do I carry?"). Nobody programmed the entries; they emerged from gradient descent.
summary: W_Q, W_K, W_V start random and are nudged by backpropagation over billions of examples until they produce meaningful Q, K, V projections.

text: Where did those numbers in the grids come from? Last chapter we saw three grids W_Q, W_K, W_V — each a table of numbers. Multiply an embedding by a grid, get Q or K or V. But who decided what numbers go in those grids? Visual: three boxes labeled W_Q (search icon), W_K (tag icon), W_V (package icon), each filled with question marks indicating unknown entries. Answer: nobody decided. The numbers were learned during training.
summary: Poses the question of where the numbers inside W_Q, W_K, W_V come from and previews the answer: they are learned, not hand-designed.

text: Day 1: Start with random garbage. Before training begins, every grid is filled with random numbers from a random number generator. Visual shows three 4×4 grids labeled W_Q (random), W_K (random), W_V (random) with values like 0.47, -0.82, 0.15, -0.63, 0.91, -0.24, 0.38, -0.71, 0.56, -0.09, 0.77, -0.45, -0.33, 0.62, -0.88, 0.19. With random grids the model computes: random W_Q × embedding = garbage Query; random W_K × embedding = garbage Key; random W_V × embedding = garbage Value. Garbage Q, K, V → garbage attention → garbage predictions. The model is terrible at this point. And that's completely fine.
summary: Before training, W_Q, W_K, W_V are filled with random numbers, so the resulting Q, K, V vectors and predictions are nonsense — and that's the expected starting point.

text: The training loop: predict, fail, nudge. Model reads millions of sentences and tries to predict the next word each time. Steps every attempt: 1) Model sees: "The cat sat on the". 2) Uses its current (bad) grids to compute attention. 3) Predicts next word: "elephant" (wrong! should be "mat"). 4) Calculates error: how wrong was that prediction? 5) Error flows backward through every grid number. 6) Each number gets nudged slightly to reduce the error. This loop repeats billions of times. Every repetition nudges the numbers a tiny bit closer to useful values.
summary: Six-step training loop where the model predicts, computes error, propagates it backward, and nudges every W matrix entry — repeated billions of times.

text: Zoom in: what does one nudge look like? Look at a single number in W_Q, row 3 column 2. Before: -0.09. After: -0.07. Change: +0.02. Reason: the error signal says "if this number were slightly larger, the Query for 'cat' would have paid more attention to 'sat' — and the prediction would have been a little better." That's it. Just +0.02. A tiny nudge. But multiply that by every number in every grid, across billions of training examples, and the grids slowly transform from random noise into something meaningful.
summary: Concrete example of one weight update: W_Q row 3 col 2 moves from -0.09 to -0.07 because the error signal says a slightly larger value would have improved the prediction.

text: After billions of examples: the grids have settled. The random numbers have been nudged millions of times into meaningful values. Visual: BEFORE (random) W_Q with values 0.47, -0.82, 0.15, -0.63, 0.91, -0.24, 0.38, -0.71, 0.56, -0.09, 0.77, -0.45, -0.33, 0.62, -0.88, 0.19 → AFTER (trained) W_Q with values 0.02, -0.15, 0.31, 0.08, 0.41, 0.07, -0.22, -0.19, -0.09, 0.33, 0.14, 0.27, 0.18, -0.05, 0.42, 0.11. Result: W_Q now produces Queries that correctly capture "what am I looking for?"; W_K produces Keys that capture "what do I offer?"; W_V produces Values that capture "what info do I carry?".
summary: Side-by-side before/after grids show how random W_Q values settle into trained values that produce meaningful Queries, Keys, and Values.

text: The key insight: nobody programmed these numbers. No human sat down and said "row 3, column 2 should be 0.14." The numbers emerged from the nudging process (backpropagation) over billions of examples. This is what "learned" means in machine learning. The model figured out, on its own, what numbers to put in each grid so that the resulting Q, K, V vectors produce useful attention patterns. Random noise in → billions of nudges → meaningful grids out.
summary: Defines what "learned" means: the W matrix entries emerge from backpropagation, not human design — random noise becomes meaningful through billions of small updates.

text: This chapter traces a complete attention example end-to-end with concrete numbers. Sentence: "The cat sat". Embeddings: "The"=[0.1,0.8,-0.2,0.5], "cat"=[0.7,-0.3,0.6,0.1], "sat"=[-0.4,0.5,0.2,0.9]. For "cat", multiplying its embedding by W_Q, W_K, W_V produces Q_cat=[0.4,0.8] (looking for verbs/actions), K_cat=[0.9,0.3] (offering: noun, subject, animal), V_cat=[0.6,-0.2] (semantic content). For "sat", Q_sat=[0.2,0.7] (looking for a subject). Attention score = Q_sat dot K_cat = (0.2x0.9)+(0.7x0.3) = 0.18+0.21 = 0.39, a high match. "sat" absorbs cat's Value and learns "I'm an action performed by a cat."
summary: Walks through a complete attention computation between "sat" and "cat" using real numbers for embeddings, Q, K, V, and dot-product score.

text: Sentence: "The cat sat" - let's trace "sat" attending to "cat". Word embeddings: "The" = [0.1, 0.8, -0.2, 0.5]; "cat" = [0.7, -0.3, 0.6, 0.1]; "sat" = [-0.4, 0.5, 0.2, 0.9]. Each word starts as a 4-number embedding before any attention happens.
summary: Sets up the running example with three words and their concrete 4-dimensional embedding vectors.

text: Creating Q, K, V for "cat" via matrix multiplication. Q_cat = embedding x W_Q = [0.4, 0.8] - "cat is looking for words related to actions/verbs." K_cat = embedding x W_K = [0.9, 0.3] - "I can be found as: a noun, a subject, an animal." V_cat = embedding x W_V = [0.6, -0.2] - rich semantic content about "cat", the actual information. Same 4-number embedding produces three different 2-number vectors by multiplying through three different W matrices.
summary: Shows how cat's single embedding is multiplied by W_Q, W_K, W_V to produce three distinct vectors with different roles.

text: "sat" makes its Query: Q_sat = embedding x W_Q = [0.2, 0.7] - "who is performing this action? I'm looking for a subject." Score = dot product: Q_sat dot K_cat = (0.2 x 0.9) + (0.7 x 0.3) = 0.18 + 0.21 = 0.39 -> HIGH MATCH. "sat"'s question ("who is my subject?") matched "cat"'s ad ("I'm a subject noun"). "sat" pays high attention to "cat" and absorbs cat's Value (the actual semantic content). After this, "sat" knows: "I'm an action being performed by a cat."
summary: Computes the attention score between sat's Query and cat's Key, yielding 0.39 and showing sat absorbs cat's Value.

text: This chapter walks through Step 1 of attention: computing Query, Key, and Value vectors for every word in 'I love cats'. Starting from 4-dim embeddings ('I' = [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8], 'love' = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1], 'cats' = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]), each word's embedding is multiplied by three learned 4x2 weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V. The same matrices are reused across all words; different inputs produce different Q/K/V outputs. A worked example shows 'I' x W_Q = [0.8, 0.2], 'I' x W_K = [0.3, 0.7], 'I' x W_V = [1.0, -0.5]. Final table gives all three words' Q, K, V (each 2D, compressed from 4D; real models use 64D from 512D).
summary: Full end-to-end Step 1: compute Q, K, V for every word in 'I love cats' using three learned weight matrices.

text: Now let's do a FULL end-to-end computation. We traced 'The cat sat' earlier to understand the concepts. Now we'll compute every single number for 'I love cats' - the sentence we've been building toward since Chapter 1. 'I love cats' embeddings (4 dims for simplicity): 'I' = [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8], 'love' = [0.2, 0.9, 0.4, -0.1], 'cats' = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6].
summary: Sets up the running example 'I love cats' with concrete 4-dim embeddings for each word.

text: The three weight matrices (learned during training). Each is a 4x2 grid. Same matrix is used for all words. The different outputs come from different input embeddings. W_Q (4x2): [[0.15, -0.03], [0.12, 0.78], [0.10, 0.51], [0.78, -0.01]]. W_K (4x2): [[-0.12, 0.16], [0.51, 0.37], [0.50, 0.22], [0.39, 0.53]]. W_V (4x2): [[0.59, -0.49], [0.32, 0.64], [-0.21, 0.77], [0.24, -0.13]]. These numbers were nudged by training over millions of examples. Each matrix learned to extract a different 'aspect' of meaning.
summary: Shows the three concrete 4x2 weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V used for all words.

text: Let's multiply 'I''s embedding by all three matrices. 'I' = [1.0, 0.5, -0.3, 0.8]. Each dim of the embedding multiplies the corresponding row of W, then we sum. For W_Q: dim 1 = (1.0 x 0.15) + (0.5 x 0.12) + (-0.3 x 0.10) + (0.8 x 0.78) = 0.8; dim 2 = (1.0 x -0.03) + (0.5 x 0.78) + (-0.3 x 0.51) + (0.8 x -0.01) = 0.2; Q_I = [0.8, 0.2]. For W_K: dim 1 = (1.0 x -0.12) + (0.5 x 0.51) + (-0.3 x 0.50) + (0.8 x 0.39) = 0.3; dim 2 = (1.0 x 0.16) + (0.5 x 0.37) + (-0.3 x 0.22) + (0.8 x 0.53) = 0.7; K_I = [0.3, 0.7]. For W_V: dim 1 = (1.0 x 0.59) + (0.5 x 0.32) + (-0.3 x -0.21) + (0.8 x 0.24) = 1.0; dim 2 = (1.0 x -0.49) + (0.5 x 0.64) + (-0.3 x 0.77) + (0.8 x -0.13) = -0.5; V_I = [1.0, -0.5]. 'love' and 'cats' go through the exact same three matrices - different embeddings in, different Q/K/V out.
summary: Full numeric walkthrough of multiplying the word 'I' embedding by W_Q, W_K, and W_V to get Q, K, V.

text: After multiplying by W_Q, W_K, W_V, the full Query/Key/Value table for 'I love cats': 'I' Q = [0.8, 0.2], K = [0.3, 0.7], V = [1.0, -0.5]. 'love' Q = [0.1, 0.9], K = [0.6, 0.4], V = [0.3, 0.8]. 'cats' Q = [0.5, 0.6], K = [0.8, 0.5], V = [-0.2, 0.9]. Q and K are 2D (compressed from 4D). In real models: 64D from 512D.
summary: Final summary table of Q, K, V vectors for all three words, with note that real models use 64D from 512D.

text: This chapter introduces Step 2 of attention: compute attention scores by taking the dot product of each word's Query with every word's Key. Worked numerical example uses Q_I = [0.8, 0.2] dotted with K_I = [0.3, 0.7] = 0.38, K_love = [0.6, 0.4] = 0.56, K_cats = [0.8, 0.5] = 0.74. Expands to a full score matrix where every Query asks every Key, producing one score per word pair. Higher score means higher relevance.
summary: Chapter-level summary: attention scores are dot products of Queries against Keys, producing a full word-by-word relevance matrix.

text: Step 2: Compute attention scores. Each word's Query asks every Key 'how relevant are you to me?' Example with Q_I = [0.8, 0.2]. Dot product Q_I with every word's Key. Q_I · K_I where K_I = [0.3, 0.7]: (0.8×0.3) + (0.2×0.7) = 0.24 + 0.14 = 0.38 (lowest). Q_I · K_love where K_love = [0.6, 0.4]: (0.8×0.6) + (0.2×0.4) = 0.48 + 0.08 = 0.56 (medium). Q_I · K_cats where K_cats = [0.8, 0.5]: (0.8×0.8) + (0.2×0.5) = 0.64 + 0.10 = 0.74 (highest). From I's perspective: 'cats' most relevant, 'love' medium, 'I' itself lowest.
summary: Worked dot-product example computing attention scores between Q_I and each Key vector for 'I love cats'.

text: Full score matrix (every word asks every word). Rows are Queries Q_I, Q_love, Q_cats. Columns are Keys K_I, K_love, K_cats. Q_I row: [0.38, 0.56, 0.74] with K_cats highest. Q_love row: [0.66, 0.42, 0.53] with K_I highest. Q_cats row: [0.57, 0.54, 0.70] with K_cats highest. Each row = one word asking 'how relevant is each word to me?' Yellow cell = highest score in that row.
summary: Shows the full 3x3 attention-score matrix where every word's Query is dotted with every word's Key.

text: This chapter explains why attention uses K transpose in the formula score = Q · K^T. T is not an exponent but the transpose operation that flips rows to columns. Without transposing K, Q (3×2) and K (3×2) cannot multiply because inner dimensions don't match. Transposing K to 2×3 makes (3×2) × (2×3) valid, producing a 3×3 score matrix where each cell is the dot product of a Query with a Key - the relevance score between two words. A classroom analogy (questions vs expertise) and a 3×3 example table reinforce the idea.
summary: This chapter shows that transposing K aligns matrix shapes so Q · K^T yields a word-by-word relevance score matrix.

text: In the attention formula you see: score = Q · K^T. The little T is not 'to the power of T'. It stands for Transpose - flipping a matrix on its side. But why flip K? Build up step by step.
summary: Introduces the superscript T in Q · K^T as the transpose operation, not an exponent.

text: Transpose means rows become columns, columns become rows. No math changes - just a flip. Original K is 3 rows × 2 cols with row 1 = K_I [0.3, 0.7], row 2 = K_love [0.6, 0.4], row 3 = K_cats [0.8, 0.5]. After transpose K^T is 2 rows × 3 cols with col 1 = K_I, col 2 = K_love, col 3 = K_cats. Same numbers, same order within each word's vector, just rotated.
summary: Visual flip showing a 3×2 K matrix becoming a 2×3 K^T - rows become columns with identical values.

text: The problem: shapes don't match. We want every Query to compute a dot product with every Key. Try Q · K directly: Q is 3×2 (3 words, 2 dims), K is 3×2 (3 words, 2 dims). 3×2 times 3×2 doesn't work. Matrix multiplication rule: the inner dimensions must match. (3×2) × (3×2) → 2 ≠ 3. Broken.
summary: Direct Q · K fails because the inner dimensions of two 3×2 matrices don't align for multiplication.

text: The fix: transpose K, then multiply. Q is 3×2 (3 words, 2 dims). K^T is 2×3 (2 dims, 3 words). Q × K^T: (3×2) × (2×3) → inner dims both 2. Works! Result is a 3×3 score matrix - every word → every word. Exactly what we need.
summary: Transposing K to 2×3 lets (3×2) × (2×3) yield a 3×3 score matrix covering every word pair.

text: What does Q · K^T actually compute? Each cell in the 3×3 result = the dot product of one Query with one Key. That's the relevance score. Example scores: Q_I row [0.38, 0.56, 0.74] (best with K_cats), Q_love row [0.66, 0.42, 0.53] (best with K_I), Q_cats row [0.57, 0.54, 0.7] (best with K_cats). Row = one word asking. Column = one word answering. Cell = how relevant the answer-word is to the asker.
summary: Each cell of Q · K^T is the dot product of a Query with a Key - the relevance score for that word pair.

text: Classroom seating chart analogy: Q (Queries) = each student's question list - 3 students, each with a question that has 2 traits (topic, urgency). K (Keys) = each student's expertise card - same 3 students, each has expertise described by 2 traits. Q · K^T = the compatibility chart - every student's question matched against every student's expertise → a 3×3 grid of 'how well can you help me?'. The transpose makes this matching possible. Without it, shapes won't fit.
summary: Classroom analogy: Q is question lists, K is expertise cards, Q · K^T is the compatibility grid.

text: K^T in one sentence: K^T is just K with its rows and columns swapped - so that Q · K^T produces a score for every word pair. Quick recap: What is T? Transpose - flip rows ↔ columns. Why do it? So the matrix shapes align for multiplication. Does K change? No - same numbers, just rearranged. What's the result? A word×word score matrix (every pair gets a score).
summary: Recap: K^T flips rows and columns so Q · K^T aligns shapes and yields a word-by-word score matrix.

text: Mathematical notation diagram of the softmax formula. Left side: σ(z)_i. Right side: a fraction with numerator e^(z_i) labeled 'this word's e^score' and denominator Σ_{j=1}^{K} e^(z_j) labeled 'sum of all e^scores'. The diagram visually shows the exponential of one score divided by the sum of exponentials over all K scores, converting raw attention scores to probabilities.
summary: Diagram of the softmax formula as a fraction: e^(z_i) over the sum of e^(z_j) across all K positions.

text: This chapter explains why attention needs softmax. Raw dot product scores can be negative or sum oddly, so plain division produces negative or over-100% weights. Softmax solves this in two steps: exponentiate (e^score) to make every value positive, then divide by the sum so weights add to 1. The formula is σ(z)_i = e^(z_i) / Σ_j e^(z_j). The exponential also amplifies the largest score, making the most relevant word dominate, but if scores are too large softmax saturates - motivating the next chapter's sqrt(d_k) scaling.
summary: Chapter overview: softmax turns raw attention scores into a valid probability distribution that sums to 1 while amplifying the winner.

text: After dot products in attention, we have raw scores. Example: I→I = 0.38, I→love = 0.56, I→cats = 0.74. Now we need to use these scores to blend the Values: output = ??? × V_I + ??? × V_love + ??? × V_cats. What should those ??? weights be? Can we just plug in the raw scores directly?
summary: Sets up the problem: raw attention scores from dot products need to be turned into blending weights for the Values.

text: Budget analogy: You have ₹100 to split among three friends based on helpfulness. Helpfulness scores: Riya=38, Aman=56, Priya=74. You can't give ₹38 + ₹56 + ₹74 = ₹168 because you only have ₹100. You need to convert scores into shares of ₹100 - percentages adding to 100%. Total = 38 + 56 + 74 = 168. Riya: 38/168 = 23% → ₹23. Aman: 56/168 = 33% → ₹33. Priya: 74/168 = 44% → ₹44. Total ₹100. That's basically what softmax does - convert raw scores into shares adding to 100%. But with one extra trick.
summary: Uses a ₹100 budget split between three friends to explain why scores need to be normalized into shares adding to 100%.

text: The problem: dot products CAN be negative. Example scores: word_A = -3, word_B = 1, word_C = 5. Simple division by total: total = -3 + 1 + 5 = 3. A: -3/3 = -100% (NEGATIVE - can't pay minus attention!). B: 1/3 = 33%. C: 5/3 = 167% (more than 100% - nonsense!). Simple division is broken when negatives are present. We need something better.
summary: Shows why plain division fails: negative dot products give negative or over-100% shares.

text: The Solution: Softmax. Before dividing, put each score through e^score (e ≈ 2.718). This function makes ANY number positive. Formula: σ(z)_i = e^(z_i) / Σ_{j=1}^{K} e^(z_j). The numerator is this word's e^score. The denominator is the sum of e^scores over all K words. In plain English: take e to the power of this score, then divide by the sum of e to the power of every score.
summary: Introduces the softmax formula σ(z)_i = e^(z_i) / Σ e^(z_j) and explains the role of the exponential.

text: Why e^x? It's ALWAYS positive. No matter what input - negative, zero, or huge - the output is always positive. e^(-3) = 0.05 (negative becomes small positive). e^(0) = 1.0 (zero becomes exactly 1). e^(1) = 2.72 (positive becomes bigger positive). e^(5) = 148.4 (large becomes much larger). Pattern: higher input → exponentially bigger output. Softmax naturally amplifies the winner.
summary: Demonstrates that e^x is always positive and grows exponentially, so it amplifies large scores.

text: Full walkthrough with scores [-3, 1, 5]. Step 1: Apply e^score to each. e^(-3) = 0.05 (red), e^(1) = 2.72, e^(5) = 148.4 (green). All negative numbers became positive. Step 2: Sum them all up (the denominator). 0.05 + 2.72 + 148.4 = 151.17.
summary: Walks through softmax steps 1 and 2 for scores [-3, 1, 5]: exponentiate to get 0.05, 2.72, 148.4 and sum to 151.17.

text: Step 3: Divide each e^score by the sum (151.17). A (score -3): 0.05/151.17 = 0.03%. B (score 1): 2.72/151.17 = 1.8%. C (score 5): 148.4/151.17 = 98.2%. Checks: all positive ✓, sum = 100% ✓, ranking preserved ✓. The highest raw score still gets the largest weight after softmax.
summary: Completes the softmax walkthrough by dividing by the sum, yielding 0.03%, 1.8%, 98.2% - all positive, summing to 100%, ranking preserved.

text: Softmax amplifies differences by design. Raw scores: -3, 1, 5 (gap from 1 to 5 is only 4). After e^x: 0.05, 2.72, 148.4 (gap from 2.72 to 148 is 145!). The highest score doesn't just win, it dominates. This is good: the most relevant word SHOULD stand out. But if scores are too huge (like 12, 18, 25), softmax amplifies TOO aggressively giving 99.99% on one word. That's why we scale first (next chapter) to keep scores in a useful range.
summary: Explains that softmax amplifies score gaps and motivates the sqrt(d_k) scaling step to prevent over-amplification.

text: Softmax complete picture, two steps: (1) e^score for each score - makes everything positive, even e^(-1000) is positive. (2) Divide each by the total sum - now everything adds up to exactly 1.0 (100%). Formula: softmax(z_i) = e^(z_i) / Σ e^(z_j). Output: valid percentages (all positive, sum to 100%) that respect the original ranking. But raw scores can be too big for softmax - that's the next problem to solve (scaling).
summary: Recap of the two-step softmax procedure with the formula and properties: positive, sums to 1, preserves ranking.

text: Step 3 of attention is scaling the raw QK^T dot product scores by dividing by √d_k. Dot products grow with dimension d_k because more product terms are summed, producing huge raw scores in high-dimensional models (around ~25 at d_k=64, ~180 at d_k=512). Large scores cause softmax saturation - one token gets ~99.6% of attention and the model can't blend information. Dividing by √d_k brings variance back to 1 (since dot product variance scales with d_k and std dev = √d_k), restoring a focused-but-not-collapsed attention distribution.
summary: Scaled dot product attention divides QK^T by √d_k to prevent softmax saturation from dimension-inflated scores.

text: The problem: dot products grow with dimensions. The dot product multiplies each pair and sums all of them, so more dimensions means more pairs being added up. 2 dimensions sums 2 terms: 0.24 + 0.14 = 0.38. 64 dimensions sums 64 terms (term_1 + term_2 + ... + term_64) and totals around ~25. 512 dimensions sums 512 terms and totals around ~180. Each individual term might be small (like 0.3 or 0.5), but adding up 64 or 512 of them makes the total very large. The score grows just because of more dimensions - not because the words are more related.
summary: Dot product scores get bigger as dimensionality grows because more product terms are summed, inflating the score independent of true similarity.

text: Why are big scores a problem? Softmax uses e^score. When scores are big, e^big_number becomes astronomically huge, and one word gets 99.99% of the attention - the model becomes blind to everything else. Small scores (2-dim) example [0.38, 0.56, 0.74] give a balanced attention: I = 28%, love = 33%, cats = 39%. The model looks at all words and can blend info from multiple sources. Huge scores (64-dim) example [12.1, 17.8, 25.6] collapse the softmax: I = 0.1%, love = 0.4%, cats = 99.6%. 99.6% lands on one word, the others become invisible, and the model can't blend information.
summary: Large dot product scores cause softmax to collapse onto a single word, making attention degenerate into a hard pick instead of a soft blend.

text: The fix: divide by √d_k. If d_k = 64, then √64 = 8. Dividing brings scores back to a manageable range. Before scaling: [12.1, 17.8, 25.6]. Divide by √64 = 8. After scaling: [1.51, 2.23, 3.20]. Softmax of [1.51, 2.23, 3.20] gives I = 14%, love = 28%, cats = 58%. The model focuses on the most relevant word (58%) but still gathers info from others (14%, 28%). That's exactly what you want.
summary: Dividing dot products by √d_k rescales them to a manageable range so softmax produces a focused but not collapsed attention distribution.

text: Why √d_k specifically? Why not ÷2 or ÷100? There's a mathematical reason. When Q and K have random values, the dot product's variance grows proportionally to d_k. The standard deviation = √d_k. So dividing by √d_k brings the variance back to 1 - normalizing scores to a consistent range no matter the dimension. In simple terms: √d_k is the exact right amount to undo the growth caused by summing d_k terms. Not too much (all scores become equal), not too little (problem remains).
summary: Square root of d_k is chosen because dot product variance scales with d_k, so dividing by its standard deviation √d_k normalizes variance to 1.

text: Back to our example with d_k = 2, √2 ≈ 1.41. Scaled score matrix: Q_I against K_I, K_love, K_cats: 0.27, 0.40, 0.52. Q_love against K_I, K_love, K_cats: 0.47, 0.30, 0.37. Q_cats against K_I, K_love, K_cats: 0.40, 0.38, 0.50. Our scores were already small (2-dim), so scaling barely changes them. In a real 64-dim model, scaling is the difference between a useful model and a broken one. Now apply softmax to these scaled scores.
summary: Running example uses d_k = 2 (√2 ≈ 1.41) which barely changes scores, but in real 64-dim models scaling makes the difference between a working and broken model.

text: Step 4 of attention: apply softmax to each row of scaled scores to convert them into attention probabilities. For each word, exponentiate the scores, sum them, and divide each by the sum. Each row becomes a probability distribution summing to 1.0 - the attention weights that decide how much each word borrows from every other word's Value vector.
summary: Chapter overview: softmax turns scaled attention scores into per-row probability distributions used as attention weights.

text: Apply softmax step-by-step to scaled scores. Word 'I' has scaled scores looking at each word: [0.27, 0.40, 0.52]. Step 1: compute e^score for each. e^0.27 = 1.31, e^0.40 = 1.49, e^0.52 = 1.68. Step 2: sum all e^scores. 1.31 + 1.49 + 1.68 = 4.48. Step 3: divide each by sum. 1.31 / 4.48 = 0.29, 1.49 / 4.48 = 0.33, 1.68 / 4.48 = 0.37. Result: [0.29, 0.33, 0.37]. Check: 0.29 + 0.33 + 0.37 = 1.0.
summary: Worked softmax calculation on scaled scores [0.27, 0.40, 0.52] producing attention probabilities [0.29, 0.33, 0.37].

text: What the softmax numbers mean. When word 'I' looks at sentence 'I love cats': I to I = 29% attention to itself, I to love = 33% attention to 'love', I to cats = 37% attention to 'cats' (most relevant). These scores are close because the 2-dim example is tiny. In a real 512-dim model, the differences would be much sharper.
summary: Interprets softmax outputs as attention percentages each word allocates to others, noting sharper contrasts at real model dimensions.

text: Full attention weight matrix - all rows softmax'd. Every word does the same process: softmax its own row of scores. Row 'I': [0.29, 0.33, 0.38] (max on cats). Row 'love': [0.36, 0.31, 0.33] (max on I). Row 'cats': [0.32, 0.32, 0.36] (max on cats). Each row sums to 1.0. These are the attention weights - they tell each word how much to borrow from every other word. Next they will be used to blend the Values.
summary: Shows the complete 3x3 attention weight matrix where every row is softmaxed and sums to 1.0.

text: This chapter covers Step 5 of attention: the weighted sum of Value vectors. Each attention weight multiplies its corresponding V vector, and the scaled vectors are summed to produce a new output vector for the query token. Worked example for 'I' with weights 0.29, 0.33, 0.38 over V_I=[1.0,-0.5], V_love=[0.3,0.8], V_cats=[-0.2,0.9] yields [0.313, 0.461]. This output is a context-aware representation that blends information from other tokens, and the same computation runs for every word.
summary: Chapter summary: Step 5 of attention multiplies each Value vector by its attention weight and sums them to produce a new context-aware vector for every token.

text:
Step 5 of attention: use attention weights to blend Value vectors. For the word 'I' with attention weights 0.29, 0.33, 0.38 over 'I', 'love', 'cats':

0.29 × V_I = 0.29 × [1.0, -0.5] = [0.290, -0.145]
0.33 × V_love = 0.33 × [0.3, 0.8] = [0.099, 0.264]
0.38 × V_cats = 0.38 × [-0.2, 0.9] = [-0.076, 0.342]

Sum: [0.290, -0.145] + [0.099, 0.264] + [-0.076, 0.342] = [0.313, 0.461].

This weighted sum of Value vectors is the attention output for 'I'.
summary: Worked example multiplies each Value vector by its attention weight and sums them to produce the attention output [0.313, 0.461] for the word 'I'.

text: The resulting vector [0.313, 0.461] is the NEW vector for 'I'. It is no longer just about 'I' alone. It has absorbed context from 'love' (33%) and 'cats' (38%). It is now a context-aware representation. The same process happens for every word in the sequence, so every token ends up with a new vector that mixes information from all other tokens according to its attention weights.
summary: Explains that the weighted-sum output is a context-aware representation of the token, blending information from other words proportional to their attention weights.

text: This chapter collapses the full attention pipeline into one equation: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(Q·K^T / √d_k) · V. Each piece has a job - Q·K^T computes similarity scores, dividing by √d_k stabilizes the softmax, softmax produces probability-like attention weights summing to 1 per row, and multiplying by V yields the context-aware output.
summary: Chapter-level recap of the scaled dot-product attention formula and the role of each of its four components.

text: Everything above in one line: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax( Q·K^T / √d_k ) · V. This single equation packs the full scaled dot-product attention computation: compare every query to every key, scale by the square root of the key dimension, normalize with softmax into probabilities, then take a weighted sum of the value vectors.
summary: Presents the canonical scaled dot-product attention formula Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V as one line.

text:
Breakdown of each piece of the formula:
- Q · K^T: Dot product of every query with every key → score matrix.
- / √d_k: Scale down to prevent extreme softmax → manageable scores.
- softmax: Convert to probabilities (rows sum to 1) → attention weights.
- · V: Weighted sum of values → context-aware output.
summary: Walks through each component of the attention formula and what it produces at each stage.

text: This chapter motivates multi-head attention. A single head must produce one set of attention weights for each word, so when a word like 'sat' has many relationships at once (subject 'cat', location 'mat', time 'last week'), the head is forced to compromise and weaker relations like time can collapse to ~5%. The fix is to run several attention heads in parallel, each with its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. Each head learns to ask a different question (subject-verb, verb-location, temporal, ...) and captures one relationship type fully, so the model preserves all the relationships at once.
summary: Chapter overview: one attention head must compromise across many relations, so the model uses multiple heads with separate W_Q, W_K, W_V to specialize.

text: Single-head attention has a problem. Our short example 'I love cats' has only 3 words and 2 main relationships (who loves? what's loved?). To really see why one head isn't enough, use a longer sentence with more relationships: 'The cat that I adopted last week sat on the mat.' The word 'sat' needs answers to MULTIPLE questions at once: Who sat? -> 'cat' (subject <-> verb relationship). Sat where? -> 'mat' (verb <-> location relationship). Sat when? -> 'last week' (verb <-> time relationship). One word, one position, but three different kinds of relationships it must encode simultaneously.
summary: A single attention head struggles because one word like 'sat' must encode multiple relationship types (subject, location, time) at once.

text: One head produces one set of attention weights, so it is forced to compromise across all relationships. For 'sat' with one head the distribution looks like: cat 40% (got some), mat 30% (got some), last week 5% (almost nothing!), others 25% (scattered). Temporal info ('last week') got only 5% - it is almost lost. A single head cannot focus on all relationships simultaneously: spreading attention over subject, location, and time at once means none get full weight, and weak signals like temporal modifiers can vanish.
summary: With one head the attention weights compromise across all relations, so weaker signals like 'last week' get only 5% and are nearly lost.

text: Solution: give attention MULTIPLE heads - like multiple ears. Each head has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, so each asks a different question. Head 1 (subject-verb): 'sat' asks 'who is my subject?' -> finds cat (70%). Head 2 (verb-location): 'sat' asks 'where am I happening?' -> finds mat (65%). Head 3 (temporal): 'sat' asks 'when am I happening?' -> finds week (55%). Now NO information is lost. Each head specializes in one relationship and captures it fully, instead of one head trying to average across all of them.
summary: Multi-head attention gives each head its own W_Q, W_K, W_V so different heads specialize in different relationships and no info is lost.

text: Multi-head attention splits the 512-dim embedding into 8 chunks of 64 dims. Each head has its own 512x64 W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices, producing 8 different Query arrows that ask 8 different questions (subject, location, time, syntax, coreference, etc.). Splitting costs the same as one full head because of diminishing returns past ~64 dims per head. Transformers stack many such layers (e.g., 96), and each layer's 8 heads specialize differently during training - giving 96 x 8 = 768 attention patterns from grammar at the bottom to reasoning at the top.
summary: This chapter explains why and how multi-head attention splits the 512-dim embedding into 8 heads of 64 dims and stacks them across layers.

text: One head equals one Query arrow equals one direction. Each word's 512-dim embedding produces ONE Query vector through W_Q. That single arrow can only point one way, so it can only ask one type of question. Pipeline: 512-dim embedding through W_Q (512x512) yields one 512-dim Query. One arrow, one question, information lost. Solution: don't make one big arrow. Make 8 smaller arrows, each pointing in its own direction.
summary: Single-head attention gives each word only one Query direction so it can only ask one question, motivating splitting into 8 smaller arrows.

text: Split the 512 dimensions into 8 chunks of 64. Imagine the embedding as a long bar sliced into 8 equal pieces labeled H1 through H8, each 64 dims wide. Total dimensions: 512. Number of heads: 8. Dims per head: 512 / 8 = 64. We don't run the full 512-dim attention 8 times. We split it - same total computation, but 8 independent perspectives.
summary: The 512-dim embedding is sliced into 8 chunks of 64 dims, giving 8 independent perspectives at the same total compute cost.

text: But wait - doesn't splitting lose precision? Compare options. Option A: 8 heads x 512 dims each. Cost: 8 x 512 = 4,096 dims total (8x the compute). Quality per head very high but diminishing returns past about 64 dims. Option B: 8 heads x 64 dims each (the split). Cost: 8 x 64 = 512 dims total (same budget as one head). Quality per head: 64 dims is plenty - 'is this the subject?' doesn't need 512 numbers to answer. The insight: more dims per head equals diminishing returns. Going from 64 to 512 dims per head barely improves quality but costs 8x more. The split gives 8 different perspectives for the price of 1 large one.
summary: Comparing 8x512 vs 8x64 heads shows the split costs the same as one full head while diminishing returns make 64 dims plenty per head.

text: Each head gets its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. 8 heads equals 8 independent sets of weight matrices. Each set is smaller: 512x64 instead of 512x512. Heads 1 through 8 each have W_Q W_K W_V at 512x64 each. Same embedding goes into all 8 heads. Each head's different W matrices produce a different Query arrow - pointing in a different direction, asking a different question.
summary: Each of the 8 heads has its own smaller 512x64 W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices that project the same embedding into different Query directions.

text: 8 arrows, 8 questions, nothing lost. For 'sat' in 'The cat sat on the mat last week' each head's Query arrow points differently. Head 1 asks 'who did the action?' finds cat at 70%. Head 2 asks 'where?' finds mat at 65%. Head 3 asks 'when?' finds week at 55%. Head 4 asks 'what comes next?' finds on at 60%. Heads 5-8 ask about grammar, coreference, proximity, etc. Each head specializes. Together they capture everything - no information lost.
summary: Worked example: each head asks a different question about 'sat' (who/where/when/next) so together the 8 heads cover what one head would miss.

text: Now zoom out: layers. A Transformer doesn't have just one set of 8 heads. It stacks multiple layers, each with its own 8 heads. Layer 1: word proximity, basic grammar. Layer 2: syntax, clause structure. Layer 3: meaning, sentiment. ... up to Layer 96: reasoning, generation. 96 layers x 8 heads = 768 attention patterns. Each layer reads the previous layer's output. Early layers see raw words. Later layers see increasingly abstract, context-rich representations. Nobody told the model which layer should handle grammar or reasoning. It organized itself this way during training.
summary: Transformers stack many layers each with 8 heads, with early layers learning grammar and later layers learning reasoning, totaling 96x8 = 768 attention patterns.

text: Inside each of the 8 attention heads, the full Steps 1-5 attention algorithm runs in 64 dimensions instead of 512: compute Q, K, V via head-specific W matrices, score = Q·Kᵀ/√64, softmax, weighted sum of V. Because each head has different W_Q/W_K/W_V, each produces different attention patterns - one head learns subject-to-verb, another verb-to-location, another temporal relations - so the model captures relationships a single head would miss.
summary: This chapter shows that each of 8 heads runs the full attention computation in 64 dims and learns distinct relationship patterns through its own W matrices.

text:
Each head runs the FULL attention algorithm. Exactly Steps 1-5 we learned, but in 64 dims instead of 512.

For Head 1:
  Q₁ = embedding × W_Q₁  [3 words × 64 dims]
  K₁ = embedding × W_K₁  [3 words × 64 dims]
  V₁ = embedding × W_V₁  [3 words × 64 dims]

  scores₁ = Q₁ · K₁ᵀ / √64  [3×3 score matrix]
  weights₁ = softmax(scores₁)  [3×3 attention weights]
  output₁ = weights₁ × V₁  [3 words × 64 dims]

Same runs for all 8 heads. Each produces [3 words × 64 dims].
summary: Each of the 8 attention heads runs the full Step 1-5 attention algorithm independently in 64 dimensions, producing a [3 words × 64 dims] output.

text:
The KEY: each head's attention weights are different, because their W_Q, W_K, W_V are different. Here is what 'sat' sees in each head:

Head 1 - Subject to Verb: cat 70%, mat 8%, week 5%, others 17%.
Head 2 - Verb to Location: cat 10%, mat 65%, week 8%, others 17%.
Head 3 - Temporal: cat 8%, mat 7%, week 55%, others 30%.

Each head found what the single head missed. Head 3 got 'last week' at 55% - single head only gave it 5%.
summary: Different W_Q/W_K/W_V give each head different attention patterns - subject-verb, verb-location, temporal - capturing relations a single head would miss.

text: Concatenate + W_O is the final step of multi-head attention. The 8 heads each output a separate vector slice for every word, which are concatenated into one long 512-number list. Concatenation alone leaves each head's slice isolated like sealed envelopes. W_O, a learned 512×512 weight matrix (262,144 parameters), multiplies the concatenated vector so every output dimension becomes a blend of all heads' findings. W_O works like W_Q/W_K/W_V mechanically but its job is to fuse head outputs. Its weights start random and are tuned by backpropagation. Different transformer layers learn different W_O matrices specializing in syntax, semantics, or reasoning. After W_O, the word "love" becomes a single integrated context-aware vector carrying subject, object, and sentiment together.
summary: Multi-head outputs are concatenated then mixed by the learned W_O matrix so every output dimension blends information from all heads.

text: After the 8 heads finish, each word has 8 separate results. For "love": Head 1 gave [0.3, 0.5] (figured out "I" is the one who loves), Head 2 gave [-0.2, 0.8] (figured out "cats" is what's loved), Head 3 gave [0.7, -0.1] (figured out the sentiment is positive), ... 8 results total. We stick them end-to-end (concatenate): [0.3, 0.5, -0.2, 0.8, 0.7, -0.1, ..., 0.1, 0.4]. One long list - 512 numbers. But there's a problem.
summary: Each of 8 heads produces its own output vector for a word, which are concatenated end-to-end into one 512-number list.

text: The problem - these results are like 8 sealed envelopes. 8 detectives investigated the same crime. Each wrote findings and sealed them: Head 1 "suspect is male, 30s", Head 2 "happened at the park", Head 3 "Tuesday evening", Head 4 "weapon was a knife". Tape them together (concatenation) with thick black walls between each section - they can't see each other. They're physically side by side, but each section is still sealed. H1's numbers have no idea what H2 found. H3 can't see H4. They're taped together but not talking. Boss asks: "one-line summary?" No envelope has the full picture. H1 only knows the suspect. H2 only knows the location. Nobody can combine them. We need someone to open all the envelopes, read everything, and write one combined summary.
summary: Concatenation alone leaves each head's findings sealed off from the others, so no dimension carries combined knowledge across heads.

text: W_O - the person who opens all the envelopes. W_O is just another weight matrix - a grid of numbers, same as W_Q, W_K, W_V. Multiply input by grid, get output. Nothing new in how it works. But what it does is special. When you multiply the concatenated list by W_O, every number in the output is computed from all numbers in the input. Before W_O: Position 1 = 0.3 (only knows Head 1's finding). After W_O: Position 1 = blend of 0.3 + (-0.2) + 0.7 + ... all positions = knows Head 1 + Head 2 + Head 3 + ... everything. It's like the combined summary: "A male suspect in his 30s used a knife at the park on Tuesday evening." One sentence that contains information from all envelopes. After W_O, every single position in the output carries a mix of what all 8 heads found. The envelopes have been opened, read, and combined.
summary: W_O is a weight matrix that mixes the concatenated head outputs so every output dimension blends information from all 8 heads.

text: Where did W_O's numbers come from? Same exact process as all other grids: 1. Start with random numbers. 2. Model tries to predict next word, gets it wrong. 3. Error flows backward, nudges each number in W_O. 4. "If this number were 0.31 instead of 0.30, the blending would have been better → change to 0.31". 5. Repeat billions of times. 6. W_O settles into numbers that produce good blends. The training discovers the best way to combine the 8 heads' findings into one useful output. Nobody programs this - it emerges from training.
summary: W_O's weights start random and are tuned by backpropagation over billions of prediction errors until they produce useful head blends.

text: Back to "I love cats" - what W_O does for "love": After 8 heads run, "love"'s concatenated vector has: Dims 1–64: Head 1 found "I" is the subject who loves; Dims 65–128: Head 2 found "cats" is the object being loved; Dims 129–192: Head 3 found this is a positive sentiment. Without W_O: each section is isolated. Dim 1 has no idea "cats" is involved. After W_O: every dimension of "love"'s output encodes the combined meaning: "affection from 'I' directed at 'cats', with positive sentiment." One rich, integrated vector. This is what we set out to do in chapter 1 - transform "love" from an isolated word into a context-aware representation.
summary: Worked example showing how W_O fuses the dim slices from 3 heads into a single context-aware vector for the word "love".

text: W_O is LEARNED - not hardcoded. The model discovers during training which combinations of head outputs are useful. Different layers learn different W_O matrices: Layer 1's W_O might focus on combining basic syntax patterns; Layer 3's W_O might focus on combining semantic relationships; Layer 6's W_O might focus on combining abstract reasoning. Nobody programs what to combine. W_O's 512×512 = 262,144 weights are all learned through backpropagation - the same process from earlier backprop chapters.
summary: Each layer learns its own W_O of 262,144 weights via backpropagation, with different layers specializing in syntax, semantics, or reasoning.

text: This chapter explains the choice of 8 attention heads as a tradeoff between head count and per-head dimensions, computes the parameter count of ~1 million per attention layer across W_Q, W_K, W_V, and W_O, shows multi-head uses the same budget as single-head but reorganized, and scales this view from the original 6-layer Transformer through GPT-2 (48 layers) to GPT-3 (96 layers, 175B params).
summary: Chapter summary: why 8 heads, ~1M params per layer, multi-head reorganizes single-head budget, and scales to GPT-3.

text: Why 8 heads, why not 4 or 16? Tradeoff: total 512 dims divided equally among heads. 16 heads = 512 / 16 = 32 dims each, more pattern types but each head weaker. 4 heads = 512 / 4 = 128 dims each, each head powerful but fewer patterns. 8 heads x 64 dims = sweet spot: enough capacity per head (64 dims) and enough perspectives (8 types).
summary: Explains 8 heads as tradeoff sweet spot between head capacity and pattern diversity.

text: Parameter count per head: W_Q (512x64) + W_K (512x64) + W_V (512x64) = 3 x 32,768 = 98,304. Across 8 heads: W_Q = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_K = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_V = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_O = 512 x 512 = 262,144. Total: 1,048,576, about 1 million parameters per attention layer.
summary: Computes total attention parameters: ~1 million per layer across W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O.

text: Surprising: same count as single-head. Single-head with 512-dim Q, K, V needs 512 x 512 per matrix = same total. Multi-head doesn't add parameters - it reorganizes them into 8 independent groups. Same budget, much better results.
summary: Multi-head attention uses same parameter budget as single-head but reorganized into independent groups.

text: How does this scale in a full model? One attention layer = ~1 million parameters. Transformers stack multiple layers, each refining context further. Original Transformer (2017): 6 layers, ~6M attention params. GPT-2 (2019): 48 layers, ~48M attention params. GPT-3 (2020): 96 layers, 175 BILLION total params. More layers = deeper understanding. Each layer's attention asks different questions about the same words, building increasingly rich representations - same mechanism, just stacked.
summary: Shows how attention parameters scale across stacked layers from original Transformer to GPT-3.

text: This chapter answers whether the output projection matrix W_O (and other weight grids) is constant. W_O changes constantly during training as gradients nudge its numbers across billions of examples, but once training finishes it is frozen to disk and never changes again. At inference every user, every sentence, and every input uses the exact same W_O. The same is true for the embedding dictionary and all W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices. What does change per input are the live computed values: Q, K, V vectors, attention scores, softmax weights, blended outputs, and the final output. The coffee-machine analogy: W_O is the fixed machine, the sentence embeddings are the beans, so same machine plus different beans yields different coffee.
summary: W_O is updated during training but frozen at inference; only the per-input Q/K/V and attention values change.

text: Is W_O constant? Does it change? There are two phases in a model's life: training and usage. The answer depends on which phase you're asking about.
summary: Whether W_O changes depends on whether the model is being trained or being used.

text: During training: W_O changes. Every time the model sees a new batch of sentences and makes prediction errors, every number in W_O gets nudged slightly. Over billions of examples, W_O gradually improves. It changes millions of times during training - getting better and better at blending heads. After training is done: W_O is FROZEN. Completely constant. Once training is complete, the model is saved to a file. W_O's numbers are written to disk and they NEVER change again. When you use the model (send it a sentence), W_O is loaded from disk and used as-is.
