Example: a 4,096-token context window gets 1,536 tokens wasted on three ~512-token duplicate chunks, leaving only 2,560 tokens for distinct answers. Three sources (Zendesk help center article #4821, Confluence IT runbook page, Notion onboarding doc section) all carry the same body 'Reset Your Password'. Three copies of the same answer equals wasted tokens, zero diversity, and the LLM has nothing distinct to triangulate against.
summary: Duplicate articles across source systems waste context-window budget and destroy retrieval diversity.

text: Exact-hash dedup is the first line of defense. Normalize the text (strip whitespace, lowercase, remove punctuation), run SHA-256, then keep a hash table of every hash already seen. If a new doc's hash matches an existing hash, the content is byte-identical, so drop it. Three-step flow: Step 1 Normalize - 'Reset Your Password. Click The Link.' becomes 'reset your password click the link'. Step 2 Hash (SHA-256) - sha256(normalized) produces hex digest like a3f2b9... Step 3 Dedup Lookup - hash table check; match equals drop, miss equals keep + store. Formula: hash = sha256(normalize(doc.content)). Worked example with three sources sharing the same hash a3f2b9...: Zendesk -> Kept (first), Confluence -> Dropped, Notion -> Dropped. Cost: O(N) hash compute + O(1) hash-table lookup. Misses: 'Same article with one typo fixed' produces a different hash, so byte-identical only catches the easy 80%.
summary: SHA-256 hashing on normalized text drops byte-identical duplicates in O(N) time but misses anything with a typo.

text: MinHash + LSH catches near-duplicates that exact hashing misses. One typo flips SHA-256, and comparing every pair of N docs is O(N^2). Standard libraries (datasketch, text-dedup) do the work. Three-step pipeline: 1. Shingle - cut each doc into overlapping 3-word windows; doc becomes a set of shingles. 2. MinHash - compress each set into a fixed 128-number fingerprint where matching positions estimate Jaccard overlap (shared / union). 3. LSH bucket - split fingerprint into bands, hash each band; docs landing in the same bucket are candidate near-duplicates, skipping the O(N^2) compare-everything scan. Tunable: Jaccard threshold (default 0.85). Catches typo-fixes, re-words, copy-paste edits that exact hashing misses. Scales to billion-doc corpora.
summary: MinHash plus LSH approximates Jaccard overlap with a 128-number fingerprint to catch near-duplicates without quadratic comparisons.

text: Embedding dedup is the only way to catch paraphrases - the same meaning in different words. Two docs can say the same thing with almost no shared words; MinHash misses these because no shingles overlap. Paraphrase pair: Support doc (formal) - 'To reset your password, click the password reset link delivered to your registered email address.' Forum post (casual) - 'Yo if you forgot your password just hit the reset thing they sent to your email.' MinHash Jaccard: 0.08 (miss). Embedding cosine: 0.94 (flagged as semantic duplicate). Cosine similarity ladder: cosine >= 0.97 = definite duplicate, drop one keep the other; 0.90 <= cosine < 0.97 = near-duplicate, send to human review queue; 0.85 <= cosine < 0.90 = topically similar, keep both; cosine < 0.85 = distinct documents, keep all. Cost reality: embedding a full corpus to dedup adds $0.10 per 1M tokens times corpus size. For 10M docs at 512 tokens each that is 5B tokens equals about $500 just to dedup. Use embedding dedup when the corpus has paraphrases (support docs + forum posts, multi-author docs, translations). Skip it when content is byte-stable (PDFs from authoritative sources) and exact hashing + MinHash already cover the threshold.
summary: Embedding cosine catches paraphrases MinHash cannot, with action thresholds at 0.97/0.90/0.85 and real per-corpus dollar cost.

text: Cleaning fixes the bytes themselves after dedup. Four steps turn raw extracted text into something the embedder and tokenizer can read consistently. 1. Unicode Normalization (NFC): two strings that look identical can have different byte sequences. Before: 'cafe' composed (5 bytes) vs 'cafe' decomposed (6 bytes). After: NFC pass collapses both to the same 5-byte form so hash dedup and embedding lookup agree. 2. Encoding Fix-Up: mojibake from latin-1 / UTF-8 mismatches breaks tokenization. Before: raw bytes rendered wrong ('naive resume' mojibake). After: ftfy or chardet detects the encoding mismatch and restores the correct characters. 3. Strip Headers, Footers, Page Numbers, Watermarks: boilerplate repeated on every page bloats chunks with noise. Before: every chunk starts with 'CONFIDENTIAL - DRAFT 2026 - Page 3 of 47'. After: layout-aware parser strips the repeating per-page header, footer, and watermark band. 4. PII Scrubbing: emails, SSNs, phone numbers, and credit cards leak into the index unless redacted. Before: 'Contact john@acme.com or call 555-1234 with SSN 123-45-6789.' After: 'Contact [EMAIL_REDACTED] or call [PHONE_REDACTED] with SSN [SSN_REDACTED].' Tool: regex + LLM NER (Presidio). Cleaning is invisible when it works and catastrophic when skipped; it stabilizes eval scores by removing irrelevant tokens that fool similarity metrics and keeps PII out of the index entirely.
summary: Four cleaning steps - NFC Unicode, encoding fix, boilerplate stripping, PII scrubbing - stabilize embeddings and keep secrets out of the index.

text: Timeline diagram showing a stale-index hallucination. Source-of-truth band (Confluence): v1 box 'No MFA mention' active 2026-04-12 to 2026-05-01; v2 box 'MFA setup note added' active 2026-05-01 onward. Three time markers on timeline: 2026-04-12 v1 published (green), 2026-05-01 v2 edited adds MFA (green), 2026-05-10 stale query hits v1 (red). Vector index band: single wide red box 'Only v1 chunks indexed - stale from 2026-05-01.' Query: 'Do I lose my MFA after password reset?' Returns v1 chunk -> LLM says 'MFA may need re-setup' (wrong). Hallucinated answer looks confident - no stale signal.
summary: Diagram of source-of-truth v1/v2 vs never-re-ingested index causing a wrong MFA answer on 2026-05-10.

text: Webhook-driven incremental sync sequence diagram with time markers T=0:00 through T=0:10. T=0:00 User edits doc (support author updates 'Reset Your Password' article in Confluence to add the MFA-preservation note). T=0:01 Webhook fires (Confluence POSTs {event: 'page.updated', page_id: 'doc-1', revision: 42} to ingestion service). T=0:02 Fetch latest (ingestion service calls Confluence API to pull doc-1 revision 42 in full). T=0:05 Re-parse + re-chunk + re-embed (parser, chunker, embedder run end-to-end, 3 chunks produced in ~150ms). T=0:08 Upsert in vector DB (same doc_id, new chunks, old chunks dropped, new chunks inserted - Section 17.2 deletes-updates pattern). T=0:10 Queryable (fresh chunks ready for retrieval, end-to-end propagation lag ~10 seconds).
summary: Sequence diagram of a webhook fire-to-queryable pipeline with ~10-second propagation across six time markers.

text: Three-row versioning timeline diagram. Row 1 t=0: doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active - queries hit v1 chunks, source-of-truth and index agree (green). Row 2 t=+1min: v2 ingested, v1 deprecated, chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active + [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated, status=active OR status=deprecated - both versions live during re-embed window, no retrieval gap (yellow). Row 3 t=+15min: v1 dropped after grace period, chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active - only v2 active, old chunks tombstoned and removed (cyan).
summary: Three-row diagram of chunk versioning across t=0, t=+1min, and t=+15min showing the grace-period overlap that avoids retrieval gaps.

text: Refresh & Sync Schedules covers how to keep a RAG index aligned with changing sources. Stale indexes cause confident hallucinations because RAG never checks the source at query time. Three sync strategies: (1) full nightly re-index with shadow-index atomic swap - simple, cheap under 10K docs ($30/mo), explodes at 1M docs ($3K/mo); (2) webhook-driven incremental sync - ~10 second propagation for SaaS sources with event hooks, needs idempotent upserts and DLQ; (3) polling with a per-source watermark and updated_at filter - fallback for sources without webhooks, lag bounded by poll interval. The hard parts: delete propagation via tombstones (avoids ghost chunks) and chunk versioning with a 5-15 minute grace period (avoids retrieval gap). Both are silent failures - build them from day one or pay 10x to retrofit.
summary: Chapter teaches three RAG sync strategies (full re-index, webhooks, polling) plus delete propagation and chunk versioning to avoid silent stale-index failures.

text: Stale RAG hallucinates with confidence. Docs change but the index does not. When the source is updated but the chunks in the vector DB still reflect the old version, RAG retrieves stale content and the LLM answers with confidence - a fluent, wrong answer. There is no automatic stale signal: the only way to know is to compare against the live source, which RAG never does at query time. Three-time-marker timeline: 2026-04-12 v1 published (no MFA mention, active 2026-04-12 to 2026-05-01); 2026-05-01 v2 edited adding MFA setup note (active 2026-05-01 onward); 2026-05-10 stale query hits v1. The vector index was never re-ingested, so only v1 chunks are indexed - stale from 2026-05-01. Query 'Do I lose my MFA after password reset?' returns v1 chunk and LLM says 'MFA may need re-setup' (wrong). Every doc has an expiry date. Stale answers feel correct because the LLM is confident.
summary: Without sync, RAG returns confidently wrong answers from a stale index because there is no automatic stale signal at query time.

text: Strategy 1: Full re-index on a schedule. The simplest sync strategy is the heavy hammer: every night, rebuild the entire index from scratch. No change detection, no event plumbing - just a cron job that re-ingests everything. Mechanism: nightly cron at 2 AM fires. The job fetches all 10,000 docs from the source, re-parses, re-chunks, re-embeds. Output is written into a SHADOW index (parallel collection). At 6 AM, an atomic pointer swap promotes shadow to primary. Retrieval traffic flips instantly with zero gap. Without the shadow-index pattern, retrieval is degraded for the full 4-hour rebuild window. Cost math: 10K docs x 1K tokens/doc x $0.10/1M tokens = $1/night, roughly $30 per month for a 10K-doc corpus - trivial. But at 1M docs: $3,000/month just for re-embedding. Re-embedding dominates the budget once the corpus crosses 100K docs. Decision card - use full re-index if: corpus is small (<10K docs), changes are infrequent (weekly batch updates), simplicity beats freshness. Avoid if: real-time updates needed, corpus large enough for re-embed to dominate budget.
summary: Full nightly re-index uses a shadow-index swap; cheap for under 10K docs ($30/mo) but blows up at 1M docs ($3K/mo).

text: Strategy 2: Webhook-driven incremental sync. When the source supports webhooks (Confluence, Notion, GitHub, Slack), the index stays fresh in seconds instead of overnight. Each edit fires an event; the ingestion service handles only docs that actually changed. No wasted re-embedding on the 99.99% of docs that did not change. Sequence diagram - 10-second propagation: T=0:00 User edits doc - support author updates 'Reset Your Password' article in Confluence to add the MFA-preservation note. T=0:01 Webhook fires - Confluence POSTs {event: 'page.updated', page_id: 'doc-1', revision: 42} to the ingestion service. T=0:02 Fetch latest - ingestion service calls Confluence API to pull doc-1 revision 42 in full. T=0:05 Re-parse + re-chunk + re-embed - parser, chunker, embedder run end-to-end, 3 chunks produced in ~150ms. T=0:08 Upsert in vector DB - same doc_id, new chunks; old chunks dropped, new chunks inserted (Section 17.2 deletes-updates pattern). T=0:10 Queryable - fresh chunks ready for retrieval, end-to-end propagation lag ~10 seconds. Pros: real-time freshness (~10s), no wasted re-embedding, upsert lines up with Section 17.2, linear cost scales with edit volume not corpus size. Cons: requires webhook support (Confluence yes, raw S3 no), webhook failures need retry/DLQ handling, upserts must be idempotent to avoid duplicates on retries, out-of-order events need revision-number guards. Webhook incremental sync is the default for SaaS sources with event hooks - build idempotent upserts and retry queue from day one.
summary: Webhooks push edits to the ingestion service within ~10 seconds; cheap, fresh, but requires idempotent upserts and retry/DLQ handling.

text: Strategy 3: Polling - the fallback for webhook-less sources. Many sources (REST APIs without event hooks, SQL databases, internal services) cannot push events. The ingestion service must pull. Pattern: track a per-source watermark, ask the source on a timer what has changed since the watermark, advance the watermark, repeat. Polling pipeline worked example - last_synced_at = 2026-05-10T14:00:00Z (service tracks per-source watermark); interval = every 15 minutes (tune to freshness budget, throttle to source's rate limits); query = WHERE updated_at > 2026-05-10T14:00:00Z LIMIT 1000 (REST or SQL filter source API exposes); result = 7 changed docs since last poll (process them, then advance last_synced_at to max(updated_at) in batch). Freshness lag edge case: doc-12 is edited at t=2 min (just after a poll fires at t=0). The change is picked up at t=15 min (next poll). Freshness lag = 13 minutes average for that doc, 15 minutes worst case across the corpus. Tighter interval = lower lag, higher API cost. Looser interval = higher lag, lower API cost. Tune poll interval to match freshness budget. Decision card: use polling when source has no webhooks but exposes a queryable updated_at field (most REST APIs, SQL databases, internal services). Cost: API calls every interval whether or not anything changed. Throttle the poller to source's rate limits. Lag is bounded by the poll interval.
summary: Polling tracks a watermark and queries updated_at on a timer; lag is bounded by poll interval, cost scales per-call not per-edit.

text: Delete propagation and versioning: where 90% of in-house RAG breaks. Sync is not just 'add new docs.' Hard parts are propagating deletes (when source removes a doc) and versioning chunks (so retrieval never sees a gap during a re-embed). Both are silent failures - eval sets catch them, but customers just get wrong answers. Three-row versioning timeline: Row 1 t=0 doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active - queries hit v1, source and index agree. Row 2 t=+1min v2 ingested, v1 deprecated - chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active + [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated, status=active OR status=deprecated - both versions live during re-embed window, no retrieval gap. Row 3 t=+15min v1 dropped after grace period - chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active - only v2 active, old chunks tombstoned and removed. Delete propagation: source removes doc-1, webhook fires event 'page.deleted', ingestion writes a tombstone in vector DB, tombstone propagates to all replicas, all chunks for doc-1 removed (Section 17.2 multi-replica pattern). Without delete propagation: deleted docs still served - ghost chunks pointing to content no longer in source. Versioning + grace period: new chunks land status=active, old chunks flip status=deprecated, both queryable during grace period (typically 5-15 min), after grace deprecated chunks dropped. Without versioning: old chunks dropped before new ones land - retrieval gap, queries during re-embed window return empty or stale results. Silent failures invisible to users - eval catches them, customers do not. Build delete propagation + versioning from day one; retro-fitting costs 10x dev time because every downstream component (retrieval, eval, monitoring) already assumes chunks are immutable.
summary: Delete propagation (tombstones) and chunk versioning with a grace period prevent ghost chunks and retrieval gaps - silent failures most in-house RAG misses.

text: Horizontal token-count bar showing a 500-page product manual at 250,000 tokens overflowing the 8,192-token embedding model ceiling. Green portion marks the 8,192 tokens that fit while the rest of the bar is tinted red as overflow (241,808 tokens exceed the ceiling). A second smaller bar below visualizes the ceiling itself, labeled "Embedding Model Ceiling = 8,192 Tokens (OpenAI ada-3)". A callout shows that even if the whole doc fit, a single vector [0.21, 0.84, 0.12, ...] for 500 pages would carry no specific signal.
summary: Diagram contrasting a 250,000-token document against the 8,192-token embedding model ceiling, visualizing the overflow region and the meaninglessness of a single vector for a whole 500-page manual.

text: Sliding window chunk diagram across doc-1 (the password-reset article) showing four colored 128-token chunk bars stacked vertically along a 0-464 token axis. Each chunk is labeled with its token range: Chunk 1 (Tokens 0-128), Chunk 2 (Tokens 112-240), Chunk 3 (Tokens 224-352), Chunk 4 (Tokens 336-464). The 16-token overlap zones between adjacent chunks are shaded a darker green band. Legend shows chunk color and the overlap (16 tokens) color.
summary: Diagram of four overlapping 128-token chunks tiled across a 464-token document with shared 16-token overlap bands highlighted between adjacent chunks.

text: Two clean rectangle chunks side by side with no overlap (Overlap = 0, Naive). A red dashed vertical line at the boundary marks the mid-sentence hard cut between Chunk 1 and Chunk 2. Label beneath the boundary reads "Hard Cut". This illustrates the naive fixed-size chunking case where adjacent chunks share no tokens and a cut sentence is lost.
summary: Diagram of two adjacent chunks meeting at a red dashed hard-cut boundary with zero overlap, showing the naive fixed-size case where sentences split across chunks are lost.

text: Two chunk rectangles with a darker yellow overlap band shared between them (Overlap = 16 Tokens), labeled as the shared 16-token region where the cut sentence appears whole in both adjacent chunks. Caption "Shared 16 Tokens" sits below the boundary. The shared text in both adjacent chunks reads: "set a new password. The link expires".
summary: Diagram of two adjacent chunks sharing a 16-token overlap band where the boundary sentence "set a new password. The link expires" appears whole in both chunks.

text: This chapter: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline. Embedding a whole document fails because embedding models cap at ~8,192 tokens and a single vector for 500 pages carries no specific signal. Production RAG always chunks for three reasons: context limits, retrieval granularity, and signal dilution. The simplest strategy is fixed-size sliding-window chunking: chunks = slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16). This produces uniform chunks but cuts land mid-sentence, breaking cross-boundary retrieval. Overlap of 16 tokens duplicates boundary text into both adjacent chunks, fixing sentence-level cuts at ~12% extra storage, but still fails on long-range pronoun references. Fixed-size is the baseline: use it for short, homogeneous docs or as a measurement reference; move to structural or semantic chunking when docs have headings, are heterogeneous, or cross-chunk facts hurt recall.
summary: Overview of why RAG chunks documents and how fixed-size sliding-window chunking with overlap works, its failure modes (mid-sentence cuts, long-range references), and when to use it as a baseline.

text: Why not embed the whole document? The intuitive first idea is to take a full 500-page product manual, embed it as one vector, and search. This fails for two hard reasons: the document is too long to fit through the embedding model, and even if it did, one vector for 500 pages would mean nothing specific. Token Budget vs Embedding Model Ceiling: a 500-page product manual is roughly 250,000 tokens. The OpenAI ada-3 embedding model ceiling is 8,192 tokens. Only the first 8,192 fit; the remaining 241,808 tokens are overflow. Even if it fit, a single vector like [0.21, 0.84, 0.12, ...] for 500 pages would carry no specific signal.
summary: Embedding an entire long document fails because it exceeds the 8,192-token embedding model ceiling and a single vector for hundreds of pages dilutes all specific meaning.

text: Three Reasons Production RAG Always Chunks. Chunking is not a workaround, it is the fundamental design choice. Three forces make it unavoidable. 1) Context Limits: embedding models cap at 8K tokens; LLMs cap at context-window length. A 500-page manual cannot fit in either. 2) Retrieval Granularity: top-K retrieval returns chunks, not whole docs. Smaller chunks = more focused matches. 3) Signal Dilution: averaging 500 pages into one vector buries any specific fact. Smaller chunks keep semantic density. Bottom line: chunking is the highest-leverage decision in RAG. Get the chunk strategy wrong and every downstream stage compounds the error.
summary: Production RAG always chunks documents because of three forces: embedding/LLM context limits, top-K retrieval granularity, and signal dilution from averaging too much text into one vector.

text: Fixed-Size Chunking: A Sliding Window With Overlap. The simplest possible strategy: slide a fixed token window across the document. Each window becomes one chunk. Adjacent chunks overlap by a few tokens so a sentence cut in half still appears whole somewhere. Example doc-1 (Password Reset Article, ~480 tokens): "To reset your password, visit account.example.com and click forgot password. Enter the email tied to your account. You will receive a reset link valid for 24 hours..." Formula: chunks = slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16). Applied to doc-1 this yields four chunks: Chunk 1 Tokens 0-128, Chunk 2 Tokens 112-240, Chunk 3 Tokens 224-352, Chunk 4 Tokens 336-464. Each adjacent pair shares 16 tokens of overlap.
summary: Fixed-size chunking slides a constant-token window with small overlap across a document, producing four 128-token chunks with 16-token overlap on the example password-reset article.

text: Fixed-Size Cuts Land Anywhere - Including Mid-Sentence. The token window does not care about grammar. The 128th token is wherever the 128th token is. Zoom into the boundary between chunk 1 and chunk 2 in doc-1. Chunk 1 ends mid-phrase: "...click the reset link in the email within 24 hours to set a new". Chunk 2 starts mid-phrase: "password. The link expires after that and you must request a new one...". The Boundary Cut Breaks Retrieval: the chunk that ends with "set a new" has no idea what the user is setting. The chunk that starts with "password." has no idea what action was being taken. Retrieval cannot match the query "password reset expiry" to either piece alone - both lose the cross-boundary fact.
summary: Fixed-size token windows ignore grammar and cut mid-sentence, splitting the phrase "set a new password" across two chunks so neither chunk can answer a cross-boundary query.

text: Overlap Helps - But Only Partially. The standard fix is overlap: duplicate the last N tokens of each chunk into the start of the next. A sentence cut at the boundary now appears whole in one of the two adjacent chunks. The cost: storage and redundant compute. Overlap = 0 (Naive): two chunks meet at a hard cut; cuts can lose context entirely; recall drops on cross-chunk facts. Overlap = 16 Tokens: chunks share a 16-token region; the shared text in both adjacent chunks is "set a new password. The link expires". Tradeoffs: overlap = duplicate storage cost; overlap = ~12% more vectors at 128/16; still breaks long-range references (entity earlier in doc, pronoun later). Overlap fixes the easy case (sentence-level cuts) but not the hard case (a definition on page 3 referenced by "it" on page 17).
summary: Adding 16-token overlap duplicates boundary text into both adjacent chunks to recover sentence-level cuts, but costs ~12% more vectors and still misses long-range entity-pronoun references.

text: Fixed-Size: The Baseline To Beat. Fixed-size chunking is the right default in a narrow band of cases. Outside that band, every other strategy beats it. Use Fixed-Size If: docs are short (< 2K tokens each); content is homogeneous (uniform paragraphs); you need a baseline to measure other strategies against; budget = zero engineering time. Move To Structural / Semantic If: docs have clear structural markers (markdown, HTML headings); content is heterogeneous (one doc = manual + FAQ + changelog); cross-chunk facts hurt recall in your eval set. Fixed-size is the baseline. Every chunking strategy in production is measured against it.
summary: Fixed-size chunking is the right default only for short, homogeneous docs or as a baseline; structural or semantic chunking should replace it once docs have headings, are heterogeneous, or cross-chunk facts hurt recall.

text:
Vertical priority tree of structural separators with four stacked boxes connected by downward arrows. Top box: Try Paragraph Break First, separator = "

", one blank line between blocks. Arrow labeled "If chunk too big" points down to: Fall Back To Line Break, separator = "
", newline inside a paragraph still respects layout. Another "If chunk too big" arrow leads to: Fall Back To Sentence Break, separator = ". ", period followed by space, smallest grammar unit. Final "If chunk too big" arrow leads to: Last Resort: Word Break, separator = " ", plain space, never cuts mid-word.
summary: Diagram showing the four-level recursive separator priority tree with each arrow gated on the chunk still being too big.

text: Recursion trace tree on doc-12 Authentication Header Format. Top red node: doc-12 # "Authentication Header Format", 300 tokens - too big (limit 128). Arrow labeled "Recurse: try \n\n" points down to a row of three green paragraph nodes: Para 1 (100 tokens - fits), Para 2 (110 tokens - fits), Para 3 (90 tokens - fits). Dashed connector lines link the heading node to each paragraph. Decision text: each sub-chunk <= 128, stop recursing. Note: if a paragraph were still > 128, recursion would try ". " (sentence) next, then " " (word). Bottom row shows three cyan emitted chunk nodes: Chunk A Emit (100 tok), Chunk B Emit (110 tok), Chunk C Emit (90 tok).
summary: Diagram of an oversized 300-token section being recursively split into three paragraph sub-chunks that fit the 128-token limit and emit as final chunks.

text:
Recursive structural chunking is the default RAG chunker. It tries an ordered list of separators from most structural (paragraph break "

") down to line break "
", sentence break ". ", and finally word break " ", recursing deeper only when a candidate chunk still exceeds the size limit. On a three-section login troubleshooting doc, fixed-size chunking (size=128) breaks across topic boundaries and creates a chunk mixing Browser Compatibility with Account Lockout; recursive splitting with a heading separator yields three single-topic chunks of ~120, ~95, and ~115 tokens. When a section like doc-12 "Authentication Header Format" runs 300 tokens, recursion falls back from heading to paragraph break and emits three sub-chunks (100, 110, 90 tokens) that all fit. It covers about 80% of production cases, preserves markdown structure, has zero embedding cost, and is implemented by LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter and LlamaIndex SentenceSplitter. It is blind to true semantic boundaries and treats two same-theme ## sections as separate chunks.
summary: Summarises recursive structural chunking as a separator-priority algorithm that yields clean topic chunks and covers ~80% of RAG cases while staying blind to semantic boundaries.

text:
Recursive structural chunking splits documents by structure rather than by raw token count. It tries the most structural separator first - a paragraph break ("

") - and only falls back to smaller separators when the resulting chunk still exceeds the size limit. The cut lands at a boundary the writer already chose. The separator priority is: paragraph break "

" (one blank line between blocks, most structural), then line break "
" (newline inside a paragraph), then sentence break ". " (period followed by space, smallest grammar unit), then word break " " (plain space, never cuts mid-word). Separators = ["

", "
", ". ", " "]. Two newlines mark a paragraph boundary.
summary: Introduces recursive structural chunking as trying the most structural separator first and falling back to smaller ones only when chunks exceed the size limit.

text: doc-7 is a login troubleshooting article (~380 tokens, markdown) with three distinct topic sections: ## Common Login Errors (wrong password, account locked after five failed attempts in fifteen minutes, session expired when an idle tab is reopened), ## Browser Compatibility (Safari blocks third-party cookies breaking SSO redirect, Chrome strict tracking protection same effect, fix is to add auth domain to site exceptions list), and ## Resetting Account Lockout (open account-admin panel, search by user email, click reset lockout, audit log captures admin action with timestamp). Apply fixed-size chunking with size=128 and overlap=0. Chunk 1 (tokens 0-128) gets all of "Common Login Errors" - one clean topic. Chunk 2 (tokens 128-256) gets the tail of "Browser Compatibility" plus the head of "Account Lockout" - spans two unrelated topics. A retrieval query for "Safari cookie SSO" matches this chunk but the answer is half-Safari, half-lockout-admin. Chunk 3 (tokens 256-380) gets the tail of "Resetting Account Lockout" - missing the heading and first sentence, topic context gone. Chunk 2 mixes browser compatibility with account lockout so retrieval cannot answer either cleanly.
summary: Shows fixed-size chunking with size 128 breaking a three-section login troubleshooting doc at the wrong places, mixing browser compatibility with account lockout in one chunk.

text:
Same doc-7 with recursive structural chunking using separators=["
## ", "

", "
", ". ", " "] and size=128. The splitter sees three top-level headings and uses the heading break first. Result is three chunks: Chunk 1 (~120 tokens) - Common Login Errors: ## Common Login Errors. Wrong password is the most common cause... Account locked appears after five failed attempts... Session expired shows up when an idle tab is reopened. Chunk 2 (~95 tokens) - Browser Compatibility: ## Browser Compatibility. Safari blocks third-party cookies... Chrome with strict tracking protection has the same effect. The fix is to add the auth domain to the site exceptions list. Chunk 3 (~115 tokens) - Resetting Account Lockout: ## Resetting Account Lockout. Open the account-admin panel... click reset lockout and confirm... Audit log captures the admin action with a timestamp. Three chunks, each is exactly one topic. Retrieval matches the topic cleanly: a Safari cookie query goes to chunk 2 and nowhere else.
summary: Same doc split recursively with a heading separator first yields three clean topic chunks under 128 tokens each, so retrieval matches one topic cleanly.

text: When a single section exceeds the size limit, recursion descends to the next separator. doc-12 (API keys reference) has an "Authentication Header Format" section that runs 300 tokens - more than double the 128-token target. Step 1: try heading split. The section comes out at 300 tokens, limit is 128, too big. Step 2: fall back to paragraph break "\n\n". The section has three paragraphs of 100, 110, and 90 tokens. Each fits under 128. Step 3: emit three chunks (Chunk A 100 tok, Chunk B 110 tok, Chunk C 90 tok). Recursion stops as soon as every produced chunk fits, no need to drop to sentence or word splits. If a paragraph were still > 128, recursion would try ". " (sentence) next, then " " (word). The recursion goes deeper only when needed; most chunks stop at the paragraph level.
summary: Shows recursion descending past heading split to paragraph break when an oversized 300-token section needs to be cut into three sub-chunks of 100, 110, and 90 tokens.

text: Recursive structural is the default chunker in every major RAG framework and covers around 80% of production cases out of the box - the 80% baseline. Pros: preserves sentence and paragraph boundaries; respects markdown/HTML structure for free; zero embedding cost at chunk time (just string operations); implemented by every major RAG framework (LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, LlamaIndex SentenceSplitter). Cons: doesn't understand semantic boundaries - a topic spanning four paragraphs gets split; heading-aware but not heading-equivalent - two ## sections on the same theme are split into two chunks; plain-text docs without structural markers fall through to sentence/word splits, closer to fixed-size behavior. Semantic chunking fixes the semantic-boundary gap, and parent-child chunking fixes the parent-child gap.
summary: Frames recursive structural chunking as the 80% baseline, listing its zero-cost structure-preserving pros and its semantic-blindness cons.

text: Diagram: line chart of cosine similarity between adjacent sentences along a 12-sentence document. The x-axis shows sentence boundaries 1-2 through 11-12 (eleven adjacent pairs). The y-axis shows cosine similarity from 0 to 1. Within the creating cluster (boundaries 1-2 through 5-6) similarity stays high at 0.85-0.92 (specifically 0.91, 0.89, 0.87, 0.90, 0.88). At boundary 6-7 - the shift from creating to revoking - similarity drops sharply to 0.41. For the revoking cluster (boundaries 7-8 through 11-12) similarity climbs back to 0.83-0.91 (specifically 0.86, 0.85, 0.91, 0.83, 0.90). A red dashed horizontal threshold line at 0.7 marks the chunk-break cutoff. Only the boundary 6-7 point falls below the threshold, producing exactly one chunk break and two output chunks. Each data point on the polyline is labeled with its cosine value; the break point is highlighted in red and labeled 'Dip below threshold -> chunk break'.
summary: Line chart showing adjacent-sentence cosine similarity across doc-12, with a single dip to 0.41 at boundary 6-7 crossing the 0.7 threshold.

text: This chapter Semantic Chunking. When a document has no structural markers (no headings, no blank lines), recursive structural chunking degrades to word splits. Semantic chunking instead: (1) splits the doc into sentences, (2) embeds each sentence to get vectors, (3) computes cosine similarity between adjacent sentence vectors, (4) emits a chunk break wherever similarity dips below a threshold like 0.7. On doc-12 (12-sentence API keys reference: 6 creating sentences then 6 revoking sentences) within-cluster similarity stays at 0.85-0.92 and the create-to-revoke shift at boundary 6-7 drops to 0.41 - producing exactly one clean topic break and two chunks that don't mix sub-topics. Cost: 10-50x more expensive than structural chunking because every sentence must be embedded before chunking (~60 ms per 12-sentence doc; ~$13,000 to chunk 10M docs), then chunks are re-embedded for the index. Use semantic chunking when structural markers are absent and retrieval quality matters more than indexing budget.
summary: Semantic chunking detects topic shifts in marker-less prose by splitting wherever adjacent-sentence cosine similarity dips below a threshold, at 10-50x the cost of structural chunking.

text: What if a document has no structural markers? doc-12 is an API keys reference written as pure flowing prose - no headings, no bullet lists, no blank-line separators. Six paragraphs of plain narrative. Recursive structural chunking falls all the way through paragraph break, line break, sentence break, and lands on word splits - essentially fixed-size. doc-12 contains 12 sentences: the first six describe creating an API key (developer settings page, New Key button, human-readable label, audit logs, scope dropdown, optional expiry, Generate button - key shown once). The last six describe revoking a key (same developer settings page, find by label in active keys list, trash icon, revocation takes effect within sixty seconds, in-flight requests return 401, revoked keys remain in audit log for compliance). The topic shift lives in the middle and nothing in the markup tells the splitter where. The structural gap: no headings (no ##, no #), no blank lines (no double newline - recursive splitter sees one long paragraph), topic shift is invisible (creating-to-revoking switch is a semantic boundary with no surface marker; structural splitter cuts mid-topic at the 128-token mark). Need a chunker that reads meaning, not markup.
summary: Flat narrative docs with no headings or blank lines defeat structural chunkers because the topic shift has no surface marker.

text: Step 1: Embed every sentence independently. Split doc-12 into individual sentences using a sentence tokenizer (period-plus-space heuristic, or a real NLP library). Twelve sentences come out. Send each sentence through the embedding model and get back a vector. Now there are 12 vectors capturing the meaning of each sentence. Formula: embed(sentence_i) -> v_i  |  12 sentences -> 12 vectors. Example vectors (8-dim, illustrative): s1 'Creating an API key starts in the developer settings page.' = [0.82, 0.14, 0.46, 0.71, 0.09, 0.55, 0.23, 0.66]; s6 'Click Generate and the key value is shown one time only - copy it now.' = [0.86, 0.13, 0.50, 0.72, 0.12, 0.57, 0.24, 0.70]; s7 'Revoking an API key is done from the same developer settings page.' = [0.21, 0.78, 0.16, 0.34, 0.74, 0.18, 0.69, 0.27]; s12 'Revoked keys remain in the audit log forever for compliance review.' = [0.24, 0.82, 0.13, 0.32, 0.78, 0.17, 0.73, 0.26]. Sentences s1-s6 (creating cluster) have vectors pointing in similar directions. Sentences s7-s12 (revoking cluster) have vectors pointing in a different direction. The 8-dim vectors shown are illustrative; production models use 768 or 1536 dims.
summary: Tokenize the doc into sentences then embed each one independently to get 12 meaning vectors per the doc-12 example.

text: Step 2: Plot cosine similarity between adjacent sentences. For each pair of adjacent sentences, compute cosine similarity between their vectors. Plot it along the doc. Inside a topic cluster similarity stays high (0.85-0.92). At the topic shift between sentence 6 (creating) and sentence 7 (revoking) it drops to 0.41. Set a threshold like 0.7 - any boundary below the threshold is a chunk break. Per-boundary cosine values: 1-2 = 0.91, 2-3 = 0.89, 3-4 = 0.87, 4-5 = 0.90, 5-6 = 0.88, 6-7 = 0.41 (DROP), 7-8 = 0.86, 8-9 = 0.85, 9-10 = 0.91, 10-11 = 0.83, 11-12 = 0.90. Algorithm: For i in 1..11: sim_i = cosine(v_i, v_i+1). If sim_i < 0.7 -> emit chunk break at boundary i. The red dashed threshold line at 0.7 marks the chunk-break cutoff. Only boundary 6-7 dips below 0.7.
summary: Compute adjacent-sentence cosine similarities, plot them, and emit a chunk break wherever similarity dips below 0.7.

text: Step 3: Each cluster of high-similarity sentences becomes one chunk. One boundary fell below the threshold (boundary 6-7 at sim 0.41). That single break splits the doc into exactly 2 chunks. Chunk 1 (creating cluster, sentences 1-6): 'Creating an API key starts in the developer settings page. Click the New Key button and provide a label. The label appears in audit logs. Choose a scope. Optional expiry sets a date. Click Generate and the key value is shown one time only.' Chunk break at boundary 6-7, sim = 0.41. Chunk 2 (revoking cluster, sentences 7-12): 'Revoking an API key is done from the same developer settings page. Find the key by its label. Click the trash icon. Revocation takes effect within sixty seconds. In-flight requests return 401 after propagation. Revoked keys remain in the audit log for compliance.' The win: semantic chunking found the topic shift that structural chunking would miss - no headings, no double newlines, no markers of any kind. Retrieval behavior: a query for 'how do I revoke a key' matches chunk 2 cleanly; a query for 'creating a scoped key' matches chunk 1. Neither chunk mixes the two sub-topics. Structural chunkers would have cut at the 128-token mark (somewhere inside sentence 5 or 6) and emitted a chunk that mixes 'optional expiry' with 'revoking the key'.
summary: The single cosine dip at boundary 6-7 splits doc-12 into a clean creating chunk and revoking chunk where structural chunkers would have cut mid-topic.

text: The cost: embed-before-chunk is 10-50x more expensive. Semantic chunking has to embed every sentence before it can decide where to chunk. That makes it an order of magnitude (or more) slower and costlier than structural chunking, where chunk-time work is just string splitting. Then you still re-embed the final chunks for the index. Structural / Fixed-Size cost: per doc 1 pass of string-splitting, about 0.1 ms. Embedding cost at chunk time is $0 - embedding happens later, once, on the final chunks. Pure CPU string ops. Semantic cost: per doc split into sentences (~0.5 ms) + embed each sentence (~5 ms per sentence x 12 = ~60 ms) + cosine math (~0.1 ms). At $0.10 per million tokens, a 30-doc corpus chunked semantically costs about $0.04. At 10M docs that is about $13,000 just to chunk - then you re-embed the final chunks for the index. Use semantic chunking when retrieval quality matters more than indexing budget, and when structural markers are absent.
summary: Semantic chunking is 10-50x costlier than structural because every sentence must be embedded before chunking, plus the final chunks are re-embedded for the index.

text: Side-by-side comparison diagram. Left panel (chunk-then-embed baseline, red): doc-1 (384 tokens) bar is sliced into 3 chunk segments. Each chunk independently arrows down into its own 'Embed (Chunk Only)' box, producing chunk vectors v1, v2, v3. Label: '3 Separate Passes - Each Vector Sees Only Its Own Chunk'. Right panel (late chunking, green): same doc-1 (384 tokens) bar arrows into a single 'Single Embedding Pass - Attention Over All 384 Tokens' box. Output is a 12-cell token hidden-state strip (N x hidden_dim). Two yellow dashed boundaries mark mean-pool segments. Label 'Mean-Pool At Boundaries' produces 3 chunk vectors v_chunk1, v_chunk2, v_chunk3. Caption: '1 Pass + Pool - Every Chunk Vector Sees The Whole Doc'.
summary: Side-by-side visual contrasts three independent chunk-embedding passes against one whole-doc pass plus boundary mean-pool producing chunk vectors.

text: Token-attention sweep diagram. A single dashed purple attention arc sweeps from token 1 to token N over a row of 12 token cells from doc-1, with t1='Sarah' (chunk 1, cyan) and t10='she' (chunk 3, orange) highlighted as the anaphora pair. Hidden state cells h1...h12 sit directly below the tokens. Two yellow dashed vertical pool boundaries at token position 128 and 256 split the strip into 3 segments feeding 3 chunk vectors v_chunk1 (cyan), v_chunk2 (blue), v_chunk3 (orange). Formula label: v_chunk_i = Mean-Pool(h_start...h_end). Annotation: v_chunk3 pools h9 to h12 - and h10 ('she') attended to h1 ('Sarah') - so v_chunk3 still encodes 'Sarah' even though the word lives in chunk 1.
summary: Sweep diagram shows a single attention arc connecting 'Sarah' (h1) to 'she' (h10) so the pooled chunk-3 vector still encodes the antecedent.

text: Late chunking (Jina AI 2024) flips the order of chunking and embedding. Baseline chunk-then-embed runs the embedding model separately on each chunk, so a chunk containing 'she' loses the antecedent 'Sarah' that lived in an earlier chunk. Late chunking runs one embedding pass over the whole doc with full attention across tokens, then mean-pools the token hidden states at chunk boundaries: H = embed_tokens(doc); v_chunk_i = mean-pool(H[start:end]). Same compute, but every chunk vector sees the whole doc. On the password-reset doc-1, the query 'Did Sarah get her reset email?' flips chunk 3 from 0.34 (rank-3 miss) to 0.81 (Top-1 hit). Requires a model that exposes token-level hidden states (jina-embeddings-v2; not OpenAI/Cohere pooled APIs) and the doc must fit in one pass (8K-32K). Best for anaphora-heavy corpora; otherwise fall back to chunk-then-embed plus reranking or hybrid search.
summary: Chapter covers late chunking - embed whole doc, then pool at chunk boundaries - which preserves anaphora and lifts recall when the model exposes token hidden states.

text: Chunk-then-embed loses cross-chunk context. Doc-1 is a password-reset article split into 3 chunks. Chunk 1 (tokens 0-128) names Sarah and sets up the reset flow - the antecedent lives here. Chunk 2 (tokens 128-256) covers the token being hashed, stored in the password_reset_tokens table, and emailed as a single-use link with subject 'Reset Your Password' arriving within thirty seconds - no mention of who requested it. Chunk 3 (tokens 256-384) says the link expires after 24 hours; 'she' must request a new one if 'she' misses the window, and the old token is invalidated on reuse. The pronoun 'she' has no antecedent in chunk 3, so its vector matches she/her queries weakly. When each chunk is embedded on its own, the chunk-3 vector has zero knowledge that 'she' refers to Sarah from chunk 1. Cross-chunk references are not a corner case - any doc with pronouns, 'the system', 'this approach', 'that field' bleeds context the moment you slice it.
summary: Chunking before embedding strips cross-chunk pronoun references, so a chunk that says 'she' has no idea who 'she' is.

text: Chunk-then-embed vs embed-then-chunk are two orderings of the same operations. Baseline chunk-then-embed: chunks the doc first, then runs the embedding model on each chunk in isolation, giving 3 separate passes where each vector sees only its own chunk. Formula: v_i = embed(chunk_i). Late chunking (embed-then-chunk): runs the embedding model once on the whole doc so attention links every token to every other token, then mean-pools the token hidden states into chunk vectors at chunk boundaries. Formula: H = embed_tokens(doc); v_chunk_i = mean-pool(H[chunk_i.start : chunk_i.end]). Same compute as baseline for the same doc, but every chunk vector sees the whole doc. The diagrams show doc-1 (384 tokens), 3 chunk segments, a single embedding pass producing a token hidden-state strip of shape N x hidden_dim, dashed yellow pool boundaries, and 3 output chunk vectors v_chunk1, v_chunk2, v_chunk3.
summary: Late chunking flips the order: embed the whole doc once with full attention, then mean-pool token hidden states at chunk boundaries.

text: Late chunking on doc-1: chunk 3's vector now encodes Sarah. The embedding model runs once over all 384 tokens. Attention links every token to every other token. The hidden state for the pronoun 'she' in chunk 3 has attended to 'Sarah' in chunk 1. When we mean-pool the chunk-3 token hidden states, the resulting v_chunk3 still carries the Sarah signal. The diagram shows a single attention arc (token 1 to token N) sweeping across 12 token cells with t1='Sarah' highlighted in chunk 1 and t10='she' highlighted in chunk 3. Hidden state cells h1...h12 sit below the tokens. Yellow dashed pool boundaries at token 128 and token 256 segment the strip into 3 chunk vectors v_chunk1, v_chunk2, v_chunk3. Annotation: v_chunk3 pools h9 to h12, and h10 ('she') attended to h1 ('Sarah'), so v_chunk3 still encodes 'Sarah' even though the word lives in chunk 1. Concrete shapes: H = embed_tokens(doc-1); H.shape = [384, 768]; v_chunk3 = mean(H[256:384]).
summary: Tracing doc-1: 'she' in chunk 3 attends to 'Sarah' in chunk 1 during the single pass, so v_chunk3 = mean(H[256:384]) still carries the Sarah signal.

text: Query 'Did Sarah get her reset email?' now matches chunk 3. Same query, same 3 chunks, same embedding model - only the embedding order changed. Chunk-then-embed (baseline) scores: chunk 1 (mentions Sarah, forgets password) = 0.78 Top-1, chunk 2 (token generation, email send) = 0.65, chunk 3 (link expiry, 'she' re-request) = 0.34. Top-1 is chunk 1 - misses the actual answer; the 'she' vector in chunk 3 has no idea who 'she' is. Late chunking (Jina 2024) scores: chunk 1 = 0.72, chunk 2 = 0.68, chunk 3 = 0.81 Top-1. Top-1 is chunk 3 - hits the answer; the chunk-3 token hidden states attended to 'Sarah' in chunk 1 before pooling. The retriever ranks chunk 3 first because its vector now encodes the antecedent 'Sarah' from chunk 1. Chunk 3 flipped from a 0.34 miss to a 0.81 hit.
summary: Concrete retrieval comparison: chunk 3 jumps from 0.34 (rank-3 miss) to 0.81 (Top-1 hit) once embeddings are computed via late chunking.
terms: Did Sarah get her reset email

text: Late chunking pros, cons, when to use. Wins: preserves cross-chunk pronouns and references (anaphora resolution); single embedding pass equals same compute as chunk-then-embed for the same doc; significant recall gains on docs with anaphora (he, she, it, this, that, the X); released 2024 by Jina AI for the jina-embeddings-v2 model family. Limits: requires an embedding model that exposes token-level hidden states (most OpenAI / Cohere APIs return only the pooled final vector - you cannot late-chunk with them); requires the whole doc to fit in one embedding pass (8K-32K token ceilings); pooling strategy matters (mean-pool vs CLS vs attention-weighted - jina-embeddings-v2 uses mean-pool). When to use: reach for late chunking when your corpus has heavy anaphora (support docs, legal text, narrative articles), the docs fit in one embedding pass, and you can run an open-weights model like jina-embeddings-v2 that exposes token-level hidden states. Otherwise keep chunk-then-embed and lean on reranking or hybrid search to recover cross-chunk recall.
summary: Late chunking is a free recall boost on anaphora-heavy docs, but only works with open-weights models that expose token hidden states and docs that fit one pass.

text: Tree hierarchy diagram for Doc-4 Refunds Policy. The root node at the top (green) holds the whole doc - 3 sections totaling 900 tokens. Three section nodes below (purple) are Eligibility, Process, and Edge Cases, each ~300 tokens. Beneath each section sit four leaf nodes (cyan, 64 tokens each): Eligibility has L1-L4, Process has L5-L8, Edge Cases has L9-L12. Edges connect root to all three sections and each section to its four leaves. Bottom annotation: index stores leaf vectors, retrieval finds leaves, and we swap each leaf for its parent before sending to the LLM.
summary: Visual tree showing root, three section nodes, and twelve leaf chunks for a refunds-policy document, with index searching leaves and parents swapped in later.

text: Leaf-to-parent swap flow diagram. On the left a cyan box shows the retrieved leaf L7 (64 tokens) containing the snippet "...no refunds after 30 days..." annotated with parent-id Sec-Edge-Cases and score 0.89. An orange arrow labeled "Parent Lookup" with formula parent_store[L7.parent_id] points to the right side. On the right a purple box labeled "Parent Section (Goes Into Prompt)" shows the Edge Cases section (300 tokens) with six bullets: 14-day full refund window, 15-30 day prorated refund, no refunds after 30 days, manual-review carve-out, appeal path (Form A-12), special-case refunds. A note reads ~4.7x more context than leaf. An orange arrow drops down to an LLM Prompt Context box.
summary: Visual flow from retrieved leaf L7 through a parent-lookup to the full Edge Cases section that is fed into the LLM prompt.

text: Tree hierarchy diagram showing the doc-4 refunds tree extended with a small yellow summary card placed beside each section node. Each summary card is labeled "Summary (LLM-Made)" with a one-sentence summary ("Who qualifies and refund window.", "Filing steps and SLAs.", "Late requests and appeals.") and a note Indexed - 1 Vector. The root is green, sections are purple, summaries are yellow, leaves are cyan and faded (since they are no longer indexed). Dashed connectors run from section to summary card and from section to leaves. Bottom annotation: index stores summary vectors, retrieval finds a summary, swap to the full parent section on a hit.
summary: Visual tree with yellow LLM-generated summary cards beside each section, showing how summaries are indexed in place of leaves with parents swapped in on a hit.

text: Hierarchical (parent-child) chunking resolves the RAG chunk-size dilemma: small chunks retrieve precisely but lack context, while large chunks have context but blur retrieval. The fix is to chunk twice into a tree - root (whole doc), section nodes (~300 tokens), and leaves (~64 tokens). Only leaf vectors are indexed and searched. After retrieval, each matched leaf is swapped for its parent section before the text enters the LLM prompt, giving precise retrieval plus rich generation context. A variation indexes LLM-generated summary chunks per section instead of leaves. Use it when docs have natural section structure and queries need surrounding context; cost is roughly 2-3x raw corpus storage and one extra parent-lookup per hit. LangChain ParentDocumentRetriever and LlamaIndex RecursiveRetriever ship this off the shelf.
summary: Hierarchical chunking indexes small leaves for precision and swaps in big parent sections at generation time, giving both precise retrieval and rich LLM context.

text: Chunk size is a tug-of-war between two phases of RAG. A retriever finds the best matching chunk; an LLM answers from the chunk text. The smaller the chunk, the more focused each vector is - but the less context the LLM sees. The larger the chunk, the more context the LLM has - but the fuzzier the vector becomes. Small chunks (64 tokens): retrieval precision is high because each chunk is one focused fact; generation context is low because the LLM may need surrounding paragraphs to answer fully. Large chunks (1024 tokens): retrieval precision is low because many topics per chunk make the vector average them; generation context is high because the LLM has the whole section. Pick one and you are forced to compromise - unless you do not have to.
summary: Small chunks retrieve precisely but starve the LLM of context; large chunks give context but blur retrieval, setting up the need for a hybrid approach.

text: Hierarchical chunking: leaves are small, parents are big. Chunk the doc twice and build a tree. The root is the whole doc. The mid-level nodes are sections of around 300 tokens each. The leaves are tiny 64-token chunks that fit one fact apiece. Only the leaf vectors live in the index - they are what we search. Parents live in a separate parent-text store, keyed by id, ready to be swapped in at generation time. Example: Doc-4 Refunds Policy has 1 root + 3 sections (Eligibility, Process, Edge Cases at ~300 tokens each) + 12 leaves (L1-L12 at 64 tokens each). The two data structures are: Index: { leaf_id -> vector } and Parent Store: { leaf_id -> parent_text }. The index stores leaf vectors so retrieval finds leaves; we swap each leaf for its parent before sending to the LLM.
summary: Hierarchical chunking builds a tree of root, section, and leaf nodes; only leaf vectors are indexed and parents are stored separately for swap-in at generation time.

text: Step 1: retrieval finds the best leaf. The user asks "Can I get a refund if I cancel after 30 days?" The query is embedded once into q_vec. We search the leaf index. The top-3 leaves come back ranked by cosine similarity: L7 scores 0.89 with snippet "...no refunds after 30 days..." and is the top-1 hit; L3 scores 0.74 with snippet "Refund eligibility window: 14 days full, 15-30 days prorated..."; L12 scores 0.68 with snippet "Appeal process for denied refund requests...". L7 wins at 0.89 because its 64-token text directly states the 30-day cutoff - a precise hit on the query.
summary: A concrete example shows the user query embedded and matched against leaf vectors, with leaf L7 scoring 0.89 as the top hit on the 30-day refund cutoff.

text: Step 2: swap the leaf for its parent before the LLM. We retrieved a leaf, but we do not send the leaf to the LLM. Each leaf carries the id of its parent section. A parent-lookup (parent_store[L7.parent_id]) pulls the full 300-token Edge Cases section from the parent-text store. That section is what enters the prompt - so the LLM has the eligibility nuance, the appeal path, and the carve-outs, not just one sentence in isolation. Concretely L7's parent is the Edge Cases section containing: 14-day full refund window, 15-30 day prorated refund, no refunds after 30 days, manual-review carve-out, appeal path (Form A-12), special-case refunds - about 4.7x more context than the leaf. Retrieval precision came from the leaf; generation context comes from the parent.
summary: After matching a leaf, hierarchical chunking looks up that leaf's parent section and sends the full section to the LLM, gaining roughly 4.7x more context than the leaf alone.

text: Variation: index summary chunks instead of leaves. Same tree, different retrieval target. Run an LLM over each section once at index time to generate a 1-2 sentence summary. Embed and index those summary chunks instead of the leaves. On a hit, swap to the full parent section (or even the whole doc). For Doc-4 the summaries are: Eligibility -> "Who qualifies and refund window.", Process -> "Filing steps and SLAs.", Edge Cases -> "Late requests and appeals." Each summary becomes one vector in the index. Summaries pack more meaning per token, so the vector match can be more semantic. Trade-off: summaries are denser semantically (one vector encodes the gist of a 300-token section), but require an LLM pass at index time to generate them.
summary: Instead of indexing leaves, generate one LLM summary per section, embed those, and swap to the full parent section on a hit for denser semantic matching.

text: Hierarchical chunking: when and at what cost. It is a workhorse pattern in production RAG: precise retrieval and rich generation context at the price of storing parent texts. Use hierarchical when: docs are long and have natural section structure (manuals, policies, RFCs); queries need surrounding context to answer well (legal, medical, technical docs where one sentence is rarely enough); storage cost for parent texts is acceptable (roughly 2-3x raw doc size). Cost and implementation: storage is index + parent-text store, roughly 2-3x raw corpus size; latency is one extra lookup per hit (parent-from-leaf-id), sub-millisecond; implementation is near zero because every major framework has it - LangChain ParentDocumentRetriever, LlamaIndex RecursiveRetriever. Reach for it when docs have section structure and queries need surrounding context.
summary: Hierarchical chunking fits long structured docs needing context-rich answers, costs about 2-3x storage and a sub-ms parent lookup, and is shipped by LangChain and LlamaIndex out of the box.

text: Horizontal bar chart showing four bars of retrieval failure rate from Anthropic's September 2024 Contextual Retrieval benchmark: Naive Embedding Only at 5.7% (baseline, gray), + Contextual Embedding at 3.7% (35% reduction, cyan), + Contextual BM25 at 2.9% (49% reduction, green), + Reranking at 1.9% (67% reduction, orange). X-axis labeled "Failure Rate (%)" with ticks at 0, 2, 4, 6 percent. Title-case axis labels. Lower is better.
summary: Bar chart of Anthropic's failure-rate benchmark: 5.7%, 3.7%, 2.9%, 1.9% across the four stack levels.

text: Contextual Retrieval (Anthropic September 2024) fixes the orphan-chunk problem where chunks sharing boilerplate phrases produce near-identical vectors. At index time a cheap LLM (e.g., Claude Haiku) writes a 1-2 sentence prefix saying what doc and section the chunk lives in. The prefix is prepended to the body and the whole thing is embedded. On Anthropic's benchmark, top-20 failure rate drops from 5.7% (naive) to 3.7% with contextual embedding (35% cut), 2.9% adding contextual BM25 hybrid (49% cut), and 1.9% adding a cross-encoder reranker (67% cut). Cost ~$0.001/chunk, ~$100 one-time per 100K chunks with prompt caching. Skip on small homogeneous corpora; use when recall is the bottleneck on multi-doc corpora with shared surface phrases. Composes with hybrid search and rerankers - doesn't replace them.
summary: Chapter summary: Contextual Retrieval prepends LLM-written doc/section context to chunks before embedding, cutting Anthropic-benchmark failure rates 35-67% when stacked with hybrid search and rerankers.

text: Three chunks from three unrelated docs all end with the same sentence "click Save to confirm." Doc-2 Email Change Guide: "...verify your new email address, then click Save to confirm." Doc-15 Role Permissions Guide: "...assign the new role to the user, then click Save to confirm." Doc-18 Notifications Settings: "...select the channels you want notifications on, then click Save to confirm." The embedding model sees only the token sequence. With nearly identical tokens the three chunks produce nearly identical vectors (around [0.31, 0.42, 0.18, 0.55, 0.27, 0.39, 0.46, 0.22]), so a query "How do I change my email?" lands on all three at the same similarity 0.71. Retrieval picks one effectively at random. Recall@1 collapses to guessing - the wrong chunk is returned roughly two times out of three.
summary: Chunks stripped of context produce near-identical vectors and tie on similarity, so retrieval becomes random.

text: Solution: prepend an LLM-generated context line. Before each chunk is embedded, run a cheap LLM call once at index time to write a short 1-2 sentence context: what doc the chunk is from, what section, and what it is about. Prepend that context line to the chunk text. Example for Chunk A: prefix = "Document: Email Change Guide. Section: Step 4 - Verify New Address. This chunk is about confirming the new email after verification." Body unchanged = "...verify your new email address, then click Save to confirm." Full Augmented Chunk = [Prefix] + [Body] -> this is what gets embedded and indexed. The body never changes - original tokens still there for the LLM to read at generation time. Only the index-side representation gets richer.
summary: Contextual Retrieval prepends a short LLM-written context prefix to each chunk before embedding so vectors carry doc/section signal.

text:
Augmentation prompt template (run once per chunk):

Here is a chunk from a documentation page.
<document_title>{document_title}</document_title>
<section_heading>{section_heading}</section_heading>
<chunk>
{chunk}
</chunk>
Write a short (1-2 sentence) context that situates this chunk within the document and section so retrieval models can find it. Output only the context, no preamble.

Model choice: Anthropic recommends Claude Haiku - a cheap model is enough; the task is short and structured. Cost at index time: roughly $0.001 per chunk. For 100K chunks that is about $100 one-time. With prompt caching the document prefix is paid once across all its chunks.
summary: The exact augmentation prompt template Anthropic uses plus model choice (Haiku) and cost (~$0.001/chunk, ~$100 for 100K).

text: Before vs after: recall on the email-change query. Without augmentation: Chunk A (Doc-2 Email Change) 0.71, Chunk B (Doc-15 Role Permissions) 0.71, Chunk C (Doc-18 Notifications) 0.71. All three tied at 0.71. Top-1 is effectively random. Recall@1 = 33%. With augmentation: Chunk A 0.93 (top-1, augmentation explicitly mentions email change - direct hit), Chunk B 0.42 (augmentation pulls vector toward roles, away from email), Chunk C 0.39 (augmentation pulls vector toward notifications, away from email). Chunk A wins at 0.93. Top-1 is stable. Recall@1 = 100%. The augmented vectors are no longer near-duplicates - Chunk A's prefix mentions email change explicitly, pulling its vector toward the query; B and C drift away because their prefixes are about roles and notifications.
summary: Worked example shows recall@1 jumping from 33% (tied 0.71) to 100% (0.93 vs 0.42 vs 0.39) after augmentation.

text: Anthropic's 2024 benchmark: 49% recall improvement. Anthropic published a paper-length blog post in September 2024 measuring contextual retrieval over multiple corpora. Top-20 retrieval failure rate (lower is better): Naive Embedding Only 5.7% (baseline), + Contextual Embedding 3.7% (35% reduction), + Contextual BM25 2.9% (49% reduction), + Reranking 1.9% (67% reduction). Contextual embedding alone cuts failures by 35%. Add contextual BM25 hybrid and it is 49%. Stack a reranker on top and the combined stack hits 67% fewer failures than naive embedding. The 49% headline number is the failure rate reduction from naive embedding (5.7%) to contextual embedding plus contextual BM25 hybrid (2.9%).
summary: Anthropic's September 2024 benchmark: failure rate drops from 5.7% naive to 3.7% (contextual embedding, 35% cut), 2.9% (+BM25, 49% cut), 1.9% (+reranker, 67% cut).

text: When contextual retrieval is overkill. Skip if: corpus is fully homogeneous (one product manual; no risk of cross-doc duplicate phrases); index size is small (under 1K chunks; recall problems can be solved by hand-tuning); index budget is hard (the one-time $100-$1,000 augmentation cost is too high for a prototype). Use if: corpus has many docs with similar surface phrases (FAQ + support + product = lots of "click Save to confirm" overlap); recall is the bottleneck in your eval metrics; hybrid and reranking are already in place and you want one more lever. Rule of thumb: if recall is not your bottleneck, do not add the augmentation pass. The 49% headline is a ceiling, not a guarantee - it only shows up when chunks would otherwise collide on surface tokens.
summary: Skip contextual retrieval on small/homogeneous corpora or tight prototype budgets; use it when many docs share phrasing and recall is the bottleneck.

text: Stacks with hybrid search and rerankers for the full 67%. Contextual retrieval is not a replacement for hybrid search or for rerankers - it composes with them. Step 1: Contextual Augmentation (Index Time) - run an LLM over each chunk once. Prepend a 1-2 sentence context line that says what doc and section the chunk lives in. Embed and index the augmented chunk. Step 2: Hybrid Retrieval (Query Time) - dense embedding search and BM25 lexical search run in parallel over the augmented chunks. Their top-20 results are merged into a single candidate list. Hybrid merges semantic and lexical recall. Step 3: Reranker (Top-20 To Top-3) - a cross-encoder scores each query-chunk pair jointly and reorders the candidates. The top 3 go into the LLM prompt. Combined: 67% failure-rate reduction over naive. Augmentation lifts dense and BM25 recall in parallel. The reranker fixes ordering on whatever the hybrid stack surfaces.
summary: Three-step stack: augment chunks at index time, hybrid (dense+BM25) retrieve top-20, cross-encoder rerank to top-3 for the full 67% failure reduction.

text: Horizontal cost-budget scale with three tier markers laid out left to right along a dashed spine with an arrow on the right. Leftmost marker: Lab / Prototype (Free), green. Middle marker: Startup (~$100 one-time), cyan. Rightmost marker: Enterprise ($100 - $10K one-time), orange. Each tier sits above its name and below its cost label. An axis label reads "Indexing Budget Grows Left To Right." Below the scale, three cards repeat each tier with its recommended chunking recipe: Lab uses Recursive Structural only; Startup uses Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical; Enterprise uses Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical plus Contextual Retrieval plus Semantic, stacked with hybrid plus rerankers.
summary: Visual cost-budget spine showing lab, startup, and enterprise tiers each with their recommended chunking stack.

text: This chapter consolidates the six chunking strategies introduced earlier in the section (Fixed-Size, Recursive Structural, Semantic, Late, Hierarchical, Contextual Retrieval) and gives a three-axis decision framework for picking among them: document structure, query type, and indexing budget. It then walks through applying the framework to a customer support corpus, showing that production systems usually mix multiple strategies per doc-type rather than committing to one.
summary: Decision framework for choosing among the six chunking strategies along three axes - structure, queries, budget - applied to a real corpus.

text: Six Strategies, One Page. Earlier chapters introduced six chunking strategies. Lined up side by side each has its own retrieval quality, embedding cost at chunk time, and implementation difficulty profile. Fixed-Size: sliding window, no structure awareness; quality 1, cost 1, difficulty 1. Recursive Structural: tries paragraph then line then sentence then word; quality 2, cost 1, difficulty 1. Semantic: embed sentences, split where cosine dips; quality 3, cost 2, difficulty 2. Late: one whole-doc attention pass, then pool per chunk; quality 3, cost 2, difficulty 3. Hierarchical: small chunks retrieve, parents go to LLM; quality 3, cost 2, difficulty 2. Contextual Retrieval: LLM-generated context prefixed to each chunk; quality 4, cost 3, difficulty 2. No strategy wins on all three axes. Fixed-Size is cheapest but loses quality; Contextual gives highest retrieval quality but adds index-time LLM cost. The next three sub-steps pick the right one along three axes: doc structure, query type, and budget.
summary: Side-by-side scorecard of all six chunking strategies on quality, cost, and implementation difficulty.

text: Axis 1: What structure do your docs have? Structure constrains the chunker more than content does. Markdown / HTML: Recursive Structural (uses heading and paragraph markers natively). PDF (scanned or extracted): Recursive Structural after extraction, or Semantic if extraction quality is bad. Code: Recursive Structural with language-aware separators (function defs, class declarations). Flat-text narrative (no headings, no markdown): Semantic Chunking (the only way to find topic boundaries). Mixed / heterogeneous corpus: apply different strategies per doc-type; don't force one strategy. The single biggest signal is whether the corpus has explicit split points. If yes, Recursive Structural is almost always the right starting baseline. Only flat narrative forces you up the cost ladder to Semantic on day one.
summary: Map each document type (Markdown, PDF, code, flat text, mixed) to a recommended chunking strategy based on whether the docs have explicit split points.

text: Axis 2: What kinds of queries will you get? Query shape changes which chunk size and which retrieval pattern wins. Factual queries ("What is X?"): small chunks are fine; Recursive Structural plus Contextual if FAQ-style overlap; Hierarchical optional for context-heavy answers. Relational queries ("How does X relate to Y?"): Hierarchical helps because the LLM needs the whole relationship section; Late Chunking helps when entities are referenced across chunks. Comparative queries ("Compare X and Y"): multi-hop retrieval on top of any chunking, bigger chunks help, Hierarchical preferred. Hierarchical is the safest middle bet: it serves factual queries through its leaves and relational queries through its parents. Comparative queries on top of any chunking still need multi-hop retrieval.
summary: Match query type (factual, relational, comparative) to the chunking and retrieval pattern that best serves it.

text: Axis 3: What's your indexing budget? Chunking cost is a one-time index-time spend that scales with corpus size. Three tiers along a cost spine: Lab / Prototype (free) - Recursive Structural only, zero embedding cost at chunk time, iterate fast. Startup (~$100 one-time) - Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical for long-form docs; skip semantic and contextual until you see recall problems in your eval. Enterprise ($100 - $10K one-time) - Recursive Structural baseline plus Hierarchical plus Contextual Retrieval plus Semantic where prose dominates; stack with hybrid plus rerankers; budget for the augmentation cost. Cost climbs with augmentation, not with chunking itself. Recursive Structural is free to run; Contextual Retrieval is the big spike because it fires an LLM call per chunk. Treat the spike as a one-time index cost amortized across every future query.
summary: Three budget tiers (lab, startup, enterprise) with the chunking stack appropriate to each and an explanation that augmentation drives cost.

text: Walkthrough: picking strategies for the customer support corpus. The support corpus splits into three doc-types with three different profiles. The right answer is a mix, not one strategy. Account & Billing: 10 docs, FAQ-style, lots of repeated phrases like "click Save" - use Recursive Structural plus Contextual Retrieval; Contextual disambiguates duplicate phrases across email, role, and notifications docs. Product Features: 10 docs, longer technical pages with sections and code samples - use Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical; sections give clean splits, parent-swap gives the LLM context for technical answers. Troubleshooting: 10 docs, free-form runbooks, narrative paragraphs - use Recursive Structural plus Semantic for longer narratives; Semantic catches topic shifts within a runbook. Production chunking is rarely one strategy: mix per doc-type, measure recall, iterate. The 30-doc support corpus uses three different chunking recipes - Contextual for FAQ-style billing docs, Hierarchical for technical product features, Semantic for free-form troubleshooting runbooks.
summary: Concrete walkthrough applying the three-axis framework to a 30-doc customer support corpus, choosing a different chunking recipe for each doc-type.

text: Four-step decision flowchart for picking an embedding model rendered as SVG. Step 1 asks 'Need multilingual?' - Yes leads to Cohere v3 / Voyage / OpenAI 3-large. Step 2 asks 'Specialized domain?' - Yes leads to domain-tuned BGE or fine-tune. Step 3 asks 'Budget sensitive?' - Yes leads to self-host BGE. Step 4 is the default pick: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3. Each 'No' arrow flows down to the next question.
summary: Diagram: four-step decision flowchart from multilingual check to default OpenAI/Cohere pick.

text: Picking an embedding model: the model sets a hard recall ceiling no reranker can fix. Compare candidates on five axes - dimension, multilingual coverage, domain, cost, max context. Dimension scales memory and compute linearly; 1024 is the production sweet spot. Match language coverage to your data - multilingual models waste capacity on English-only corpora. Cost at 100M token ingest ranges from ~$50 self-hosted BGE to $13,000 OpenAI text-embedding-3-large. MTEB is a hint but over-fit risk is real - run your own eval. Use the four-step decision flow: multilingual? specialized domain? budget sensitive? else default to OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3.
summary: End-to-end framework for picking an embedding model across recall, five axes, cost, and a decision flow.

text: The embedding model decides the recall ceiling for retrieval. Chunking decides how text is split; the embedding model decides what those chunks mean as vectors. Two systems on the same corpus, with the same chunks and the same vector DB, can have wildly different recall just because one uses a stronger embedding model. The chosen model sets a hard ceiling: no reranker or hybrid trick can recover documents your embedder failed to bring close enough in the first place. Recall@10 on a support corpus (same chunks, same index): OpenAI ada-002 (older) = 0.62, Cohere embed-v3 = 0.83, BGE-Large = 0.81. Recall is 'did the right doc land in the top 10?' Higher is better.
summary: Embedding model sets the hard recall ceiling that no reranker can recover.

text: Five axes that matter when picking an embedding model: Model, Dim (output vector length), Multilingual (trained on multiple languages), Domain (general purpose vs specialized), Cost (price per million input tokens or self-host), Max Context (longest single input). Comparison table: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large - dim 3072, multilingual Yes, general domain, $0.13/M tokens, context 8191. Cohere embed-v3 - dim 1024, multilingual Yes, general, $0.10/M tokens, context 512. BGE-Large - dim 1024, EN + CN, general, free self-host, context 512. SBERT all-mpnet-base - dim 768, English mostly, general, free self-host, context 384. Voyage-2 - dim 1024, multilingual Yes, domain variants available, $0.10/M tokens, context 16000.
summary: Compares five embedding models across dimension, multilingual support, domain, cost, and max context.

text: Higher dim costs more memory and compute. Every dimension is a 4-byte float. Memory scales linearly with dim. At 1 million vectors (float32): d=384 = 1.5 GB, d=768 = 3 GB, d=1024 = 4 GB, d=3072 = 12 GB. Difference between d=384 and d=3072 is 1.5 GB versus 12 GB - same corpus, 8x the RAM bill and 8x the dot-product cost at query time. Bigger dim helps quality up to a point. Beyond ~1024 the marginal gain shrinks fast - returns diminish while memory and compute keep growing linearly. 1024 is the sweet spot for most production systems.
summary: Memory and dot-product cost scale linearly with embedding dimension; 1024 is the production sweet spot.

text: Multilingual models underperform on English-only data. A multilingual model spends representation capacity on every language it was trained on. If your corpus is only English, that extra capacity is wasted and the English representation is slightly weaker than a same-size English-only model. Match the model's language coverage to your data. 2x2 matrix: English-only model (e.g. SBERT) + English data = best quality, smallest dim, cheapest. English-only model + multilingual data = will fail on non-English queries. Multilingual model (e.g. Cohere v3) + English data = quality slightly worse, extra capacity wasted. Multilingual model + multilingual data = right tool for the job. If corpus is medical, legal, or code, off-the-shelf general models often underperform a domain-tuned model. Domain adaptation and fine-tuning are covered in the next chapter.
summary: Match model language coverage to data; multilingual capacity is wasted on English-only corpora.

text: Cost math at 100M token ingest. Ingesting a 100M-token corpus is a one-time charge but dominates the bill. Same corpus runs from ~$50 (self-hosted GPU for two days) to $13,000 (managed API on largest OpenAI model). OpenAI text-embedding-3-large: 100M / 1M x $0.13 = $13,000. Cohere embed-v3: 100M / 1M x $0.10 = $10,000. Self-hosted BGE on g5.xlarge (2 days): $1.006/hour x 48 hours = ~$50. Self-hosting is dramatically cheaper at scale. Managed APIs win on operational simplicity for small corpora where the per-token rate is rounding error compared to engineering time.
summary: Walks through ingest cost at 100M tokens showing $13k OpenAI vs $10k Cohere vs ~$50 self-hosted BGE.

text: MTEB is a hint, not an answer. MTEB is a public benchmark with 50+ retrieval and classification tasks. It is the best public signal for ranking embedding models - and also the trap most teams fall into. The MTEB caveat: MTEB is a public leaderboard. Models can be over-fit to it: trained on data resembling the test sets, tuned for MTEB metrics, or both. The leader on MTEB may underperform on YOUR data. Always run a small eval on your own queries before committing. Four-step decision flow: Step 1 - Need multilingual? Yes = use Cohere v3 / Voyage / OpenAI 3-large. Step 2 - Specialized domain? Yes = domain-tuned BGE or fine-tune (see next chapter). Step 3 - Budget sensitive? Yes = self-host BGE. Step 4 - Default = OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3. Follow flow top to bottom. Each 'No' sends you to next question. Run your eval on your own data before locking it in.
summary: MTEB benchmark is over-fit risk; use the four-step decision flow then eval on your own data.
