text: Triplet-loss diagram: an anchor point A in the center (purple circle), with a green arrow labeled 'Pull Close' pulling a positive sample P (green circle) close, and a red arrow labeled 'Push Away' pushing a negative sample N (red circle) farther away. Visualizes the contrastive update direction in embedding space.
summary: Visual of anchor, positive, and negative points with pull/push arrows illustrating triplet loss.

text: Before-versus-after bar chart comparing Recall@5, MRR, and Latency for off-the-shelf vs fine-tuned embeddings on the support corpus. Red 'Before' bars beside green 'After' bars per metric. Recall@5 0.78 to 0.91, MRR 0.62 to 0.84, Latency stays at 30 ms.
summary: Bar chart showing recall and MRR gains while latency stays flat after fine-tuning.

text: Training loss curve showing triplet loss dropping from 0.50 at epoch 0 to 0.22 at epoch 1, 0.10 at epoch 2, and 0.05 at epoch 3 over a fine-tuning run on 50k support-corpus triples. Pink line plot with labeled data points; x-axis epochs, y-axis loss 0.00 to 0.50.
summary: Loss curve illustrating triplet loss convergence over three epochs of fine-tuning.

text: Domain Adaptation - Fine-Tuning Embeddings: general-purpose embedders miss domain-specific semantics (medical abbreviations like MI, tPA). Contrastive fine-tuning with triplet loss L_triplet = max(0, d(A,P) - d(A,N) + margin), margin = 0.2 typical, pulls positives closer and pushes negatives away. Training data = (anchor query, positive chunk, negative chunk) triples; hard negatives mined from top-20 retrieval results teach the boundary. Fine-tune when vocabulary is specialized, recall < 80% on a 200-500 pair golden set, and 5k+ labeled pairs exist. Example: 50k support-corpus triples on BGE-large, ~$200 managed cost, lifts Recall@5 from 0.78 to 0.91 and MRR from 0.62 to 0.84 while latency stays at 30 ms. Re-train every 3-6 months for drift.
summary: Overview of fine-tuning embedding models with triplet contrastive learning for domain-specific RAG retrieval.

text: Off-the-shelf embeddings sometimes miss domain semantics. General-purpose embedding models are trained on web text - they know cat and dog are similar but have barely seen MI (myocardial infarction) or tPA (tissue plasminogen activator). On a medical corpus this shows up as low cosine similarity between an abbreviation and its full term, which wrecks retrieval. Cosine similarity on a medical corpus: 'MI' vs 'Myocardial infarction' - off-the-shelf 0.42, fine-tuned 0.95. 'tPA contraindicated' vs 'Tissue plasminogen activator should not be given' - off-the-shelf 0.51, fine-tuned 0.90. Recall@10 on a held-out medical query set: off-the-shelf embedder 0.62, fine-tuned on medical corpus 0.88 - a 26-point recall lift. That is the value of teaching the embedder your domain.
summary: Off-the-shelf embedders fail on specialized vocabulary, causing huge recall drops on domain corpora.

text: Contrastive fine-tuning: pull positives close, push negatives away. We do not retrain the embedder from scratch - we take the existing model and nudge its vector space using triples: an anchor query, a positive document that should match, and a negative document that should not. Each training step pulls anchor closer to positive and farther from negative. Triplet loss formula: L_triplet = max(0, d(A, P) - d(A, N) + margin), where d = distance, A = anchor, P = positive, N = negative, margin = 0.2 typical. The loss is zero only when the negative is at least 'margin' farther from the anchor than the positive. If the negative is too close, the gradient pushes it away. The margin prevents the loss from going to zero just because positives are slightly closer than negatives.
summary: Triplet loss formula and contrastive learning mechanism that warps embedding space to favor true matches.

text: Pair construction decides quality - the training data is the model. Each row is one triple: anchor (real user query), positive (chunk that should match), negative (chunk that should not). Example triples from a support corpus: anchor 'How do I reset my password?' / positive '[doc-1, chunk 1] - To reset your password, visit the account settings page and click Forgot Password' / negative '[doc-3, chunk 1] - Refunds are processed within 5-7 business days of approval'. Anchor 'I can't sign in' / positive '[doc-25] Login Troubleshooting: clear cookies, verify caps lock, and check for typos in your email' / negative '[doc-7] Dashboard tour: the home screen shows your usage metrics and recent activity'. Anchor 'Cancel my subscription' / positive '[doc-15] Account Deletion Flow: go to billing settings and click Cancel Plan to stop renewal' / negative '[doc-2] Email Change: update your contact email from the profile settings page'. Hard negatives teach the most: a hard negative sits NEAR the positive in vector space but is irrelevant - same vocabulary, wrong topic. Mining hard negatives (top-20 retrieval results minus the true positive) teaches the actual semantic boundary. Easy negatives (random docs from unrelated topics) waste compute because the gap is already obvious to the base model.
summary: How to construct anchor/positive/negative triples and why hard-negative mining matters more than random sampling.

text: When is fine-tuning worth the cost? Fine-tuning takes labeled data, compute time, and a recurring re-training budget as your corpus drifts. Fine-tune when: highly specialized vocabulary (medical, legal, code); off-the-shelf recall below 80% on your golden set; you have 5k+ query-positive pairs labeled; budget for periodic re-training as embeddings drift. Skip and use off-the-shelf when: general English domain; common domain (e-commerce, support, news); fewer than 5k labeled pairs available; recall already above 80% on the golden set. The cleanest signal: build a small golden set (200-500 query-positive pairs) and measure recall with the off-the-shelf model. Below 80%, fine-tuning is usually worth it. Above 80%, the marginal gain rarely pays for the recurring training cost.
summary: Decision rubric for when to fine-tune embeddings vs sticking with off-the-shelf based on domain, data, and golden-set recall.

text: Before vs after on the support corpus: fine-tuning on 50k support-ticket triples on BGE-large. Recall and MRR jump, latency does not - fine-tuning changes vector geometry, not the index or query path. Recall@5: before 0.78, after 0.91. MRR: before 0.62, after 0.84. Latency: before 30 ms, after 30 ms. Training loss over 3 epochs (50k triples): epoch 0 loss 0.50, epoch 1 loss 0.22, epoch 2 loss 0.10, epoch 3 loss 0.05. Cost: ~$200 for a 50k-pair fine-tune on BGE-large via a managed service. Quality delta: usually 10-20 points on recall. Re-evaluate every 3-6 months as the corpus drifts and new query patterns appear.
summary: Concrete before/after metrics, loss curve, and cost for a 50k-triple fine-tune on the support corpus.

text: Three-bar chart of recall@5 comparing retrieval methods on a 50-query held-out set from the support corpus. BM25 Only bar at 0.68 (red). Dense Only bar at 0.74 (yellow). Hybrid (RRF) bar at 0.86 (green). Caption: Recall@5 On 50 Held-Out Queries. Hybrid RRF is the tallest bar, showing +12 points over dense alone and +18 points over BM25 alone.
summary: Bar chart visualizing recall@5 for BM25 (0.68), dense (0.74), and hybrid RRF (0.86) on a 50-query held-out set.

text: This chapter - Hybrid Retrieval for RAG. Dense embeddings miss exact terms (API keys, error codes) while BM25 misses synonyms ("cancel" vs "Account Deletion"). Hybrid retrieval runs both and fuses rankings. Two fusion strategies: Reciprocal Rank Fusion RRF(d) = sum 1/(k + rank_r(d)) with k=60, parameter-free and robust; and weighted score Score(d) = alpha * bm25 + (1-alpha) * dense after min-max normalization. RRF worked example shows doc-25 wins because both retrievers placed it top-2. Empirically recall@5 jumps from 0.68 (BM25) and 0.74 (dense) to 0.86 (hybrid RRF) on a 50-query held-out set, costing only +30ms. Alpha can be tuned per query type: 0.7 factual, 0.3 conceptual, 0.5 default; a small LLM classifier picks alpha at runtime for 4-8 extra recall points. Decision rule: always start with RRF, add weighted-alpha and classifier only if metrics demand, re-evaluate quarterly.
summary: Hybrid retrieval fuses BM25 and dense rankings via RRF or weighted alpha, lifting recall@5 from ~0.7 to 0.86 with +30ms latency; default to RRF and tune alpha by query type only if needed.

text: Dense retrieval misses exact terms; BM25 misses synonyms. Hybrid search ships dense vectors and BM25 side by side and fuses their rankings. Example 1 - Query: "How do I get an API key?" Truth: doc-13 (API Keys). Dense Top-3: [doc-7, doc-12, doc-5] missed doc-13 because the embedding of "API key" sat closer to generic dev-tools docs than the actual API-Keys page. BM25 Top-3: [doc-13, doc-7, doc-12] found it. Example 2 - Query: "Subscription cancel" Truth: doc-15 (Account Deletion). Dense Top-3: [doc-15, doc-19, doc-3] found it. BM25 Top-3: [doc-3, doc-19, doc-25] missed it because the cancel page is titled "Account Deletion" and does not contain the token "cancel". Hybrid keeps both retrievers and fuses rankings - each catches what the other misses.
summary: Shows two concrete queries where dense and BM25 each fail, motivating hybrid retrieval.

text: Two fusion strategies dominate production RAG. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): RRF(d) = sum over retrievers r of: 1 / (k + rank_r(d)), where k = 60 (standard). Robust default. No weights to tune. Each retriever contributes a rank-reciprocal, so doc-1 always outweighs doc-2 regardless of underlying score scales. Weighted Score Fusion: Score(d) = alpha * bm25_score(d) + (1 - alpha) * dense_score(d), where alpha is in [0, 1]. Tunable per query type. Requires score normalization first - BM25 scores are unbounded positive while dense cosine sits in [-1, 1], so min-max scaling each list to [0, 1] is standard prep.
summary: Defines the two main fusion formulas - parameter-free RRF and tunable weighted score - with k=60 and alpha in [0,1].

text: RRF Worked Example. Query: "I can't sign in". BM25 Top-3: 1. doc-25, 2. doc-7, 3. doc-13. Dense Top-3: 1. doc-12, 2. doc-25, 3. doc-7. Compute RRF(d) with k = 60. doc-25: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 1) = 0.01639, Dense term = 1 / (60 + 2) = 0.01613, RRF sum = 0.03252. doc-7: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 2) = 0.01613, Dense term = 1 / (60 + 3) = 0.01587, RRF sum = 0.03200. doc-12: BM25 term = 0 (not in BM25 top-3), Dense term = 1 / (60 + 1) = 0.01639, RRF sum = 0.01639. doc-13: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 3) = 0.01587, Dense term = 0 (not in dense top-3), RRF sum = 0.01587. Final Ranking: doc-25 (0.03252) > doc-7 (0.03200) > doc-12 (0.01639) > doc-13 (0.01587). doc-25 wins because both retrievers ranked it in their top-2. doc-7 is second because both kept it in top-3. Singletons trail behind.
summary: Walks through RRF math step by step for the query 'I can't sign in', showing doc-25 wins because it appears high in both lists.

text: Complementary Recall: Hybrid catches what either misses. Recall@5 measured on a 50-query held-out set against the support corpus. BM25 Only: 0.68. Dense Only: 0.74. Hybrid (RRF): 0.86. Hybrid is not just an average of the two retrievers - the failures of BM25 and dense are largely uncorrelated, so fusion picks up the union of their wins. Hybrid is rarely worse than the best single method, often noticeably better. Complementary recall gain here is +12 points over dense alone. Latency Cost: +30 ms for the BM25 lookup on top of the dense search. For most RAG systems that is well below the LLM generation tail, so the recall lift is almost always worth it.
summary: Empirical recall@5 numbers show hybrid (0.86) beats BM25 (0.68) and dense (0.74) due to uncorrelated failures, at +30ms latency cost.

text: Tune Alpha By Query Type. One alpha for everything leaves recall on the table. Factual lookups (account IDs, error codes) lean BM25. Conceptual questions lean dense. A small classifier picks the right alpha per query before retrieval. Factual / Lookup: example "What is my account ID?", alpha = 0.7, Lean BM25, why: exact tokens like account IDs, error codes, and SKUs dominate the answer. Conceptual / How-Does-X-Work: example "How does our pricing model work?", alpha = 0.3, Lean Dense, why: synonyms and paraphrase dominate, the answer rarely uses the same words as the query. Mixed / Ambiguous (Default): example "Why is my charge wrong?", alpha = 0.5, Balanced, why: mix of keywords (charge, refund) and intent (something is wrong), hedge with 0.5. A simple LLM-based classifier (one call with a 3-class prompt) can label each query at runtime before retrieval. Cost: 1 extra LLM call (~$0.0002 with a small model). Adds ~150 ms but unlocks 4-8 points of recall on mixed query workloads.
summary: Per-query-type alpha tuning: 0.7 for factual lookups, 0.3 for conceptual, 0.5 default; a small classifier picks alpha at runtime for 4-8 points of recall.

text: Hybrid Defaults For RAG. Hybrid is the production default for RAG. Start simple with RRF and only add complexity if your evaluation metrics demand it. (1) Always start with RRF (no tuning needed). (2) Move to weighted-alpha only if you have query-type labels. (3) Add a classifier when your queries naturally split into factual vs conceptual. (4) Re-evaluate alpha quarterly as your corpus and query mix drift. The cheapest big win in RAG retrieval. If you only do one thing after picking an embedding model, ship hybrid with RRF first - it almost always beats either retriever alone with a tiny latency cost.
summary: Decision rules: start with RRF, upgrade to weighted-alpha + classifier only when metrics demand, re-evaluate quarterly.

text: Three-stage RAG cascade funnel: vector retrieval narrows 1M chunks to top-50, a cross-encoder reranker narrows top-50 to top-10, and an LLM reads the top-3 to generate the final answer. The funnel shape shows the candidate pool shrinking ~5x at each stage. Each stage is labeled with input -> output, cost per query, and role (cheap and lossy, medium cost sharp scores, expensive final answer).
summary: Visualizes the 3-stage RAG cascade funnel from 1M chunks down to top-3 chunks consumed by the LLM.

text: Horizontal stacked-bar chart showing the latency budget for a 3-stage RAG cascade: 30 ms for vector retrieval, 80 ms for the cross-encoder reranker, and 800 ms for LLM generation, totalling 910 ms p50. The bar widths are proportional to milliseconds, making it visually obvious that the LLM generation swallows most of the budget.
summary: Stacked-bar visualization showing LLM generation dominates the 910 ms total RAG cascade latency.

text: Horizontal stacked-bar chart of the per-query cost breakdown for a 3-stage RAG cascade: $0.0001 for vector retrieval, $0.0003 for the cross-encoder reranker, and $0.0120 for LLM generation, totalling $0.0124. Bar widths are proportional to dollars per query, making it obvious that LLM generation dominates total cost.
summary: Stacked-bar visualization showing LLM generation dominates the $0.0124 per-query RAG cascade cost.

text: The reranker cascade is a 3-stage RAG pipeline: a cheap vector retriever narrows 1M chunks to top-50, a cross-encoder reranker narrows top-50 to top-10, then the LLM reads top-3 to generate the answer. Each stage shrinks the candidate pool ~5x and pays for more compute per item. Typical p50 latency is ~910 ms (30 + 80 + 800 ms) and cost is ~$0.0124 per query (LLM dominates both). The two main tuning knobs are how many candidates to retrieve and how many to rerank; 50 -> 10 -> 3 is the sweet spot, with wider configs giving diminishing recall gains. Caching the LLM stage is the biggest available latency and cost win.
summary: Summarizes the 3-stage reranker cascade, its latency and cost breakdown, and the recommended tuning sweet spot.

text: Each stage of a RAG cascade cuts the candidate set and pays for better quality. Rerankers re-score a candidate list using a cross-encoder that reads the query and the doc together. In a 3-stage RAG cascade: the vector retriever is cheap and lossy, the reranker is expensive and precise, and the LLM is most expensive and uses the smallest set. Each stage filters the work for the next one. The 3-stage cascade: Vector top-50 (cheap) -> Cross-encoder rerank top-10 (medium) -> LLM reads top-3 (expensive).
summary: Introduces the 3-stage RAG cascade where each stage shrinks candidates and trades cost for precision.

text: Stage 1 Vector Top-50 -> Stage 2 Reranker Top-10 -> Stage 3 LLM Top-3. The candidate pool shrinks by 5x at each stage. Each shrink lets us spend more compute per remaining item. Stage 1 Vector Retrieval: 1M Chunks -> Top-50, ~30 ms / $0.0001, HNSW or IVF-PQ scan, approximate but blazing fast, role cheap and lossy. Stage 2 Cross-Encoder Reranker: 50 Candidates -> Top-10, ~80 ms / $0.0003, reads query + each candidate jointly, 50 forward passes through a small transformer, role medium cost and sharp scores. Stage 3 LLM Reads Top-3 + Generates: 10 Reranked -> Answer, ~800 ms / $0.0120, top-3 chunks stuffed into the prompt; the LLM does the reasoning and writes the answer, role expensive and final.
summary: Walks through the funnel diagram showing inputs, outputs, latency, and cost for each of the three cascade stages.

text: Latency Budget: 30 + 80 + 800 = ~910ms end-to-end p50 latency on a typical RAG deployment. Vector retrieval (HNSW, top-50): 30 ms. Cross-encoder reranker (50 -> 10): 80 ms. LLM generation (200 tokens, top-3): 800 ms. Total p50 latency: 910 ms. LLM generation dominates. Optimizing reranker speed alone gives small wins. Caching the LLM call gives the biggest latency win.
summary: Breaks down the 910 ms p50 latency budget across the three cascade stages and shows the LLM dominates.

text: Cost Per Query: $0.0001 + $0.0003 + $0.012 = $0.0124. Vector retrieval (managed HNSW): $0.0001. Cross-encoder reranker (50 cands): $0.0003. LLM generation (4000 in + 200 out): $0.0120. Total: $0.0124. LLM cost dominates. Reranker is cheap, vector retrieval is essentially free. At 1M queries per day, daily cost = $12,400. Caching can cut this 50-90%.
summary: Breaks down the $0.0124 per-query cost across the three cascade stages and projects daily cost at scale.

text: The two knobs: Top-K retrieved and Top-K reranked. Too narrow and recall slips, too wide and you pay for compute that does not move the needle. Three operating points: 20 -> 5: recall 0.82, latency 850 ms, cost $0.011, narrow, saves a touch of latency and cost but recall slips. 50 -> 10 (Default): recall 0.89, latency 910 ms, cost $0.012, default sweet spot, best recall per millisecond. 100 -> 20: recall 0.92, latency 990 ms, cost $0.013, wider, buys +3 points of recall for +80 ms, diminishing returns. Sweet spot is usually 50 -> 10 -> 3. Going wider buys diminishing returns; going narrower trades recall for latency.
summary: Compares three Top-K retrieved/reranked operating points on recall, latency, and cost to identify the 50 to 10 sweet spot.

text: Three-branch interpretation diagram showing the ambiguous query "How do I export?" fanning out to three doc destinations: export formats (doc-9, cyan branch), bulk operations (doc-26, purple branch), and export failures (doc-29, pink branch). The source query box sits on the left, with curved connectors leading to three stacked destination boxes on the right, each labeled with an intent and target doc.
summary: Diagram fans an ambiguous query to three different interpretation branches and their target docs.

text: This chapter motivates query transformation in RAG. Naive retrieval embeds the user's exact words and often misses the right doc. Three failure shapes: Lexical Mismatch (user and doc use different words like sign in vs log in), Ambiguity (one query has multiple plausible intents like "How do I export?"), and Multi-Intent (one query asks two questions like "Cancel my subscription and get a refund"). Four strategies fix them: HyDE for lexical mismatch, Multi-Query Expansion for ambiguity, Query Decomposition for multi-intent, and Query Routing to pick the right index. A classifier or LLM picks which strategy to run.
summary: Chapter overview: three RAG retrieval failure modes (lexical mismatch, ambiguity, multi-intent) and four fixes (HyDE, multi-query, decomposition, routing).

text: User queries are rarely optimal for retrieval. The user types a sentence in their own words. The retriever embeds it and finds nearest chunks, but the user's wording is rarely the wording that lives in the index. The right doc exists in the corpus and the retriever still misses it. Concrete miss: User Query "I can't sign in" vs Best Doc In Corpus "Login Troubleshooting". Cosine(user, best_doc) = 0.61. Top-3 misses it. Correct doc lands at rank 7. Three failure shapes ruin naive retrieval. Four query-transformation strategies fix them.
summary: Naive retrieval fails because user wording differs from doc wording, even when the correct doc is in the corpus.

text: Lexical Mismatch: the user and the doc use different words. Users and docs share an idea but not vocabulary. The retriever sees two strings of tokens that look unrelated and the right chunk falls out of the top-k. Examples: User "I can't sign in" vs Doc "Login Troubleshooting" (sign in vs log in). User "How do I cancel?" vs Doc "Account Deletion Flow" (cancel vs delete). User "My screen is frozen" vs Doc "Browser Performance Issues" (screen frozen vs browser performance). Dense embeddings help a bit (sign-in and log-in sit close in vector space) but only partially. HyDE and multi-query close the gap by rewriting the user query into wording that matches the doc.
summary: Lexical mismatch is when the user and doc express the same idea with different words, causing the retriever to miss the right chunk.

text: Ambiguity: the query has multiple plausible intents. One short question, three different things the user might want. Example: "How do I export?" could mean Export Data To CSV (doc-9 Export Formats), Export User List For Admin (doc-26 Bulk Operations), or Cancel An Export Job That Failed (doc-29 Export Failures). A naive retriever picks one direction and quietly drops the other two. Multi-query expansion generates paraphrases that cover each branch, and query routing sends each branch to the index that fits best.
summary: Ambiguous queries have multiple plausible interpretations; naive retrieval picks one and silently drops the others.

text: Multi-Intent: one query asks two or more questions. The user packs two asks into one sentence. The retriever embeds the whole thing and lands on docs that cover one ask, missing the other entirely. Example: User Query "Cancel my subscription and get a refund" has Sub-Intent 1 "How to cancel subscription" -> doc-15 (Account Deletion Flow), and Sub-Intent 2 "How to get a refund" -> doc-4 (Refunds). Naive retrieval finds docs for one intent and misses the other. Query Decomposition splits the query into two sub-queries, retrieves for each, then combines the results.
summary: Multi-intent queries combine two asks in one sentence; naive embedding finds docs for only one, fixed by query decomposition.

text: Four Strategies Map To Three Failures. Each transformation tackles a different failure shape. HyDE: Fixes Lexical Mismatch - ask an LLM to draft a hypothetical answer, then embed the answer instead of the question. Multi-Query Expansion: Fixes Ambiguity - generate N paraphrases of the query, retrieve for each, then merge results. Query Decomposition: Fixes Multi-Intent - split a compound query into independent sub-queries, retrieve for each, then combine. Query Routing: Picks The Right Index - classify the query and send it to the index, tool, or filter that best matches. Picking the right transformation is itself a routing decision. In production, a small classifier or LLM call inspects the query and chooses which strategy (or combination) to run.
summary: Four query-transformation strategies (HyDE, multi-query, decomposition, routing) each fix a specific retrieval failure.

text: Five-box horizontal flow diagram showing the HyDE pipeline: User Query (orange) -> LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer (purple) -> Embed Hypothetical Answer (cyan) -> Vector Retrieval (yellow) -> Top-K Real Docs (green). Each stage is a colored rounded rectangle connected by orange arrows. The diagram shows the order of operations and emphasizes that the LLM call is inserted between the query and the embedding step, so the embedding sent to the vector index represents the drafted answer rather than the original question.
summary: Diagram of the five-stage HyDE pipeline from user query through LLM draft, embedding, vector retrieval, to top-K real docs.

text: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) flips standard RAG retrieval: instead of embedding the user's question directly, an LLM first drafts a plausible hypothetical answer to the question, and that answer is embedded and used to search the vector index. The intuition is that questions and answers occupy different regions of vector space, so a hypothetical answer is text-shape-closer to real docs than the raw query is. The five-stage pipeline runs User Query, LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer, Embed Hypothetical Answer, Vector Retrieval, Top-K Real Docs. The user never sees the LLM draft - only the retrieved docs. HyDE helps with long descriptive queries, lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in), conceptual 'why does X happen' questions, and queries that don't share vocabulary with docs. It hurts on short factual queries that already embed well, queries where the LLM might hallucinate the wrong answer shape, and latency-sensitive pipelines because it adds an LLM call of roughly 200-400 ms. Cost is roughly +1 LLM call per query (~$0.0005, ~250 ms) and can be amortized by caching the hypothetical answer by query hash. If recall on the golden set is already above 0.85, HyDE likely won't help.
summary: HyDE replaces query embedding with the embedding of an LLM-drafted hypothetical answer to bridge the shape gap between questions and docs.

text: Don't embed the question - embed the hypothetical answer. A user's question and the doc that answers it often live far apart in vector space. They use different words and have different shapes. HyDE flips the order: have an LLM draft a plausible answer first, then embed THAT, then retrieve. Normal RAG: Embed("Why is my dashboard slow?") -> retrieve. HyDE: LLM("Why is my dashboard slow?") = "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions, or network latency." -> Embed that ANSWER -> retrieve. The hypothetical answer is text-shape-closer to actual docs than the question is. Cosine similarity wins.
summary: Introduces HyDE's core insight: questions and answers sit far apart in vector space, so embedding a drafted hypothetical answer scores higher cosine similarity against real docs than embedding the raw question.

text: The HyDE flow has five stages in order: User Query -> LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer -> Embed Hypothetical Answer -> Vector Retrieval -> Top-K Real Docs. HyDE adds one extra step before retrieval: an LLM call that writes a hypothetical answer. That answer is what gets embedded and sent to the vector index. The user never sees it directly. The hypothetical answer is throwaway. Only its embedding goes to the index. The user gets the real top-K docs back, never the LLM's draft.
summary: Walks the five-stage HyDE pipeline (query, LLM draft, embed, retrieve, top-K docs) and clarifies that the hypothetical answer is throwaway - only its embedding hits the index.

text: Worked example on 'Why is my dashboard slow?'. Three panels: what the user typed, what the LLM drafted, what the retriever returned. User Query: "Why is my dashboard slow?" LLM Hypothetical Answer: "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions interfering with rendering, network latency, or background sync jobs consuming resources. Try refreshing, disabling extensions, or checking the network panel." Top-3 Retrieved Docs: doc-22 Slow Page Load Troubleshooting, doc-23 500 Errors, doc-27 Browser Compatibility Issues. The LLM's draft pulled in vocabulary like "page load", "browser extensions", and "network latency" - exactly the words that appear in docs that actually help. Without HyDE, retrieval returned doc-7 (Dashboard Tour) - a generic doc that doesn't help. The question "why slow" embedded poorly; the answer embedded well.
summary: Concrete walkthrough showing how HyDE on 'Why is my dashboard slow?' produces a draft with doc-vocabulary that pulls relevant troubleshooting docs instead of the generic Dashboard Tour that vanilla retrieval returned.

text: Why HyDE works - three reasons the hypothetical-answer trick beats embedding the raw question. Even if the LLM's draft is factually wrong, the SHAPE of the answer matches the SHAPE of real docs - and that is what cosine similarity scores. Reason 1: Different shapes in vector space - questions look different from answers in vector space. Reason 2: Borrows doc vocabulary - hypothetical answers borrow doc vocabulary even if the LLM doesn't know the actual answer. Reason 3: Robust to lexical mismatch - because the LLM does the rewriting for you.
summary: Three mechanisms behind HyDE: answers share doc shape in vector space, drafts borrow doc vocabulary even when factually wrong, and the LLM rewriting absorbs lexical mismatch.

text: When HyDE helps vs when it hurts. HyDE is not free and is not universal. It earns its keep when the user's words are far from the doc's words. It hurts when the query is already short, factual, and embeds well. Helps: long descriptive queries; lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in); conceptual / 'why does X happen' questions; queries that don't share vocabulary with docs. Hurts: short factual queries with good embeddings (e.g., 'API key'); queries where the LLM might hallucinate the wrong answer shape; latency-sensitive pipelines (HyDE adds an LLM call: +200-400 ms). If recall on your golden set is already above 0.85, HyDE likely won't help and will add latency.
summary: Decision rules for when to apply HyDE - good for long, conceptual, vocabulary-mismatched queries; bad for short factual queries, latency-sensitive paths, or systems already at >0.85 recall.

text:
The HyDE prompt template - exact prompt fired at the LLM. The placeholder in pink gets filled with the user's question. The LLM's response is the hypothetical answer that gets embedded.

Prompt Template:
You are a helpful assistant. Write a single concise paragraph that
answers the user's question as if you knew the answer. The paragraph
will be used to search a documentation index, so include domain terms
and likely doc vocabulary. Do not say "I don't know" - guess plausibly.

Question: {query}

Hypothetical Answer:

Cost: +1 LLM call per user query (~$0.0005 + ~250ms). Cache the hypothetical answer by query hash to amortize.
summary: Provides the literal HyDE prompt template (instructing the LLM to write a plausible doc-shaped paragraph) plus per-query cost and the caching trick to amortize it.

text: Fan-out diagram showing a single user query expanding into 3 LLM-generated variants, each retrieving its own top-K, then converging into a single fused ranking via RRF. Layout: a red 'User Query' node on the left, three orange variant nodes in the middle column, three yellow 'Top-K Docs' nodes, all converging into a green 'Fused Ranking (Via RRF)' node on the right.
summary: Diagram fans out one user query into three variants, each retrieving top-K docs, then converges into one fused RRF ranking.

text: Three-box horizontal pipeline diagram for multi-query expansion: an LLM generates 3-5 query variants (orange), vector retrieval runs for each variant (yellow), and the rankings are fused with RRF into one final list (green). Arrows point left to right between the three stages.
summary: Three-stage horizontal pipeline diagram: LLM variant generation, per-variant vector retrieval, and RRF fusion.

text: Multi-query expansion (a.k.a. RAG-Fusion) handles brittle query wording by asking an LLM to rewrite the user query into 3-5 variants, retrieving top-K for each in parallel, and fusing the rankings with RRF (sum of 1 / (k + rank), k = 60). Best for ambiguous or multi-intent queries; the worked example splits 'cancel my subscription and get a refund' so doc-15 (cancellation) and doc-4 (refund) both surface. Step-back prompting is a variant style that adds a broader version of the query. Skip it for short factual lookups or latency-critical paths; pairs well with HyDE.
summary: Chapter overview: rewrite the query into LLM variants, retrieve per variant in parallel, fuse with RRF; use for ambiguous multi-intent queries, skip for short factual lookups.

text: One query becomes many; fuse the results. If the user's exact wording missed the right doc, one of the variant wordings might catch it. Ask an LLM to rewrite the query into 3-5 variants, retrieve top-K for each, then fuse the rankings into one. RAG-Fusion is the same idea under a different name; this chapter absorbs it.
summary: Core insight of multi-query expansion: rewrite the user query into multiple variants, retrieve for each, then fuse rankings.

text: Three steps: generate variants, retrieve each, fuse. Stage 1: LLM generates 3-5 query variants. Stage 2: run vector retrieval for each variant. Stage 3: fuse rankings with RRF. The LLM rewrites; the vector index runs N independent retrievals; RRF combines the N rankings into one final list. Stage 2 runs in parallel - N retrievals fire at once, not in sequence. Latency is dominated by stage 1 (the LLM call), not stage 2.
summary: The multi-query expansion pipeline has three stages: LLM variant generation, parallel vector retrieval per variant, and RRF fusion.

text: Worked example: 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund'. One query, two intents. A single vector lookup is forced to compromise. With multi-query expansion the LLM splits the wording: Variant 1 'How to cancel my subscription', Variant 2 'How to get a refund after cancellation', Variant 3 'Stop billing immediately'. Top-3 retrieved docs per variant: V1 -> doc-15, doc-7, doc-3; V2 -> doc-4, doc-15, doc-3; V3 -> doc-5, doc-15, doc-21. Final fused top-3 after RRF: Rank 1 doc-15 (covers cancellation), Rank 2 doc-4 (covers refund), Rank 3 doc-3 (subscription tiers). Each variant brings a different doc to the top; fusion captures both intents - doc-15 for cancellation, doc-4 for refund.
summary: Concrete walkthrough showing how a two-intent query is split into three LLM variants, each retrieves its top-3, and RRF fuses them to cover both intents.

text:
Fuse with RRF (same formula as chapter on hybrid search fusion). Reciprocal Rank Fusion takes N rankings and produces one. Each doc's score is the sum of 1 / (k + its rank) across every ranking it appears in. The constant k = 60 dampens the influence of any single list so agreement across variants matters more than a top-1 appearance.

RRF(d) = sum over variants v of: 1 / (k + rank_v(d))
where k = 60 (standard)

Same fusion math as hybrid search. Different inputs - here the rankings come from N variants of the same query rather than from BM25 + dense.
summary: The RRF formula sums 1/(k+rank) across variant rankings with k=60, rewarding agreement across variants over single top-1 hits.

text: Step-back prompting: add a more general variant. Step-back is a specific variant style - ask a broader, less specific version of the question. The intuition: a doc might not match the user's exact narrow wording, but it almost certainly matches a more general phrasing of the same problem. Example: Original (very specific) 'Why is the user role page failing on Chrome v124 with error E_PERM?' vs. Step-back (more general) 'Why does the user role page fail?'. The original is too specific - the doc might not mention Chrome v124. The step-back catches more general docs. Reasoning models reduce the need for explicit step-back; include it when your queries are unusually specific. This is one of many possible variant prompts; the main approach covers the common case and step-back is a niche addition.
summary: Step-back prompting generates a broader, less specific variant so retrieval can match docs that lack the user's narrow detail wording.

text: When multi-query helps. Multi-query expansion is not a default - it earns its keep on hard, ambiguous, or multi-intent queries. On short factual lookups it just burns budget. Helps: ambiguous queries with multiple plausible intents; complex multi-clause queries; recall is the priority over latency; reranker can absorb the wider candidate set. Skip: short factual lookups ('What is my account ID?'); latency-critical paths (adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals); when HyDE already solves the same failure. Adds ~300 ms (1 LLM call) + N parallel retrievals (cheap). Often pairs well with HyDE for hard cases.
summary: Multi-query expansion fits ambiguous, multi-clause, recall-priority queries; skip it for short factual or latency-critical lookups, especially if HyDE already covers the case.

text: Diagram: Router decision tree with four leaves. The root node is labeled 'Classify Query Type' (cyan). Four branch lines drop to four leaf boxes laid out left-to-right: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K = 5 (cyan); Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K = 5 (purple); Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K = 8 (yellow); Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly (red). The tree visually conveys that classification routes each query to one of three indexes or skips retrieval entirely.
summary: Diagram of a four-leaf router decision tree mapping query types to indexes (or skip-retrieval) with Top-K values.

text: Query Routing and Decomposition are two query-transformation strategies in RAG. Routing decides WHERE a query goes (which index or tool, or skip retrieval entirely for chitchat). Decomposition decides HOW MANY sub-queries are needed (split complex queries into 2-3 parts, retrieve each, then assemble). Routing implementations: semantic router (embedding-based, ~$0.0001/query, ~10ms, 85-90% accuracy) vs LLM classifier (~$0.0002/query, ~150ms, 92-97%). Decomposition fits comparison, multi-hop, and disjunction query shapes. The 2x2 decision: Route only (default for production), Decompose only (multi-hop heavy), Both (compose), Neither (simple lookups). Combined overhead ~300-450 ms / $0.0006-0.0007 per query.
summary: Chapter overview of query routing (where) and decomposition (how many) as composable RAG query-transformation strategies.

text: Routing decides WHERE; Decomposition decides HOW MANY. Two related query-transformation strategies share this chapter. Routing answers a where question - which index or tool should the query hit. Pick one target. Skip retrieval entirely for off-topic chitchat. Decomposition answers a how-many question - does the query need to be split into smaller sub-queries first. Split complex queries into 2-3 sub-queries; retrieve and assemble. Both rely on an LLM (or a cheaper classifier) to inspect the query and decide. They compose - a router can send a query to a decomposition step, or a decomposed sub-query can itself be routed to a different index.
summary: Introduces routing as a where-question and decomposition as a how-many-question, both LLM-driven and composable.

text: Routing sends each query to the right target. On the running support corpus, queries split cleanly into four buckets. A router classifies each query into one of them, then sends it to the matching index - or skips retrieval entirely for chitchat. The four leaves: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K = 5. Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K = 5. Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K = 8. Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly. Routing avoids retrieving from indexes that can't help. Skipping retrieval on chitchat saves latency and cost - and prevents the LLM from hallucinating about a doc it doesn't need to read.
summary: Shows a four-leaf router decision tree mapping each query type to a target index and Top-K, including a skip-retrieval branch for chitchat.

text: Two router implementations: Embedding-Based vs LLM Classifier. Semantic Router (Embedding-Based): embed the query, compare to embeddings of route prototypes ('Billing question', 'Troubleshooting question'). Pick highest cosine. Cost ~$0.0001 / query, Latency ~10 ms, Accuracy 85-90%. LLM Classifier: pass the query + route labels to a small LLM ('Classify this query: billing / feature / troubleshoot / chitchat'). Pick the label. Cost ~$0.0002 / query, Latency ~150 ms, Accuracy 92-97%. Embedding-based is ~15x faster and slightly cheaper; LLM classifier handles ambiguous wording better. Start with semantic; switch to LLM if routing accuracy stalls below your bar.
summary: Compares semantic (embedding) router vs LLM classifier across cost, latency, and accuracy with concrete numbers.

text: Decomposition splits 'Compare X and Y' into two sub-queries. User query: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' LLM splits into sub-queries. Sub-Query 1: 'What's in the Pro plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers) and doc-10 (Team Seat Management). Sub-Query 2: 'What's in the Enterprise plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers), doc-10 (Team Seat Management), and doc-21 (Quota Exceeded - Enterprise-Only Feature). LLM assembly step: union of retrieved docs (doc-3, doc-10, doc-21) is passed to a single LLM call that writes the side-by-side comparison. Without decomposition, a single vector lookup gets pulled toward whichever subject has stronger phrasing and misses the other. Decomposition guarantees both sides of the comparison are retrieved before the LLM has to answer.
summary: Worked example: comparing Pro vs Enterprise plans is split into two sub-queries, retrieved separately, then unioned for one LLM assembly call.

text: When to decompose: Multi-Hop, Comparison, Disjunction. Three query shapes consistently benefit from being split before retrieval. Comparison example: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' Split: Sub-1: What's in the Pro plan? + Sub-2: What's in the Enterprise plan? Multi-Hop example: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' Split: Sub-1: How to recover email? + Sub-2: How to reset password? Disjunction (Mixed Types) example: 'Why is the dashboard slow and showing 500 errors?' Split: Sub-1: Dashboard slow troubleshooting + Sub-2: 500 error troubleshooting. If the query has 'and' / 'compare' / 'or' between two distinct subjects, decomposition usually helps. Single-subject queries don't benefit and just pay the extra LLM call for nothing.
summary: Lists the three query shapes (comparison, multi-hop, disjunction) that benefit from decomposition with concrete examples and a heuristic.

text: The decision: Route, Decompose, Both, or Neither. Four combinations cover every production case. Route Only: most production RAG systems start here. Cheap, easy, high-impact. Decompose Only: use when queries are consistently complex (multi-hop, comparisons). Adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals. Both: route first to pick the right index, then decompose if the query is complex. They compose - they don't interfere. Neither: default for simple lookup queries. Don't add complexity you don't need. Cost summary: Routing adds +10-150 ms / +$0.0001-0.0002 per query. Decomposition adds +200-300 ms / +$0.0005 per query. Combined: ~+300-450 ms total query-transformation overhead.
summary: 2x2 decision grid for route/decompose/both/neither with cost-and-latency overhead numbers for each combination.

text: Stacked horizontal bar visualizing how an 8000-token LLM context budget splits across system prompt (200, cyan), retrieved chunks (5000, green), user question (50, purple), reserved completion (1500, orange), and headroom (1250, yellow). Segment widths are proportional to token counts on a centered bar inside an 800x130 viewBox. Legend and numeric breakdown table show each slot's token count and percent share of the 8000-token total.
summary: Stacked horizontal bar diagram showing the proportional split of an 8000-token context window across five prompt slots.

text: Context packing turns retrieved chunks into a final prompt that fits a token budget. The 8000-token window splits into system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom. Chunks can be ordered relevance-first (best at position 1, default for single answers), chronologically (for time-sensitive docs), or deduplicated with MMR (lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked))) to drop near-duplicates. The packed prompt combines a system instruction, citation-marked chunks, the question, and an allowed refusal phrase, fitting within budget.
summary: This chapter covers token budgeting, chunk ordering strategies (relevance-first, chronological, MMR deduplication), and assembling the final packed prompt.

text: Every prompt has a token budget. An 8000-token context window splits across four jobs: the system prompt that frames the model (200 tokens, 2.5%), the retrieved context with the answer (5000 tokens, 62.5%), the user question (50 tokens, 0.6%), and the reserved completion tokens the model uses to write its answer (1500 tokens, 18.8%). Headroom (1250 tokens, 15.6%) is the slack you keep so a long answer never overflows. Slots: System Prompt 200, Retrieved Context 5000, User Question 50, Reserved Completion 1500, Headroom 1250. Total = 8000.
summary: An 8000-token context window splits across system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom.

text: Pack Top-3 chunks for the password reset query. The running query 'How do I reset my password?' returned three chunks from doc-1 with cosine scores. The scores tell us how to order; the chunks tell us what to pack. [doc-1, chunk 1] Score 0.91: 'To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.' [doc-1, chunk 2] Score 0.84: 'An email with a reset link will be sent to your registered address.' [doc-1, chunk 3] Score 0.77: 'Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.'
summary: Three retrieved chunks with cosine scores form the candidate set for packing the password reset query.

text: Ordering Option 1: Relevance-First. Sort by retrieval score, highest first. The most-relevant chunk lands at position 1 where the model attends best. Default for single-best-answer queries. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] (Score 0.91), 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] (Score 0.84), 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] (Score 0.77). Most relevant first. Cheap to do - just sort by score. Risk: tail-position chunks lose attention weight.
summary: Relevance-first ordering sorts packed chunks by descending retrieval score so the best chunk lands where the model attends most.

text: Ordering Option 2: Chronological. Sort by publication date or document position. Useful for time-sensitive docs - changelogs, policy histories, incident timelines - where the natural reading order helps the model follow cause-effect. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] - Document Position 1, 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] - Document Position 2, 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] - Document Position 3. Time-sensitive ordering preserves the natural sequence so the model reads cause then effect.
summary: Chronological ordering packs chunks by date or document position so the model follows the natural cause-effect sequence.

text: Ordering Option 3: Deduplicate with MMR. Maximal Marginal Relevance keeps high-score chunks while dropping near-duplicates. Same budget, more unique information. Before MMR (5 chunks, redundant): chunk 1, chunk 2, chunk 3, chunk 4 (duplicate - wastes budget, struck through), chunk 5. After MMR (4 chunks, diverse): [doc-1, chunk 1], [doc-1, chunk 2], [doc-1, chunk 3], [doc-9, chunk 1] 'MFA must be re-verified after a reset (different but related).' The MMR formula: MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked)). Lambda = 0.7 weights relevance over diversity. Lambda = 0.3 weights diversity over relevance. Tune per workload.
summary: MMR drops near-duplicate chunks using a lambda-weighted formula that balances query relevance against similarity to already-picked chunks.

text:
The final packed prompt: system instruction, retrieved chunks with citation markers, user question, and an allowed refusal phrase. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer.
If the docs don't contain the answer, say "I don't have enough information".

Documentation:
[doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.
[doc-1, chunk 2] An email with a reset link will be sent to your address.
[doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.

Question: How do I reset my password?
Answer:

Within budget: ~4,800 / 8,000 tokens (60%).
summary: The final packed prompt combines system instruction, citation-marked chunks, the user question, and a refusal phrase, fitting within ~60% of the 8000-token budget.

text: U-shaped accuracy curve plotting answer correctness against the position of the relevant chunk within a 20-chunk context. The Y axis is accuracy in percent (0 to 100), the X axis is position of relevant chunk (1 to 20). The curve starts at about 85% at position 1, drops smoothly to a middle dip around 55% spanning positions 8-10, and climbs back to roughly 82% at position 20. A dashed annotation labels the bottom region "Middle Dip ~55%". Source: Liu et al. 2023 lost-in-the-middle finding.
summary: Diagram of the U-shaped accuracy-by-position curve from Liu et al. 2023 with high accuracy at ends and a middle dip near 55%.

text: Front-load strategy strip showing 10 chunk slots in a horizontal row labeled "Sort By Score Descending". Positions 1, 2, 3 are highlighted in bright green and labeled Top-1 (score 0.91), Top-2 (0.88), Top-3 (0.84). Positions 4 through 10 are faded green holding the lowest-scored chunks (0.78, 0.71, 0.66, 0.59, 0.52, 0.45, 0.38) trailing down toward the tail.
summary: Diagram of the front-load strategy with top-scored chunks placed at positions 1-3 and lower scores trailing to position 10.

text: Sandwich strategy strip showing 10 chunk slots in a horizontal row labeled "Best Chunks Bookend Start And End". Positions 1 and 2 are highlighted in cyan as Top-1 (0.91) and Top-2 (0.88). Positions 9 and 10 are also cyan-highlighted as Top-3 (0.84) and Top-4 (0.78), bookending the end. Positions 3 through 8 are faded cyan holding lower-scored chunks (0.66, 0.59, 0.52, 0.45, 0.38, 0.31) filling the middle dip region.
summary: Diagram of the sandwich strategy with top-2 chunks at positions 1-2, next-best 2 at positions 9-10, and lowest scores filling the middle.

text: LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window and ignore the middle - the lost-in-the-middle effect (Liu et al. 2023), shown as a U-shaped accuracy curve over chunk position. The same retrieved chunk can produce a right or wrong answer depending solely on its slot. Two cheap fixes reorder packed chunks: front-load (sort by score descending so the best chunk goes to position 1) and sandwich (top-2 at front, next-best at back, weakest in middle). Both fail for truly long multi-fact queries, tail-buried critical facts, or models with flat curves; those need hierarchical summarization, multi-hop retrieval, or harder reranking. Always benchmark on your own model.
summary: This chapter explains the lost-in-the-middle effect and two reordering strategies (front-load and sandwich) that exploit the U-shaped attention curve.

text: Liu et al. (2023) showed that LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window, and the middle gets ignored. Plotting 20 packed chunks, answer accuracy is highest when the relevant chunk sits at position 1 or position 20, and lowest in the middle. The curve is U-shaped: accuracy starts near 85% at position 1, dips to about 55% around positions 8-10 (the middle dip), then climbs back toward 82% at position 20.
summary: LLMs read long contexts in a U-shaped way - accuracy peaks at the ends and dips in the middle.

text: Query: "Does the Pro plan include SSO?" The answer-bearing chunk is "SSO is Enterprise-only" (doc-15, chunk 3). With 10 packed chunks, the position of that chunk decides whether the model finds the fact. Row A - Answer at position 5 (middle): 10 packed chunks; the SSO chunk lands at position 5. Model: "I don't see SSO mentioned for the Pro plan." Wrong - the model ignored the middle chunk. Row B - Answer at position 1 (front): same 10 chunks reordered; the SSO chunk lands at position 1. Model: "No, SSO is Enterprise-only." Correct - same chunk, better position.
summary: Same chunk content gives wrong vs right answers based purely on where it sits in the context.

text: Strategy 1: Front-load the most relevant chunk. Relevance-first ordering means sort chunks by retrieval score descending. The top-scored chunks land in positions 1, 2, 3 - the high-attention region. Tail positions hold the lowest-scored chunks. Example slot scores: Pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), Pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), Pos 3 = 0.84 (Top-3), tailing down to Pos 10 = 0.38. Cheap. Helps single-best-answer queries. Risk: tail context still ignored, but the top-scored chunks land in the attention sweet spot.
summary: Front-load strategy sorts chunks by score descending so best chunks hit the high-attention front positions.

text: Strategy 2: Sandwich the best chunks at start and end. Put the top-2 chunks at the front and the next-best two at the back. The middle dip on the U-curve becomes the least-relevant region. Same total tokens; reordering is negligible compute. Slot layout: Pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), Pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), Pos 9 = 0.84 (Top-3), Pos 10 = 0.78 (Top-4), with lower-scored chunks (0.66 down to 0.31) filling positions 3-8. More robust for multi-fact queries. Same total tokens. Costs reordering compute (negligible).
summary: Sandwich strategy bookends best chunks at start and end so the U-curve's middle dip holds the least-relevant content.

text: When reordering isn't enough: front-load and sandwich help most workloads but not all. Three failure modes need different fixes than position tricks. (1) Truly long multi-fact queries: 30 chunks all needed; even sandwich leaves middle facts ignored. Fix: hierarchical summarization or multi-hop retrieval. (2) Tail-buried critical detail: the killer fact lives at chunk 18; both strategies miss it. Fix: rerank harder, fetch fewer. (3) Model-specific curve shape: some models have flatter curves than others; benchmark on your own model. Lost-in-middle is a model fingerprint, not a universal law. Always benchmark on your own model and workload.
summary: Reordering tricks fail for long multi-fact queries, tail-buried facts, and model-specific curve shapes - each needs a different fix.

text: Citations and refusal make RAG answers auditable and safe. Inline [doc-N] markers (or structured JSON citations) let every claim trace back to a retrieved chunk, while an explicit refusal instruction gives the model an authorized exit when context is missing so it stops hallucinating. After generation, a regex parser maps markers back to chunks for footnotes/tooltips, and a 3-step audit converts citations into a faithfulness score from 0 to 1. A production-ready template combines citation rules, quoted-span requirement, and the exact refusal sentence as numbered RULES so the model treats them as non-optional.
summary: Overview of citations, refusal, faithfulness audit, and a combined production prompt for grounded RAG generation.

text: Without citations, the user cannot verify the answer and a reviewer cannot audit it. Hallucinations slip through silently. With inline [doc-N] markers, every claim is traceable. The same answer with citations becomes an artifact you can review, replay, and trust. Example - No Citations: Query 'Why is my account locked?' Answer: 'Your account may be locked due to too many failed logins or a billing issue.' User cannot verify. Reviewer cannot audit. Hallucination has nowhere to land. With Citations: Same query. Answer: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' Every claim traces to a specific chunk. Audit-friendly. Hallucinations exposed.
summary: Citations turn an opaque answer into an auditable artifact - every claim traces to a specific chunk via [doc-N].

text: The citation instruction lives in the system prompt. Use a literal [doc-N] marker the model can emit inline. Specify that N must come from the Documentation block, not be invented. Prompt template: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer the user's question. For every claim you make, cite the source as [doc-N], where N is the doc number from the Documentation section. Do NOT cite a doc you did not use. Documentation: {context} Question: {query} Answer:'
summary: Sample system prompt that instructs the model to emit inline [doc-N] citations sourced from the documentation block.

text: JSON output is machine-parseable and downstream audit pipelines can ingest it without regex. The schema pairs the answer with its citations and a confidence score. Example structured output: { "answer": "Your account locks after 5 failed logins.", "citations": [ { "doc_id": "doc-12", "chunk_id": 3, "quote": "5 failed login attempts" } ], "confidence": 0.92 }. Structured output makes citations machine-parseable. Better for downstream audit pipelines.
summary: Alternative to inline markers: ask the model for a structured JSON object containing answer, citations array, and confidence.

text: Without an explicit refusal instruction, models tend to invent. With one, the model has an authorized exit for 'I don't know'. The exact wording matters - models follow the most precise instructions in the prompt. Refusal prompt: 'If the documentation does not contain enough information to answer, respond with EXACTLY this sentence and nothing else: "I don't have enough information to answer that." Do not invent facts. Do not guess. Do not cite a doc you didn't use.' Without refusal instruction: Query 'What's the refund window for monthly plans?' (no matching doc). Answer: 'Monthly plans have a 7-day refund window.' Hallucinated - the model guessed because it had no exit. With refusal instruction: Same query, same missing doc. Answer: 'I don't have enough information to answer that.' Safe - the user knows the system was honest about its limit.
summary: An explicit refusal instruction gives the model a sanctioned 'I don't know' exit so it stops fabricating answers when context is missing.

text: Faithfulness is the share of claims in an answer that are supported by the retrieved chunks. A 3-step audit converts citations into a measurable score. Step 1: Parse Answer Into Atomic Claims - Example: '5 failed logins triggers a lock' / 'Billing issues hold for 24 hours'. Step 2: Locate The Cited Chunk For Each Claim - Follow the [doc-N] marker back to the retrieved chunk text. Step 3: Score: claim_supported = LLMJudge(claim, chunk) - Aggregate per-claim scores into a faithfulness number from 0 to 1. Output is a single number from 0 (none supported) to 1 (every claim has evidence in the retrieved chunks). RAGAS faithfulness is covered in depth in later chapters.
summary: Three-step faithfulness audit: split answer into atomic claims, follow each [doc-N] to its chunk, and use an LLM judge to score support, then aggregate to a 0-1 score.

text: After generation, parse [doc-N] markers out of the answer with a regex. Map each marker back to the retrieved chunk and render as footnotes or hover tooltips in the UI. Example model output: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' Regex: \[doc-\d+\]. Resolved citation table: marker [doc-12] -> doc-12 (Account Lockouts), chunk 3, quoted span '5 failed login attempts'. Marker [doc-7] -> doc-7 (Billing Holds), chunk 1, quoted span '24-hour hold after a failed charge'. Render as footnotes or inline tooltips in your UI so the user can click a citation and see exactly what the model used to write that sentence.
summary: Post-generation step: regex-extract [doc-N] markers, resolve to source chunk + quoted span, and render as footnotes or tooltips.

text: One combined template does both citations and refusal. Numbered RULES make the contract explicit so the model treats citations and refusal as non-optional. Production prompt: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use ONLY the documentation below to answer the user's question. RULES: 1. Cite every claim inline as [doc-N], where N is the doc number. 2. Quote 3-7 words from the cited chunk after each [doc-N]. 3. If the docs do not answer the question, respond with EXACTLY: "I don't have enough information to answer that." 4. Do not invent facts. Do not paraphrase what isn't in the docs. Documentation: {context} Question: {query} Answer:' Numbered rules survive long context better than prose instructions. The model attends to short structured constraints more reliably than to a paragraph.
summary: Production-grade combined template uses numbered RULES to enforce inline citations, short quoted spans, an exact refusal sentence, and no invention.

text: SVG diagram: Multi-hop retrieval control-loop flowchart showing User Query box at top flowing into Retrieve Top-K For Current Query box, then into a yellow diamond labeled 'Does Context Answer The Question?'. YES (green arrow) exits right to Generate Answer box. NO with Hops < max_hops (orange arrow) exits left to Reformulate Query box, then loops back up to Retrieve. NO with Hops >= max_hops (red arrow) exits down to Refuse box labeled 'I Don't Have Enough Information'. Colors: purple for query, cyan for retrieve, yellow for decision diamond, green for generate, orange for reformulate, red for refuse.
summary: Visual SVG flowchart of the multi-hop loop: retrieve, sufficiency diamond, reformulate loop-back, refuse on cap.

text: This chapter covers multi-hop retrieval in RAG: queries that need a chain of facts across multiple documents. Single-hop retrieval fails when one chunk only contains half the answer. The multi-hop control loop retrieves, checks sufficiency, then either generates the answer or reformulates the query and loops again, capped by max_hops (typically 3-5). A sufficiency-check prompt asks the model to respond SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT with a follow-up query. Multi-hop is worth it for compound questions, cross-doc reasoning, and step-dependent flows, but each hop multiplies latency and cost (~3x for 3 hops). Failure modes: infinite loops, divergence from original intent, and overconfident sufficiency judges. Mitigations: hard cap max_hops, anchor original query, separate calibrated judge model.
summary: Overview of multi-hop retrieval: why, how the control loop works, sufficiency prompts, costs, and failure modes.

text: Some queries need more than one retrieval. Single-hop retrieval works when the answer fits in one chunk. Multi-hop retrieval kicks in when the question depends on a chain of facts that live in different docs. The signal you need it: even a perfect retrieval returns only half of what is needed. Single-Hop Works example: Query 'How do I reset my password?' One retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset). Answer: 'Go to Settings then Security, click Reset Password.' Multi-Hop Needed example: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' One retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset) but the question also needs doc-3 (email change / recovery). One retrieval misses half. Multiple retrievals needed.
summary: Single-hop suffices for self-contained queries; multi-hop is required when answers depend on facts spread across multiple documents.

text: Two hops on the running query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?'. Hop 1: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?', Retrieved: doc-1 (password reset). Reasoning: Reset requires email access. The user says they forgot email - need email recovery info before answering. Decision: INSUFFICIENT -> Reformulate as: 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'. Hop 2: Query 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?', Retrieved: doc-3 (email change / recovery). Reasoning: Now both pieces are in hand: the reset flow (doc-1) and the email-recovery path (doc-3). Decision: SUFFICIENT. Final answer combines doc-1 + doc-3 with citations. Each hop reformulates the query to fetch the missing piece.
summary: Walkthrough of a 2-hop trace showing how Hop 1 finds doc-1, reformulates, and Hop 2 fetches doc-3 to complete the answer.

text: The multi-hop control loop. Loop structure: retrieve, check sufficiency, generate or reformulate. A hard cap on max_hops prevents infinite loops. If the cap is hit without sufficiency, the system refuses. Flow: User Query -> Retrieve Top-K For Current Query -> diamond 'Does Context Answer The Question?'. YES branch -> Generate Answer. NO branch with Hops < max_hops -> Reformulate Query -> loop back to Retrieve Top-K. NO branch with Hops >= max_hops -> Refuse: 'I Don't Have Enough Information'. max_hops typically 3-5. Each hop adds 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call latency. Tune based on workload and budget.
summary: Control-loop diagram for multi-hop retrieval with sufficiency check, reformulation loop-back, and refusal on max_hops.

text:
Asking the model: do you have enough? The sufficiency check is a small structured task. The model decides whether the retrieved text answers the question. SUFFICIENT means 'I can answer now'; INSUFFICIENT means 'fetch this follow-up'. Prompt Template - Sufficiency Check:

You are an assistant deciding whether you have enough information.

Question: {query}
Retrieved so far:
{context}

Respond with EXACTLY one of:
- SUFFICIENT (you can answer the question from the retrieved text)
- INSUFFICIENT: <one short follow-up query to retrieve more>

Do not answer the question yet.

Example Trace: Hop 1: 'INSUFFICIENT: How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'. Hop 2: 'SUFFICIENT'.
summary: Exact prompt template that asks the LLM to output SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT plus a follow-up query, with an example trace.

text: When to reach for multi-hop. Multi-hop is not free. Each hop multiplies latency and cost. Use it only when the query genuinely needs chained facts, and always with a hard cap on hops. Worth It: compound questions (password reset if you forgot your email), cross-doc reasoning (compare Pro vs Enterprise plans), step-dependent flows (cancel subscription then refund). Not Worth It: single-doc lookups, simple FAQ-style queries, cost outweighs benefit when one hop already answers. Cost Multiplier: each hop = 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call; 3 hops = ~3x latency, ~3x cost vs single-hop; budget per query becomes a hard constraint. Mitigations: cap max_hops at 3, cache reformulated queries, use a smaller model for the sufficiency check.
summary: When multi-hop is worth it versus not, the cost multiplier per hop, and mitigations like caps, caching, and smaller judge models.

text: Failure modes: infinite loops and divergence. Three known failure modes show up in production multi-hop systems. Each has a known mitigation that costs either an extra check or stricter caps. Infinite Loop: model never says SUFFICIENT. Each hop reformulates without making progress. Mitigation: hard cap on max_hops + log every reformulation. Divergence: each reformulation drifts farther from the original intent. 'How do I reset my password?' becomes 'How do I configure SSO?' by hop 3. Mitigation: keep the ORIGINAL query in the reformulation prompt as an anchor. Stuck Sufficiency Judge: model always says SUFFICIENT after hop 1 even when context is incomplete (overconfident). Mitigation: separate judge model, calibrate via a golden dataset.
summary: Three multi-hop failure modes (infinite loop, divergence, stuck overconfident judge) and their mitigations.

text: Self-RAG token-emission timeline diagram showing the model alternating between special control tokens like <retrieve>, <isrel>, and <issup> across the generation sequence. Tokens are interleaved with regular answer text on a single left-to-right timeline. Each marker is a runtime decision point: gate retrieval, judge relevance of returned docs, generate answer, then judge whether the answer is supported.
summary: Diagram of the Self-RAG token timeline: control tokens interleaved with answer text along a left-to-right generation axis.

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve decision diamond that gates whether the Self-RAG model fetches external context for a given query. The diamond branches a User Query into YES (need external knowledge -> <retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>) or NO (<no-retrieve> -> Answer). The gate is trained via instruction tuning plus reinforcement learning and reduces unnecessary retrievals by about 40%.
summary: Diagram of the Self-RAG decision diamond branching a query into retrieve-with-critique or skip-retrieval-and-answer paths.

text: Self-RAG (Asai et al. 2023) teaches a model to decide per-query whether to retrieve by extending the vocabulary with four control tokens: <retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel>, and <issup>. During generation the model emits these tokens inline, gating retrieval up-front, judging relevance of each retrieved doc, and scoring whether the final answer is supported. Trained via instruction tuning plus RL, the gate cuts unnecessary retrievals ~40%. The runtime drops irrelevant docs, down-weights partial ones, and labels the answer FULLY-SUPPORTED, PARTIALLY-SUPPORTED, or NO-SUPPORT. Self-RAG wins on mixed-knowledge, latency-sensitive workloads but requires a fine-tuned model and well-calibrated special-token classifier.
summary: Self-RAG is a fine-tuned model contract using four control tokens to gate retrieval, critique relevance, and grade answer support, cutting unnecessary retrievals ~40%.

text: Should the model retrieve, or just answer? Always-retrieve wastes tokens and latency. Never-retrieve risks hallucination. Self-RAG (Asai et al. 2023) teaches the model to decide per-query whether retrieval is needed. Examples: 'What is 2 + 2?' -> <no-retrieve> (factoid the model already knows from pre-training). 'How do I reset my password?' -> <retrieve> (custom-corpus knowledge the model cannot answer without retrieval). 'Summarize what we just discussed.' -> <no-retrieve> (context already in the conversation; no external lookup needed).
summary: Self-RAG lets the model decide per-query whether to retrieve, avoiding wasted retrievals on factoids and conversational queries while still fetching for custom-corpus questions.

text: Self-RAG extends the model vocabulary with four control tokens. The model emits them inline as part of generation. The runtime intercepts each one and acts. The four tokens: <retrieve> = retrieve a document now. <no-retrieve> = do not retrieve, answer from the model's own knowledge. <isrel> = is this retrieved doc relevant? Emitted after each retrieved doc. <issup> = is the answer supported by the retrieved doc? Emitted with the answer.
summary: Self-RAG adds four special control tokens (retrieve, no-retrieve, isrel, issup) to the model vocabulary that the runtime intercepts during generation.

text: Token emission timeline on a real query. For 'How do I reset my password?' the model emits a sequence of control tokens interleaved with regular answer text. Each marker is a decision point the runtime watches. Sequence left-to-right: <retrieve> (model gates retrieval) -> Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce a search query) -> doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc) -> <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant) -> Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text) -> <issup> (model judges answer supported). Generation order is left-to-right.
summary: On a real query, Self-RAG emits an interleaved sequence: gate, query reformulation, doc retrieval, relevance check, answer generation, then support check.

text: The Retrieve / No-Retrieve Gate. The decision diamond sits at the top of every generation. Trained via instruction tuning plus RL on retrieval decisions, the gate reduces unnecessary retrievals by ~40% in the original Asai et al. paper. Flow: User Query -> 'Need External Knowledge?' diamond -> YES branch goes to <retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>; NO branch goes to <no-retrieve> -> Answer. Trained via instruction tuning + RL.
summary: A decision diamond gates each generation, routing 'need external knowledge' queries through retrieval+critique and skipping retrieval entirely otherwise, cutting retrievals ~40%.

text: Self-Critique: Re-Score Every Retrieved Doc. After retrieval, the model emits an <isrel> token per doc to grade relevance. Irrelevant docs are dropped; partially relevant docs are kept but down-weighted. After the answer, the model emits an <issup> token to grade whether the answer is supported. Per-doc critique example: doc-1 (Password Reset) -> RELEVANT -> Keep. doc-9 (Login Troubleshooting) -> PARTIALLY RELEVANT -> Keep But De-Weight. doc-22 (Rate Limits) -> IRRELEVANT -> Drop. Final <issup> verdict possibilities: FULLY-SUPPORTED | PARTIALLY-SUPPORTED | NO-SUPPORT. Emitted after the answer to label how grounded the answer is in the kept docs.
summary: Self-RAG self-critiques retrieved docs with <isrel> verdicts (keep/down-weight/drop) and labels the final answer's groundedness with an <issup> verdict.

text: Self-RAG Wins And Limits. Self-RAG is a model contract, not a prompting trick. Use it when you can fine-tune. The wins are real but the cost is owning the special-token classifier calibration. Wins: mixed-knowledge corpora (some queries need retrieval, some don't); latency-sensitive workloads (skips retrieval for ~40% of queries); adaptive depth (decides how many docs to fetch). Limits: requires a model trained for Self-RAG (not plug-and-play); token vocabulary increases serialization cost; calibration of the special-token classifier is the bottleneck. Pick Self-RAG when fine-tuning is on the table and the workload mixes retrieval-needing and already-knew-it queries.
summary: Self-RAG wins on mixed-knowledge, latency-sensitive workloads with adaptive depth, but needs a fine-tuned model and careful special-token classifier calibration.

text: CRAG three-branch decision tree visualization: an evaluator score routes retrieved docs to a CORRECT path (use directly), an AMBIGUOUS path (combine internal + web), or an INCORRECT path (web-search fallback), all converging into knowledge refinement before generation.
summary: SVG decision tree showing CRAG's evaluator routing docs into three branches that converge into knowledge refinement and generation.

text: Corrective RAG (CRAG, Yan et al. 2024) inserts a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation that scores each doc and routes it into three branches. CORRECT (score > 0.8): use the doc directly. INCORRECT (score < 0.5): rewrite query and fall back to external web search (300-800 ms latency cost). AMBIGUOUS (0.5-0.8): combine internal doc with web results, the hedge bet. All three branches then pass through knowledge refinement, which decomposes docs into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic strips, and keeps only on-topic ones (~50% token reduction in the Doc-7 example: 3 of 6 strips kept). Finally the refined context generates the answer.
summary: CRAG scores each retrieved doc, routes it through CORRECT/AMBIGUOUS/INCORRECT branches, then runs knowledge refinement on strips before generation.

text: CRAG (Corrective Retrieval-Augmented Generation, Yan et al. 2024) inserts a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each retrieved doc is scored and classified into one of three buckets: CORRECT (use it), AMBIGUOUS (combine with external sources), or INCORRECT (fall back to web). Example query: 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' Three scored docs: doc-7 (Refunds + Cancellation) score 0.92 -> CORRECT, snippet 'Customers can cancel monthly plans any time before the next billing cycle.' doc-22 (API Rate Limits) score 0.31 -> INCORRECT, snippet 'Free tier allows 100 requests per minute; Pro tier 1000 per minute.' doc-15 (Subscription Tiers) score 0.62 -> AMBIGUOUS, snippet 'Monthly and annual plans differ in pricing and feature access.' The evaluator can be a small classifier model or an LLM judge. Thresholds: below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS.
summary: CRAG adds a retrieval evaluator that scores each doc and routes it into CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT buckets using thresholds 0.5 and 0.8.

text: CORRECT branch: highest-confidence path. The doc passes the evaluator. CRAG routes it through knowledge refinement (next sub-step) and then to the LLM with citations. Flow: Retrieved Doc -> Knowledge Refinement -> Generate Answer With Citations. Same as baseline RAG once the docs pass the evaluator.
summary: When a retrieved doc passes the evaluator as CORRECT, CRAG sends it through knowledge refinement and generates an answer with citations like baseline RAG.

text: INCORRECT branch: doc fails the evaluator. CRAG rewrites the query for web search, calls an external search API, and uses the web results in place of the internal doc. Flow: Failed Doc -> Rewrite Query -> External Web Search -> Use Web Results -> Generate Answer. Web fallback costs latency (300-800 ms) and requires a search API. Trade-off versus admitting 'I don't know'. Use when refusal is unacceptable.
summary: When a doc is classified INCORRECT, CRAG rewrites the query, calls an external web search API, and uses web results instead at a latency cost of 300-800 ms.

text: AMBIGUOUS branch: doc partially answers the query. CRAG keeps it AND also fetches external web results, then merges both into the generation prompt. Flow: Partial Doc + Web Results -> Merge Into Context -> Generate Answer. This is the hedge bet: highest cost path, lowest miss rate. Use when refusal is unacceptable but confidence in internal docs is low.
summary: When evaluator scores fall between thresholds the AMBIGUOUS branch combines internal docs with web results, the costliest path but lowest miss rate.

text: CRAG knowledge refinement splits each retrieved doc into 100-150 token strips and scores each strip individually. Off-topic strips are dropped; on-topic strips are packed. Example: Doc-7 (Refunds) starts at 800 tokens. Strip breakdown: 1 'Refund Eligibility' KEEP, 2 'Annual Plans Prorating' KEEP, 3 'Refund Processing Time' KEEP, 4 'Tax Handling' DROP, 5 'Legal Disclaimers' DROP, 6 'Chargeback Policy' DROP. Final: 3 kept strips, ~50% of original tokens, all on-topic. Packed into the prompt for generation.
summary: Knowledge refinement splits docs into 100-150 token strips, scores each, drops off-topic strips, and in the Doc-7 example keeps 3 of 6 strips cutting tokens by ~50%.

text: Full CRAG pipeline as a decision tree: Query + Retrieved Docs -> Evaluator Score (diamond) splits into three branches. CORRECT -> Use Docs (left, green). AMBIGUOUS -> Combine internal + web (center, yellow). INCORRECT -> Web search (right, red). All three branches converge into Knowledge Refinement (Strips, purple), which feeds Generate Answer (blue). 3 branches, 1 evaluator, all converge into knowledge refinement and generation (Yan et al. 2024).
summary: Diagram shows the full CRAG pipeline: query + docs hit one evaluator that splits into CORRECT/AMBIGUOUS/INCORRECT branches, all converging into knowledge refinement before answer generation.

text: Fifteen-node legal citation graph used as the GraphRAG running corpus, with cases as nodes and directed citation edges between them.
