Cases shown: Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State, Miller v EEOC, Apex v Beta, Cosmos v Stellar, Delta v Epsilon, Falcon v GoTech, Hera v Olympus, Aurora v Beacon, Cipher v Decode, Eagle v Falcon IP, Sigma v Tau. 27 citation edges connect citing cases to cited cases.
summary: Visual of the 15-node, 27-edge legal citation graph that serves as the running GraphRAG corpus.

text: Subgraph retrieval highlight on the 15-node citation graph showing how a query selects a 5-node connected component (a precedent chain) as the retrieved context. Highlighted nodes: Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State. The remaining 10 nodes stay dim because they are outside the retrieved subgraph.
summary: Visual of subgraph retrieval: 5 highlighted nodes form a precedent chain extracted from the 15-node graph.

text: Same 15-node citation graph re-colored into three communities (Civil Rights, Contract Law, IP Law) for global-query summarization. Civil Rights community contains 6 nodes (top cluster); Contract Law contains 5 nodes (middle cluster); IP Law contains 4 nodes (bottom cluster). Each community is colored differently to show the partition produced by Leiden or Louvain.
summary: Visual of the 15-node graph partitioned into three colored communities used for global-query map-reduce.

text: GraphRAG (Microsoft 2024) is a RAG variant that builds a knowledge graph from documents at index time and retrieves subgraphs or community summaries at query time. An LLM extracts entities and relations from each document (~$0.02 per doc), builds a graph (here: 15 legal cases as nodes, 27 citation edges), and runs community detection (Leiden or Louvain) to cluster densely-connected nodes. Each community gets an LLM-generated summary. Local queries retrieve a small subgraph (e.g., 5-node precedent chain); global queries (themes, comparisons) read the community summaries via map-reduce. Worth it for rich entity-relationship corpora (legal, biomedical, code) with frequent global queries; not worth it for self-contained FAQ docs or schema churn. Mitigations: cache extractions, run community detection nightly, hybrid GraphRAG + naive RAG.
summary: End-to-end overview of GraphRAG: offline entity/relation extraction, citation graph, subgraph retrieval, community detection, and when it pays off.

text: The customer-support corpus is mostly self-contained how-to pages with sparse relationships, so a GraphRAG graph over it would be thin and demonstrate nothing. This chapter switches to a 15-node legal-citation network because the customer-support corpus lacks rich entity-relationship structure. Customer Support Corpus - Won't Fit: 30 docs, mostly self-contained how-to pages, relationships sparse, graph would have few edges. Legal Citation Network - Fits: 15 case decisions where each cites 2-5 prior cases, rich entities (Plaintiff, Defendant, Court, Statute) and a real graph topology. The customer-support corpus returns in the next chapter; this chapter only uses the legal-citation secondary corpus.
summary: Why this chapter swaps the customer-support corpus for a 15-node legal-citation graph: GraphRAG needs rich entity-relationship structure.

text: Offline extraction: each case runs through an LLM extraction prompt once at index time. The LLM emits entities (people, organizations, statutes) and relations (sued, decided-in, applies, holding). Cost is ~$0.02 per doc. Example for 'Smith v Jones (2018)'. Entities: Smith (Plaintiff), Jones (Defendant), Northern District Court, Title VII (Statute), 'Discrimination Found' (Holding). Relations: Smith -- sued --> Jones; Smith v Jones -- decided-in --> Northern District Court; Smith v Jones -- applies --> Title VII; Smith v Jones -- holding --> Discrimination Found.
summary: How an LLM extracts entities and relations from each document at index time, with a Smith v Jones worked example.

text: The 15-Node Citation Graph: built once offline. Each node is a case; each directed edge is a citation from the citing case to the cited case. 15 cases, 27 citation edges. Built once offline; updated incrementally as new cases arrive. Nodes include Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State, Miller v EEOC, Apex v Beta, Cosmos v Stellar, Delta v Epsilon, Falcon v GoTech, Hera v Olympus, Aurora v Beacon, Cipher v Decode, Eagle v Falcon IP, Sigma v Tau.
summary: Describes the 15-node, 27-edge directed citation graph used as the running GraphRAG corpus.

text: Query Time: Retrieve A Subgraph. Query: 'What is the precedent chain for workplace discrimination cases?' The retriever finds a 5-node subgraph (Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State) and sends only those nodes + their edges to the LLM. Entity-aware retrieval; the graph topology is the signal. 5 nodes highlighted in orange. The LLM reads only the highlighted subgraph - not the whole 15-node corpus.
summary: Subgraph retrieval at query time: pick a connected component of relevant nodes and pass only those to the LLM.

text: Communities: Cluster The Graph For Global Queries. Community detection (Leiden or Louvain) groups densely-connected nodes into clusters. Each community gets an LLM-generated summary at index time. Global queries hit the community summaries; local queries hit the subgraph. Three communities: Civil Rights (6 nodes) - cases established protected-class precedents under Title VII and the Equal Protection Clause. Contract Law (5 nodes) - cases interpreted breach-of-contract remedies, specific performance, and damages caps. IP Law (4 nodes) - cases shaped fair-use boundaries, trademark dilution, and software copyrightability.
summary: Community detection clusters densely-connected nodes; each cluster gets an LLM summary used for global queries.

text: Global Queries Use Communities, Local Queries Use Subgraphs. Two query patterns, two routing paths. Local queries need a specific subgraph; global queries need an overview that no single subgraph contains. Local Query example: 'How did the court rule in Smith v Jones?' Plan: Retrieve the Smith v Jones node + 1-hop neighbors (5 nodes total). Output: Direct answer from the subgraph. Global Query example: 'What are the main themes across this corpus?' Plan: Retrieve all 3 community summaries; map-reduce summarize them. Output: High-level themes answer the question without retrieving every doc. Global queries are GraphRAG's killer feature - naive RAG cannot answer them without retrieving everything; GraphRAG answers from the community summaries.
summary: Local queries route to subgraphs, global queries route to community summaries via map-reduce; global queries are GraphRAG's edge over naive RAG.

text: When To Reach For GraphRAG. GraphRAG is expensive to build and maintain. Use it when the corpus has rich entity-relationship structure and global queries are common. Worth It: rich entity-relationship corpus (legal, biomedical, code); global queries common (themes, summaries, comparisons); long-term value (build the graph once, query many times). Not Worth It: mostly self-contained docs (FAQ, support knowledge base); only local/factoid queries; schema churn (entities change weekly). Cost: per-doc LLM extraction at index time (~$0.02 per doc); re-extract on doc changes; graph storage + community detection compute. Mitigations: cache extractions; run community detection nightly, not per-query; hybrid - GraphRAG for global queries, naive RAG for local.
summary: Decision framework: when GraphRAG pays off, when it doesn't, cost drivers, and mitigations.

text: Tool-call loop flowchart for agentic RAG: model emits a tool_call, the runtime executes the tool and feeds the response back, looping until the model emits a final answer or hits max_iterations. Nodes: User Query (blue) at top, Model (purple) below it, then a yellow diamond Need A Tool? Branches: YES goes right to a cyan tool_call(...) box whose tool_response loops back into the Model. NO goes left to a green Final Answer box. Footer text reads max_iterations = 10, hard cap prevents runaway loops.
summary: Flowchart shows the agentic RAG loop: User Query to Model to Need A Tool diamond, YES into tool_call with tool_response feedback, NO into Final Answer, capped at 10 iterations.

text: Tool-augmented (agentic) RAG treats retrieval as one of several tools an LLM can invoke alongside SQL query, calculator, web search, and internal APIs. The model uses function-calling to emit structured tool_call tokens; the runtime executes the tool and feeds the tool_response back, looping until the model emits a final answer or hits max_iterations. A worked four-turn trace shows vector_search, sql_query, and calculator chained to answer a multi-part question. Loop control combines hard caps (max_iterations, rate limits, wall-clock budget), soft signals (final-answer marker, duplicate tool-call detection, error-rate abort), cost tracking, and mitigations (cache tool responses, smaller orchestrator model). Orchestration frameworks like LangGraph offer graph-shaped state machines with checkpointing and retries but lock you in; the DIY while-loop works without any framework. Pick orchestration last.
summary: Agentic RAG makes retrieval one of many tools an LLM picks via function-calling, looped under hard caps and cost controls.

text: Retrieval Is One Tool. Add More. Tool-augmented (agentic) RAG treats retrieval as one of several tools the LLM can invoke. SQL, calculators, web search, and internal APIs sit alongside it. The model picks the right tool per query. Four tools shown: Vector Search - semantic retrieval over your embedded chunks, the RAG we have been building. SQL Query - structured lookups over a relational store such as pricing tables and user accounts. Calculator - precise arithmetic; models are bad at math, calculators are not. Web Search - fresh or out-of-corpus info, used as fallback or for time-sensitive queries. The LLM decides which tool to call. Function-calling pattern. Each tool returns text the model can read.
summary: Vector retrieval is just one of several tools (SQL, calculator, web search) the LLM picks among per query.

text:
Function-Calling: How The Model Picks A Tool. The model emits structured tool_call tokens; the runtime intercepts, executes the tool, and feeds the response back. The loop continues until the model emits a final answer or hits max_iterations. Example trace:

User: Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.

Model: I need the pricing table. Calling tool.
  tool_call: sql_query(table="plans", filter="name in ('Pro','Enterprise')")
  tool_response: [{"plan":"Pro","price":29,"users":10}, {"plan":"Enterprise","price":199,"users":"unlimited"}]

Model: I also need feature comparison docs. Calling tool.
  tool_call: vector_search(query="Pro vs Enterprise features", k=3)
  tool_response: [doc-15 chunk 1, doc-15 chunk 2, doc-19 chunk 1]

Model: Now I can answer. Generating response with citations.
summary: Function-calling has the model emit tool_call tokens; the runtime runs the tool and returns tool_response until a final answer.

text: The Tool-Call Loop. The control loop runs until the model declares done or max_iterations is reached. A loopback path lets the model chain multiple tool calls. Flow: User Query feeds into Model. Model decides Need A Tool? If YES, emits tool_call(...) which executes and the tool_response is fed back into Model for the next iteration. If NO, the model returns Final Answer. Hard cap max_iterations = 10 prevents runaway loops.
summary: Control loop sends user query to model; if a tool is needed it executes and feeds response back, else returns final answer, capped at max_iterations.

text: Worked Example: Multi-Tool Trace. Query: "Compare Pro and Enterprise plans, then estimate cost for 25 users on Pro." Four turns, three tools, one final answer. Turn 1: vector_search("Pro Enterprise comparison") returns 3 chunks (doc-15 + doc-19). Turn 2: sql_query(SELECT price FROM plans WHERE name='Pro') returns $29 per seat. Turn 3: calculator(25 x 29) returns 725. Turn 4: Final Answer - Pro is $29/seat. 25 users => $725/month. Pro includes features X, Y, Z [doc-15]; Enterprise adds SSO + audit logs [doc-19].
summary: A four-turn agent trace chains vector_search, sql_query, and calculator to answer a multi-part pricing comparison.

text: Loop Control: Termination Criteria. Termination is a layered defense. Hard caps stop runaway loops; soft signals catch loops that are technically progressing but diverging; cost tracking catches expensive flows; mitigations cut the rate. Hard Caps: max_iterations (typically 5-10); total tool calls per query (rate-limit by tool); wall-clock budget (timeout in seconds). Soft Signals: model emits 'final answer' marker; same tool called with same args twice in a row -> divergence flag; tool error rate above threshold -> abort and refuse. Cost Tracking: each tool call has a fixed cost; sum across iterations; alert if over per-query budget; bill chargebacks to feature owners. Mitigations: cap iterations strictly; cache tool responses by exact args (especially vector_search); use a smaller orchestrator model.
summary: Layered termination: hard caps, soft divergence signals, cost tracking, and mitigations like caching and smaller orchestrator models.

text: Orchestration: Frameworks Like LangGraph. Pick orchestration last. The patterns above work without any framework. Frameworks are a productivity choice, not a correctness one. DIY (Custom While Loop): you implement the function-calling parse, tool dispatch, max_iterations check, response merge yourself. Full control. More code. LangGraph And Similar Frameworks: graph-shaped state machine for tool flows. Nodes are tool calls, edges are transitions. Built-in checkpointing, retries, human-in-the-loop. Less code. Framework lock-in. Pick a framework when it saves more time than it costs. Patterns above are framework-agnostic.
summary: Choose DIY while-loop for control or LangGraph-style graph state machines for built-in checkpointing and retries; pick the framework last.

text: Long-context vs RAG compares three strategies for a 200-page (~120k token) product manual: stuff the whole document into the prompt (long-context-only), chunk and retrieve top-5 (RAG-only), or retrieve top-30 and pack 50k tokens with reordering (Hybrid). Long-context-only costs $0.36/query, takes 5-8 seconds, and suffers lost-in-the-middle. RAG-only is ~$0.008/query and ~1.5s but risks missing the relevant chunk in top-5. Hybrid sits in the middle on cost ($0.15) and latency (~3s) but wins on accuracy (92% golden recall) because more candidates lower miss rate. Hybrid is the production default; RAG-only fits high-volume cost-sensitive workloads; long-context-only fits single-doc, low-volume, no-retrieval-infra cases.
summary: Chapter-level summary of the three strategies (RAG-only, Hybrid, Long-Context-Only) with their cost, latency, accuracy tradeoffs for a 200-page product manual.

text: This chapter switches corpus from the customer-support 30 small docs (~50k tokens total) to a single 200-page product manual (~120k tokens) that fits inside a 200k context window. The support corpus is distributed across 30 distinct docs of 500-2000 tokens each so retrieval is the natural pattern even though it already fits in modern windows. The product manual is one long document - the 'do I even need RAG?' test case. When the entire knowledge base is one long document that fits in the context window, the retrieval question changes shape.
summary: Switches the running example to a single 200-page (~120k token) product manual to set up the long-context vs RAG comparison.

text: Long-Context Only: pack ALL 120k tokens of the manual into the prompt. The LLM reads everything and answers. Flow: User Query -> Pack ALL 120k Tokens -> LLM Reads -> Answer. Cost: at $3 per 1M input tokens, this is $0.36 per query. Latency: 120k tokens to read produces 5-8 seconds first-token latency. Simple. Expensive. Slow. Subject to lost-in-the-middle for facts buried mid-document.
summary: Long-context-only strategy stuffs all 120k tokens into the prompt: $0.36/query, 5-8s latency, lost-in-the-middle risk.

text: RAG Only: chunk the 200-page manual into 240 chunks of 512 tokens. At query time, embed the query, retrieve top-5 (2,560 tokens), pack into prompt. Flow: Chunk Manual Into 240 Chunks -> Retrieve Top-5 -> Pack ~2,560 Tokens -> LLM Reads -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.008 per query - about 45x cheaper than long-context-only. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 1-2s generation = ~1.5s. Risk: if the relevant chunk is not in top-5, the answer is wrong.
summary: RAG-only strategy chunks the manual into 240 chunks, retrieves top-5 (2,560 tokens): ~$0.008/query, ~1.5s, miss risk if relevant chunk excluded.

text: Hybrid: retrieve top-30 chunks (15,000 tokens), optionally rerank, then pack 50k tokens into the prompt. Reorder using lost-in-middle strategies (front-load or sandwich). Flow: Retrieve Top-30 -> Optional Rerank -> Pack 50k Tokens -> Front-Load Or Sandwich -> LLM -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.15 per query (5x cheaper than long-context-only). Latency: 80ms retrieval + 3s generation = ~3s. Accuracy: highest. More candidates means lower miss rate. The pragmatic middle - most production systems land here.
summary: Hybrid strategy retrieves top-30, reranks, packs 50k tokens with front-load/sandwich ordering: $0.15/query, ~3s, highest accuracy.

text: Grouped bar chart with three metric groups - Cost (Per Query) in dollars, Latency P50 in seconds, Accuracy (Golden Recall) in percent. Each group has three bars: RAG-Only (green), Hybrid (yellow), Long-Context-Only (red). Values: Cost - RAG-Only $0.008, Hybrid $0.150, Long-Context-Only $0.360 (max $0.40). Latency - RAG-Only 1.5s, Hybrid 3.0s, Long-Context-Only 6.0s (max 7.0s). Accuracy - RAG-Only 78%, Hybrid 92%, Long-Context-Only 84% (max 100%). Hybrid wins accuracy; RAG-only wins cost and latency. Hybrid is the production default; long-context-only is niche; RAG-only is for high-volume cost-sensitive systems.
summary: Grouped bar chart comparing cost, latency, and accuracy across RAG-only, Hybrid, and Long-Context-Only for the 200-page manual.

text: Decision guide for when each strategy wins. RAG Only: cost-critical at scale (~$0.008/query), latency-critical (~1.5s p50), large multi-doc corpus, citations required (chunk-level provenance). Hybrid: default for production, best accuracy on accuracy-critical workloads, acceptable cost + latency tradeoff, citations still feasible. Long-Context Only: single long document corpus, low query volume (cost not pressing), citations not required (or post-extracted), no retrieval infrastructure available. Hybrid is the production default. Long-context-only is a niche. RAG-only is for high-volume cost-sensitive systems.
summary: Decision framework listing the workload shapes where RAG-only, Hybrid, and Long-Context-Only each are the right choice.

text: Triangle diagram of the three RAG evaluation layers. Top vertex: Retrieval (cyan) - did we find the right docs? Lower-left vertex: Generation (purple) - did the model use them faithfully? Lower-right vertex: End-To-End (orange) - did the final answer help the user? Edges connect all three vertices to show the layers form a closed evaluation framework where each vertex is a distinct failure surface.
summary: Triangle SVG showing the three RAG evaluation layers as vertices with one-line descriptions of what each layer measures.

text: The RAG Eval Triangle splits evaluation into three layers so a bad final answer can be traced to a specific root cause: Retrieval (did we find the right docs?), Generation (did the model use them faithfully?), and End-to-End (did the final answer help the user?). Retrieval metrics: Recall@k, MRR, Precision@k, nDCG@k. Generation metrics: Faithfulness, Answer Relevancy. End-to-end metrics: Correctness (binary vs gold), Helpfulness (1-5 Likert). Three failure scenarios show retrieval, generation, and product faults each map to one layer. BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR are deprecated for RAG because n-gram overlap does not measure faithfulness.
summary: Overview of the three-layer RAG evaluation triangle, the metrics at each layer, why three layers enable root-cause tracing, and why BLEU/ROUGE are deprecated.

text: Three Layers Of RAG Evaluation. A bad final answer can come from bad retrieval OR bad generation OR a product-side mismatch. We need a metric for each layer so a failure traces to one place. The three layers form a triangle: Retrieval (did we find the right docs?), Generation (did the model use them faithfully?), and End-To-End (did the final answer help the user?).
summary: Introduces the three layers of RAG evaluation as a triangle so failures can be isolated to retrieval, generation, or end-to-end.

text:
Layer 1: Retrieval Metrics. Retrieval metrics measure whether the search step found the right docs. Four metrics dominate the literature:

Recall@k = relevant_retrieved / total_relevant - what fraction of all the relevant docs were retrieved?
MRR = (1/N) * sum( 1 / rank_of_first_relevant ) - mean reciprocal rank of the first relevant doc per query.
Precision@k = relevant_retrieved / k - what fraction of the top-k retrieved are actually relevant?
nDCG@k = DCG@k / IDCG@k - normalised discounted cumulative gain, rank-weighted relevance.

Example: top-3 returned for 'How do I reset my password?': doc-1 (relevant), doc-23 (not), doc-7 (relevant) -> Precision@3 = 2 / 3 = 0.667.
summary: Defines the four core retrieval metrics with formulas and a worked Precision@3 example.

text:
Layer 2: Generation Metrics. Generation metrics measure whether the model used the retrieved context to produce a grounded answer. Faithfulness is the headline metric, paired with answer relevancy.

Faithfulness: Are the claims in the answer supported by the retrieved context? Example: Answer claims '24-hour expiry'; retrieved doc-1 says 'the link expires after 24 hours' -> claim supported -> score 1.0.

Answer Relevancy: Does the answer actually address the question? Example: Question 'How do I reset my password?' Answer 'Click forgot password and check your email' -> relevancy 0.9.
summary: Defines faithfulness and answer relevancy as generation-layer metrics with concrete scored examples.

text:
Layer 3: End-To-End Metrics. End-to-end metrics measure whether the user actually got what they needed. Correctness compares to a gold answer; helpfulness asks the user on a 1-5 Likert scale.

Correctness: Did the final answer match the gold answer? (binary). Example: Gold 'Click forgot password and check email.' Generated 'Click forgot password link and check your registered email.' -> 1.

Helpfulness: How useful was the answer to the user? (1-5 Likert). Example: User rated this answer 4/5: 'Solved it but had to scroll'.
summary: Defines correctness (binary vs gold) and helpfulness (1-5 Likert) as end-to-end metrics with examples.

text:
Why Three Layers? Failures Trace To A Layer. With three layers, a wrong final answer locates to a specific root cause. Walk the multi-hop query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' through three failure scenarios.

Scenario A - Retrieval Fault: Top-k returns doc-4 (refunds) for a password-reset query; doc-1 never surfaces. Recall@10 drops from 0.95 to 0.30. Root cause: retrieval. Fix in the retriever.

Scenario B - Generation Fault: Retrieval returns doc-1 + doc-7 correctly. The model ignores them and hallucinates a phone-support step. Faithfulness drops to 0.50. Root cause: generation. Fix the prompt / model / refusal logic.

Scenario C - Product Fault: Retrieval and generation both score above 0.9. Yet user reports 'this did not help'. Helpfulness drops to 2/5. Root cause: product / prompting / answer style. Fix online.
summary: Three failure scenarios show how a wrong RAG answer traces to retrieval, generation, or product layer with specific metric drops.

text: Deprecated For RAG: BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR. These are word-overlap metrics: they count how many n-grams in the generated answer also appear in a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness. A perfectly faithful answer written in different words scores 0; a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS and LLM-as-judge instead.
summary: BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR are deprecated for RAG because n-gram overlap does not capture faithfulness; use RAGAS or LLM-as-judge.

text: Judge calibration before-and-after line chart. The x-axis is the judge score percentile (low to high) and the y-axis is agreement with human labels. The before curve (red) sits around 60% agreement with a long divergence tail across percentiles 0.1 to 0.9. The after curve (green), drawn after rubric refinement plus position and verbosity mitigations were applied, rises from 78% at the low percentile to about 92% agreement at the high percentile. A dashed green gridline marks the 0.85 target threshold. The visual shows how calibration narrows the gap between automated judge scores and human judgement.
summary: Line chart contrasts pre-calibration (~60% agreement) and post-calibration (~92% agreement) judge-human correlation, with a 0.85 target gridline.

text: LLM-as-Judge uses one LLM to evaluate another's outputs at near-human quality, sitting between slow expensive human annotation and shallow BLEU/ROUGE heuristics. The judge prompt frames the model as an evaluator and asks for 1-5 scores on faithfulness, answer relevancy, and helpfulness, returned as JSON. A concrete anchored rubric (not prose framing) is what makes scores reproducible. Three known biases must be mitigated: position bias (first slot wins; fix by swapping orders), verbosity bias (longer answers score higher; fix by adding conciseness or length-normalising), and self-preference bias (judges inflate same-family outputs; fix with cross-family judges). Calibrate against 50-100 human-labeled cases and refine until agreement exceeds 0.85. Prefer a stronger or cross-family judge model over the candidate.
summary: End-to-end overview of LLM-as-judge: prompt and rubric design, three biases with mitigations, and calibration to above 0.85 human agreement.

text: Why use an LLM to judge an LLM? Human annotation is the gold standard but does not scale. Word-overlap heuristics like BLEU and ROUGE are fast but miss faithfulness. LLM-as-judge sits between the two: fast, medium cost, and near-human quality on well-rubric'd tasks. Comparison of three evaluation methods: Human Annotation (Very Slow, Expensive, High quality; 1-2 minutes per example, costs scale with team size). Heuristic BLEU/ROUGE (Instant, Free, Low quality; N-gram overlap misses faithfulness and groundedness entirely). LLM-as-Judge (Fast, Medium cost, Near-Human quality; seconds per example, agreement with human reaches 0.85+ when calibrated).
summary: Compares human annotation, BLEU/ROUGE heuristics, and LLM-as-judge across speed, cost, and quality to motivate using an LLM evaluator.
Shows the full judge prompt template with three criteria (faithfulness, answer relevancy, helpfulness) and JSON output schema.

text: Rubric: 1-5 per criterion. The rubric is the actual calibration tool. Prose framing in the prompt sets tone; the rubric anchors every integer score to a concrete behaviour. Faithfulness rubric: 5 = All claims supported, none missing (every factual statement in the answer is verifiable in the retrieved context). 4 = Mostly supported, minor unsupported detail (core claims grounded; one peripheral detail not in context). 3 = About half supported (a roughly even mix of supported and ungrounded statements). 2 = Mostly unsupported; some grounded (most claims are made up; one or two are anchored in the context). 1 = Hallucinated; nothing in context supports it (answer is a confident hallucination with no grounding at all). Rubric design beats prompt prose. The rubric is what makes scores reproducible.
summary: Defines a concrete 1-5 faithfulness rubric anchoring each integer score to specific behaviour for reproducible LLM judging.

text: Bias 1: Position Bias. In pairwise judging, the order of the two candidates affects the verdict even when their content is identical. The first slot wins more often. Order 1 (A, B): Judge picks position 1 (A). Same two answers; same judge model. The first slot wins. Order 2 (B, A): Judge picks position 1 (B). Swap the order, the judge's pick swaps too. Identical content. Mitigation: Randomize or swap. Run both orders for every pair and average; or randomize position per query before passing to the judge.
summary: Explains position bias where pairwise LLM judges favour the first candidate, and the swap-and-average mitigation.

text: Bias 2: Verbosity Bias. Judges (especially weaker models) prefer longer answers, mistaking length for thoroughness. The longer answer can be wrong and still score higher than a tight correct one. Answer A - 50 words, accurate: "Go to Settings, click Forgot Password, check your email, use the link within 24 hours." All claims supported. No padding. Answer B - 200 words, one hallucination: "To begin the secure password recovery process, navigate to your account dashboard... You may also call our 24/7 support line." (Phone support claim is unsupported.) Longer feels thorough. Judges (especially weaker ones) score it higher. Mitigation: Weight conciseness or length-normalise. Add an explicit conciseness criterion to the rubric, or normalise by answer length before averaging scores.
summary: Illustrates verbosity bias where judges prefer longer answers and shows conciseness weighting and length normalization as fixes.

text: Bias 3: Self-Preference Bias. Judges score outputs from their own model family higher than human-aligned scores would. The bigger the model, the bigger this effect typically gets. Examples: Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = GPT-4o gives +12% vs human (same family). Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = Claude 3.5 gives -3% vs human (different family). Judge = Claude 3.5, Candidate = Claude 3.5 gives +9% vs human (same family). Mitigation: Third-party / cross-family judging. Pick a judge from a different model family than the candidates. Or average across multiple judge families to wash out single-family bias.
summary: Documents self-preference bias where judges inflate scores for their own model family and recommends cross-family judging.

text: Calibrate: Spot-check 50-100 cases. A judge is a hypothesis. Before trusting its scores in production, verify agreement against 50-100 human-labeled cases. Refine the rubric and mitigations until correlation lands above 0.85. The before curve sits around 60% agreement; the after curve rises to about 92% agreement once the rubric is refined and position plus verbosity mitigations are applied. Judge Model Selection: Use a stronger judge model than the candidate (e.g., judge with GPT-4 a candidate from a 7B model). Or use the same model with documented caveats. Cross-family judging is the strongest setup.
summary: Explains the calibration loop: spot-check 50-100 human-labeled cases, refine until agreement exceeds 0.85, and pick a stronger or cross-family judge.

text: RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) is a 2023 reference-free metric suite for RAG that uses an LLM-judge to compute four core scores: Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy for the generation side, Context Precision and Context Recall for the retrieval side. Each metric maps to a plain-English question - are the claims supported, does it answer the question, are top results relevant, did we retrieve everything. A worked password-reset example gives Faithfulness 0.8, Answer Relevancy 0.913, Context Precision 0.667, Context Recall 1.0, producing a per-query report card averaging ~0.845. BLEU and ROUGE are explicitly NOT RAGAS - they measure n-gram overlap against a reference, not faithfulness.
summary: Chapter-level overview of RAGAS, its four reference-free metrics, worked-example scores, and why BLEU/ROUGE are inadequate.

text: RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) is a 2023 metric suite that uses an LLM under the hood to compute eval scores without needing a reference answer. It has four core metrics, two for retrieval and two for generation. Faithfulness asks: are the claims supported? (purple). Answer Relevancy asks: does it answer the question? (orange). Context Precision asks: are top results actually relevant? (green). Context Recall asks: did we retrieve everything? (red).
summary: Introduces RAGAS as a reference-free LLM-based eval suite with four metrics split across retrieval and generation.

text: Faithfulness asks whether every claim in the generated answer can be verified in the retrieved context. The LLM-judge extracts atomic claims and labels each as supported or not. Formula: Faithfulness = | verifiable_claims | / | total_claims_in_answer |. Worked example - Password Reset Query. Retrieved context (doc-1): 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security. Click Forgot Password. An email is sent to your registered address. The link expires after 24 hours.' Generated answer: 'Go to Settings > Security, click Forgot Password, check your email, and use the link within 24 hours. You can also call support.' Claims: (1) Go to Settings > Security - Supported. (2) Click Forgot Password - Supported. (3) Check your email - Supported. (4) Use the link within 24 hours - Supported. (5) You can also call support - Not Supported. Faithfulness = 4 / 5 = 0.8.
summary: Defines Faithfulness, its formula, and walks through a 5-claim password-reset example yielding 0.8.

text: Answer Relevancy is a round-trip check: the LLM-judge reads the generated answer and writes n new questions that the answer would best answer. Each generated question is embedded and compared with cosine similarity to the original query. The average is the relevancy score. Formula: Answer Relevancy = (1/n) * sum( cosine( embed(q), embed(q_i_generated) ) ). Worked example with n = 3. Original query: 'How do I reset my password?' Q1: 'What are the steps to reset my password?' cos 0.94. Q2: 'How can I change my password if I forgot it?' cos 0.91. Q3: 'How do I update my account password?' cos 0.89. Answer Relevancy = (0.94 + 0.91 + 0.89) / 3 = 0.913.
summary: Explains Answer Relevancy as a round-trip cosine check between original query and LLM-regenerated questions, scoring 0.913 in the example.

text: Context Precision asks what fraction of the top-k retrieved chunks were actually relevant. RAGAS adds a rank-aware weighting in the full version, but the simplified ratio is taught here. Formula: Context Precision = relevant_chunks_at_top_k / k. Worked example - Top-3 for password reset query. Rank 1: doc-1 chunk-1 - Password Reset Intro - Relevant. Rank 2: doc-23 chunk-2 - 500 Error Troubleshooting - Not Relevant. Rank 3: doc-1 chunk-2 - Password Reset Email Step - Relevant. Context Precision = 2 / 3 = 0.667. Naming note: DeepEval / TruLens call this ContextualRelevancyScore - same idea under a different framework name. Older RAGAS shipped a separate 'Context Relevancy' metric; it was deprecated in favor of Context Precision plus Context Recall.
summary: Defines Context Precision as relevant-at-k over k, with a top-3 example yielding 0.667 and a naming note about DeepEval/TruLens equivalents.

text: Context Recall asks what fraction of all the relevant chunks in the corpus were retrieved. It is the dual of precision: precision says 'are top results clean?', recall says 'did we miss anything?'. Formula: Context Recall = relevant_chunks_retrieved / all_relevant_chunks_in_corpus. Worked example - the corpus has 2 chunks relevant to 'How do I reset my password?': doc-1 chunk-1 and doc-1 chunk-2. Both are retrieved, so Context Recall = 2 / 2 = 1.0. Counter-example: if retrieval had returned only doc-1 chunk-1 and missed doc-1 chunk-2, then Context Recall = 1 / 2 = 0.5.
summary: Defines Context Recall as the dual of precision, with a 1.0 example and a 0.5 counter-example when one relevant chunk is missed.

text: Putting it together: a per-query report card. One query, four scores. The retrieval pair (precision + recall) covers the search step; the generation pair (faithfulness + answer relevancy) covers the model. Scores from the password-reset example: Faithfulness 0.80, Answer Relevancy 0.91, Context Precision 0.67, Context Recall 1.00. Average = 0.845 - the headline score per query. How RAGAS computes the scores: an LLM-judge extracts atomic claims for faithfulness, generates round-trip questions for relevancy, and labels each retrieved chunk as relevant or not for precision and recall.
summary: Aggregates the four RAGAS metrics into a per-query report card with bar chart and a 0.845 headline average.

text: Not RAGAS: BLEU and ROUGE. BLEU and ROUGE compute n-gram overlap between the generated answer and a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness. A perfectly faithful answer in different wording scores low; a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS or LLM-as-judge instead.
summary: Explains why BLEU and ROUGE n-gram metrics are unsuitable for RAG and recommends RAGAS or LLM-as-judge.

text: Golden datasets are the human-verified ground-truth records that every RAG eval metric (RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, recall@k) depends on. Each record holds a question, expected answer, expected doc IDs, and a refusal flag. Build a 30-100 hand-written seed covering five edge cases (multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, time-sensitive), grow it via LLM-bootstrapping with human review to 500-2000 records (60-80% acceptance), capture every production bug as a regression case, and review monthly to archive obsolete entries and add new ones. Track health via coverage, freshness, and pass-rate.
summary: End-to-end recipe for building, growing, and maintaining a golden eval dataset for RAG.

text: Golden Datasets: The Ground Truth For Every Other Metric. Every eval metric in this section (RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, recall@k) needs ground-truth labels. The golden dataset is the held-out, human-verified set of records you run every eval against. Without it you have no signal; with a flaky one you have noise. One golden record has four columns: Question (e.g., 'How do I reset my password?'), Expected Answer (e.g., 'Click Forgot Password and check your registered email.'), Expected Docs (e.g., [doc-1]), and Refusal (e.g., No). Multiply by 30-2000 records and you have a defensible eval set.
summary: A golden record is a human-verified tuple of question, expected answer, expected docs, and refusal flag - the ground truth every RAG eval metric needs.

text: Start With 30-100 Hand-Written Examples. Begin small and human-curated. The goal of the first batch is breadth across query types, not volume. A tight 30-100 hand-written seed is the foundation everything else extends. Three curation steps: (1) List query types from logs or product spec - mine a week of production queries (or the product spec) for the categories you must support; (2) Write 5-10 examples per type by hand - cover normal cases plus the edge cases shown in the next sub-step; (3) Write expected answer + expected docs per example - each tuple becomes one golden record (question + answer + doc IDs + refusal flag). Week 1 tally by query type: Single-Doc Lookups 20, Multi-Hop 15, Aggregation 10, Refusal-Required 10, Empty-Context 10. Total Week 1 = 65.
summary: Begin with a hand-curated 30-100 seed: mine query types, write 5-10 per type, label each with expected answer and docs.

text: Five Edge Cases That Break Naive RAG. The first hand-written batch must cover these five edge cases. Skip any and you ship blind to a class of failures. (1) Multi-Hop: answer requires combining 2+ docs, e.g. 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?'. (2) Empty-Context: no doc in the corpus answers this - must refuse, e.g. 'What is your stock price?'. (3) Ambiguous: two docs conflict - newest-wins or refusal, e.g. 'What is the refund window?' (doc-A: 14 days; doc-B: 30 days). (4) Refusal-Required: out-of-scope or unsafe query, e.g. 'Give me another customer's email.'. (5) Time-Sensitive: answer depends on today's date, e.g. 'Is feature X live yet?'.
summary: Five edge cases the golden set must include: multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, and time-sensitive queries.

text: Regression Set: Every Production Bug Becomes A Golden Case. Anything that failed in production once must never silently fail again. The regression set is the self-healing memory of your eval suite. Workflow: (1) Production user reports a bad answer; (2) Engineer reproduces, captures query, docs, answer, expected answer; (3) Add tuple to the regression-golden set; (4) Every future eval includes it - failure cannot silently re-emerge. Example regression tuples captured so far: 'Reset Password Forgot Email' - multi-hop missed; bot suggested support phone. 'Cancel And Refund' - refused incorrectly; user blocked from valid request. 'Pro Plan SSO' - hallucinated SSO inclusion in Pro tier.
summary: Capture every production failure as a permanent regression-golden tuple so it can never silently fail again.

text: LLM-Bootstrapped: Generate Then Human-Review. To scale past the hand-written seed, generate question-answer pairs with an LLM grounded in corpus docs, then human-review each pair. Acceptance rate is 60-80% for clear docs. A 30-100 seed grows to 500-2000 reviewed records this way. Pipeline has two lanes: LLM Lane (Generate): Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Human Lane (Review): Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept / Edit / Reject -> Golden Record. Caution: Never Deploy Without Human Review. The LLM can encode its own bias - generating good test cases for itself while failing on adjacent phrasings. Acceptance rate matters; so does reviewer diversity.
summary: Scale the seed to 500-2000 records by LLM-generating Q-A pairs grounded in docs, then human-accepting/editing/rejecting each (60-80% acceptance).

text: Two-lane LLM-bootstrapped golden-dataset pipeline. Upper LLM lane (cyan): Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Bridge arrow drops to lower Human lane (green): Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept / Edit / Reject -> Golden Record. Illustrates how a small hand-built seed scales to 500-2000 reviewed records without losing human oversight.
summary: Diagram of LLM-generate-then-human-review pipeline that scales the golden seed without dropping human oversight.

text: Monthly Review: Archive Obsolete, Add New. Like code, golden sets rot. A monthly review cadence keeps the set defensible: spot-check 20 cases, archive anything tied to deleted docs or changed product behavior, add new cases captured from the past month's production incidents. Each monthly tile (Jan-Dec) is a cycle; every 4th month adds an audit. Archive obsolete cases to /archived/ (audit-kept), and re-run regression to confirm green. Three health metrics track set quality: Coverage = query types covered / total query types; Freshness = median age of golden cases in months; Pass-Rate = % of golden set currently green on production pipeline.
summary: Run a monthly cadence to spot-check, archive obsolete cases, and add new ones; track health via coverage, freshness, and pass-rate.

text: Online eval and A/B testing for production RAG. Offline gates merges; online catches regressions users actually hit. Implicit signals (thumbs, dwell, copy-paste, follow-up rephrase) combine into a composite quality score - noisy alone, reliable in 3+. Explicit feedback (rating widgets at 2-8% capture, free-text prompts) is the highest-signal diagnosis source; redact PII before storage under GDPR/CCPA. Shadow eval runs a candidate pipeline on real traffic but logs answers without serving them; an LLM-judge scores both, graduating winners to A/B. A/B: 50/50 bucket, 200 queries/day, judge rubric, mean + significance (~7-14 days at effect size 0.1, p<0.05), no peeking, plus auto-rollback guardrails on faithfulness drop or refusal spike. Closing the loop: online detect -> golden regression set -> offline reproduce and fix with RAGAS + judge -> shadow + A/B verify -> back to online. Offline gives reproducibility, online gives reality; the loop ensures the same bug never ships twice.
summary: Chapter overview: offline + online eval, implicit and explicit signals, shadow eval, A/B with judge rubric and guardrails, and the offline-online feedback loop that locks in regressions.

text: Offline eval gates merges before deploy. Online eval detects regressions production users will hit but the golden set cannot anticipate. You need both. Offline eval: controlled and reproducible across runs; fast iteration with the same golden set every time; but does not reflect production query distribution and misses rare-but-bad cases users actually hit. Online eval: real query distribution from real users; catches edge cases offline never sees; but the signal is noisy and needs aggregation over many sessions, plus privacy concerns when storing user feedback. Use both: offline gates merges before deploy; online catches regressions after deploy and feeds them back into the golden regression set.
summary: Why production RAG needs both offline eval (reproducible gate before merge) and online eval (real-traffic regression detector after deploy).

text: Implicit signals are behavioral traces from session telemetry. Each is individually noisy; combining three or more makes a reliable quality regression detector. Four implicit signals: Thumbs Up / Down - explicit-light; most users do not click. Dwell Time - long dwell means user read carefully; very short dwell means likely irrelevant. Copy-Paste Rate - user copied the answer means it was useful enough to reuse. Follow-Up Rephrase - user re-asks the same intent with different words, meaning the previous answer failed. Any single signal is too noisy alone. A composite quality score over 3 or more implicit signals tracks user satisfaction reliably; calibrate the composite against explicit feedback.
summary: Four implicit behavioral signals (thumbs, dwell, copy-paste, rephrase) combined into a composite to detect quality regressions without explicit ratings.

text: Explicit feedback (a rating widget or a free-text 'what went wrong?' prompt) is the highest signal per sample and the lowest volume. Use it for diagnosis, not for primary quality measurement alone. Rating Widget: post-answer thumbs, star rating, or Did-This-Help Yes/No. Capture rate: 2-8% of sessions. Free-Text Feedback: 'Tell us what went wrong.' Higher signal, much lower volume. Single best source of qualitative failure modes. Privacy: redact PII before storage. Store feedback with conversation context, but redact PII (names, emails, account numbers) before retention. Follow regional privacy law: GDPR, CCPA. Document the retention period.
summary: Explicit feedback widgets and free-text prompts give the highest per-sample signal at low volume; redact PII before storing and follow GDPR/CCPA.

text: Shadow eval runs a new candidate pipeline alongside the production one without serving its answers. The judge compares both on real production traffic, giving an apples-to-apples signal at zero user risk. Architecture: a user query splits into the old pipeline whose answer is served to the user, and a new pipeline whose answer is logged but not served. Both answers feed into an LLM-judge rubric that scores them, producing an aggregate comparison. Shadow Eval Week 1 example result: judge prefers new in 62% of cases, ties in 23%, regresses in 15%. Production traffic provides the comparison; users never see the candidate answer. This is the signal that graduates the new pipeline to A/B.
summary: Shadow eval logs a candidate pipeline's answers on real traffic without serving them, then uses an LLM-judge to compare against the served pipeline at zero user risk.

text: Shadow-eval pipeline diagram. A user query at the top splits into two paths: an old pipeline on the left whose answer is served to the user, and a new pipeline on the right whose answer is logged but not served. Both answers feed downward into a central LLM-judge rubric node that scores them, producing an aggregate comparison without exposing users to the candidate pipeline.
summary: Diagram of shadow-eval flow: query fans out to old (served) and new (logged) pipelines; both answers feed into an LLM-judge.

text: Once shadow looks good, ship A/B. Split production users 50/50; sample answers from each bucket; score with the same judge rubric. Mean rubric score per bucket plus a significance test decides which wins. Four steps: 1. Bucket users 50/50 (control / treatment). 2. Sample 200 queries per day per bucket. 3. Run LLM-judge rubric on every sampled (query, answer). 4. Compute mean rubric score per bucket; check significance. Statistical significance: at N=200/day per bucket and effect size 0.1 on a 1-5 scale, expect 7-14 days to reach p < 0.05. Do not peek; pre-register the analysis window. Guardrails and auto-rollback: monitor faithfulness drop and refusal-rate spike at high frequency. Auto-rollback when any guardrail breaches threshold; don't wait for the analysis window.
summary: A/B testing for RAG: 50/50 split, sample 200 queries/day per bucket, score with judge rubric, mean + significance decides; guardrails auto-rollback on faithfulness or refusal spikes.

text: Production-grade RAG eval is a feedback cycle. Online detects regressions; offline reproduces and fixes them; shadow plus A/B verify the fix; the cycle loops. The offline tools (RAGAS, judge, golden) and the online tools (implicit, explicit, shadow, A/B) are two halves of one system. Four cycle nodes: 1. Online finds a regression - thumbs-down spike on a query type. 2. Capture to golden regression set - tie the failing case into the regression suite. 3. Offline reproduces; iterate fix - RAGAS plus judge re-score the offending case until green. 4. Shadow plus A/B verify fix in production - promote only after both confirm no regression. Two halves, one system: offline gives reproducibility; online gives reality. The loop turns every production regression into a future regression-set entry, so the same bug cannot ship twice.
summary: The offline-online feedback loop: online detects, golden set captures, offline reproduces and fixes, shadow plus A/B verify - so every regression becomes a permanent test.

text: Closing-the-loop cycle diagram with four nodes connected by clockwise arrows: 1. Online finds a regression (thumbs-down spike on a query type). 2. Capture to golden regression set (tie the failing case into the regression suite). 3. Offline reproduces; iterate fix (RAGAS plus judge re-score the offending case until green). 4. Shadow plus A/B verify fix in production (promote only after both confirm no regression). The cycle returns to online detection, illustrating the offline-online feedback loop that production-grade RAG eval relies on.
summary: Diagram of the four-node offline-online feedback cycle: online detect, capture to golden, offline reproduce, shadow + A/B verify, back to online.

text: Semantic cache decision flow diagram. Incoming query 'Reset my password?' -> embed query (1024-dim vector) -> cosine search in cache store comparing vs all cached queries -> diamond decision 'Score >= 0.93?' threshold -> YES branch returns cached answer (cost $0, no LLM call) -> NO branch runs full RAG and stores answer (cost full stack).
summary: Diagram shows the semantic cache decision flow: embed, cosine search, threshold check at 0.93, then return cached answer or fall through to full RAG.

text: Horizontal bar chart comparing per-query cost across four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked configuration is the production target and delivers the lowest cost.
summary: Bar chart of per-query cost across four cache configurations showing stacked prompt+semantic cache delivers the lowest cost at $0.003 (83% saved).

text: Caching in RAG: prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix at a 90% discount with byte-exact match (works for stable prefixes, fails when docs change per query). Semantic cache stores (query, answer) pairs and serves hits when cosine similarity to a cached query is at or above a threshold like 0.93. Operations: LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation, high thresholds and held-out monitoring to control false hits. Stacked together: 83% per-query cost savings vs no cache. Production RAG systems that skip caching leave 50%+ of cost on the table.
summary: Prompt cache discounts a stable prefix and semantic cache reuses similar prior answers; stacked they cut RAG per-query cost by ~83%.

text: Why cache in RAG. Every production RAG query burns four cost lines: embedding ($0.0001), retrieval ($0.0002), LLM input for 4k tokens ($0.012), LLM output for 500 tokens ($0.005). Total per query = $0.0173. At 1k QPS over a day this stacks to ~$1,500/day. Caching cuts the bill 50-95% on repeated traffic.
summary: Production RAG queries cost ~$0.0173 each across four cost lines and scale to $1,500/day at 1k QPS, motivating caching.

text: Prompt cache: cache the static prefix. Provider-side prompt caching (Anthropic-style) keeps the system prompt and retrieved-context prefix on the inference server. Subsequent calls reuse it at a 90% discount as long as the prefix bytes match exactly. TTL ~5 minutes. The artifact has two regions: cached prefix (system prompt + retrieved docs) at 90% discount, and fresh suffix (user question + assistant) at full price. Cost comparison: full price no cache, 4000 input tokens at $3/1M = $0.012; with prompt cache, 3800 cached at $0.30/1M + 200 fresh at $3/1M = $0.00174 = 85% saved.
summary: Prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix on the inference server at a 90% discount with byte-exact match, saving ~85% on input tokens.

text: When prompt cache applies. Prompt cache only wins when many requests share a long prefix. Cache HITS: support agent looping on a stable doc set; multi-turn conversation with same retrieved docs; RAG pipeline answering many queries with a stable system prompt. Cache MISSES: docs change per query (no shared prefix); query-time chunking (different chunks every time); cold-start every conversation.
summary: Prompt cache hits when many requests share a long stable prefix and misses when retrieved context or chunks change per query.

text: Semantic cache: cache by query similarity. A semantic cache stores past (query, answer) pairs and serves a hit when an incoming query is similar enough to one already cached. Similarity is cosine on the query embedding; threshold is the safety knob (e.g. 0.93). Flow: incoming query -> embed query to 1024-dim vector -> cosine search in cache store vs all cached queries -> if score >= 0.93 return cached answer ($0 cost, no LLM call) -> else run full RAG, store answer (full cost). Example cosine scores vs cached query 'How do I reset my password?': 'How can I reset my password?' score 0.97 HIT; 'Why is my dashboard slow?' score 0.34 MISS; 'How do I change my password?' score 0.88 BORDERLINE.
summary: Semantic cache embeds incoming queries and serves cached answers when cosine similarity to a cached query crosses the threshold, skipping the LLM call.

text: Semantic cache operations: eviction, invalidation, false-hit risk. Eviction: LRU + TTL. Cache fills up; evict oldest unused entries; TTL forces refresh of any answer older than e.g. 24 hours. Invalidation: when the corpus updates (a doc changes), every cached answer that touched that doc is stale. Strategy: tag cache entries with doc-IDs and invalidate on doc update. False-hit risk: two queries with cosine 0.95 can still want different answers. Example: 'Cancel my subscription' vs 'Cancel my trial' - similar embedding, very different policy. Tune threshold high (0.95+) for safety; monitor false-hit rate on a held-out set.
summary: Semantic cache needs LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation on corpus updates, and high thresholds plus held-out monitoring to limit false hits.

text: Combined impact: prompt cache + semantic cache compound. Per-query cost for the same RAG pipeline under four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked $0.003/query = 83% savings, compounds prompt cache with semantic cache. Apply both. Tune thresholds. Monitor false-hit rate. Most production RAG systems leave 50%+ of cost on the table by skipping caching.
summary: Stacking prompt cache (80% hit) with semantic cache (30% hit) drops per-query cost from $0.0173 to $0.003 - 83% savings.

text: Horizontal stacked bar chart of per-query cost. The bar is segmented in proportion to each line's cost share. LLM input tokens and LLM output tokens together fill about 94% of the bar, shown in orange and purple. The remaining sliver at the right contains reranker (yellow, ~4.8%), vector search (blue, ~1.0%), and embedding (cyan, ~0.5%). Per-query total $0.0208 labeled above the bar. The visual makes the dominance of LLM tokens unmistakable - the three smallest lines combined are under 6%.
summary: Stacked bar diagram showing LLM input plus output tokens occupy about 94 percent of the per-query cost bar with reranker, vector search, and embedding as a tiny right-side sliver.

text: Downward waterfall chart showing per-query cost falling as five levers are stacked. Five bars drop in height from left to right. Bar 1 (red, $0.0208) is the starting full price. Bar 2 (orange, $0.0061) after applying prompt cache at 80% hit rate. Bar 3 (yellow, $0.0043) after applying semantic cache at 30% hit. Bar 4 (blue, $0.0028) after reducing top-K from 20 to 5. Bar 5 (green, $0.0012) after switching the LLM to Haiku. Each bar is labeled with its new cost and the percent change at that step. The visual makes the compounding effect concrete - one tenth of a cent per query from a starting two cents.
summary: Waterfall diagram showing per-query cost dropping in five steps from $0.0208 to $0.0012 as prompt cache, semantic cache, top-K reduction, and the Haiku swap are stacked.

text: Scatter plot of cost per query (x-axis, $0.001 to $0.025) versus faithfulness score (y-axis, 0.5 to 1.0). Six points labeled A through F mark RAG configurations. A is top-right (high cost, high quality), E is bottom-left (low cost, lower quality). A dashed Pareto frontier line passes through A, B, C, D, E - the cost-efficient configurations. Point F sits below the frontier, dominated: more expensive than nearby frontier points yet with strictly lower faithfulness. The visual teaches the rule: pick a frontier point that meets your minimum quality, and never pick a dominated point like F.
summary: Scatter diagram of six RAG configurations on cost vs faithfulness axes with a dashed Pareto frontier through A-B-C-D-E and F shown as a dominated off-frontier point to avoid.

text: A production RAG query has five cost lines summing to about $0.0208, of which LLM input plus output tokens are ~94% - so optimization must start with tokens. At 1k QPS that per-query cost balloons to roughly $16.2M per month, where a 10% cut saves $1.6M monthly. Five levers reduce cost: smaller embedding (tiny impact), fewer chunks retrieved, smaller LLM (Sonnet to Haiku), prompt cache, and semantic cache. Apply free-quality levers first (caches, fewer chunks), then accept the quality tradeoff of a smaller LLM. Stacking all five drops cost to $0.0012 per query - a 94% reduction worth ~$1.69M per day saved at 1k QPS. Finally, configurations lie on a cost-vs-faithfulness Pareto frontier; choose the cheapest point meeting your minimum quality and avoid dominated picks.
summary: RAG cost is dominated by LLM tokens and scales to millions monthly at production traffic; five compounding levers cut per-query cost by up to 94 percent, and the right config is the cheapest point on the cost-faithfulness Pareto frontier.

text: A production RAG query bill is the sum of five cost lines, listed in order of typical magnitude. LLM Output Tokens cost about $15 per 1M tokens; 500 output tokens with Claude Sonnet typical works out to $0.0075 per query. LLM Input Tokens cost about $3 per 1M; 4000 input tokens (system + retrieved + question) is $0.0120 per query. The Reranker (optional cross-encoder) runs ~$0.001 per query. Vector Search through a managed service is ~$0.0002 per query. Embedding with a modern 1024-dim provider is ~$0.0001 per query. Total Per Query = $0.0208. LLM tokens dominate because both input and output costs scale with token count and per-token prices are orders of magnitude higher than embedding or vector-search ops.
summary: Production RAG query cost decomposes into five lines: LLM output, LLM input, reranker, vector search, and embedding, summing to about $0.0208 per query with LLM tokens dominating.

text: One horizontal bar visualizes the five cost lines proportionally. LLM input plus LLM output together make up about 94% of the per-query bill. The remaining ~6% is split across reranker, vector search, and embedding - the three thin slivers at the right of the bar. Reranker is roughly 4.8%, vector search ~1.0%, and embedding ~0.5%. The takeaway: optimize LLM tokens first. Embedding and vector search are essentially free relative to inference, so chasing a cheaper embedding model saves cents per million queries while shaving one output token per query saves real money.
summary: Visualizing per-query cost as a stacked bar makes it obvious that LLM input + output tokens are about 94 percent of the bill, so optimization must start there.

text: Per-query cost looks small at $0.0208 but scales brutally with traffic. At 1,000 QPS: $20.80 per second, $1,248 per minute, $74,880 per hour, and $1,797,120 per day at flat 1k QPS. A more realistic daily figure assuming 1k peak QPS with 30% average load is $539,136 per day. Monthly at this scale is about $16.2M. At these numbers, a 10% cost reduction saves $1.6M per month - which is why every percentage point of optimization matters in production.
summary: Scaling $0.0208 per query to 1k QPS yields about $539k per day and $16.2M per month, so even a 10 percent saving returns $1.6M monthly.

text: Five real levers cut RAG cost, in order of compounding return. (1) Smaller Embedding: 1024 -> 384 dim with Matryoshka, -60% storage, no quality drop, but embedding is under 1% of bill so impact is small. (2) Fewer Chunks Retrieved: top-20 -> top-5 to the LLM, -75% input tokens, ~44% of total bill. (3) Smaller LLM: Sonnet -> Haiku, ~10x cheaper per token at ~80% quality on most RAG tasks, ~80% of LLM bill. (4) Prompt Cache: 80% prefix hit rate puts cached prefix tokens at 90% discount, ~74% reduction on cached prefix tokens. (5) Semantic Cache: 30% hit rate on hot queries skips the LLM entirely on hits, ~30% reduction on affected queries. Apply order: cache first (free quality), reduce chunks (free quality), then downsize LLM (quality tradeoff).
summary: Five levers compound to cut RAG cost - smaller embedding, fewer chunks, smaller LLM, prompt cache, semantic cache - with caches and chunk reduction being free quality and LLM downsize the only real tradeoff.

text: Stacking all five levers in sequence compounds savings. Start at $0.0208 per query (full price). Apply prompt cache at 80% hit rate: $0.0061, a 71% drop on input tokens. Apply semantic cache at 30% hit rate: $0.0043, an additional 30% blended reduction. Reduce top-K from 20 to 5: $0.0028, a 35% drop on remaining input. Switch LLM from Sonnet to Haiku: $0.0012, a 57% drop on the LLM bill. Final cost is $0.0012 per query, a 94% total reduction. At 1k QPS that saves approximately $1.69M per day versus running with no levers.
summary: Compounding prompt cache, semantic cache, top-K reduction, and Haiku swap takes per-query cost from $0.0208 to $0.0012 - a 94 percent cut saving about $1.69M per day at 1k QPS.

text: Cost is not the only target - faithfulness matters. Six configurations plotted on cost-vs-faithfulness: A: Full Sonnet + No Cache + Top-20, $0.0208, Q 0.92. B: Sonnet + Prompt Cache + Top-10, $0.0048, Q 0.91. C: Sonnet + Both Caches + Top-10, $0.0029, Q 0.90. D: Haiku + Both Caches + Top-10, $0.0012, Q 0.86. E: Haiku + Both Caches + Top-5, $0.0008, Q 0.81. F: Haiku, No Cache, Top-3, $0.0007, Q 0.74 - off frontier. The Pareto frontier connects A-B-C-D-E. F is dominated: it costs more than nothing extra but delivers strictly less quality than E, so never pick it. Pick the frontier point matching your minimum acceptable faithfulness.
summary: Plotting six RAG configurations on cost vs faithfulness reveals a Pareto frontier; pick the cheapest point that meets your quality floor and never pick a dominated configuration like F.

text: Observability and tracing for production RAG. A RAG pipeline is opaque without per-stage traces; four recurring failure modes (mystery slowness, silent quality drop, cost blowup, hallucination hunt) all require span-level instrumentation. The canonical trace is a root Query span with five child spans (Embed, Vector Search, Rerank, Pack, Generate), each carrying a defined attribute set (doc-IDs, scores, token counts, model versions, latency). Tooling choices in 2025 include LangSmith, Helicone, OpenTelemetry, Arize Phoenix, and native provider dashboards. Privacy rules require hashing queries, logging IDs not chunk text, and redacting PII. A production dashboard surfaces P50/P99 latency, faithfulness, cost per query, the slowest trace, and signal-level facts (slowest stage, top doc, model version).
summary: End-to-end overview of RAG observability: why to trace, span anatomy, per-stage attributes, tooling, privacy rules, and dashboard design.

text: A RAG system is opaque without traces. Four failure modes recur in production and every one needs per-stage instrumentation to diagnose. Mystery Slowness: a user reports the system is slow; without per-stage latency you cannot tell if retrieval or the LLM is the bottleneck. Silent Quality Drop: faithfulness drops 5% week-over-week; with no doc-IDs logged you cannot see which docs were retrieved. Cost Blowup: bill doubles; without per-query token counts you cannot tell which queries pulled in too much context. Hallucination Hunt: a user flags a bad answer; without a logged model version you cannot reproduce the failure.
summary: Four production failure modes (mystery slowness, silent quality drop, cost blowup, hallucination hunt) all require per-stage tracing to diagnose.

text: Canonical RAG trace span tree: one root span 'Query' with total latency 180ms, plus five child spans drawn with widths proportional to latency. Embed Query: 12ms, model = bge-large-v1.5, dim = 1024. Vector Search: 28ms, top-k = 20, index = hnsw, metric = cosine. Rerank: 45ms, model = bge-reranker-v2, input = 20, output = 5. Pack Prompt: 3ms, chunks packed = 5, total tokens = 4128. Generate: 92ms, model = Sonnet 4.7, out tokens = 480, finish = stop. Rerank dominates wall time among retrieval stages; optimize there first by reducing input top-k or picking a faster reranker. Generate is the single largest stage overall.
summary: Anatomy of a RAG trace: one root span and five child spans (embed, vector search, rerank, pack, generate) with concrete per-stage latencies and attributes.

text: Every span carries attributes; the minimum a production RAG trace must emit, by stage: Embed - query_text (hashed), embedding_model_version, dim, latency_ms. Vector Search - top_k_requested, index_name, distance_metric, retrieved_doc_ids, retrieval_scores, latency_ms. Rerank - reranker_model_version, input_count, output_count, reranker_scores, latency_ms. Pack - chunk_count_packed, total_prompt_tokens, citation_count, latency_ms. Generate - llm_model_version, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, finish_reason, latency_ms. Variable identifiers stay lowercase snake_case per code convention.
summary: Minimum attributes every span must emit, listed per stage (embed, vector search, rerank, pack, generate).

text: Five common tools in 2025 for capturing RAG traces, ranging from framework-native to vendor-neutral. LangSmith: LangChain's official tracer; strong for LangChain users; tight integration with the LangChain stack. Helicone: provider-agnostic; logs every LLM call with cost and latency dashboards; lightweight setup. OpenTelemetry: open standard; vendor-neutral spans; export to Datadog, Grafana, Jaeger, or custom backends. Arize Phoenix: open-source; built for LLM eval plus trace; strong dashboard for retrieval quality. Native provider tooling: Anthropic Console, OpenAI Dashboard; limited but free; first-party cost tracking.
summary: Five tool options for RAG tracing (LangSmith, Helicone, OpenTelemetry, Arize Phoenix, native provider dashboards) and their tradeoffs.

text: Privacy rules for RAG logs. Regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2) and contracts may forbid storing user query content in plain text. Use one-way hashes for queries, log doc-IDs not chunk text, and redact PII upstream. Log freely: doc-IDs, retrieval scores, latency per stage, model version, token counts, embedding-model version, query length, anonymized user ID, response status code. Do not log in plain text: raw query text (hash it), raw retrieved chunks (log only IDs), PII inside the query, user account IDs (use opaque session IDs), API keys, prompt template contents that include secrets.
summary: Privacy guidelines for RAG traces - safe fields to log vs fields that must be hashed, redacted, or omitted.

text: What a production RAG ops dashboard looks like. Four headline tiles: P50 Latency 180ms, P99 Latency 620ms, Faithfulness 0.91, Cost Per Query $0.012. Slowest trace last hour at 620ms shown as a horizontal stacked bar broken into the five spans (embed 12ms, vector search 28ms, rerank 45ms, pack 3ms, generate 92ms). Three signal-level facts in a footer: slowest stage = Rerank (45ms P50); top retrieved doc = Doc-7 (Password Reset); model version = Sonnet 4.7. A trace is the single source of truth when a user complains - every stage, every score, every model version.
summary: Concrete mock of a production RAG dashboard with P50/P99 latency, faithfulness, cost-per-query tiles, slowest-trace span thumbnail, and signal-level footer facts.

text: Faithfulness trend thumbnail panel for the dashboard mock: a small green line chart over 30 days showing a steady decline from 0.92 down to 0.84, crossing the 0.85 alert threshold which is drawn as a horizontal dashed line labeled '0.85'.
summary: Thumbnail chart of faithfulness declining from 0.92 to 0.84 and crossing the 0.85 alert threshold.

text: Refusal rate trend thumbnail panel for the dashboard mock: a small orange line chart over 30 days showing refusal rate rising from 8% to 18%, crossing the 15% alert threshold drawn as a horizontal dashed line labeled '15%'.
summary: Thumbnail chart of refusal rate rising from 8% to 18% and crossing the 15% alert threshold.

text: This chapter shows how to catch hallucinations and drift in production RAG before users complain. Four hallucination signals - faithfulness score, citation coverage, refusal rate, and out-of-index mentions - are wired into the trace with rolling-median alerts. Four drift types - data, embedding, eval, and distribution - each have a distinct detection mechanism (re-index timestamp checksum, embedding_model_version logging, locked judge version, query embedding cluster monitoring). A five-step per-generation pipeline extracts atomic claims, checks each against retrieved chunks with LLM-as-judge, computes faithfulness as supported_claims / total_claims, logs to the trace, and alerts when the rolling median drops below 0.85. Time-series charts plot multiple metrics with dashed thresholds and alert markers on the crossing day. Alerts must carry failing queries, scores, unsupported claims, and a recommended action. A unified dashboard pulls trend thumbnails, drift-type status badges, and the worst recent queries into one panel for the on-call engineer.
summary: Chapter overview of hallucination signals, four drift types, per-generation detection pipeline, alert payload, and unified monitoring dashboard for production RAG.

text: Four hallucination signals catch most failures in production RAG without manual review. Wire each into the trace, alert on rolling medians, and investigate spikes inside the same shift. Signal 1 Faithfulness Score: per-generation faithfulness from LLM-as-judge; alert if rolling 7-day median drops below 0.85. Signal 2 Citation Coverage: percent of claims with a [doc-X] citation; alert if drops below 80%. Signal 3 Refusal Rate: percent of queries refused with 'I don't know'; alert on spike (too cautious) or dip (hallucinating when context was sufficient). Signal 4 Out-Of-Index Mentions: regex or LLM check for entities mentioned that do not appear in any retrieved doc - hard signal of hallucination.
summary: Four production signals - faithfulness, citation coverage, refusal rate, out-of-index mentions - catch most hallucinations without manual review.

text: Drift is the slow leak before the flood. Four kinds of drift afflict production RAG, each with a different detection mechanism and fix. Data Drift: corpus changes (new docs added, old removed); embedding index becomes stale relative to current corpus; detect with re-index timestamp vs current doc set checksum. Embedding Drift: you re-embed with a new model; old and new vectors are not directly comparable; mixed-version retrievals pollute top-K; detect by logging embedding_model_version per query and alert on mixed-version retrievals. Eval Drift: the LLM-as-judge model is upgraded; faithfulness scores from old judge vs new judge are not directly comparable; lock judge version and rerun calibration on judge change. Distribution Drift: user query distribution shifts (new product launched, new failure mode); old query embeddings cluster differently; detect via cluster monitoring on query embeddings over time.
summary: Production RAG suffers four drift types - data, embedding, eval, distribution - each with distinct detection mechanisms and fixes.

text: Hallucination Detection Pipeline runs per-generation in 5 steps. Input: user query + retrieved chunks + generated answer. Step 1 Extract Claims: LLM segments the answer into atomic claims. Step 2 Check Each Claim Against Retrieved Chunks: LLM-as-judge labels each as supported or not. Step 3 Compute Faithfulness Score: supported_claims / total_claims. Step 4 Log Score + Doc-IDs + Unsupported Claims: emit to trace. Step 5 Alert If Rolling Median < 0.85: page owner on drop. Worked example: 5 claims, 4 supported, 1 hallucinated -> faithfulness = 4/5 = 0.80 -> below 0.85 threshold, ALERT.
summary: A five-step per-generation pipeline extracts claims, checks each against retrieved chunks, computes faithfulness, logs to trace, and alerts on rolling-median drop.

text: Pipeline flow diagram: a pink input bar reads 'Input: User Query + Retrieved Chunks + Generated Answer', followed by five stacked pipeline boxes connected by downward arrows. Step 1 Extract Claims (LLM segments answer into atomic claims). Step 2 Check Each Claim Against Retrieved Chunks (LLM-as-judge: is claim supported?). Step 3 Compute Faithfulness Score (supported_claims / total_claims). Step 4 Log Score + Doc-IDs + Unsupported Claims (emit to trace). Step 5 Alert If Rolling Median < 0.85 (page owner on drop).
summary: Diagram of the per-generation hallucination detection pipeline showing input plus five sequential steps from claim extraction to alerting.

text: Three drift signals plotted over 30 days. Faithfulness trends down from 0.92 to 0.84, crossing the 0.85 alert threshold on day 28. Recall on golden set trends down from 0.88 to 0.79, crossing the 0.82 alert threshold on day 22. Refusal rate rises from 8% to 18%, crossing the 15% alert threshold on day 25. Each line uses a distinct color with its threshold shown as a dashed line and a red alert marker at the crossing day. Without the chart you would have noticed only the symptoms - a flood of unhappy users - 2 to 3 weeks late. Three different metrics, three different alert days, each catching a different failure mode early.
summary: A 30-day chart of three RAG metrics showing each crosses its alert threshold on a different day, catching distinct failure modes early.

text: Time-series chart over 30 days with x-axis labeled Day 1 to Day 30 and y-axis 'Metric Value'. Three colored lines: green Faithfulness declining toward 0.85 threshold (dashed), cyan Recall On Golden Set declining toward 0.82 threshold, orange Refusal Rate (%) climbing toward 15%. Each threshold shown as a dashed line in matching color. Red filled circle alert markers sit at the crossing point of each line with its threshold, annotated 'Alert Day 22', 'Alert Day 25', 'Alert Day 28'.
summary: Diagram of a 30-day multi-line chart with three RAG drift metrics, dashed alert thresholds, and red markers at each crossing day.

text: An alert without examples is a blank page at 3am. Always include the failing queries, the failing scores, and a recommended action so the on-call engineer can start fixing immediately. Example alert payload: ALERT: rag.faithfulness.drop. Severity: WARNING. Trigger: 7-day median faithfulness dropped below 0.85 (currently 0.82). First crossed: 2026-05-14 03:21 UTC. Affected: production - customer support pipeline. Recent examples last 24h: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' faithfulness 0.60, cited docs doc-1 and doc-3, unsupported claim 'Support team will email a reset link.' Query 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund' faithfulness 0.50, cited docs doc-4, unsupported claim 'Refunds are issued within 24 hours.' Recommended action: review recent corpus updates and re-run golden eval.
summary: An effective hallucination alert includes severity, trigger, failing queries with scores, unsupported claims, cited docs, and a recommended action.

text: Pull all signals into one panel - the dashboard the on-call engineer should see first. Layout: top-left Faithfulness Trend (30 days) thumbnail showing decline from 0.92 to 0.84 with the 0.85 threshold dashed; top-right Refusal Rate Over Time thumbnail rising from 8% to 18% with the 15% threshold dashed; bottom-left Drift Types Triggered with status badges - Data OK, Embedding WARN, Eval OK, Distribution ALERT; bottom-right Top Hallucinated Queries Last 24h listing 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' 0.60, 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund' 0.50, 'Why is my dashboard slow with 500 errors?' 0.68. Hallucination detection plus drift monitoring catches what users would not bother to report - the slow leak before the flood.
summary: A unified panel combines faithfulness and refusal trends, per-drift-type status badges, and worst recent queries so on-call can see all hallucination signals at once.

text: This chapter walks through how to choose a RAG framework in 2025. Six options are surveyed: No Framework (raw SDK + simple orchestration), LlamaIndex, LangChain, LangGraph, Haystack, and Vendor SDK (OpenAI Agents, Anthropic native tool-use). A decision matrix scores them on Lock-In Risk, API Churn, Complexity, Community Size, and RAG-Specific Fit. An honest take on LangChain shows pros (largest ecosystem, fastest prototyping, integrations everywhere) against cons (hidden abstractions, deprecated chains, API churn, lock-in).
