A fit table maps each framework to a team and product profile. A three-question decision tree (Is RAG the primary feature? Mid-sized team? Multi-step agent? Want explicit state? Single-provider OK?) yields one of six recommendations. Finally, the framework-agnostic core: chunking, embedding choice, hybrid + reranker cascade, prompt template + context packing, eval, and tracing are decisions independent of any framework, so production RAG code should be thin enough that a framework swap is a 2-day job, not a 2-month rewrite.
summary: Survey of six RAG framework options, an honest LangChain critique, a decision matrix and tree to pick one, and the framework-agnostic core decisions that matter more than the choice.

text: Six common framework choices for RAG in 2025. No Framework: raw SDK (Anthropic / OpenAI / Cohere) plus simple Python or TypeScript orchestration; most popular for new production RAG in 2025. LlamaIndex: RAG-focused framework with good defaults for retrieval, less abstracted than LangChain. LangChain: the original, large ecosystem, abstractions evolving with some deprecated within the framework itself. LangGraph: graph-based agent orchestration from the LangChain team, newer, explicit state machine. Haystack: enterprise-leaning, modular pipelines, smaller community than LangChain. Vendor SDK: OpenAI Agents, Anthropic native tool-use, tight integration but vendor lock-in. Brand names preserve official capitalization (LlamaIndex, LangChain).
summary: Introduces the six common 2025 RAG framework options and a one-line description of each.

text: Decision matrix scoring six frameworks across five criteria. Lock-In Risk: No Framework=None, LlamaIndex=Low, LangChain=Medium, LangGraph=Medium, Haystack=Low, Vendor SDK=High. API Churn: No Framework=None, LlamaIndex=Low, LangChain=High, LangGraph=Medium, Haystack=Low, Vendor SDK=Low. Complexity (build vs hidden): No Framework=High Build / Low Hidden, LlamaIndex=Low Build / Medium Hidden, LangChain=Low Build / High Hidden, LangGraph=Medium Build / Medium Hidden, Haystack=Medium Build / Medium Hidden, Vendor SDK=Low Build / High Hidden. Community Size: No Framework=Huge, LlamaIndex=Large, LangChain=Huge, LangGraph=Growing, Haystack=Medium, Vendor SDK=Vendor-Specific. RAG-Specific Fit: No Framework=You Build, LlamaIndex=Strong, LangChain=OK, LangGraph=OK, Haystack=Strong, Vendor SDK=OK.
summary: A matrix rating each of the six frameworks on lock-in, API churn, build vs hidden complexity, community size, and RAG fit.

text: Honest take on LangChain. Pros: largest ecosystem; biggest community; tutorials everywhere; prototype in 30 minutes; integrations for every vector DB, embedding model, and LLM. Cons: abstractions hide what is actually happening (lost-in-middle invisible until you trace); some abstractions deprecated within the framework itself (Stuff / MapReduce / Refine, original AgentExecutor); fast-moving API churn requires upgrade cycles; framework lock-in concerns once production code depends on chains. LangChain is still actively developed and dominant for prototyping. For production-grade RAG, many teams move to raw SDK plus LlamaIndex retriever, or stay on LangChain but carefully avoid deprecating abstractions.
summary: Balanced view of LangChain showing its prototyping strengths against hidden abstractions, deprecated chains, API churn, and lock-in risk.

text: When each framework fits. No Framework: small team, full control needed, production-grade RAG with custom orchestration; fits 2-3 engineers building a single product. LlamaIndex: RAG-heavy product, want good retrieval defaults, accept some framework dependency; fits teams shipping a RAG-centric app. LangChain: early prototyping, large team needs shared vocabulary, ecosystem integrations matter more than minimalism; fits teams that staff for framework upgrades. LangGraph: explicit agent state machine, multi-step workflows, less hidden magic than LangChain; fits teams building structured agents. Haystack: enterprise / regulated environment, modular pipelines, smaller-community tradeoff is acceptable; fits orgs with security review on every dependency. Vendor SDK: committed to one provider, lock-in is acceptable, tight integration is the priority; fits internal Anthropic / OpenAI shops.
summary: Maps each of the six frameworks to the team size and product profile where it fits best.

text: Decision tree with three questions and six leaves. Root: Is RAG the primary product feature? YES branches left to: Mid-sized team with full control (3+ engineers)? YES leaf = No Framework + LlamaIndex Retriever; NO leaf = LlamaIndex. NO branches right to: Is it a multi-step agent (tool use, loops, workflows)? YES branches to: Want explicit state? YES leaf = LangGraph; NO leaf = LangChain. NO branches to: Single-provider OK? YES leaf = Vendor SDK; NO leaf = No Framework. Three questions, six leaves, one choice. Anything more complex than this is over-thinking the framework decision.
summary: A three-question decision tree that maps RAG primacy, team size, agent complexity, and provider lock-in tolerance to one of six framework recommendations.

text: What stays the same regardless of framework. Every framework wraps the same six core decisions: 1) Chunking strategy is a data-pipeline decision, not a framework decision. 2) Embedding model choice is a data-pipeline decision. 3) Hybrid plus reranker cascade is a retrieval-quality decision. 4) Prompt template plus context packing is a generation decision. 5) Eval is a measurement decision. 6) Tracing is an ops decision. Frameworks are wrappers. Get these decisions right; the framework is replaceable. Production RAG code should be thin enough that a framework swap is a 2-day job, not a 2-month rewrite.
summary: The six framework-agnostic decisions (chunking, embeddings, hybrid+reranker, prompt packing, eval, tracing) that matter more than the framework choice.

text: Diagram: a nine-row decision tree spanning every act of the RAG-in-production section. Each row is a colored horizontal bar containing a numbered decision label on the left and the originating chapter references on the right, connected top to bottom by purple arrows. The rows from top to bottom are colored to match their domain: red for 'Should I use RAG at all?' (19.1 + 22.9), cyan for 'How do I chunk?' (20.1 - 20.10), purple for 'Which embedding + how do I index?' (21.1, 21.2, 21.3, 21.4), orange for 'Do I transform the query?' (21.5 - 21.8), yellow for 'How do I pack context + generate?' (22.1, 22.2, 22.3), blue for 'Do I need advanced retrieval?' (22.4 - 22.9), green for 'How do I evaluate?' (23.1 - 23.5), pink for 'How do I run production?' (23.6 - 23.9), and purple for 'Which framework?' (23.10).
summary: Diagram showing the nine-row colored decision tree linking each RAG production decision to the chapters that taught it.

text: This chapter is the capstone for the RAG in Production track. It assembles a nine-decision framework that covers every act of the section - whether to use RAG, chunking, embedding and index, query transformation, context packing and generation, advanced retrieval patterns, evaluation, production operations, and framework choice - and then walks the entire framework end to end for a new domain: Q&A over 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions. The capstone use case has a quality bar of partner-lawyer rating >= 4/5, latency budget 5-10 seconds, cost budget $0.05/query, mandatory citations, and a mandatory jurisdiction filter. Decisions made: hierarchical + contextual chunking; domain-adapted embedding on legal-BERT with HNSW + BM25 hybrid retrieval, jurisdiction filter, and cross-encoder rerank; multi-query expansion and decomposition (HyDE skipped); 16-30k context with sandwich packing, mandatory citations, and refusal; GraphRAG for citation graph plus multi-hop retrieval (long-context skipped); LLM-as-judge with legal rubric, 500-pair partner-curated golden set, RAGAS faithfulness and citation precision, online A/B; semantic cache disabled, prompt cache enabled, full tracing, hallucination detection, drift monitoring; framework choice is No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever + raw Anthropic SDK. Final stack achieves ~$0.04/query, ~6s latency, and full citation audit.
summary: Capstone chapter applies the nine-decision RAG framework end to end to design a production legal-case-law Q&A system meeting its quality, latency, cost, and audit budgets.

text: The complete RAG decision framework is presented as nine ordered rows, each a decision tied back to the chapters that taught it. The nine rows in order are: 1) Should I use RAG at all? (chapters 19.1 and 22.9). 2) How do I chunk? (chapters 20.1 through 20.10). 3) Which embedding and how do I index? (chapters 21.1, 21.2, 21.3, 21.4). 4) Do I transform the query? (chapters 21.5 through 21.8). 5) How do I pack context and generate? (chapters 22.1, 22.2, 22.3). 6) Do I need advanced retrieval? (chapters 22.4 through 22.9). 7) How do I evaluate? (chapters 23.1 through 23.5). 8) How do I run production? (chapters 23.6 through 23.9). 9) Which framework? (this chapter). Every production RAG decision lives on this map. The chapter then walks the framework top to bottom for one new use case.
summary: Introduces the nine-row decision framework that organizes every production RAG choice and maps each row to its teaching chapter.

text: Capstone use case: Q&A over case law for a legal research firm, a fully different domain from a customer support corpus. Design brief: Corpus is 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions. Metadata includes jurisdiction tag, year, and citation tree. Quality bar is partner-lawyer rating >= 4/5 on a legal research rubric. Latency budget is 5-10 seconds (legal research is patient). Cost budget is $0.05 per query. Audit requirements: every answer cited, jurisdiction filter mandatory. Example queries: 'What's the precedent for tortious interference in California state court?', 'Cases citing Smith v Jones about negligence in medical contexts', and 'How has the standard for qualified immunity evolved 2010-2025?'. The chapter walks every decision in the framework for this brief; each choice points back to the chapter that taught it.
summary: Sets up the capstone brief - legal Q&A over 200k court cases with explicit quality, latency, cost, and audit requirements.

text: Decision 1 - Chunking strategy. Choice: hierarchical chunking plus contextual retrieval. Per-paragraph chunks with parent (case-level) metadata and an LLM-injected 'This paragraph is from case X about Y' preamble. Why: case opinions have natural hierarchy (intro, facts, reasoning, holding). Hierarchical preserves parent-child relationships. Contextual retrieval injects the per-chunk preamble that disambiguates citation-level retrieval. Plain fixed-size chunking would mid-sentence-split a court's analysis. Tradeoff: more storage because each chunk now carries parent metadata; higher index build cost because contextual augmentation is an LLM call per chunk. Worth it because case-law precision demands citation-level retrieval.
summary: Picks hierarchical plus contextual chunking for case opinions to preserve structure and disambiguate retrieval, accepting higher storage and indexing cost.

text: Decision 2 - Embedding and index. Choice: domain-adapted embedding on top of a base legal-BERT or Cohere Embed Multilingual. HNSW index with hybrid (BM25 + dense). Jurisdiction stored as filter metadata. Cross-encoder cascade rerank. Why: general-purpose embeddings underweight legal jargon such as 'tortious' and 'qualified immunity'. Hybrid catches both exact-citation lookups ('Smith v Jones') and semantic queries ('medical negligence cases'). Jurisdiction filter eliminates 96% of irrelevant cases before reranking. Cross-encoder lifts precision on legal-language matching. Tradeoff: domain adaptation requires labeled training pairs (expensive but one-time). Cross-encoder adds 50-100ms per query, which the latency budget allows.
summary: Selects domain-adapted legal embeddings with HNSW hybrid retrieval, jurisdiction filter, and cross-encoder rerank, trading training cost and 50-100ms latency for legal precision.

text: Decision 3 - Query transformation. Choice: multi-query expansion plus decomposition for complex multi-hop queries. HyDE is SKIPPED. Why: a query like 'cases citing X about Y' naturally decomposes into 'find X' then 'find cases citing X' then 'filter for cases about Y'. Multi-query rewriting using legal synonyms (e.g., 'negligence' / 'duty of care' / 'breach of duty') improves recall on lexically narrow legal language. HyDE is skipped because case law is precise and fake documents hurt more than they help. Tradeoff: more retrievals per query (3-5x), so cost goes up linearly. Mitigated by aggressive deduplication after RRF merge.
summary: Uses multi-query expansion and decomposition for legal queries while skipping HyDE, paying 3-5x retrieval cost but cutting it with deduplication after RRF.

text: Decision 4 - Context packing and generation. Choice: high token budget (16-30k tokens of retrieved context per query). Relevance-first ordering with sandwich pattern to fight lost-in-middle. Mandatory inline citations via prompt template. I-don't-know clause if no jurisdiction-matching cases. Why: legal work needs many cases to reason across, so token budget is high because cost budget is high. Sandwich (place top hits at start AND end) anchors model attention. Citations are mandatory for audit. Refusal preferable to fabrication. Tradeoff: higher per-query LLM cost, accepted given the $0.05 budget. Capstone prompt template: 'System: You are a legal research assistant. Cite every case you reference using [Case Name, Year] format. Only use the provided cases. If no provided case answers, say "I don't have case law for this question in the provided jurisdiction". Provided Cases: [Case A] {summary + key holdings} ... [Case J] {summary + key holdings}. Question: {user question}. Jurisdiction: {jurisdiction}. Answer:'.
summary: Picks 16-30k context with sandwich ordering, mandatory citations, and an I-don't-know clause, plus shows the full capstone prompt template.

text: Decision 5 - Advanced retrieval patterns. Choice: GraphRAG for the case-citation graph (cases citing X / cases cited by X). Multi-hop retrieval for 'cases citing X about Y' decomposition. Long-context is SKIPPED. Why: legal citation is a graph; GraphRAG indexes the relationships. Without it, 'cases citing X' requires querying every case in the corpus; with it, you follow the citation edges. Multi-hop chains the steps for complex precedent queries. Long-context is skipped because 200k cases is far larger than any context window, so RAG is mandatory. Tradeoff: GraphRAG indexing is 10x slower than vector-only, and re-indexing the citation graph on corpus update is a nightly batch. Multi-hop is slower per query, but precision gain on multi-hop queries is large.
summary: Adds GraphRAG and multi-hop retrieval for the citation graph while skipping long-context, accepting 10x slower indexing and a nightly graph rebuild.

text: Decision 6 - Evaluation. Choice: LLM-as-judge with a legal-specific rubric. Golden dataset of 500 partner-curated query/expected-answer pairs. RAGAS metrics: faithfulness, citation precision, jurisdiction match. Online A/B on partner-rated 4/5 score. Why: general-purpose RAG eval misses legal correctness. Partner-curated golden set is the ground truth because no automated metric reaches partner-quality. RAGAS faithfulness catches hallucinations. Citation precision (% of cited cases that are real and on-point) is critical. Tradeoff: golden dataset is expensive to build in partner hours. 500 examples covers ~80% of the query distribution; the remaining 20% is caught by online feedback. Rerun golden eval weekly on prod.
summary: Combines LLM-as-judge with a legal rubric, a 500-pair partner golden set, RAGAS metrics, and online A/B, rerunning golden eval weekly.

text: Decision 7 - Production operations. Choice: semantic cache DISABLED. Prompt cache ENABLED for system prompt + few-shot examples. Full tracing per query. Hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process. Drift monitoring on case-law updates. Why: semantic cache fails when 'negligence in CA' and 'negligence in NY' have cosine similarity ~0.96 but the answers differ entirely. Prompt cache is safe because system + few-shot are constant. Tracing every query is non-negotiable for audit. Tradeoff: no semantic cache means higher per-query cost, mitigated by the prompt cache. Drift monitoring catches when judicial reasoning shifts (rare but high-impact).
summary: Disables semantic cache, enables prompt cache, requires full tracing, hallucination post-checks, and drift monitoring to balance audit needs and cost.

text: Putting it all together - the capstone stack has five layers. Layer 1 Ingest: hierarchical + contextual chunking; domain-adapted embedding; HNSW + BM25 hybrid index; jurisdiction metadata; case-citation graph index. Layer 2 Retrieve: multi-query expansion; jurisdiction filter; hybrid retrieval; cross-encoder rerank; GraphRAG for citation queries. Layer 3 Generate: 16-30k token budget; sandwich ordering; mandatory citations; I-don't-know refusal if no jurisdiction-matching cases. Layer 4 Eval: partner-curated golden set (500 pairs); LLM-as-judge with legal rubric; RAGAS faithfulness; online A/B. Layer 5 Ops: prompt cache (semantic cache disabled); full tracing per query; hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process; drift monitoring on case-law updates. Framework choice: No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever for the RAG core, raw Anthropic SDK for generation. Result: cost ~$0.04/query (under budget), latency ~6 seconds, every answer carries [Case Name, Year] citations. The closing message: you now have everything to design production-grade RAG for any new domain - apply the framework, adjust per use case, and build.
summary: Pulls the seven decisions into a five-layer stack (Ingest, Retrieve, Generate, Eval, Ops) hitting ~$0.04/query and ~6s latency using No Framework + LlamaIndex + Anthropic SDK.

text: This chapter Anatomy of an LLM Call. Breaks one LLM call into its parts: (1) Messages list with System/User/Assistant roles, sent in full each turn. (2) Tokens are the real unit - sub-word chunks billed and counted against the context window (e.g. 200k tokens ~ 150k words). (3) Sampling knobs - temperature, top-p (nucleus), top-k - shape the next-token probability distribution. (4) Generation ends on one of four stop conditions producing finish_reason values: length, stop_sequence, end_turn, tool_use. (5) Response object returns content, stop_reason, and usage with input_tokens + output_tokens. (6) The complete six-step cycle: build messages, set parameters, send request, stream tokens, reach stop reason, return final response.
summary: Chapter-level overview of all parts that make up a single LLM API call: messages, tokens, sampling, stops, response, and cycle.

text: Every LLM call is an ordered list of messages, each tagged with a role. You don't 'chat' with the model - you send the whole list and the model writes the next assistant turn. Three roles: System sets the role, rules, and tone of the assistant (e.g. 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant. Be concise. Cite docs when possible.'). User is the end-user's turn, the thing the model has to respond to (e.g. 'I can't log in - reset my password for alice@example.com.'). Assistant is the model's reply that goes back into the messages list for the next turn (e.g. 'I'll help you reset it. I just sent a one-time link to alice@example.com.'). Every later request includes the prior assistant replies, so the model always sees the full conversation.
summary: Introduces the three message roles (System, User, Assistant) that make up every LLM API call.

text: The model doesn't see characters or words - it sees tokens, sub-word chunks from the tokenizer. Both input and output are billed and limited in tokens. User message 'I can't log in - reset my password' is 9 tokens: I, can, 't, ' log', ' in', ' -', ' reset', ' my', ' password'. Assistant reply 'I'll help you reset it.' is 7 tokens: I, 'll, ' help', ' you', ' reset', ' it', '.'. Context window = total tokens across all messages (system + user + assistant history + new reply). A 200k context window holds roughly 150k English words. Cross it and the oldest messages get dropped or the call fails.
summary: Explains tokens as the unit of input/output and how the context window limits the total tokens per call.

text:
Three sampling knobs shape how the model picks the next token from its probability distribution. Lower = safer and more repeatable. Higher = more varied.

Temperature - sharpness of the distribution. Stops: 0.0 Deterministic, 0.3 Focused, 0.7 Default, 1.0 Creative, 2.0 Wild. At T=0 the model always picks the most likely next token. At T=2 unlikely tokens get a real chance.

Top-P (Nucleus) - keep tokens until cumulative probability hits P. Example distribution: reset p=0.42 cum=0.42, change p=0.21 cum=0.63, update p=0.16 cum=0.79, fix p=0.09 cum=0.88, recover p=0.05 cum=0.93, send p=0.04 cum=0.97, create p=0.03 cum=1.00. With top_p = 0.9, sample only from the smallest set whose probabilities add to 0.9. Here 4 tokens stay in, 3 are cut.

Top-K - keep the K most likely tokens. Candidates: reset 0.42, change 0.21, update 0.16, fix 0.09, recover 0.05 (kept); send 0.04, create 0.03 (cut). With top_k = 5, everything past the 5th most-likely token is discarded before sampling.
summary: Walks through temperature, top-p (nucleus), and top-k sampling with concrete probability distributions.

text:
One LLM call doesn't 'just stop' - it stops for a specific reason and the response tells you which. Four stop conditions and four finish reasons.

1. Max Tokens Reached - config: max_tokens: 1024. Hard ceiling on how many tokens the model is allowed to generate this call. finish_reason: length.

2. Stop Sequence Hit - config: stop: ['</answer>']. The model produced one of the configured stop strings. Useful for structured output. finish_reason: stop_sequence.

3. End-Of-Turn Token - model decides on its own. The model emitted its special end-of-turn token. The natural end of a reply. finish_reason: end_turn.

4. Tool Call Requested - only with tool use enabled. The model paused to ask for a tool. Generation halts so your code can run the tool. finish_reason: tool_use.

Always check finish_reason. A length stop means the answer got cut off mid-thought - retry with a higher max_tokens.
summary: Lists the four ways an LLM call can end and the matching finish_reason values to inspect in the response.
Shows the JSON response object with content, stop_reason, and usage fields plus what each means.

text:
Every single call your agent makes follows this exact six-step cycle:

1. Build Messages - combine system + history + new user turn into the messages list. Arrow: Roles assigned.
2. Set Parameters - pick temperature, top-p, top-k, max_tokens, stop. Arrow: Sampling locked.
3. Send Request - POST messages + params to the model endpoint. Arrow: HTTPS POST.
4. Tokens Stream Back - model emits one token at a time over the wire. Arrow: SSE chunks.
5. Stop Reason Reached - one of: end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use. Arrow: end_turn fired.
6. Final Response - content + stop_reason + usage delivered to your app.

Typical latency: ~1.5s median for a 7-token response. Long replies stream tokens as they're generated. Messages in, parameters in, request out. Tokens stream back until a stop reason fires. Response object lands with content, stop_reason, and usage.
summary: Summarizes the full six-step lifecycle of one LLM call from building messages to receiving the final response.

text: The system prompt is the role contract for an LLM agent - a constitution re-read on every turn that anything not in it does not exist for the agent. It has four moving parts: persona (who am I, voice, role), capabilities (which tools and data I may use), constraints (what I must never say or do), and output rules (how my reply must be formatted, cited, structured). The chapter walks through each part using a customer-support assistant example for Alice's knowledge base at docs.example.com, with tools search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human, constraints like no refunds over $200 without escalation, and output rules like greeting by name and wrapping tool calls in <thinking> tags. The full ten-line system prompt at the end weaves all four parts into one artifact.
summary: Overview of the system prompt as a four-part role contract (persona, capabilities, constraints, output rules) using a customer-support agent example.

text: The system prompt is the role contract. It is the constitution every later message is interpreted under. The model re-reads it on every single turn, so anything not in it does not exist for the agent. Four moving parts: Persona (who am I, what is my voice and role), Capabilities (what tools and data am I allowed to use), Constraints (what must I never say, promise, or do), Output Rules (how must my reply be formatted, cited, and structured). Get all four right and the agent behaves predictably. Skip one and it goes off-script.
summary: Introduces the system prompt as a four-part contract re-read each turn covering persona, capabilities, constraints, and output rules.

text: Persona answers 'who am I, what is my voice, and what is my role?' A vague persona produces a generic chatbot. A sharp persona produces an assistant that sounds like it works at your company. BEFORE - VAGUE: 'You are a chatbot.' No role, no voice, no domain. The model fills the gap with a generic helpful tone. AFTER - PERSONA-SPECIFIC: 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer support knowledge base. You are friendly, concise, and you never invent policies.' Names the user (Alice), names the role (customer-support assistant), names the voice (friendly, concise) and one guardrail (no invented policies). Three rules of thumb: name the user, name the role, name one thing the assistant must never do.
summary: Compares a vague persona to a sharp persona and gives three rules: name the user, name the role, name one forbidden action.

text: Capabilities tell the model which tools are available, what each one does, and when to use it. Without this section, the model either refuses or hallucinates tool calls. Tools: search_kb - searches the customer support knowledge base at docs.example.com; lookup_customer - fetches the profile for a customer id like c-9924; process_refund - issues a refund up to a configured dollar limit; escalate_human - hands the conversation off to a human support agent. In the prompt: 'Tools available to you: search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human.' The system prompt declares the tools. The API schema (this chapter) is what actually wires them up. Both sides have to agree.
summary: Lists the four capability tools (search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human) and explains the prompt declares them while the API schema wires them up.

text: Constraints are the negative-space rules. Every refund-gone-wrong, every leaked customer record, every invented policy traces back to a missing line here. Be explicit about the 'never's. The four NEVER rules: Promise refunds over $200 without escalation; Share another customer's data; Claim policies not in the knowledge base; Call tools without first looking up the customer. The $200 line and the escalation line live here, not in code. The model checks them on every reply, so a policy change is a one-line edit, not a deploy.
summary: Explains constraints as explicit NEVER rules and lists four guardrails including the $200 refund cap that lives in prompt rather than code.

text: Output rules shape the surface of every reply: tone, structure, citations, and what each turn must end with. They are what makes the assistant feel consistent across thousands of conversations. The four numbered output rules: 1) Always greet the customer by name; 2) Cite the knowledge base doc when quoting policy; 3) Wrap tool calls in <thinking> tags first; 4) End with a follow-up question or escalation offer. Citations and follow-up offers are not polish - they are how you keep the agent honest and the conversation moving. Without them, replies dead-end and trust erodes.
summary: Defines output rules as surface-shaping rules and lists four: greet by name, cite KB docs, wrap tool calls in <thinking>, end with follow-up or escalation.

text: All four parts woven into one artifact. This is exactly what gets sent as the system message on every turn for the customer-support agent. Full system prompt: 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer support knowledge base at docs.example.com. You are friendly, concise, and you never invent policies. Tools available to you: search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human. Always call lookup_customer (e.g. c-9924) before any other tool. Never promise refunds over $200 without escalating to a human. Never share another customer's data. Never claim a policy that is not in the knowledge base. Always greet the customer by name. Cite the knowledge base doc when quoting policy. Wrap any tool call in a <thinking> block first. End every reply with a follow-up question or an escalation offer.' Ten lines, four parts, zero ambiguity. The agent's behavior in production is mostly the quality of this one block of text.
summary: Shows the complete ten-line system prompt for the customer-support agent combining persona, capabilities, constraints, and output rules.

text: This chapter covers few-shot prompting plus structured output for reliable LLM classification. Few-shot replaces English instructions with 3 to 5 worked input-output examples so the model pattern-matches the format. JSON schemas with enums, required fields, and numeric ranges lock the response shape so the API rejects anything malformed. The two techniques complement each other: examples teach values and style, schemas enforce structure. Production rules: 3-5 examples, diverse not similar, include edge cases, span all enum values, put the hardest case last. The chapter ends with a complete reusable ticket-classifier template (system line + three examples + schema + ticket slot) returning JSON like { "category": "billing", "urgency": "high", "confidence": 0.85 }.
summary: Combining few-shot examples with JSON schema constraints produces reliable, drift-free structured LLM output suited for production classification.

text:
Show, don't just tell. Zero-shot prompts describe the task in English. Few-shot prompts demonstrate the task with 3 to 5 worked examples first. The model is far better at pattern-matching examples than parsing English instructions.

Zero-shot example: 'Classify this ticket as billing, product, or troubleshooting: "Charged twice for last month."' Model output is unpredictable - it returns prose like 'This looks like a billing issue, possibly a duplicate charge...' That's the wrong shape, not a label.

Few-shot example: three labeled pairs then the query.
Input: "I can't log in." → billing
Input: "Charged twice." → billing
Input: "500 errors." → troubleshooting
Input: "Charged twice for last month." →
Model snaps to the pattern and outputs one token: 'billing'. Same model, same task, but examples raise classification accuracy from wobbly to reliable.
summary: Few-shot prompts demonstrate the task with worked examples, producing reliable structured output where zero-shot prompts produce unpredictable prose.

text:
Three examples, same format. Anatomy of a few-shot block: a list of input-output pairs in identical format. The format is the lesson - the model learns the shape from these three then applies it to the real query.

Example 1: Input 'I can't log in - reset my password.' → Category: billing, Urgency: low.
Example 2: Input 'Charged twice for last month.' → Category: billing, Urgency: high.
Example 3: Input 'Dashboard shows 500 errors.' → Category: troubleshooting, Urgency: medium.

Three categories (billing, product, troubleshooting) and three urgency levels (low, medium, high). Every example uses the same labels in the same slots. Consistency is the whole point.
summary: Three labeled input-output examples in identical format teach the model the exact shape and vocabulary to reuse.
JSON schema with enums, required fields, and numeric ranges forces the model's output into a fixed shape the API can validate.

text:
Together they eliminate drift. Few-shot and structured output do different jobs. Examples teach VALUES and STYLE. Schemas enforce STRUCTURE. Run them together and the model produces the same shape with the same vocabulary every single call.

Few-shot teaches values + style: which categories exist, what urgency means here, how a typical ticket maps to a label.
Schema enforces structure: field names, allowed enum values, types, required keys. API rejects anything that doesn't match.
Together = zero drift: same shape, consistent labels, every call. Safe to feed straight into a database or downstream tool.

Final output: { "category": "billing", "urgency": "high", "confidence": 0.85 }

Belt and suspenders. Each technique catches a different failure mode, so together they leave almost nothing to chance.
summary: Few-shot teaches values and style while schemas enforce structure - combined they eliminate drift and produce identical-shape outputs every call.

text:
What actually matters in few-shot. Five rules from production:

Tip 1: Use 3 to 5 examples. More than 5 often hurts more than helps. Diminishing returns set in fast.
Tip 2: Diverse examples beat similar ones. Three near-duplicates teach one pattern. Three different cases teach three.
Tip 3: Edge cases improve robustness. A tricky example moves the needle more than a typical one. Include cases that break naive rules.
Tip 4: Span all enum values. Every category and urgency level should appear at least once across your examples.
Tip 5: Put the hardest case last. The last example sets the strongest pattern. End on the case you most want the model to remember.

Diverse, edge-case-heavy, enum-spanning, hardest-last. The ordering rule is the one teams forget most - and it matters more than count of examples.
summary: Five production rules for few-shot: 3-5 examples, diverse, edge-case-heavy, span all enum values, hardest case last.

text:
The ticket-classifier we'll reuse. All pieces stitched into one prompt: system line on top, three few-shot examples in middle, output schema, then ticket slot.

Template:
SYSTEM: You classify support tickets for Alice's customer-support team. Return JSON only, matching the provided schema.

EXAMPLES:
Input:  "I can't log in - reset my password."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "low",    "confidence": 0.91 }

Input:  "Charged twice for last month."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "high",   "confidence": 0.95 }

Input:  "Dashboard shows 500 errors."
Output: { "category": "troubleshooting", "urgency": "medium", "confidence": 0.88 }

OUTPUT SCHEMA:
{ "category": enum["billing", "product", "troubleshooting"], "urgency": enum["low", "medium", "high"], "confidence": number in [0, 1] }

TICKET TO CLASSIFY: "{{ ticket_text }}"

Reusable: one template, thousands of tickets per day, identical output shape. Auditable: every field logged, can review wrong calls and tune examples. Tunable: swap one example, redeploy, measure - no model retraining required.
summary: Complete ticket-classifier template combining system instruction, three labeled examples, output schema, and a ticket slot for the live query.

text: Chain Of Thought + Self-Consistency. Chain of thought (CoT) makes the model show its reasoning so multi-step errors get caught. Zero-shot CoT uses a trigger phrase like 'Let's think step by step' or 'Think carefully, show your work'. Few-shot CoT goes further by including full reasoning traces in examples to transfer reasoning style. Self-consistency samples N CoT runs at temperature 0.7 and takes the majority vote to collapse variance. Costs: CoT is 5x tokens and latency for +15% on math; self-consistency N=5 is 25x tokens for +20%, with parallel sampling holding latency at 5x. Use CoT for multi-step math, multi-hop reasoning, ambiguous questions. Skip it for lookups, simple classification, single-fact Q&A. Reasoning models reason internally, often making explicit CoT prompts redundant.
summary: Chapter overview of chain of thought prompting, zero-shot and few-shot variants, self-consistency voting, cost-accuracy tradeoffs, and when to use or skip CoT.

text: Reasoning In The Open. Some questions look simple but hide multi-step math. Asking for the final number alone gives the model nowhere to recover when it slips. Chain of thought forces the work onto the page - and the work catches the mistake. Question: Ticket T4: customer on annual plan cancels in month 7. Annual plan is $120/year. Cancellation fee is 10%. What is the refund? Direct Answer (WRONG): $45 with no reasoning shown - the model guessed and stopped. Chain Of Thought (CORRECT): 1) Annual plan = $120 for 12 months. 2) 5 months remain (12 minus 7 used). 3) Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 4) Cancellation fee = 10% of $50 = $5. 5) Final refund = $50 minus $5 = $45. Final: $45. Same model, same question - only difference is whether model shows its work. CoT exposed prorating and fee; direct call had nothing to anchor to.
summary: Side-by-side refund example showing how chain of thought exposes the prorating and fee math while a direct answer hides reasoning.

text: The 'Let's Think Step By Step' Trick. Zero-shot CoT is the cheapest reasoning upgrade you can buy: one extra sentence on the user turn. No examples, no system-prompt changes. Two phrases work reliably. Classic Trigger: 'User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Let's think step by step.' Stronger Trigger: 'User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Think carefully before answering. Show your work.' Zero examples, just a trigger phrase. Model has seen so much 'step by step' reasoning in training that this single phrase flips it into reasoning mode for free.
summary: Zero-shot CoT uses one trigger phrase like 'Let's think step by step' or 'Think carefully, show your work' to unlock reasoning without examples.

text: Show The Reasoning In Examples. Few-shot CoT raises the bar. Each example includes the reasoning trace, not just the answer. This transfers the reasoning STYLE, not just the answer format - so the model imitates how you think, not just what you output. Example 1: Question: Ticket T1: annual plan $120, cancelled month 3. No fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1) 9 months remain out of 12. 2) Prorated refund = 120 x 9 / 12 = $90. 3) No fee applied. Answer: $90. Example 2: Question: Ticket T4: annual plan $120, cancelled month 7. 10% cancellation fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1) 5 months remain out of 12. 2) Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 3) Fee = 10% of $50 = $5. Answer: $45. Example does the work. Model now knows: list months remaining, prorate, apply fee, then state answer. Few-shot CoT teaches that exact chain.
summary: Few-shot chain of thought shows full reasoning traces in examples so the model imitates the style of thinking, not just the answer format.

text: Run N Times, Vote. Self-consistency is a simple trick: ask the same CoT question N times with temperature 0.7, collect the answers, and take the majority vote. Variance shrinks. Wrong runs get out-voted. Question: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan $120, cancelled month 7, 10% fee. Sample 5 times with temperature = 0.7. Run 1: Prorated $50 minus 10% fee = $45 - $45 correct. Run 2: 5/12 x 120 = $50. Fee $5. Final $45 - $45 correct. Run 3: Refund $50 prorated. Forgot the fee - $50 wrong. Run 4: $50 prorated, $5 fee, $45 refund - $45 correct. Run 5: Months left 5. Refund $50. Fee 10% - $45 correct. Majority Vote: $45 x 4 vs $50 x 1. Winner: $45. One run can slip and forget the fee. Five runs disagree, the correct answer wins by majority. Variance collapses without retraining anything.
summary: Self-consistency samples N reasoning chains at temperature 0.7 and takes the majority vote so wrong runs get out-voted.

text: What CoT Buys And What It Costs. Reasoning isn't free. CoT generates extra tokens. Self-consistency multiplies that by N. Accuracy gain is real but the bill is real too. Tradeoff table - Direct Answer: 1x tokens, 1x latency, Baseline accuracy. CoT Alone: 5x tokens, 5x latency, +15% on math. Self-Consistency (N=5): 25x tokens, 5x (parallel) to 25x (serial) latency, +20% on math. Self-consistency tradeoff: 5x to 25x the cost for ~5% extra accuracy over CoT alone. Run samples in parallel and latency drops back to 5x. Cost stays at 25x. Only worth it when accuracy is critical - billing, refunds, dosage, legal answers.
summary: Tradeoff table comparing token cost, latency, and accuracy of direct answers, CoT alone (5x cost +15%), and self-consistency N=5 (25x cost +20%).

text: When Chain-Of-Thought Wins. CoT is a Decision Matrix problem. Technique helps when there is real reasoning to do. It hurts when the task is a lookup or one-step classification - extra tokens, extra latency, no payoff. Use CoT (Reasoning Required): Multi-Step Arithmetic - refunds, prorating, tax breakdowns, discount stacking - any math with two or more steps. Multi-Hop Reasoning - questions that chain facts: who did X for the customer that signed up in 2023 and is in tier B? Ambiguous Questions - cases where user's intent isn't obvious from words alone; reasoning surfaces the assumption. Skip CoT (Direct Answer Is Better): Simple Classification - map ticket text to one of three labels; few-shot already nails it, CoT just adds latency. Lookup Tasks - fetch customer's email or plan; no reasoning needed, it's a retrieval. Single-Fact Q&A - what time does support open? CoT will hallucinate a derivation where a direct answer suffices. Reasoning Models Change The Calculus: reasoning models like ones from Section 14.4 Thinking do CoT internally before replying. You no longer prompt for 'step by step' - the model thinks first, then emits the answer. CoT prompts still help on non-reasoning models, but with a reasoning model the technique often becomes redundant. Match the technique to the task. Multi-step math gets CoT. Category lookup gets direct answer. When the model already reasons internally, save the tokens.
summary: Decision matrix for CoT - use it for multi-step math, multi-hop reasoning, ambiguous questions; skip for classification, lookups, single-fact Q&A; reasoning models make CoT prompts redundant.

text: Data freshness axis diagram. A horizontal arrow runs from 'Never' on the left through 'Weekly' in the middle to 'Real-Time' on the right, labeled 'How fresh can the data be?'. Four colored markers position the techniques: Fine-Tuning sits at the far left (never to weekly because retraining is slow and expensive), Prompting sits just right of Fine-Tuning (never - prompt is frozen), RAG sits past the middle (hourly - re-index docs and the model sees them next call), and Agent sits at the far right (real-time per query - tools hit live APIs).
summary: Diagram placing fine-tuning, prompting, RAG, and agent along a horizontal freshness axis from Never to Real-Time.

text: Capability gap axis diagram. A vertical arrow runs from 'Base model already does it' at the bottom to 'Skill never seen in training' at the top. Four markers placed alternately left and right show where each technique unlocks new behavior: Prompting near the bottom (already in base model - trigger the skill that's already there), RAG below the middle (knows the skill, missing the facts - reading comprehension is in the model but your private docs are not), Agent above the middle (knows the skill, missing the actions - reasoning is in the model but side-effects need tools), and Fine-Tuning at the top (specialized skill never seen - style, jargon, structured output the base model can't imitate).
summary: Vertical diagram showing where each technique unlocks capability, from prompting (existing skill) up to fine-tuning (specialized skill never seen).

text: This chapter explains how to choose among prompting, fine-tuning, RAG, and agents. Each technique changes a different thing: prompting changes only the message, fine-tuning changes weights, RAG changes the data retrieved per call, and agents add a tool-using loop. Four axes drive the choice: (1) data freshness - frozen weights and prompts can't hold moving facts, RAG refreshes hourly, agents query live; (2) capability gap - prompting triggers existing skills, RAG fills fact gaps, agents fill action gaps, fine-tuning fills missing skills; (3) latency - prompting and fine-tuning are fastest, RAG adds retrieval, agents are slowest; (4) cost - prompting cheapest per call, fine-tuning has upfront training cost, agents scale cost with tool steps. A four-question decision tree maps directly to a technique. Production systems stack all four layers, and the four classic anti-patterns all come from skipping the axis walk.
summary: Summarizes the four LLM techniques, the four decision axes (freshness, capability, latency, cost), the decision tree, the stacking pattern, and the anti-patterns.

text:
Four Tools In The Toolbox. Every customer-support feature you ship is one of four things: a prompt change, a weight change, a retrieval change, or a tool loop. Knowing which is which prevents months of wasted work.

Prompting - Changes nothing in the model. Nothing in the data. Write better messages. Same base weights, same world knowledge, same call. At inference: 1 LLM call.

Fine-Tuning - Model weights change. Data is baked in. Train on examples of the new behavior. The new skill lives inside the parameters. At inference: 1 LLM call (specialized model).

RAG - Data is retrieved at call-time. Vector search pulls fresh chunks into the prompt. Same base model, new context per call. At inference: 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call.

Agent - Model uses tools in a loop. LLM decides what to do, calls a tool, reads the result, loops until done. At inference: multiple LLM calls + tool calls.

These aren't competitors. They solve different problems.
summary: Introduces the four LLM application techniques (prompting, fine-tuning, RAG, agent) and what each one changes at inference time.

text:
Does The Data Change? Data freshness is the first filter. If the answer depends on facts that move - prices, inventory, the ticket the user just filed - those facts cannot live inside the model weights.

Freshness positions:
- Fine-Tuning: Never to weekly. Retraining is slow and expensive.
- Prompting: Never. Whatever you wrote into the prompt is frozen.
- RAG: Hourly. Re-index docs and the model sees them next call.
- Agent: Real-time per query. Tools hit live APIs at the moment of the question.

Rule of thumb: data that moves fast belongs OUTSIDE the model. Prompt and weights are frozen between calls; retrieval and tools are not.
summary: Compares how fresh the data can be for each technique, from never (prompting/fine-tuning) to real-time (agent tools).

text:
Is The Capability Missing? The second axis is the capability gap. Ask: does the model already know how to do this, or is the skill itself missing? A missing capability needs different medicine than a missing fact.

Capability bands (bottom = already in base model, top = specialized skill never seen):
- Prompting: Already in base model. Trigger the skill that's already there. Cheapest path.
- RAG: Knows the skill, missing the facts. Reading-comprehension is in the model. Your private docs are not.
- Agent: Knows the skill, missing the actions. Reasoning is in the model. Side-effects need tools.
- Fine-Tuning: Specialized skill never seen. Style, domain jargon, structured output the base model can't imitate.

RAG fills factual gaps. Agent fills action gaps. Fine-tuning fills skill gaps. Prompting only works when the skill is already inside the model and you just need to point at it.
summary: Maps each technique to the type of gap it fills: prompting triggers existing skills, RAG fills facts, agent fills actions, fine-tuning fills missing skills.

text:
Latency And Cost Budget. The third and fourth axes are budgets. A real-time chat reply has a latency budget; a nightly batch job has a cost budget. The four approaches land in different quadrants.

Quadrants:
- Low Latency, Low Cost -> Prompting. 1 call, small prompt. Cheapest and fastest by default.
- Low Latency, Higher Cost Upfront -> Fine-Tuning. Training is expensive. Inference after that is short prompt + 1 call.
- Higher Latency, Low Cost -> RAG. Add vector search before the call. Tokens stay small, freshness is free.
- Highest Latency, Variable Cost -> Agent. Each loop is another call. Cost scales with tool steps.

Latency: Prompting ~ Fine-Tune < RAG << Agent. Cost per call: same order, but Agent loops can balloon.

Budgets break ties. If two approaches both work technically, the one that fits your latency floor and cost ceiling wins.
summary: Places the four techniques into latency vs cost quadrants and gives the ordering: prompting is cheapest/fastest, agent loops are slowest and most variable.

text:
Which One? Walk The Tree. Combine the axes into one decision tree. Walk it top to bottom. The first Yes is the answer - or the starting point if you need to stack.

Question 1: Does the model already know it well enough? Yes -> Prompting alone.
Question 2: Knowledge needs to stay current? Yes -> RAG.
Question 3: Need a specialized skill the model doesn't have? Yes -> Fine-Tuning (plus RAG if data also moves).
Question 4: Need to take actions in the world via tools? Yes -> Agent.

Need to take actions via tools? That answer always points at an Agent.

Decision tree, not flowchart. Each leaf is a single technique. If two questions answer Yes, combine them.
summary: Provides a four-question decision tree that maps to one of the four techniques, with stacking when multiple questions answer Yes.

text:
Most Production Systems Stack Them. A real customer-support assistant rarely uses one technique. The shipping pattern is a stack: each layer covers what the layer below cannot.

Layer 1 - Fine-Tuned Base Model: teaches the support voice and the refund-policy JSON shape.
Layer 2 - + RAG Layer: pulls today's order history, today's billing record, today's playbook.
Layer 3 - + Agent Loop: reads tickets, issues refunds, sends emails. Real side-effects.
Layer 4 - + Prompting / Few-Shot Per Task: system prompt + examples shape each individual call.

Stack pattern: fine-tune + RAG + agent loop + prompting combine into one system.

These aren't either / or. Production systems pick what fits each axis and combine. Knowing each layer's job is how you debug the stack when something breaks.
summary: Shows the production stack pattern where fine-tuning, RAG, agent loops, and prompting layer together in a real customer support system.

text:
Common Misuses. Four anti-patterns show up over and over. Each one picks the wrong tool for an axis the team didn't think about.

1. Fine-Tuning To Add Fresh Facts. Wrong: retraining every time the pricing page changes. Fix: use RAG. Facts that move belong outside the weights.

2. RAG For Behavior The Model Doesn't Know. Wrong: stuffing style guides into the prompt and hoping the tone sticks. Fix: fine-tune. Behavior lives in weights, not in retrieved chunks.

3. Agent Loop For A Simple Lookup. Wrong: multi-step planning to answer 'what's the refund window?'. Fix: direct call + 1 tool. Loops are for tasks that branch.

4. Massive Prompt Instead Of Fine-Tuning A Small Model. Wrong: sending 30 examples on every call to a frontier model. Fix: fine-tune a small model once. Cost runaway stops, latency drops.

The pattern under every misuse is the same: someone picked their favorite tool instead of walking the four axes.
summary: Lists four common anti-patterns where teams pick the wrong technique and gives the correct fix for each.

text: Horizontal stacked bar showing how 128k tokens of context window are split across five zones: System Prompt 4k (cyan), Conversation History 50k (blue), Retrieved Docs 69k (purple), User Input 1k (green), Reserved For Output 4k (orange). Bar spans 0 to 128k hard cap.
summary: Diagram of the 128k context budget split into system, history, retrieval, input, and reserved-output zones.

text: U-shaped recall-accuracy curve plotted against position in a long context window. The curve peaks near 95% at position 0 (Start) and near 95% at position 128k (End), and dips to roughly 45% near the middle (64k position) - the lost-in-the-middle effect. X-axis is position 0 to 128k, Y-axis is recall accuracy 0% to 100%.
summary: Diagram of the lost-in-the-middle U-shaped recall curve across a 128k context window.

text: Context engineering is the discipline of packing a fixed 128k-token window. Every call shares the same five-layer assembly stack (system prompt, few-shot examples, conversation history, retrieved chunks, user query) read top-to-bottom. The 128k budget is a fixed pie, so trim history or trim retrieval to leave headroom for output. Models suffer lost-in-the-middle: recall is high at start and end, ~45% in the middle, so put critical facts at the edges. Within layers, order by relevance for factual lookups or by recency for multi-turn chats. When the budget overflows, follow an eviction ladder: drop tail retrieved chunks first, summarize mid-history, prune extra few-shots, never touch the system prompt or last user message. Pick the layers, set the order, evict in the right order - same model, very different behavior call to call.
summary: Context engineering picks layers, orders them, and evicts cheaply to fit a 128k window while exploiting the U-shaped recall curve.

text: Every LLM has a context window: a hard token budget the call must live inside. A frontier model today is 128k tokens. Every prompt is a packing problem against that ceiling. The 128k budget splits into reserved zones: System Prompt (4k tokens, rules + persona, always present), Conversation History (50k, prior turns, grows every turn), Retrieved Docs (69k, RAG chunks, refilled per query), User Input (1k, the new question, capped), Reserved For Output (4k, headroom the model writes into). Anything past 128k gets dropped or summarized. Context engineering is the meta-skill of deciding what survives the cap on every single call.
summary: Context window is a fixed 128k-token budget split across system prompt, history, retrieved docs, user input, and reserved output zones.

text: The model reads top-to-bottom. Order is not cosmetic - it changes what the model attends to. The assembly stack is the same every call: Layer 1 System Prompt (persona, rules, tone, refusal policy, read first, anchors everything below). Layer 2 Few-Shot Examples (2-5 input + output pairs that pin the format, read before any real content). Layer 3 Conversation History (prior turns of this thread, older turns nearest the top). Layer 4 Retrieved Chunks (RAG hits for this query, reranked closer-to-bottom = closer-to-query attention). Layer 5 User Query (the thing to actually answer, sits at the very bottom for maximum recency). Recency = bottom. Whatever you want the model to weight most goes nearest the user query. Same five layers every turn. The history grows, the retrieved chunks rotate, the system prompt stays - but the order does not change.
summary: Prompts are assembled in a fixed five-layer order from system prompt at top to user query at the bottom for maximum recency.

text: 128k is a fixed pie. Every call is a tradeoff between more retrieval, more history, or more output. Same long support ticket, three packings. Cram Everything (at-risk, lost-in-middle kicks in): System 4k + History raw 50k + Retrieved top-20 70k + User 1k + Output 3k. Trim History (wins for fact-heavy tickets, keeps room for retrieval): System 4k + History summary 8k + Retrieved 80k + User 1k + Output 35k. Trim Retrieval (wins for long multi-turn convos, keeps room for output): System 4k + History raw 50k + Retrieved top-3 8k + User 1k + Output 65k. Rule: summarize what you can compress, evict what you cannot, never starve the output. Trim and summarize are the two levers. Which wins depends on the ticket type, not on taste.
summary: The same 128k budget can be allocated three ways - cram, trim history, or trim retrieval - each tuned to a different ticket type.

text: Even with a 128k window, the model does not attend evenly. Recall drops in the middle and spikes at the start and end. This is the lost-in-the-middle effect, and it is the single biggest reason long prompts fail. Sample recall curve: position 0 = 93%, 15% = 78%, 30% = 60%, 45% = 48%, 50% middle = 45%, 55% = 48%, 70% = 62%, 85% = 82%, 100% end = 95%. The curve is U-shaped. Rule: put the most important info at the START or END of context, never in the middle. Buried in the middle is buried full stop. If a fact has to land, place it next to the user query or inside the system prompt - those are the two recall peaks.
summary: Lost-in-the-middle: recall accuracy is U-shaped across position, peaking at start and end (~95%) and dipping to ~45% mid-context.

text: Inside the retrieved-chunks and history layers, ordering is its own choice. Two strategies cover the common cases. Relevance-First (for factual queries, one-shot lookups): rerank retrieved chunks by similarity, top-3 sit closest to the user query. Example - ticket 'What's the refund window for plan Pro?' -> top-3 RAG hits next to the query. Recency-First (for multi-turn conversations, long debugging threads): latest history at the bottom near query for conversational coherence. Example - ticket with 6-turn back-and-forth -> last 2 user / assistant turns sit nearest the query. Heuristic: factual lookup -> relevance-first. Long conversation -> recency-first. Hybrid -> rerank then recency-bias the last 2 turns. Same chunks, different order, different answer. Order is a free knob and routinely costs nothing to flip.
summary: Within retrieval and history layers, order chunks by relevance for factual lookups and by recency for multi-turn conversations.

text: When the packed prompt exceeds 128k, something has to leave. Eviction is not random - there is a ladder. Drop the things whose loss costs least, keep the things whose loss breaks the call. #1 Older Retrieved Chunks Beyond Top-3 - Evict First (rerank surfaced them but top-3 carry the answer almost always, drop the long tail). #2 Mid-Conversation History Raw Turns - Summarize Then Evict (compress middle 5+ turns into one summary line, keep newest 2 turns raw). #3 Few-Shot Examples Beyond The First Two - Evict Soon (two examples pin the format, examples 3-5 are insurance and cost tokens). #4 Top-3 Retrieved Chunks - Rarely Evict (these ARE the answer, only drop if user input is huge). #5 Latest Assistant Response - Rarely Evict (removing it loses the model's own context). #6 System Prompt + Last User Message - Never Evict (rules and the question, evicting either breaks the call). Summarize before you evict. A 50-turn history compresses to a few hundred tokens with most signal intact.
summary: When prompts overflow, evict in a fixed priority: tail RAG chunks first, summarize mid-history, drop extra few-shots, never touch system prompt or last user message.

text: Three-box flow diagram for a tool call. The Model on the left decides WHEN to call the function. An arrow labeled tool_use points from the Model to the Runtime in the middle, which executes the real function. An arrow labeled Execute points from the Runtime to the Result on the right. A dashed return arrow loops from the Result back to the Model, labeled 'Return: tool_result becomes the next input.' Title across the top reads 'One tool call: Decide -> Execute -> Return.' Conveys the strict separation: the model is the brain (decides), the runtime is the hands (executes), and the result flows back as the model's next input.
summary: Diagram of the three-stage Model to Runtime to Result flow for one tool call, with tool_use, Execute, and Return arrows.

text: Triangular agent-loop diagram. Three labeled circular nodes: Reason at the top (cyan, 'Which tool?'), Act at the bottom-right (purple, 'Call tool'), Observe at the bottom-left (green, 'Read result'). Curved arrows form a clockwise cycle: Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason. A central label reads 'Loop until done.' Visualizes the ReAct-style agent loop where the model alternates between deciding which tool to invoke, emitting a tool_use, reading the tool_result, and reasoning again with the new evidence until it can answer.
summary: Diagram of the clockwise Reason-Act-Observe triangle showing the agent loop that repeats until the model finishes.

text: Tool Use - LLM as Orchestrator. A bare LLM only knows training data; tools are how it touches the real world. A tool is a function: you declare a signature with three parts (name, description, parameters), the model decides when to call, and your runtime executes for real. The model never runs code itself - it emits a tool_use block and halts; the runtime validates inputs, calls the function, appends a tool_result, and re-invokes the model. A single tool call is the atom; the agent loop (Reason -> Act -> Observe) is the molecule. The running example is a customer-support agent with 8 tools: 3 read-only (search_kb, lookup_customer, lookup_subscription) and 5 side-effectful (reset_password, change_email, process_refund, escalate_human, send_email), with process_refund escalating any refund over $200.
summary: Summarizes how an LLM uses tools to reach the real world: declared functions, runtime execution, the reason-act-observe loop, and the canonical 8-tool support inventory.

text: Pure LLM vs LLM With Tools. A bare LLM only knows what was in its training data. The moment your support agent has to look at the live knowledge base, your customer record, or trigger a real refund, text generation alone is not enough. Tools are how the model reaches out and touches the real world. Running ticket T5: 'What's the difference between Pro and Enterprise?' Pure LLM mode: relies on training data, plans may be outdated, may hallucinate features. Flow: User asks T5 -> LLM guesses from memory -> Returns possibly-stale answer. Sample answer: 'Pro has more features than Enterprise, I think... (Guessed from training data.)' Verdict: Risky - facts can be wrong, no source. LLM + Tools mode: Calls search_kb to fetch the live comparison doc, then answers from that source. Flow: User asks T5 -> LLM calls search_kb('Pro vs Enterprise') -> Knowledge base returns current doc -> LLM answers from doc. Sample answer: 'Pro: 10 seats, $49/mo, email support. Enterprise: unlimited seats, SSO, dedicated CSM.' Verdict: Grounded - cites live docs, traceable to source. Same model, same prompt. The only difference: one of them can call search_kb. That single hook is the difference between a chatty box and a working agent.
summary: Compares a bare LLM relying on stale training data against an LLM that can call search_kb to ground answers in live docs.

text: A Tool Is A Function The Model Can Call. A tool is exactly one thing: a function. You declare its signature. The model decides when to call it. Your runtime executes the function for real. The result flows back into the conversation. Example signature: lookup_customer(email: string) -> Customer. Flow has three boxes: Model (decides WHEN to call) -> Runtime (executes the real function) -> Result (flows back to the model). Arrows: tool_use from Model to Runtime, Execute from Runtime to Result, Return where tool_result becomes the next input. One tool call: Decide -> Execute -> Return. Model = brain. Runtime = hands. The brain never touches the database directly. Critical separation: the LLM never executes code itself. It only emits a tool_use block. Your runtime does the actual call. This boundary is what makes tool use safe enough to ship.
summary: Defines a tool as a function whose signature the model knows but only the runtime executes, with results returning to the model.

text: Name, Description, Parameters. Every tool definition has exactly three parts you write. The model reads all three at every turn and uses them to decide whether to call and how. Part 1 Name: process_refund. The identifier the model emits when it decides to call this tool. Part 2 Description: 'Refund a paid invoice. Refunds over $200 require human approval.' Plain-English contract. This is what the model reads to decide WHEN to call. Part 3 Parameters: { invoice_id: string, reason: string }. The arguments the model must produce. Validated before the runtime executes. Description is the most important field. Spend most of your editing time there. The model has never seen your codebase. The description is its only manual. If the description is vague, the model guesses; if it is specific, the model rarely misfires.
summary: Lists the three parts of every tool definition - name, description, parameters - and stresses that the description is the model's only manual.

text: The Model Decides, The Runtime Executes. Two distinct roles. The model is in charge of decisions - which tool, what arguments. Your code is in charge of side effects - reading the DB, charging the card, sending the email. Never mix them. Step 1 User: Sends a message: 'Refund my last invoice, the charge was wrong.' Step 2 Model Reads: Compares the message to every tool's description. Looks for a match. Step 3 Model Decides: Thinks: 'User asked for a refund - I should call process_refund.' Or chooses NOT to call any tool. Step 4 Model Emits Tool_Use: Generation halts. The response is a tool_use block: { name: 'process_refund', input: {...} }. Step 5 Runtime Executes: Your code sees the block, validates inputs, calls the real function, gets a result. Step 6 Result Returns: The runtime appends a tool_result message and re-invokes the model so it can continue. The model can also choose NOT to call any tool and just answer. That is a valid outcome. The model halts at tool_use. It does not execute anything itself. The runtime is the single chokepoint where every real-world side effect happens - which is exactly where you place auth, logging, and rate limits.
summary: Walks through the six-step refund flow showing how the model decides and emits tool_use while the runtime alone touches side effects.

text: One Call, Or A Loop Of Calls? A single tool call is the atom. An agent is what you get when you put that atom in a loop: reason, act, observe, then reason again with the new evidence. Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason again. Loop until done. Reason: Read messages so far. Decide: do I need a tool, and which one? Act: Emit a tool_use block. The runtime calls the function for real. Observe: Read the tool_result. Did it succeed? Was the info enough to answer? A single tool call is the atom. An agent loop is the molecule. One ticket might be a single search_kb call and done. Another might need lookup_customer, then lookup_subscription, then process_refund - three turns of the loop before the model finally answers.
summary: Introduces the reason-act-observe agent loop as repeated tool calls that build up evidence until the model can answer.

text: The 8 Tools Our Support Agent Has. For the rest of this section we work with one fixed inventory. Every example uses these eight tools - the shape of the schemas, the lifecycle of a call, the retry strategy when one fails. 1) search_kb(query) - Search the customer support knowledge base. 2) lookup_customer(email) - Find a customer by email address. 3) lookup_subscription(customer_id) - Get current plan and status for a customer. 4) reset_password(customer_id) - Send a password-reset email to the customer. 5) change_email(customer_id, new_email) - Update the email on record. 6) process_refund(invoice_id, reason) - Refund a paid invoice. Refunds over $200 require escalation. 7) escalate_human(transcript, urgency) - Hand off the conversation to a human agent. 8) send_email(template_id, vars) - Send a templated email to the customer. 8 tools, 3 read-only (search, lookup, lookup), 5 side-effectful (reset, change, refund, escalate, send). Read-only tools are cheap to retry; side-effectful tools must be guarded with auth, validation, and approvals. process_refund is the canary - any refund over $200 escalates instead of executing.
summary: Lists the canonical eight-tool support-agent inventory and splits them into three safe read-only tools and five side-effectful ones.

text: This chapter teaches how to write JSON-Schema definitions for LLM tools: the four schema pieces (name, description, input_schema, required), the required-vs-optional distinction, narrowing values with enum and format hints, the five rules for great tool descriptions, a bad-vs-good description comparison for process_refund, and the canonical lookup_customer reference schema used by the rest of Section 13. The schema is the only contract the model ever sees - never the implementation.
summary: Overview of writing JSON-Schema tool definitions including schema pieces, required/optional fields, enums, format hints, description rules, and the canonical lookup_customer reference.
Introduces the four pieces of a tool schema (name, description, input_schema, required) and the principle that the model only ever sees the schema, not the implementation.
Shows how the required array marks non-negotiable fields, using process_refund with invoice_id required and reason optional, and advises minimizing required fields.
Explains how enum locks parameters to a fixed string set and format hints expected shapes like email, using escalate_human urgency low/medium/high as the example.

text:
Descriptions Are Read By The Model. The description field is plain language for the model. It is the most important text in your whole tool definition. Treat it like a manual entry the model will skim every turn. Five rules turn a bad description into a great one.

RULE 1: State what the tool does in plain language. One sentence the model can match against the user's request.
RULE 2: State the side effects. Does it mutate state? Send email? Cost money? The model needs to know before calling.
RULE 3: State when NOT to use the tool. Negative examples prevent over-eager calls. "Do not use for refunds over $200" saves real dollars.
RULE 4: Use the same vocabulary the user would use. If the user says cancel, do not name the tool terminate_subscription. Match the word.
RULE 5: Mention any constraints. Rate limits, dollar thresholds, retry semantics, idempotency - put them all in the description.

Description is the model's manual. Write it like you would write a runbook for a new hire. Spend ten times longer on the description than on the parameter names. A great description prevents wrong-tool calls, prevents the model from inventing arguments, and tells the model exactly when to call escalate_human instead of process_refund.
summary: Five rules for writing effective tool descriptions: state what it does, state side effects, state when not to use it, match user vocabulary, and mention constraints.

text:
One Sentence Changes Everything. Same tool, same parameters. The only difference between a refund that goes wrong and one that goes right is the description string. Read both versions of process_refund below.

BAD (Vague): "description": "Refund function." Why bad: No side effects mentioned. No dollar threshold. No when-not-to-use. The model will call it for anything that sounds refund-shaped.

GOOD (Specific): "description": "Refund a paid invoice. Use only when the customer has confirmed cancellation. Refunds over $200 require human approval - call escalate_human instead for those." Why good: What it does. When to use. When NOT to use. The $200 threshold. The fallback tool. The model now has a real contract.

Bad description: model refunds a $500 invoice. Good description: model calls escalate_human instead. The vague version costs you real dollars in mis-refunds. The specific version threads the $200 ceiling and the escalation fallback right into the model's prompt - so the model is choosing escalate_human before it ever attempts the refund.
summary: Side-by-side bad vs good description for process_refund, showing how a specific description with thresholds and fallback prevents the model from mis-refunding a $500 invoice.
Presents the canonical lookup_customer schema and an example tool_use invocation that later Section 13 chapters reuse, highlighting name, required email input, and read-only side-effect profile.

text: Six-row swim-lane for the tool-call lifecycle showing the message kinds exchanged between User, Model, Runtime, and Tool actors. Rows from top to bottom: User (user message), Model (tool_use), Runtime (function call), Tool (function return), Runtime (tool_result), Model (assistant message). Each row shows the actor as a colored card on the left and the message kind it emits in a labeled box on the right. Dashed arrows connect each row to the next, showing how one message triggers the next.
summary: Diagram: six-row swim-lane of actors and messages in the tool call lifecycle.

text: Horizontal timeline showing how tool calls interleave with a streaming assistant response. Time flows left to right. Phases include stream begin, partial text streaming, stream pause, tool_use block emitted whole, runtime executes, tool_result delivered, and stream resume. Each phase is a colored segment along a single horizontal bar with phase numbers and labels.
summary: Diagram: horizontal stream timeline showing tool_use, execution, and tool_result interleaved with token streaming.

text: The tool call lifecycle traces a single tool invocation through six actors: User sends a request, Model emits a tool_use block, Runtime validates and executes the function, Tool returns a value, Runtime wraps it as tool_result, Model reads the result and writes the final answer. Two message kinds carry the call: tool_use (type, id, name, input) flows from model to runtime; tool_result (type, tool_use_id, content, is_error) flows back. The model never touches the tool directly; the runtime is the single boundary for real-world effects. Ticket T1 ("reset my password") loops twice (lookup_customer then reset_password) across 3 LLM turns. Tool blocks are not streamed token-by-token; they arrive whole, pausing the text stream until the tool_result lands. Of T1's 3.4s end-to-end latency, LLM calls take 3.0s (88%) and tool execution only 0.4s (12%) - the model is the bottleneck, so cutting LLM turns matters more than tool speed.
summary: Overview of tool call lifecycle: six actors, two message types, T1 trace, streaming behavior, and latency budget.

text: One tool call passes through six actors. The user starts it, the model decides, the runtime executes, the tool returns, the runtime wraps the result, and the model writes the answer. Every arrow is a message of a specific kind. Step 1 User: sends a request like "I can't log in - reset my password." Step 2 Model: reads context, picks a tool, emits a tool_use block with name and input. Step 3 Runtime: validates the tool_use, looks up the real function, calls it with the input. Step 4 Tool: runs the real side effect (DB, email, refund) and returns a value or an error. Step 5 Runtime: wraps the return value in a tool_result message and appends it to the conversation. Step 6 Model: re-reads the conversation, sees the result, writes the final answer for the user. Six actors. Two messages (tool_use, tool_result) carry the call from model to runtime and back. The model never touches the tool directly - it only emits tool_use blocks. The runtime is the single boundary where every real-world effect (DB read, email send, refund) actually happens.
summary: Six-actor swim-lane from user request to final answer: User, Model, Runtime, Tool, Runtime, Model.
Anatomy of the tool_use message: type, id, name, input fields and what each does.

text:
After the runtime executes the real function, it wraps the return value into a tool_result block and appends it to the conversation. The model then re-reads everything (user, prior assistant messages, tool_use, tool_result) and continues. Canonical shape:

{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01abc...",
  "content": "Password reset email sent to alice@example.com.",
  "is_error": false
}

Field meanings: type is always "tool_result" - tells the model this block carries a return value. tool_use_id MUST match the id from the tool_use; this is how the model pairs request to response. content holds the return value (usually a string, can be structured) - this is what the model reads next. is_error is a flag; true means the runtime failed and the model should recover, not pretend it worked. tool_use_id pairs the response to the request: same id in, same id out. If the tool failed (DB down, validation error, network blip), the runtime still produces a tool_result but flips is_error to true. The model reads the failure and adapts instead of assuming success.
summary: Anatomy of tool_result message: type, tool_use_id pairing, content, and is_error flag for failures.

text: End-to-end trace of ticket T1 "I can't log in - reset my password." The model loops twice (lookup_customer, then reset_password) before it can answer. Step 1 User: "I can't log in - reset my password." - plain English, no customer_id, just an intent. Step 2 Model: tool_use { name: lookup_customer, input: { email: "alice@example.com" } } - email comes from conversation header; model needs customer_id before it can reset. Step 3 Runtime: tool_result { content: { customer_id: "c-9924", plan: "Pro" } } - DB hit, runtime turns the function return into a tool_result message. Step 4 Model: tool_use { name: reset_password, input: { customer_id: "c-9924" } } - now it has c-9924 from step 3, second turn of the loop. Step 5 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Email sent." } - side effect happens here, runtime is the single chokepoint. Step 6 Model: "Done - check alice@example.com for the reset link." - final assistant message, no more tool_use, model is satisfied. T1: 2 tool calls (lookup_customer + reset_password), 3 LLM turns, 1 final answer. The model never has c-9924 in step 2 - it only learns the id from step 3's tool_result. That is why the loop matters: each turn unlocks the next call by giving the model new evidence.
summary: Concrete six-step trace of ticket T1 showing two chained tool calls (lookup_customer then reset_password) across three LLM turns.

text: In production you usually stream assistant tokens to the UI for that typewriter effect. Tool calls fit cleanly inside a stream but with one twist: text streams token by token, tool blocks arrive whole. Stream timeline phases (left to right = time): 1) Stream begins - assistant tokens start arriving one by one. 2) Partial text streams - "Let me look that up for you..." appears word by word. 3) Stream pauses - model decides to call a tool, token stream halts. 4) tool_use block emitted - the block arrives as one complete JSON object, NOT token-by-token. 5) Runtime executes - your code runs the real function, could take 200ms or 2s. 6) tool_result delivered - result message appended, model re-invoked to continue. 7) Stream resumes - new assistant tokens start flowing again, building the final answer. Tool blocks aren't streamed token-by-token; they arrive complete. Your UI shows the partial text as it streams but holds the spinner during tool execution.
