When the tool_result lands and the stream resumes, the next batch of assistant tokens picks up where it left off and writes the answer that uses the result.
summary: How tool calls interleave with streaming: text streams token by token, tool_use blocks arrive whole, stream pauses during execution.

text: Ticket T1 took 3.4 seconds end-to-end. Where did the time go? Most of it lives in the LLM calls, not the tool calls. Latency budget breakdown: LLM call 1 (reads user, emits lookup_customer) = 1200ms. lookup_customer tool execution = 200ms. LLM call 2 (reads result, emits reset_password) = 1100ms. reset_password tool execution = 200ms. LLM call 3 (reads result, emits final answer) = 700ms. Total = 3400ms (3.4s). LLM calls: 3.0s (88%). Tool calls: 0.4s (12%). The model is the bottleneck. Every extra LLM call adds about 1 second. Speeding up T1 means cutting LLM turns, not tool latency. Parallel tool calls (next chapter) let you fire lookup_customer and lookup_subscription in one turn instead of two - shaving a whole LLM call from the budget.
summary: Latency budget for T1 showing LLM calls dominate (88% of 3.4s) while tool execution is only 12%.

text: Serial timeline diagram for Ticket T2: bars stacked left to right showing LLM call 1 (~80 width), then lookup_customer (200ms, 40 width), then LLM call 2, then lookup_subscription (200ms). Time axis below. Labels: "Total: 400ms tool work + 2 LLM calls" and "Each tool waits for the prior LLM turn." Shows that in serial execution each tool call requires its own preceding LLM turn, so two lookups take two full LLM-call + tool-call cycles.
summary: Diagram of the serial timeline for ticket T2 with two LLM calls each followed by a 200ms tool lookup, totalling 400ms tool work plus 2 LLM calls.

text: Parallel timeline diagram for Ticket T2: one LLM call 1 bar followed by lookup_customer and lookup_subscription bars stacked vertically to show they run concurrently in the same 200ms slot. Time axis below. Labels: "Total: 200ms tool work + 1 LLM call" and "Both tools fan out at once." Visualizes that both tool_use blocks are emitted in the same assistant turn and execute concurrently, halving the tool-work latency and removing one LLM turn compared to the serial version.
summary: Diagram of the parallel timeline for ticket T2 where one LLM call fans out into two concurrent 200ms lookups, totalling 200ms tool work plus 1 LLM call.

text: This chapter covers two related agent controls: parallel tool calling and tool_choice. Parallel tool calling lets the model emit multiple tool_use blocks in one assistant turn that the runtime fans out concurrently, but only works when the calls don't read each other's output. Independent reads (lookup_customer + lookup_subscription on the same email) parallelize; dependent calls (process_refund needs customer_id) must serialize. Ticket T2 example halves tool latency from 400ms to 200ms and cuts one LLM turn. Ticket T4 example shows 6.8s serial → 4.7s parallel (31% faster). tool_choice has four modes: auto (model decides, default), required (must call some tool), none (must not call any tool), and specific {type: "tool", name: "X"} (force a named tool, used for deterministic routing where a classifier already picked the API). Mutations must still serialize even within a parallel flow - change_email before reset_password - because side effects depend on order.
summary: Parallel tool calling speeds up independent reads while dependent calls serialize, and tool_choice (auto/required/none/specific) controls how much routing autonomy the model has.

text: Ticket T2: "Reset my password but my email also changed to new@example.com" needs two facts before the agent can act: who is this customer, and what is their subscription. A naive serial runtime asks one tool, waits, asks the next, waits again. A parallel runtime emits both tool_use blocks in the same assistant turn and runs them concurrently. Serial steps: Step 1 LLM call 1 (1200ms), Step 2 lookup_customer (Tool, 200ms), Step 3 LLM call 2 (1200ms), Step 4 lookup_subscription (Tool, 200ms). Parallel steps: Step 1 LLM call 1 (1200ms), Step 2 lookup_customer + lookup_subscription concurrent (Tool, 200ms). Same two tool calls. Serial = 400ms tool work + 2 LLM turns. Parallel = 200ms tool work + 1 LLM turn. Parallel only works when the two calls do not read each other's output. For independent lookups, parallel halves the tool latency and cuts an entire LLM turn.
summary: Ticket T2 shows how serial vs parallel tool execution differs: parallel halves tool latency and saves an LLM turn when calls are independent.

text: Rule: independent calls go parallel, dependent calls must serialize. If call B needs an argument from call A's result, you cannot parallelize - the model has to wait for A before it can even form B. If neither call reads the other, fan out. Independent (Verdict: Parallel): lookup_customer(email) and lookup_subscription(email). Neither call reads the other's output. Both take an email and return a record. Both tool_use blocks are emitted in the SAME assistant turn. Runtime fans out, awaits both, returns both tool_results. Dependent (Verdict: Serial): lookup_customer(email) and process_refund(customer_id, ...). process_refund needs customer_id from lookup_customer's result. The second call cannot be formed until the first returns. Two assistant turns are required. Turn 1 = lookup. Turn 2 = refund (after the tool_result is appended). process_refund needs customer_id so lookup_customer must run first in its own turn. But lookup_customer and lookup_subscription both take the same email and return independent records, so they share one turn.
summary: Two tool calls can parallelize only when neither needs the other's output; dependent calls (process_refund needs customer_id) must serialize across turns.

text: Tool Choice = Auto (Default). By default, the model decides whether to call a tool, which tool, and how many tools. You set tool_choice: "auto" (or leave it unset) and let the model route. For a customer-support agent this covers most flows. Config: tool_choice: "auto". The model decides per-message. Scenarios under auto: (1) Ticket T5: "Pro vs Enterprise - what's different?" Model picks search_kb(query="Pro vs Enterprise"). Knowledge question. Model picks the docs tool. (2) User: "Hey, good morning!" Model picks no tool, plain assistant reply. Chitchat - calling a tool would be wasted latency. (3) Ticket T2: "Reset my password and email also changed." Model picks lookup_customer AND lookup_subscription. Two tool_use blocks in one turn - auto still picks, and it can pick more than one. Auto covers chitchat (no tool), single lookups, and parallel lookups all from one config. You only switch modes when you need to control the model's hand.
summary: tool_choice auto is the default mode where the model picks zero, one, or several tools per message, covering chitchat through parallel lookups.

text: Four modes of tool_choice. Each shape locks down the model's behavior a different amount. (1) auto: tool_choice: "auto". Model decides whether to call a tool, and which one(s). Use as default for most flows - chitchat, mixed Q&A, anything where the next step depends on the input. (2) required: tool_choice: "required". Model MUST call at least one tool. Plain-text replies are blocked. Use when intent is already routed (e.g., user clicked "Run a search") - forces the tool path. (3) none: tool_choice: "none". Model MUST NOT call any tool. It can only emit plain text. Use for final summarization, drafting a reply from already-gathered context, or pure chitchat. (4) specific: tool_choice: { type: "tool", name: "search_kb" }. Force a specific tool. Model can ONLY call this one named tool. Use for deterministic routing - your classifier already decided which API to call. The model fills in the arguments. Summary: auto = decide. required = must call something. none = must not call. specific = force this tool. The specific shape {type: "tool", name: "X"} is how you wire a deterministic router into the agent.
summary: The four tool_choice modes (auto, required, none, specific) trade model autonomy for caller control, from full-decision to deterministic forced routing.

text: Numbers - how much does parallel save? Ticket T4: "Cancel my subscription, refund this month, and have someone call me." Three independent lookups (customer, subscription, refund policy) feed one mutation (process_refund). Serial (4 turns): LLM call 1 (1500ms), lookup_customer (200ms), LLM call 2 (1500ms), lookup_subscription (200ms), LLM call 3 (1500ms), lookup_refund_policy (200ms), LLM call 4 (1500ms), process_refund (200ms). Total: 4 LLM calls of 1.5s + 4 tool calls of 200ms = 6.8s. 4 LLM calls. Parallel (2 turns): LLM call 1 (1500ms), lookup_customer + lookup_subscription + lookup_refund_policy concurrent (200ms), LLM call 2 (1500ms), process_refund (200ms), LLM call 3 (1300ms). Total: 3 LLM calls + 3 lookups concurrent + 1 mutation = 4.7s. 3 LLM calls. Savings: 6.8s → 4.7s (31% faster, one fewer LLM call). Each LLM turn costs ~1.5s, and you also save tool latency that would have stacked. For a busy support agent firing thousands of tickets a day, that 31% is real money and real customer wait time.
summary: Ticket T4 measures the speedup: collapsing three independent lookups into one turn drops 6.8s serial to 4.7s parallel, a 31% latency cut.

text: Trace: Ticket T2 with parallel lookups, end to end. Turn 1 is parallel (two lookups). Turns 2 and 3 are sequential because change_email must complete before reset_password sends the link to the new address. Step 1 User: "Reset my password but my email also changed to new@example.com" - two intents in one ticket. Step 2 Model (turn 1) [PARALLEL]: tool_use [lookup_customer(email=old) AND lookup_subscription(email=old)] - both blocks emitted in the SAME assistant turn, runtime fans them out in parallel. Step 3 Runtime [PARALLEL]: tool_result [customer_id=c-9924 AND subscription=active] - both finish at ~200ms concurrent, two tool_result blocks appended in parallel. Step 4 Model (turn 2): tool_use { name: change_email, input: { customer_id: "c-9924", new_email: "new@example.com" } } - change_email must complete before reset_password (reset link goes to NEW email). Step 5 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Email updated." } - side effect: customer record mutated. Step 6 Model (turn 3): tool_use { name: reset_password, input: { customer_id: "c-9924" } } - reset link sent to new@example.com. Step 7 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Reset email sent." } - runtime is the single chokepoint where real-world effects happen. Step 8 Model (turn 4): "Updated your email to new@example.com and sent a reset link there." Final assistant message, no more tool_use. First turn parallel; second turn sequential. Split is determined by data flow, not the model.
summary: Full ticket T2 trace shows parallel lookups in turn 1, then serialized change_email and reset_password mutations because order matters for side effects.

text: This chapter covers tool errors, retries, and validation in an agent loop. Production tools fail in four classes: Transient (retry with exponential backoff), Permanent (do not retry, surface), Malformed (return schema error to the model, one retry), and Business-rule (return structured policy reason, model adapts or escalates). Errors are returned as data not exceptions - a tool_result with is_error: true and an error_class field - so the model can read them like any other field and route accordingly. Retry budgets match the class: 3 attempts with exponential backoff (200ms, 400ms, 800ms) for transient, 0 for permanent and business-rule, 1 for malformed. A schema-validation gate runs in microseconds before the actual tool call, catching malformed arguments and giving the model a precise reason to fix on retry. Mutations safe to retry require an idempotency key derived from a stable input (invoice_id, request_id, UUID) so a retried 503 cannot double-refund. The Ticket T4 trace shows the loop working: a $350 refund over the $200 cap returns a business_rule error, the model autonomously switches to escalate_human, and the user sees a calm escalation message instead of a raw policy failure.
summary: Tool errors fall into four classes that drive distinct retry policies, validation gates the runtime cheaply, idempotency keys make mutations safe to retry, and structured errors let the model self-route to recovery.

text: Production tools fail and the retry strategy depends on WHY the call failed. A rate limit is not the same as a missing record, and a schema typo is not the same as a policy violation. Group every failure into one of four classes: Transient, Permanent, Malformed, Business-rule. Transient (error_class: "transient"): rate limit hit (HTTP 429), network timeout, upstream 503 - retry with exponential backoff because the call would probably succeed if you tried again in a moment. Permanent (error_class: "permanent"): auth failed (HTTP 401), customer not found, missing record - surface to user, do not retry because retrying changes nothing, the state, not the network, is the problem. Malformed (error_class: "malformed"): schema validation failed, missing required field, wrong type - return the error to the model and let it self-correct because the model wrote bad arguments and given the error message it usually fixes them on the next turn. Business-rule (error_class: "business_rule"): refund $350 > $200 auto-approve cap, blocked customer, outside policy window - return a structured "rule violated" reason so the model adapts or escalates because the call is syntactically valid but policy says no and the model needs the WHY to pick a different path. Classify on the way out of the tool, attach the class to the tool_result, and the rest of the agent loop knows what to do.
summary: Tool failures group into four classes (Transient, Permanent, Malformed, Business-rule) and each class dictates the retry and recovery strategy.

text:
The runtime does not throw a Python exception up the stack. It returns a structured tool_result with is_error set to true and an error_class field. The model reads those same fields it reads on a successful call and adapts. This is the whole reason an agent can recover from a business-rule violation without you writing special code. Example tool_result message (error):
{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01xyz...",
  "content": "Refund denied: amount $350 exceeds $200 auto-approve cap.",
  "is_error": true,
  "error_class": "business_rule"
}
is_error: true - the model reads this flag the same way it reads any other field. If true, treat content as the failure reason, not the answer. error_class: "business_rule" - the class tells the model whether to wait and retry, fix its arguments, escalate, or apologize. Same payload shape, different next action. $350 over the $200 cap = structured business_rule error, not a stack trace. Because the error is data, the model can route on it. The next assistant turn picks a different tool (escalate_human) on its own. You did not write "if business_rule then escalate" - the model inferred it from the message.
summary: Errors return as a structured tool_result with is_error and error_class fields so the model treats failures like any other data and adapts.

text: One retry policy across all errors is wrong. Retrying a permanent auth failure burns three round-trips for no reason. Not retrying a transient 503 fails a perfectly good call. The class drives the retry budget, the backoff schedule, and who handles it. Retry policy per class: Transient - 3 retries, exponential backoff 200ms, 400ms, 800ms, because brief downtime usually clears in under a second. Permanent - 0 retries, surface immediately, no backoff, no second attempt, because re-asking the same impossible question wastes turns and tokens. Malformed - 1 retry, return schema error to model, one retry, then escalate, because the model fixes its own arguments on the second try ~95% of the time. Business-rule - 0 retries, return policy reason, model chooses an alternate tool or escalates, because retrying never flips a policy and the model must adapt the plan. Transient retry budget: 3 attempts with exponential backoff (200ms, 400ms, 800ms). Exponential backoff matters because thundering-herd retries amplify outages. Each retry waits twice as long, which spreads load and gives the dependency a chance to recover before you slam it again.
summary: Retry budget and backoff schedule depend on the error class - transient gets 3 retries with exponential backoff, permanent and business-rule get 0, malformed gets 1.

text: Most malformed errors never need to hit the tool function. The runtime already has the JSON schema from the tool definition - it can check the model's arguments against required fields, types, enums, and formats in microseconds, before it ever opens an HTTP socket or starts a transaction. The validation gate has three steps: Step 1 - Model emits tool_use: assistant turn includes a tool_use block with input arguments. Step 2 - Runtime validates against schema: check required fields, types, enums, formats - cheap and instant. Step 3 - Branch on result: if invalid, return malformed error immediately with is_error: true and error_class: "malformed"; if valid, execute the tool function (HTTP call, DB write, etc.) which is expensive. Schema check (microseconds) gates the tool call (hundreds of milliseconds). Schema-level validation also gives the model a precise reason - "missing required field invoice_id" - which is much easier to fix than a generic 400 from the API. One retry usually does it.
summary: Schema validation at the runtime gate catches malformed tool calls in microseconds before any expensive HTTP or DB work runs.

text: Diagram: validation-layer flow for a tool call. The model emits a tool_use block at the top, then an arrow points down to a green Runtime: Schema Validation box that checks types, required fields, enums, and formats. From validation, two arrows branch: the left "Invalid" arrow points to a purple Return Malformed Error box that returns is_error: true with error_class: "malformed". The right "Valid" arrow points to a cyan Execute Tool Function box (HTTP call, DB write, etc.) labeled expensive. Footer reads: Cheap validation saves expensive function calls. The visual shows that schema validation is the gate between the model's emitted tool_use and the actual tool execution, with invalid arguments returning a structured malformed error without ever touching the real function.
summary: Diagram of the validation flow showing tool_use emitted by the model, validated by the runtime, then branched to malformed error or actual tool execution.

text: Reads are naturally safe to retry - they just return the same data. Mutations are different. process_refund without an idempotency key can double-refund if the runtime retries on a flaky 503 that actually succeeded. The fix is a key derived from a stable input so the backend can detect and dedupe the retry. Without Idempotency Key (unsafe to retry): signature process_refund(invoice_id, reason). Scenario: runtime retries on a 503 - but the first call actually succeeded. Outcome: customer gets refunded TWICE, $700 charged back instead of $350. Rule: never auto-retry a mutation without an idempotency contract. With Idempotency Key (safe to retry): signature process_refund(invoice_id, reason, idempotency_key). Scenario: key = hash(invoice_id), backend stores it, dedupes any retry. Outcome: second call returns the SAME refund_id as the first, customer charged once. Rule: derive the key from a stable input (invoice_id, request_id) so retries collide cleanly. idempotency_key = hash(invoice_id) - retries collide, no double refund. The pattern is universal: any mutation that can be retried needs a deterministic key. For refunds, invoice_id works. For password resets, request_id. For new orders, a client-generated UUID. Without it, retries are unsafe and you must either give up retries or risk double-spending.
summary: Mutations need an idempotency key derived from a stable input so retries collide and dedupe instead of causing double-spends like a double refund.

text: Ticket T4 trace: user says "Cancel my subscription and refund my last invoice ($350)." - one user message contains two intents and a refund amount over policy. Step 1 User: T4 message. Step 2 Model (turn 1): tool_use { name: lookup_customer, input: { email: "..." } } - read first, need customer_id before any mutation. Step 3 Runtime: tool_result { content: "customer_id: c-7741", is_error: false } - clean read, customer_id appended to the conversation. Step 4 Model (turn 2): tool_use { name: process_refund, input: { invoice_id: "inv-882", reason: "customer requested" } } - model attempts the refund directly, amount is $350 over the $200 auto-approve cap but the model does not know that yet. Step 5 Runtime: tool_result { is_error: true, error_class: "business_rule", content: "Refund $350 exceeds $200 auto-approve cap." } - business-rule error, no retry, the structured reason is the signal the model uses to adapt. Step 6 Model (turn 3): tool_use { name: escalate_human, input: { customer_id: "c-7741", reason: "refund $350 over policy cap", urgency: "medium" } } - adapts, switches plan to escalate_human with full context. Step 7 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Escalation queued: ticket-9921 assigned to billing-team." } - successful escalation, human will review and approve the $350. Step 8 Model (turn 4): final reply "I've escalated your refund to a human agent who can approve the $350. You'll hear back within one business day." - no more tool_use, agent recovered without surfacing a raw error to the user. Step 5 business_rule error -> step 6 escalate_human. Same loop, structured recovery. The user never sees the raw policy error - they see a calm escalation message.
summary: End-to-end trace of ticket T4 showing the model hit a business_rule refund cap error and adapt by escalating to a human without any special-case code.

text: Diagram: five agents on the top row (Agent A through E) and six tools on the bottom row (Search, Email, Refund, Calendar, CRM, Files), every agent connected to every tool by a red line, producing thirty crossed connectors. Visualizes the M times N ad-hoc tool sprawl problem - the hand-wired tangle when no protocol exists.
summary: A sprawl diagram with thirty crossed lines between five agents and six tools illustrates the M times N integration cost of hand-wired connectors.
terms: Search Email Refund Calendar CRM Files

text: Diagram: five agents (M = 5) on top connect down to a single Protocol hub box in the middle labeled Shared hub, which then connects to six tools (N = 6) below. Only eleven clean lines instead of thirty crossed ones. Visualizes how a protocol collapses M x N to M + N by routing every connection through one shared contract.
summary: A hub-and-spoke diagram shows agents and tools each connecting once to a central Protocol box, reducing thirty connectors to eleven and turning M times N into M plus N.

text: Diagram: concentric rings showing the sandbox contract. Outer teal ring is the Host (Trusted) holding user data, the model, and allowed mutations. Inner red dashed ring is the Server (Untrusted), third-party tool code. Three labeled arrows cross the boundary: Capabilities Listed (server to host), Tool Calls Authorized (host to server), Results Returned (server to host). Caption: 'The protocol is the only legal crossing.' Visualizes the trust boundary the protocol enforces.
summary: A concentric ring diagram shows a trusted host wrapping an untrusted tool server with three labeled arrows (capabilities listed, tool calls authorized, results returned) as the only protocol crossings of the sandbox boundary.

text: Why protocols matter for agents: ad-hoc tool wiring scales as M x N (5 agents x 6 tools = 30 connectors). A protocol turns the cost into M + N (11 connectors via a hub-and-spoke). Three protocols define different edges: MCP (Anthropic, agent to tools), A2A (Google, agent to agent), and HTTP + OpenAPI (legacy service transport). The production threshold is roughly 3 tools and 3 agents. Beyond wiring, a protocol enforces a sandbox contract between trusted host (user data, model) and untrusted server (third-party code) with capabilities listed, tool calls authorized, and results returned as the only legal crossings.
summary: This chapter explains why agent systems adopt protocols: to collapse integration cost from M times N to M plus N, to choose among MCP, A2A, and OpenAPI, and to enforce a sandbox trust boundary.

text: Ad-hoc tools don't scale. Every agent needs to call every tool. Without a shared protocol you end up writing a custom connector for each pair. With 5 agents and 6 tools that is already 30 bespoke integrations. Each new tool forces every agent to learn it; each new agent forces every tool's API to be re-wrapped. 5 Agents x 6 Tools = 30 Connectors. Hand-wired sprawl. The connectors grow as M x N - five times six is the small case, and it already looks like a tangle.
summary: Without a shared protocol, connecting M agents to N tools requires M times N bespoke integrations, which becomes unmanageable as either side grows.

text: A protocol turns M x N into M + N. A protocol is a shared contract that sits in the middle. Each agent speaks it once; each tool speaks it once. Same 5 agents, same 6 tools, but now 5 + 6 = 11 clean lines instead of 30. The hub-and-spoke shape is what makes the system additive instead of multiplicative. Add a 6th agent -> 1 new connector. Add a 7th tool -> 1 new connector. No agent rewrites a tool API ever again. Same number of components, drastically different cost of change. The math turns from O(M x N) to O(M + N).
summary: A shared protocol acts as a hub so each agent and each tool implements the contract once, collapsing integration cost from O(M times N) to O(M plus N).

text: MCP, A2A, OpenAPI. Three protocols you will run into. Each one picks a different shape of edge to standardize. MCP (Model Context Protocol) is Anthropic-led, connects Agent to Tools, Resources, Prompts; the dominant pattern for production agent tooling, production today. A2A (Agent To Agent) is Google-driven, connects Agent to Agent; lets a planner agent delegate work to specialist agents over a shared message envelope, newer Google-driven. HTTP + OpenAPI is the older general-purpose stack the rest of the internet runs on, Service to Service legacy transport; agents can call these but the agent host has to translate every endpoint into a tool definition by hand, legacy still common. MCP is the one most production agent stacks use today for tool access. A2A is newer and built for multi-agent systems. OpenAPI is not agent-specific - it is the underlying HTTP transport.
summary: Three protocols standardize different edges: MCP for agent-to-tool, A2A for agent-to-agent, and HTTP plus OpenAPI as the legacy service transport.

text: Production sign: 3+ tools or 3+ agents. When does the protocol layer pay off? Use the decision grid. Few Tools / Few Agents (1-2 tools, 1-2 agents): hand-wired connectors are still readable, skip the protocol layer until growth forces it; verdict Ad-Hoc Fine. Many Tools / Few Agents: tool list keeps growing but agent count is stable, standardize the tool side so new tools self-describe to the same agent; verdict Protocol On Tool Side. Few Tools / Many Agents: handful of high-value tools but many agents (user, planner, evaluator), standardize the agent side so every agent reads the same tool catalog; verdict Protocol On Agent Side. Many Tools / Many Agents: production scale, both sides must converge on same wire format or every release introduces O(M x N) integration work; verdict Full Protocol Mesh. Decision rule: cross 3 + 3 (3 tools and 3 agents) and you want a protocol. If new tools land monthly you cross the threshold sooner; if your tool set is frozen at 6, ad-hoc can survive longer.
summary: A two-by-two grid shows when to adopt a protocol; the 3 tools plus 3 agents threshold is the typical production tipping point, adjusted by rate of change.

text: Protocol = sandbox contract. Beyond reducing wiring, a protocol enforces a trust boundary. On the inside sits the host: user data, the model, and the mutations you are willing to authorize. On the outside sits the tool server: third-party code you do not control. The protocol is the only channel that crosses the boundary, and it dictates what may pass. The host is trusted (user data, model, allowed mutations); the server is untrusted. Across the boundary: capabilities listed (server to host), tool calls authorized (host to server), results returned (server to host). The protocol is the only legal crossing. The protocol defines what the inner can ask for and what the outer must approve. That sandbox boundary makes it safe to plug a third-party tool server into an agent that holds user data.
summary: A protocol also acts as a sandbox contract enforcing a trust boundary between the trusted host and untrusted tool server, with capability listing, authorized calls, and returned results as the only legal crossings.

text: Diagram of the three MCP roles stacked vertically. Top box (purple) is the Host - the user-facing app like Claude Desktop, your IDE, or a custom agent - owning the model, the UI, and the user data. Middle box (indigo) is the Client, which lives inside the host (one per server connection) and speaks the MCP wire protocol over a chosen transport. Bottom box (blue) is the Server, a separate process or remote service that provides tools, resources, and prompts. Arrows flow Host down to Client and Client down to Server, showing how a host hosts a client connection that speaks to a tool server.
summary: Describes the three-role stack diagram showing host hosting client speaking to server.

text: Topology diagram of one host containing three internal client connections, each linked downward to a separate MCP server. The outer purple box is the Host (Claude Desktop) which holds three indigo client boxes labeled Client 1, Client 2, and Client 3 - each marked 'MCP connection'. Lines drop from each client to its own blue server box at the bottom: Server 1 (Postgres tool), Server 2 (Linear tool), and Server 3 (Filesystem tool), each a separate process. The caption notes that one host orchestrates many client connections, each to its own server.
summary: Describes the hub-topology diagram with one host, three clients, and three separate tool servers.

text: Five-step horizontal flow diagram of the MCP session lifecycle. Step 1: Host Launches Client - host creates a client connection per server. Step 2: Client Connects To Server - stdio child process or HTTP/SSE socket opens. Step 3: Initialize Handshake - both sides exchange protocol version and capabilities. Step 4: List Available Tools / Resources / Prompts - server tells the host what it can do. Step 5: Call A Tool, Get A Result - host invokes a named capability and receives the response. Each step is a cyan box numbered 1 through 5 with a short note underneath; arrows connect them left to right.
summary: Describes the five-step horizontal flow diagram of the MCP session lifecycle.

text: This chapter covers MCP architecture: three roles (Host, Client, Server) where the host owns the user app, the client is an in-process adapter, and the server is a separate process exposing tools, resources, and prompts. Topology is a hub - one host runs many clients, each connected to a distinct server, and the host merges all tool catalogs for the model. Three transports carry the same JSON-RPC wire format: Stdio for local child processes, HTTP for remote hosted servers, SSE for streaming updates over HTTP. A session follows five lifecycle steps: host launches client, client connects to server, Initialize Handshake exchanges protocol version and capabilities, list returns available tools/resources/prompts, then call a tool. Discovery via tools/list returns each tool's name, description, and input JSON schema, which the host passes to the model as tool definitions - enabling plug-and-play new capabilities without host code changes.
summary: Recaps the MCP roles, hub topology, transports, session lifecycle, and discovery mechanism in one overview.

text: MCP splits the agent stack into three roles: Host, Client, Server. The Host is the application the user runs - it owns the UI, the model, and the user data. Examples: Claude Desktop, your IDE, a custom agent app. The Client is a connection living inside the host; the host creates one client per server it wants to talk to, and the client speaks the MCP wire protocol. The Server is a separate process or remote service that exposes tools, resources, and prompts. The host never knows about them until the server lists them. Three roles, one wire format. The host always owns the trust boundary; the server always sits on the other side; the client is the in-process adapter that connects them.
summary: Introduces the three MCP roles - Host, Client, Server - and how they divide responsibility in an agent stack.

text: The typical MCP topology is a hub. One host launches many clients - one per server it wants to talk to. Each client maintains its own connection. The host stitches all the listed tools into a single catalog the model can pick from. Example: one host with 3 clients, each wired to a different server: Client 1 to a Postgres tool server, Client 2 to a Linear tool server, Client 3 to a Filesystem tool server. Each client owns one server connection. The host is the hub - it adds or drops servers without the servers ever talking to each other. The Postgres server has no idea the Linear server is also connected, and the model sees one merged toolset from all three.
summary: Explains the standard MCP hub topology where one host runs many clients each connected to its own server.

text: The MCP wire format is the same JSON-RPC regardless of how the bytes travel. Three transports in common use: Stdio - Local only. Host launches the server as a child process and pipes JSON-RPC messages over stdin and stdout. Fastest, no network. Used by Claude Desktop and local MCP servers running on your machine. HTTP - Network. Standard request and response over HTTPS. Supports remote servers across the internet. Needs auth headers and TLS like any web API. Used when the server is a hosted service. SSE (Server-Sent Events) - Network plus streaming. One-way streaming channel from server to host on top of HTTP. Lets the server push intermediate updates without polling. Used when the server needs to push partial results, progress, or long-running notifications. Pick stdio when the server runs on the same machine and you want zero network surface. Pick HTTP when the server is hosted elsewhere. Pick SSE on top of HTTP when the server needs to stream progress back.
summary: Covers the three MCP transports - Stdio, HTTP, and SSE - and when to choose each.

text: An MCP session follows a fixed lifecycle: connect, then negotiate, then discover, then call. Five steps: 1. Host Launches Client - the host creates a client connection per server. 2. Client Connects To Server - stdio child process or HTTP/SSE socket opens. 3. Initialize Handshake - both sides exchange protocol version and capabilities. 4. List Available Tools / Resources / Prompts - server tells the host what it can do. 5. Call A Tool, Get A Result - host invokes a named capability and receives the response. The lifecycle matters because the host cannot just call a tool blind. It must finish the Initialize Handshake (so both sides agree on protocol version) and then call list before it knows any tool names. Only after list returns does the model see a populated tool catalog.
summary: Walks through the five-step MCP session lifecycle from launch through handshake, discovery, and tool call.
Shows a concrete tools/list JSON response and how the host merges catalogs into model tool definitions.

text: This chapter introduces the three primitives an MCP server exposes: Tools (callable actions the model invokes, e.g. process_refund), Resources (read-only data the host fetches by URI, e.g. kb://articles/password-reset), and Prompts (parameterized templates the user picks, e.g. summarize_ticket). Tools mutate state and are model-chosen; Resources supply context and are host-chosen; Prompts are slash-command templates user-chosen. Decision rule: tool if side-effecting, resource if read-only data, prompt if reusable template.
summary: Overview of the three MCP primitives - Tools, Resources, Prompts - and which actor invokes each.

text: An MCP server exposes three kinds of capabilities. Tools are callable actions the model can invoke, e.g. process_refund(invoice_id, reason) - action, the model picks one and calls it. Resources are readable data the host can pull in, e.g. kb://articles/password-reset - data, the host pulls it in and shows it to the model. Prompts are parameterized templates the host can surface to the user, e.g. summarize_ticket(ticket_id, max_words) - template, the user picks one and the host fills the slots. The split matters because each primitive has a different access pattern: the model decides when to call a tool, the host decides when to read a resource, the user picks which prompt to invoke.
summary: Side-by-side intro to the three MCP primitives with their example signatures and who decides when each is invoked.

text: Tool: process_refund. A Tool is a named action with a typed input schema. It usually has side effects: it mutates state, calls an external API, or spends money. The model picks one from the tool list and emits a tool call with the required arguments. inputSchema: { invoice_id: string, reason: string }. SIDE EFFECT - Mutation: $-amount returned to the customer's card. The model chooses when to call. Tools always go through validation before the server actually runs the action. The inputSchema is how the host (and the model) know what arguments are required and what type each one must be.
summary: Detailed look at MCP Tools using process_refund - typed input schema, side effects, model-chosen invocation, and validation.

text: Resource: kb://articles/password-reset. A Resource is read-only data the server exposes under a URI. The host fetches it on demand and feeds the content into the model's context, the same way you might attach a PDF in a chat. The model never calls a resource itself. RESOURCE URI: kb://articles/password-reset → Markdown body of the doc (read-only). The host decides when to read - e.g., before calling the model on a ticket. Resources are READ-ONLY by convention. The URI scheme is server-defined: it could be kb://, file://, github://, postgres://, or anything else the server exposes. The host treats it as an opaque identifier and asks the server to resolve it when needed.
summary: Resources are read-only URI-addressed data the host pulls into model context, with server-defined URI schemes.

text: Prompt: summarize_ticket. A Prompt is a parameterized template the server publishes. The host surfaces these as slash-commands or quick actions for the user. When the user picks one, the host collects the arguments, fills the template, and sends the resulting message to the model. MCP Prompt (Shape): { "name": "summarize_ticket", "description": "Summarize a support ticket conversation.", "arguments": [ { "name": "ticket_id", "required": true }, { "name": "max_words", "required": false } ] }. The arguments array lists each parameter the template needs, with a required flag. ticket_id is required, max_words is optional. The host can either prompt the user for missing values or fill defaults before invoking.
summary: Prompts are user-invoked parameterized templates shown as slash-commands, with named required/optional arguments.

text: Action vs Data vs Template - the decision rule. When you design an MCP server, every capability falls into exactly one bucket. Ask what the model or host needs to DO, READ, or REUSE. Need to DO something (mutate state, call API)? → TOOL. Need to READ something (fact, doc, file)? → RESOURCE. Need a reusable prompt template the user can invoke? → PROMPT. Decision rule: tool if side-effecting; resource if read-only data; prompt if template the user picks. Putting a read-only doc behind a tool wastes a model turn. Putting an action behind a resource hides it from the model entirely. Putting a one-shot question behind a prompt hides it from end users. The split is the contract.
summary: Decision framework for picking the right MCP primitive based on whether the need is action, data, or template - plus failure modes of miscategorizing.

text: Four-stage server lifecycle diagram showing REGISTERED then LISTENING then ACTIVE handling requests then CLOSED shutdown, with transition arrows labeled connect, initialize, and shutdown. Each state box shows label and a short note: REGISTERED (Definitions Declared), LISTENING (Transport Open), ACTIVE (Handling List/Call Requests), CLOSED (Shutdown). Arrows: REGISTERED -connect()-> LISTENING -initialize-> ACTIVE -shutdown()-> CLOSED.
summary: Diagram of the four MCP server lifecycle states with labeled transitions connect, initialize, and shutdown.

text: This chapter walks through building an MCP server: declare identity (name, version), register tools, resources, and prompts, then start the transport. Each registration carries a handler that runs server-side. Example registrations: a search_kb tool with input schema, a kb://articles/{id} resource with URI pattern, and a summarize_ticket prompt with arguments. The server moves through four lifecycle states - REGISTERED, LISTENING, ACTIVE, CLOSED - via connect(), initialize, and shutdown(), spending most of its life in ACTIVE handling list/call/read/prompt-get. Test handlers, list responses, transport handshake, and use a mock host before connecting to a real LLM.
summary: Overview of building an MCP server: declarations of tools/resources/prompts, the four-state lifecycle, and testing strategies before connecting to a real host.

text: An MCP server is mostly a sequence of declarations followed by a start. You give it a name and version, then register tools, resources, and prompts, then call start. The server itself is short. The handlers do the real work. The five phases of an MCP server skeleton: (1) Define Identity - Name, Version. (2) Declare Tools - callable actions with schemas. (3) Declare Resources - URI patterns the host can read. (4) Declare Prompts - parameterized templates users can pick. (5) Start - begin listening on transport. Each declaration registers what the server offers; start opens the connection.
summary: MCP server skeleton has five phases: define identity, declare tools, declare resources, declare prompts, then start listening on transport.
Registering a tool requires name, description, input schema, and a server-side handler; only the handler stays on the server.

text:
Resources are addressed by URI patterns. The server registers a template like kb://articles/{id}; the host requests a concrete URI; the server matches the template and runs the handler to produce the content. Resource registration shape:

Server.registerResource({
  uriPattern: "kb://articles/{id}",
  description: "Knowledge base article by ID.",
  handler: async ({ id }) => readMarkdown(id)
})

Example flow: Host requests kb://articles/password-reset. Server matches the template, reads the markdown doc, returns the body.
summary: Resources are URI-patterned content; the server matches a host's concrete URI against the template and the handler returns the body.
Prompts are named templates with declared arguments; the host collects values from the user and the server materializes the final prompt text.

text: From the moment the server boots until it shuts down, it moves through four states. Most of the lifetime is spent in ACTIVE, where it handles list and call requests from the host. Four lifecycle states with transitions: REGISTERED (definitions declared) -> connect() -> LISTENING (transport open) -> initialize -> ACTIVE (handling list/call requests) -> shutdown() -> CLOSED (shutdown). Most requests are handled in ACTIVE: list, call, read, prompt-get.
summary: Server lifecycle moves through REGISTERED, LISTENING, ACTIVE, CLOSED via connect, initialize, and shutdown transitions; ACTIVE handles list/call/read/prompt-get.

text: An MCP server is just a process with handlers and a transport. You can - and should - test every piece in isolation before plugging it into a real host with a real model. Four testing strategies: (1) Test handler functions as plain unit tests - call them directly with sample inputs and assert the returned value. (2) Test list responses - assert all declared tools, resources, and prompts appear in the catalog. (3) Test transport connection - spin the server up over stdio or HTTP and assert the initialize handshake completes. (4) Use a mock host before connecting to a real LLM - drive list and call from a scripted host to catch contract bugs without paying for tokens. Production hygiene: test the server in isolation, then wire it to the host.
summary: Four testing strategies for an MCP server: unit-test handlers, verify list responses, test transport handshake, and use a mock host before a real LLM.

text: Trust boundary diagram: outer ring is the Host (Trusted) holding user data, the model, and allowed mutations. Inner dashed red ring is the Server (Untrusted) running third-party tool code. Three arrows cross the boundary: List (server -> host, cyan), Call (host -> server, blue), Result (server -> host, green). Caption: the MCP transport is the only legal crossing.
summary: Diagram of MCP trust boundary with outer trusted host ring around inner untrusted server ring and List/Call/Result arrows crossing the transport.

text: Process isolation diagram: a Host Process box on top (owns user data and model) spawns a Server Process box below it via a dashed Spawn arrow. The server process sits inside a dashed Sandbox container. The MCP transport is the only legal channel between host and server.
summary: Diagram showing host process spawning a sandboxed server process with MCP transport as the only legal channel between them.

text: OAuth flow diagram: four numbered boxes left to right - 1) User Authorizes, 2) Host Gets Access Token, 3) Token Passes To Server, 4) Server Checks Scope - with arrows between them. Title: Grant -> Token -> Request -> Check. Footer: The Access Token is the proof the server demands on every call.
summary: Diagram of four-step OAuth flow from user authorization through access token issuance to per-request scope check at the server.

text: MCP security stacks five defenses around untrusted third-party server code: (1) Trust boundary - host owns user data and model, server runs untrusted code, MCP transport is the only legal crossing. (2) Sandbox process isolation - separate OS process, transport-only communication, no filesystem by default, no outbound network by default. (3) Capability scope per host - allow/deny on tool names, URI prefix patterns for resources, global read-only flag. (4) OAuth over HTTP - user grants scopes once, host gets access token, token rides every request, server checks scope (stdio skips this). (5) Consent prompts and audit log - user approves side-effecting calls, every call logged with timestamp/tool/input/result/user/decision.
summary: MCP security combines trust boundary, sandbox isolation, capability scope, OAuth, and consent plus audit to make third-party tool code safe to run.

text: An MCP server is third-party code (open source, vendor-supplied, or written by a teammate), and the host never assumes it is safe. The host holds the user data, the model, and every mutation worth caring about. The server holds the tool implementation. The boundary between them is what makes the architecture safe. The host owns the user data and the model; the server runs third-party tool code; the MCP protocol crossing the boundary is the only thing the host has to police. Crossings: List (server to host), Call (host to server), Result (server to host).
summary: MCP server code is untrusted by default and the MCP transport is the only legal crossing between trusted host and untrusted server.

text: Sandbox: Process Isolation. The host launches the server in its own OS process. The host can spawn it, kill it, and shape what it sees, but never shares memory with it. Whatever capability the server wants, it must ask for over the transport. Rules: Transport Only - the server communicates with the host only over the MCP transport, no back channel. Filesystem Walled Off - no filesystem access except directories the host explicitly grants. Network Off By Default - no outbound network unless the host opts in for this server. The sandbox is the OS-level fence; even if the tool code is buggy or hostile, it cannot reach beyond the resources the host hands it.
summary: MCP servers run in an isolated OS process sandbox with no shared memory, no back channel, no filesystem access by default, and no outbound network.

text: Capability Scope Per Host. Even when a server declares ten tools and a hundred resources, the host narrows what this agent may touch. Scope is a subset of what the server advertises, configured per host. Three dimensions: ALLOWED TOOLS - subset of declared tools; the host picks which tools each agent may call (example: deny `escalate_human` for junior agents). ALLOWED RESOURCES - prefix-matched URIs; resource access gated by URI prefix patterns (example: allow `kb://articles/*` but not `kb://internal/*`). ALLOWED MUTATION - read-only mode flag; forces every tool call to be non-mutating regardless of declaration (example: read-only mode for evaluation runs). Capability scope is allow/deny lists at three levels: tool names, resource URI prefixes, and a global read-only flag.
summary: Hosts narrow what an agent can do via three scope dimensions: allowed tools, prefix-matched resource URIs, and a global read-only flag.

text: OAuth: Who Granted What. When the server lives over HTTP, the host must prove the user authorized it. OAuth is the standard answer: the user grants scopes once, the host exchanges that grant for an Access Token, and that token rides along on every server request. Four steps: 1) User Authorizes, 2) Host Gets Access Token, 3) Token Passes To Server, 4) Server Checks Scope. Flow: Grant -> Token -> Request -> Check. The Access Token is the proof the server demands on every call. Stdio servers skip OAuth because they run under local trust. HTTP servers require it so the host can prove which user authorized the call.
summary: HTTP-based MCP servers use OAuth so the host can prove user authorization by attaching an access token whose scope the server checks on every call.

text: Consent + Audit: User Sees Everything. Sandbox, scope, and OAuth handle the machine side; consent and audit handle the human side. The host asks the user before any side-effecting call and writes every call to a log the user can review. Consent Prompts: the host blocks the call until the user clicks approve or deny. Example prompt: `About to call process_refund / For invoice INV-9924 / Reason: "Customer Requested" / Allow? [Approve] [Deny]`. Required for side-effecting tools. Audit Log records timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and consent decision. Example rows: 14:02:11 lookup_customer { id: "C-104" } OK alice@co Auto; 14:02:34 process_refund { invoice: "INV-9924" } OK alice@co Approved; 14:03:02 send_email { to: "cust@x" } OK alice@co Approved; 14:05:50 delete_account { id: "C-104" } Blocked alice@co Denied. Sandbox keeps server from reaching outside; scope keeps it from doing more than allowed; OAuth proves user granted access; consent and audit keep user in control of every effect.
summary: Consent prompts block side-effecting calls until user approves and an audit log records timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and decision for every call.

text: Two row comparison diagram. Top row labeled MCP shows one agent box on the left connected by dashed lines to four separate tool boxes on the right, with caption 'Agent calls function'. Bottom row labeled A2A shows one agent box on the left with a solid arrow into a larger Other Agent box on the right that contains three smaller tool boxes inside its boundary, with caption 'Agent delegates whole task to another agent'.
summary: Side-by-side diagram contrasting MCP (agent fans out to four external tools) with A2A (agent delegates to another agent that owns its own tools).

text: Five step delegation flow diagram for ticket T4. Vertical stack of five numbered blue boxes connected by downward arrows: step 1 triage agent classifies ticket T4 as billing, step 2 triage looks up agents with skill 'billing', step 3 triage sends delegation request with full ticket context, step 4 billing agent works the ticket, step 5 billing agent returns result to triage.
summary: Numbered five-step vertical flow showing classify, lookup, delegate with context, work, and return result for ticket T4.

text: Vertical timeline diagram of streaming updates. Top: Triage Agent box. A 'Delegate' arrow points down to a Billing Agent box. Below the billing agent, four stacked update boxes stream leftward arrows back toward the triage column: 'Looked up customer...' (blue), 'Processing refund...' (blue), 'Refund > $200, need approval' (yellow), and 'Final result returned' (green). Footer caption: 'Triage stays informed without polling'.
summary: Timeline diagram showing the billing agent streaming four intermediate updates (lookup, processing, approval, final) back to the triage agent.

text: A2A is the agent-to-agent protocol that complements MCP. Where MCP lets an agent borrow a single tool or resource, A2A lets an agent delegate a whole sub-problem to a peer agent that runs its own model, memory, and tools. Agents publish an agent.json card (name, description, skills, endpoint, auth) so peers can discover them by skill. A delegation flow classifies the work, finds a matching skill, packages context (including conversation history), sends the request, and waits for a result. Because delegated work can take minutes or need approval, A2A supports streaming intermediate updates back to the caller. Choose MCP for function calls and document reads; choose A2A only when you need an entire collaborator.
summary: Chapter overview of A2A: discovery via agent.json, delegation flow with full context, streaming updates, and when to pick A2A over MCP.

text: Agent to tool, agent to agent. MCP handles one direction: an agent reaches out to a tool and gets a value back. A2A handles the other: an agent hands an entire problem to a different agent and lets that agent run its own loop. Same wire idea, very different scope. With MCP, the agent stays in charge and just borrows a tool. With A2A, the agent hands the wheel to a peer that runs its own model, memory, and tools to finish the job.
summary: Contrasts MCP (agent calls a tool) with A2A (agent delegates a whole task to another agent that runs its own loop).
Each A2A agent publishes an agent.json card with name, description, skills, endpoint, and auth so peers can discover and delegate.

text:
Triage delegates to billing. A real delegation has a shape: a triage agent classifies an incoming ticket, looks up who has the right skill, packages the context, sends the request, and waits for the specialist to come back with a result.

Delegation flow for ticket T4:
1. Triage agent classifies ticket T4 as billing
2. Triage looks up agents with skill 'billing'
3. Triage sends delegation request with full ticket context
4. Billing agent works the ticket
5. Billing agent returns result to triage

Delegation includes the conversation history so the billing agent has context.
summary: Walks through five concrete delegation steps: classify ticket, look up skill, send request with context, work the ticket, return result.

text:
Long tasks stream progress. A delegated task is not a single function call. The receiving agent may need minutes, human approval, or several tool calls of its own. A2A solves this by letting the receiving agent stream intermediate updates back instead of going silent until the final result is ready.

Streaming updates from billing agent:
- Looked up customer...
- Processing refund...
- Refund > $200, need approval
- Final result returned

Streaming lets triage show the user progress in real time and react if an intermediate update needs human approval before the long task finishes.
summary: A2A supports streaming intermediate updates so a delegating agent sees progress and approval needs in real time.

text:
Two protocols, two roles. MCP and A2A live side by side, not in competition. The decision is about what you need from the other side: a function, a document, or an entire collaborator.

Decision rows:
- Need to call a function? -> MCP tool
- Need to read a doc? -> MCP resource
- Need an entire other agent to handle a sub-problem? -> A2A delegation

A2A is heavier than MCP. Use it when the delegated work needs the receiving agent's own loop, memory, and tools - not just a single function call.
summary: Decision rules: call a function uses MCP tool, read a doc uses MCP resource, hand off a sub-problem uses A2A delegation.

text: Four node directed acyclic graph showing fixed deterministic workflow with predictable edges. Nodes labeled A, B, C, D are arranged horizontally with arrows pointing A -> B -> C -> D. Every run follows the same path; the developer wires the edges in advance and the model executes each step.
summary: Diagram of a fixed four-node DAG (A B C D) illustrating a deterministic workflow.

text: Central decision node with multiple branching options showing an open loop where the model picks different paths each run. A red 'Decide' circle in the middle has dashed lines reaching out to four tool boxes labeled Tool 1, Tool 2, Tool 3, Tool 4 placed at the corners, plus a self-loop arrow indicating the loop repeats. Each iteration the model chooses which tool to invoke; different runs traverse different sequences.
summary: Diagram of an agent open loop: a central Decide node branches to four tools with a self-loop iteration arrow.

text: Hybrid workflow showing a four node DAG where the second node is replaced by an agent loop with a question mark indicating the model decides while the other three nodes remain deterministic. Sequence: Classify (yellow rectangle, deterministic) -> Handle (red circle with self-loop and '?', agent step) -> Reply (yellow rectangle, deterministic) -> Log (yellow rectangle, deterministic). Demonstrates the most common production pattern: a deterministic skeleton around a single creative agent node.
summary: Diagram of a hybrid pipeline (Classify, Handle-agent, Reply, Log) with one agent node inside a deterministic DAG.

text: Workflows and agents are two shapes of control. A workflow is a fixed DAG that you wire in advance and the model follows the wires. An agent is an open loop where the model picks the next step, tool, and stopping point every iteration. Pick workflow when steps are knowable, outputs are predictable, and cost/latency must be bounded. Pick agent when steps are variable, the model must choose tools and order, and a task could take 2 or 20 calls. Most production systems are hybrid: a deterministic outer DAG with one agent step inside (Classify -> Handle (agent) -> Reply -> Log). Failure shapes differ too: workflow fails loud and bounded; agent fails soft and unbounded. Decision rule: workflow when you can, agent when you must.
summary: Overview of workflow vs agent control shapes, when to pick each, hybrid pattern, and cost/failure tradeoffs.

text: Two shapes of control. A workflow is a fixed DAG: you wire the steps and the model follows the wires. An agent is an open loop: the model picks the next step every iteration. Same building blocks, very different control flow. Left side shows a workflow as nodes A -> B -> C -> D with fixed edges - the flow is the same every run. Right side shows an agent as a central Decide node with dashed branches to Tool 1, Tool 2, Tool 3, Tool 4 and a self-loop arrow - different runs take different paths. Workflow is a fixed graph you draw. Agent is an open loop where the model picks the next move. Choose the shape that matches how much variance the task actually has.
summary: Introduces workflow as a fixed DAG and agent as an open loop driven by the model's choices each iteration.

text: Workflow: known inputs, known steps. Workflow is the right shape when you already know what to do. The model is a smart cell inside a bigger pipeline you wrote, not the conductor. Pick this when the task is repeatable and the steps are knowable in advance. Pick workflow when: you can list the steps in advance; each step's output type is predictable; you need to guarantee certain steps execute; the cost / latency must be bounded. Example: when a ticket comes in: Classify -> Route -> Handle. Always those 3 steps. The model still calls tools and writes text. But you, not the model, decide which step runs in which order. The output of each step is predictable enough to wire to the next.
summary: Workflow fits when steps are known, outputs predictable, and cost/latency bounded; the developer is the conductor.

text: Agent: variable steps, variable tools. Agent is the right shape when you can't pre-draw the graph. The model is the conductor: it decides which tool to call, in what order, and when to stop. You pay for that flexibility with cost and unpredictability. Pick agent when: you don't know the steps in advance; the model needs to decide which tools to call and in what order; the task could take 2 calls or 20; you're willing to pay for flexibility. Example: research-style task - 'Summarize all customer issues from the past week.' Agent decides which docs to read, which to skip, and when it has enough. One ticket might need 2 tool calls. The next might need 20. You can't draw that graph in advance because the shape of the task only becomes visible after the model starts digging. That is the whole point of an agent.
summary: Agent fits when steps are unknown, tool order is model-driven, and tasks span 2 to 20+ calls.

text: Most production systems are hybrid. Real systems are rarely pure. The common pattern is a workflow with one agent step inside. You keep the outer skeleton deterministic and let the model loose only inside the one node that actually needs creativity. Hybrid: outer DAG, inner agent loop. Diagram shows Classify (Deterministic) -> Handle (Agent step, drawn as a circle with a self-loop and a question mark) -> Reply (Deterministic) -> Log (Deterministic). The Classify step is deterministic. The Handle step is an agent. Reply and Log are deterministic again. This is the hybrid shape almost every production agent ships with. You get the safety and predictability of a workflow around the parts you can fully spec, and the freedom of an agent only where you actually need it.
summary: Production systems usually wrap a single agent step inside an otherwise deterministic outer DAG.

text: Predictable cost vs variable cost. The biggest practical difference between workflow and agent is the shape of failure. Workflow failures are loud and bounded. Agent failures are quiet and unbounded - the agent keeps trying and the bill keeps climbing. Workflow - bounded and predictable: Cost fixed per run; Latency bounded by slowest step; Failure hard crash, loud error. Agent - variable and soft-failing: Cost variable, depends on iterations; Latency variable, 2 calls or 20; Failure soft-fail, gets stuck instead of crashing. Decision rule: Workflow when you can; agent when you must. The cost of an extra workflow node is one more tool call. The cost of an extra agent iteration is an unbounded number of tool calls. That asymmetry is why teams default to workflow and reach for an agent only on the steps that genuinely need it.
summary: Workflow cost/latency/failure are bounded and loud; agent's are variable and soft-fail, so default to workflow.

text: Three node chain showing step A flowing to step B flowing to step C as sequential arrows in a horizontal pipeline. Illustrates the chaining primitive as a left-to-right sequence of three boxes labeled A, B, C connected by arrows.
summary: Mini diagram of a three-node A-B-C chain illustrating sequential chaining.

text: Routing topology showing an input flowing to a classifier node which then branches out to three different downstream paths based on intent. Illustrates how one input fans out through a classifier into multiple possible branches, only one of which runs at a time.
summary: Mini diagram of routing topology showing input flowing through a classifier to one of three branches.

text: Parallelization topology showing an input fanning out to three concurrent worker nodes that all feed into a single aggregator node. Illustrates the fan-out plus merge pattern where N workers run simultaneously and their outputs combine at an aggregator.
summary: Mini diagram of the parallelization primitive showing fan-out to three workers and a merging aggregator.

text: Three node chain with JSON output labels above each arrow showing how the structured output of one step becomes the structured input of the next step in a billing ticket example. The arrows are annotated with JSON objects like { "text": "can't bill" } and { "category": "billing" } to show typed data flowing between Ticket, Classifier, and Handler.
summary: Detailed chain diagram annotated with JSON payloads on each arrow, demonstrating structured output flowing between steps.

text: Routing diagram showing a ticket flowing to an intent classifier that branches to billing, product, troubleshooting, and chitchat handler boxes based on classified intent. Illustrates how one classifier dispatches to four distinct branch handlers.
summary: Detailed routing diagram showing an intent classifier dispatching a ticket to one of four branch handlers.

text: Fan-out diagram showing one query branching to three concurrent workers running in parallel then merging into a single aggregator node with timing bars showing that total latency equals the slowest worker time. Worker timings shown are 180ms, 140ms, and 210ms, with horizontal bars visualizing concurrent execution; aggregate latency is 210ms, not the 530ms sum.
summary: Detailed parallelization diagram with timing bars showing total latency equals the slowest of three concurrent workers.

text: Composed workflow topology stacking all three primitives where a ticket chains into a classifier that routes to one of three branches, one branch fans out to three parallel retrieval calls that merge before chaining into a final answer node. The retrievals shown are search_kb, search_docs, and search_tickets feeding a Merge node which then chains to the Answer node.
summary: Composed topology diagram stacking chain, route, parallel, and chain into one production pipeline.

text: Workflows are composed from three primitives: chaining (sequential steps where output of A feeds input of B via JSON), routing (an LLM classifier picks one of N branches), and parallelization (fan out N concurrent workers, latency = max not sum, aggregator merges results). Real production pipelines stack all three: chain into a classifier, route to a branch, parallelize heavy retrieval, chain to a final answer. A support workflow uses routing at step 2, chaining inside the billing branch, and parallelization inside the troubleshooting branch. The model is a smart cell inside a graph wired in advance - this distinguishes a workflow from an agent.
summary: Chapter summary of the three workflow primitives, how they stack, and how a wired workflow differs from an agent.

text: Workflows are built from three composable primitives: chaining, routing, and parallelization. Chaining wires step A's output into step B as a sequence. Routing classifies the input and picks one branch out of N. Parallelization fans out N workers concurrently and merges their results. Every production workflow stacks these three primitives in layers. Chaining is a sequence, routing is a fork, parallelization is fan-out plus merge. These are the lego bricks of every multi-step LLM pipeline.
summary: Introduces the three workflow primitives - chain, route, parallelize - as the building blocks of every multi-step LLM pipeline.

text: Chaining is the simplest primitive: the structured output of one LLM call becomes the structured input of the next. The glue is JSON. Step A returns a typed object, your code reads it, and feeds the next step. Example chain: Ticket -> Classifier -> Handler. Step A output: { "category": "billing", "confidence": 0.92 }. Step B input: { "category": "billing", "ticket": "Can't bill my card." }. Because each step returns a typed object (the JSON schema from this chapter), the next step can read fields like ticket.category by name. Chaining is normal function composition with LLM calls as the functions.
summary: Explains chaining as JSON-glued function composition where each LLM call's typed output becomes the next call's input.

text: Routing classifies the input and picks one branch. The classifier is itself an LLM call that returns a typed label. Your code then dispatches to the matching handler. Only the chosen branch runs. Example: an intent classifier picks one of four branches - Billing, Product, Troubleshooting, or Chitchat. One classification call replaces a hand-written if/else tree. The model labels the input, your code dispatches the matching branch handler. The classifier uses few-shot examples (see section 24.3). Adding a new branch is one new few-shot example plus one new handler.
summary: Shows how routing uses an LLM classifier to dispatch input to one of several branches, replacing hand-written if/else trees.

text: Parallelization runs N independent calls concurrently and aggregates the results. The total latency is the time of the slowest worker, not the sum. This is the cheapest way to cut wait time on independent sub-tasks. Example: fan out to 3 workers with timings 180ms, 140ms, and 210ms, then aggregate. If three retrieval calls each take 150-210ms, running them in series costs ~540ms. Running them concurrently costs ~210ms, the slowest one. Latency = max(worker time), not sum. The aggregator then merges all results into one structured object.
summary: Explains parallelization as concurrent fan-out where total latency equals the slowest worker, not the sum of workers.

text: Production workflows compose all three primitives. A typical pipeline: chain into a classifier, route to one of several branches, parallelize the heavy retrieval inside one branch, then chain the merged result into a final answer step. Example composed topology: Ticket -> Classifier -> [Billing | Troubleshoot | Chitchat] where the Troubleshoot branch fans out to search_kb, search_docs, and search_tickets in parallel, merges into Merge, then chains to Answer. Every primitive does one thing well; the real power comes from stacking them. You combine sequence, branch, and fan-out the same way you combine if, switch, and map in normal code.
summary: Demonstrates a composed workflow that stacks chain, route, parallel, and chain into a single production pipeline.

text: Concrete support agent workflow written as a primitive stack: Step 1 Ticket Arrives (Chain) - user message hits the inbound queue. Step 2 Classifier / Intent Classification (Route) - routes to Billing, Troubleshooting, or Escalation. Step 3 Billing Branch (Chain) - lookup_customer -> lookup_subscription -> Respond. Step 4 Troubleshooting Branch (Parallel) - parallel search_kb for top-3 hypotheses, then chain to response. Step 5 Escalation Branch (Chain) - escalate_human directly. Step 2 routes, step 3 chains three tools, step 4 parallelizes search_kb. Each branch picks the primitive that fits its shape. This is a workflow, not an agent: every step is wired in advance. The only LLM decision-making is inside the classifier in step 2. The model is a smart cell inside a graph you fully control.
summary: Walks through a concrete five-step customer support workflow showing where each primitive fits and contrasts a workflow with an agent.

text: Three-node circular flow diagram showing the reason, act, observe cycle of the agent loop. Three orange node boxes arranged in a triangle around a central "Loop" label: REASON at the top (Model Thinks), ACT at lower-right (Tool_use Emitted), and OBSERVE at lower-left (Tool_result Returned). Curved arrows bow outward between the boxes pointing REASON → ACT → OBSERVE → REASON.
summary: Diagram of the circular reason-act-observe agent loop with three labeled node boxes.

text: State machine diagram with four primary states across the top — WAITING (Idle, Awaiting Input), REASONING (Model Call In Flight), ACTING (Tool Call In Flight), OBSERVING (Got Tool Result) — connected by forward arrows labeled User Input, LLM Response, and Tool Result. A loop-back arrow runs from OBSERVING back to REASONING labeled "Loop: Next Iteration". Below sits a row of three terminal state boxes: DONE (green, Final Answer Emitted), FAILED (red, Unrecoverable Error), and ESCALATED (orange, Budget / Max Iter Hit), reached by dashed arrows on the termination check.
summary: State machine diagram showing four looping primary states and three terminal outcomes for one agent iteration.

text: Inner three-node cycle of REASON, ACT, OBSERVE on the left, with arrows REASON → ACT → OBSERVE. From OBSERVE an arrow leads into a red diamond decision node labeled "Terminate? Each Iter". The diamond branches to three boxes on the right: top branch (green, "Yes / Final Answer?") → DONE; middle branch (amber, "Max Iter Hit?") → ESCALATE; bottom branch (red, "Yes / Budget Out?") → ABORT. A dashed loop-back arrow runs from the diamond's "No" answer back to REASON labeled "No → Next Iteration".
summary: Diagram inserting a diamond termination decision after OBSERVE that branches to done, escalate, or abort.

text: This chapter defines the agent loop as a three-beat cycle — REASON, ACT, OBSERVE — that repeats with growing history. The single iteration is formalized as a state machine with four primary states (WAITING, REASONING, ACTING, OBSERVING) and three terminal outcomes (DONE, FAILED, ESCALATED). After every OBSERVE the runtime runs a cheap termination check that branches to DONE, ESCALATE, or ABORT based on final answer, max iterations, or budget. Cost is roughly one LLM call plus one tool call per iteration, and grows because the full history is replayed each REASON. Ticket T2 traces four iterations where the model discovers the right step order from observations. A loop is only needed when a later tool's input depends on an earlier tool's output; otherwise prefer a single call or fixed workflow.
summary: Chapter overview of the three-beat agent loop, its state machine, termination check, cost model, and when a loop is warranted.

text: An agent is not a single call. It is a loop with exactly three beats: REASON (the model thinks), ACT (the runtime emits tool_use), and OBSERVE (the runtime returns tool_result). Then the model reasons again with the new observation glued to the history. Every agent system is some shape of this three-beat cycle. REASON reads the entire history so far — the model's next decision is always conditioned on every previous tool call and result, not just the original prompt.
summary: Introduces the three-beat agent cycle of reason, act, observe that repeats with full history conditioning.

text: Zoom in on a single iteration and the loop becomes a state machine. There are four primary states the agent moves through — WAITING (idle, awaiting input), REASONING (model call in flight), ACTING (tool call in flight), OBSERVING (got tool result) — and three terminal states it eventually lands in: DONE (final answer emitted), FAILED (unrecoverable error like a tool throw or schema break), and ESCALATED (budget or max iter hit). Primary states loop; terminal states end the run. Every iteration checks whether it should cross the bottom boundary into a terminal state. DONE is the happy path. FAILED is an unrecoverable error. ESCALATED is the safety net when the agent runs too long or spends too much.
summary: Models a single agent iteration as a state machine with four primary states and three terminal outcomes.

text: Every iteration the runtime asks: should we keep looping? Without this guardrail an agent can spin forever on a tool that keeps almost-working. The termination check happens after OBSERVE and before the next REASON. Three exit conditions branch from the diamond decision: Final answer emitted? Yes → DONE (green). Max iterations exceeded? Yes → ESCALATE (amber). Budget exceeded? Yes → ABORT (red). Otherwise No → next iteration. The termination check is cheap (no model call) and happens on every iteration. It is the difference between a system you can run in production and a runaway model that bills your card flat. The conditions are formalized in the loop termination chapter.
summary: Explains the per-iteration termination check that branches to done, escalate, or abort to prevent runaway agents.

text: What each beat costs per iteration. REASON: 1 LLM call, roughly $0.02 for a 5k-token reasoning context plus a 200-token response. ACT: 0 model cost (tool_use is emitted inside REASON's response), plus 1 tool execution cost (varies). OBSERVE: 0 model cost; result is appended to history. TOTAL: roughly 1 LLM call + 1 tool call per iteration. Rule of thumb: cost per iteration = 1 LLM call + 1 tool call, scaling linearly with iteration count. Reasoning context grows every iteration because the full history is replayed. By iteration 8 you might be paying for 40k tokens of context per call. This is why long agent runs get expensive fast, even when each individual call looks small.
summary: Breaks down per-iteration cost across the three beats and shows context-growth scaling for long runs.

text: Loop Trace: Ticket T2 — "I forgot my password and my email also changed." Two problems, order matters. The agent discovers the order through reasoning, not because steps were hard-coded. Iter 1 — REASON: need customer info before anything else. ACT: lookup_customer(email="ada@example.com"). OBSERVE: got customer_id c-9924, Plan=Pro, Email Verified=False. Iter 2 — REASON: email outdated, update first before reset lands in the wrong inbox. ACT: change_email(customer_id="c-9924", new_email="ada+new@example.com"). OBSERVE: email updated, verification pending. Iter 3 — REASON: now reset the password. ACT: reset_password(customer_id="c-9924"). OBSERVE: reset email sent to ada+new@example.com. Iter 4 — REASON: done, summarize. ACT: emit final answer, no tool call. OBSERVE: TERMINATE, loop exits. Outcome: 4 iterations, 3 tool calls, 1 final answer. Loop terminates cleanly because the model emitted a final response instead of a tool call. You never told the agent "update email before reset". It worked that out in iteration 2 from the observation in iteration 1. That step ordering is the value of the loop: the model adapts to what it just learned.
summary: Walks through a four-iteration trace of ticket T2 where the agent discovers step order via reasoning over observations.

text: Not every task needs a loop. A single tool call is cheaper, faster, and easier to debug. SINGLE CALL: one tool, one response, done. Example: "What is the weather in Tokyo?" → get_weather("Tokyo") → return the result. No adaptation needed. LOOP: multiple iterations, model adapts based on intermediate results.
