Example: ticket T2 — iter 1's observation changes what iter 2 decides to do. Decision rule: if the second tool's input depends on the first tool's output, you need a loop. Otherwise stay with a single call or a fixed workflow. That dependency is the whole reason the loop exists. The first tool's output reshapes what the model knows, and the model must re-reason before picking the second tool. No loop, no adaptation, no agent.
summary: Provides a decision rule for using a single tool call versus an adaptive loop based on inter-tool dependency.

text: This chapter ReAct Pattern. ReAct = Reasoning + Acting. Model writes Thought, Action, Observation as visible structured output each iteration of the agent loop instead of hiding reasoning internally. Thought explains why next action is right. Action is exactly one tool call matching JSON schema. Observation is the runtime-supplied tool result (model does not invent). Loop repeats until model emits an Answer instead of an Action. Worked example: Ticket T2 password reset with changed email runs 4 cycles, 3 tool calls (lookup_customer, change_email, reset_password), then final answer. Trade-off: each Thought costs tokens and slows response - modern tool_use APIs already give structured calls without forcing written reasoning. Use ReAct when you need to audit reasoning, debug failures, fine-tune smaller models on traces, or build user trust.
summary: ReAct forces the agent to write Thought, Action, Observation each loop so reasoning is visible for audit, debug, fine-tuning, and trust.

text: Make The Reasoning Visible. ReAct stands for Reasoning + Acting. Instead of hiding the model's reasoning inside its head and only emitting a tool call, ReAct asks the model to write the thought out loud, then the action, then the observation, in that order, every iteration. The loop is the same; what changes is that each block is now visible structured output. Three labeled blocks: Thought = what the model is thinking about the next step. Action = which tool to call and with which arguments. Observation = what the tool returned, fed back into the next thought. Key idea: Thought is INSIDE the model's response now, not hidden. Same three beats as the agent loop. The Thought becomes a written block in the response, so evaluators, debuggers, and downstream tools can read it directly.
summary: ReAct = Reasoning + Acting: model writes Thought, Action, Observation as visible blocks each loop iteration instead of hiding reasoning internally.

text: Thought: Why The Next Action. The Thought block answers one question: why is the next action the right next action? It names the plan in plain language before any tool fires. A good Thought references the user's request, the state so far, and the order of remaining steps. Example: 'Thought: The user asked to reset their password but mentioned the email changed. I should first lookup_customer with the OLD email, then change_email to the NEW one, then reset_password.' Why it matters: the Thought makes the plan visible for evals and debugging. When a reset later goes wrong, you can open the trace and read the model's plan in English. You learn whether the failure was bad reasoning (wrong plan) or bad execution (right plan, wrong tool argument). That split is impossible without a written Thought.
summary: The Thought block is a plain-language plan justifying the next action, enabling separation of reasoning failures from execution failures.

text: Action: The Concrete Step. After the Thought, the model emits exactly one Action. It is a tool name plus an argument object that matches the tool's JSON schema. No second tool, no batching - one call, one step. Example: 'Action: lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"})'. Rule: one Action per iteration. Why only one? Because the next Thought must be free to react to whatever this Action returned. Batching two tool calls into one Action makes the model commit to a second step before it has seen the first result, which is exactly the trap the loop is supposed to avoid.
summary: Each ReAct iteration emits exactly one Action - a tool name plus JSON-schema-matching args - so the next Thought can react to the actual result.

text: Observation: What Came Back. The Observation block holds the result the runtime received from the tool. The runtime writes this in - the model does not invent it. The Observation is appended to the conversation so the next Thought has the new ground truth to reason over. Example: 'Observation: { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }'. The model reads this and decides the next Thought. Notice the structure - keys like customer_id, tier, primary_email. The model is reading a JSON-shaped result, not a free-text paragraph. Keeping the Observation strictly structured is what lets the next Thought pull out specific fields like c-9924 instead of paraphrasing them.
summary: The Observation is the runtime-supplied, JSON-structured tool result appended to the conversation as ground truth for the next Thought.

text: ReAct Trace: Ticket T2. Full four-cycle ReAct trace. Cycle 1: Thought - Customer wants reset, mentioned old email. Look them up first. Action - lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"}). Observation - { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }. Cycle 2: Thought - Email on record is outdated. Update it before the reset link goes to the wrong inbox. Action - change_email({"customer_id": "c-9924", "new_email": "alice+new@example.com"}). Observation - { ok: true, primary_email: "alice+new@example.com", verification: "pending" }. Cycle 3: Thought - Email is fixed. Trigger the password reset now so the link lands at the new address. Action - reset_password({"customer_id": "c-9924"}). Observation - { ok: true, reset_link_sent_to: "alice+new@example.com" }. Cycle 4: Thought - All steps done. Write the user-facing reply and stop the loop. Answer - 'I updated your email to alice+new@example.com and sent a fresh reset link there. Click it within 30 minutes to set a new password.' Outcome: 4 cycles, 3 tool calls. Cycle 4 ends with an Answer instead of an Action, so the loop stops. A human reviewer can audit every decision, and a downstream evaluator can score the Thoughts independently from whether the final answer was right.
summary: Worked four-cycle ReAct trace for a password reset ticket: lookup, change email, reset password, then final answer terminates the loop.

text: When To Force ReAct. ReAct is not free. Each Thought costs tokens and slows the response. Modern tool-use APIs already produce structured tool_use blocks without forcing the reasoning to be written out. Plain tool-use: model emits tool_use blocks, reasoning stays in the model's head, invisible to user / observability. ReAct: same loop, but model required to emit Thought + Action explicitly, reasoning written out as text. When to use ReAct: when you need to audit reasoning; when you need to debug failures; when you train a smaller model on the traces; when you need to build trust with the user. Once the trace is written down, it becomes a teaching signal. You can fine-tune a smaller model on big-model traces, you can show the Thoughts in a UI to make the agent feel transparent, and you can write evals that score the plan separately from the final answer.
summary: ReAct adds token cost and latency - force it only when you need audit, debug, distillation, or user trust; otherwise plain tool_use is enough.

text: Linear ReAct chain showing four reactive steps S1 -> S2 -> S3 -> S4 connected by question-mark arrows representing the model choosing the next action on the fly each iteration. Caption: model picks next action each turn. Conveys reactive, step-by-step decision making with no plan ahead of step N+1.
summary: Diagram of a four-step ReAct chain where dashed question-mark arrows show the model deciding each next step iteratively.

text: Plan tree topology where a single root node labeled Root Plan fans out to four leaf nodes L1, L2, L3, L4 representing the full plan written up front before any leaf is executed. Caption: whole plan drawn before any leaf runs. Contrasts ReAct's reactive chain by showing deliberate, visible-up-front planning structure.
summary: Diagram of a generic plan tree with one root fanning out to four leaves drawn before execution begins.

text: Four-leaf plan tree for ticket T4 (cancel and refund) showing leaves: 1. lookup_customer (to get customer_id), 2. lookup_subscription (to get invoice_id), 3. cancel (subscription), 4. process_refund (business_rule error -> escalate). The refund leaf is colored red to mark the anticipated escalation. Caption: tree drawn before any tool fires. Concrete example of structured plan output for a customer support ticket.
summary: Concrete four-leaf plan tree for cancel-and-refund ticket T4 with the refund leaf flagged for escalation before any tool fires.

text: Plan execution sweep showing four leaves running with their results next to them: 1. lookup_customer -> customer_id: c-9924 (Ok), 2. lookup_subscription -> invoice_id: inv-7741 (Ok), 3. cancel -> status: cancelled (Ok), 4. process_refund -> Error: business_rule (Err). The fourth leaf fails with a business_rule error, and a fifth escalate_human leaf is appended by the executor (reason: business_rule, Done) to adapt to the error. Caption: adapted plan closes the loop. Annotation: executor adapts -> adds a leaf.
summary: Execution sweep showing three successful leaves, a failed refund leaf, and an executor-appended escalate_human leaf closing the loop.

text: Plan-Execute writes the whole plan tree first then a thin executor walks the leaves, in contrast to ReAct which picks each step on the fly. Plans are structured trees of tool-call leaves with arguments threaded between them; when a leaf fails the executor appends new leaves instead of rebuilding. Reflection adds a 0-10 critic that grades drafts and asks the model to revise via a fix_list, bounded by a revision cap that escalates to a human. A 2x2 matrix of task complexity vs auditability picks the right shape: Plain Tool-Use, ReAct, Plan-Execute, or Plan-Execute + Reflection - and each extra structure costs tokens, latency, and review surface.
summary: Chapter overview of Plan-Execute, plan trees, executor adaptation, reflection critic loops, and the 2x2 loop-pattern decision matrix.

text: ReAct chooses the next move iteration by iteration. Plan-Execute does the opposite: the model writes the full plan tree up front, then a simple executor walks the leaves. Same tools, same model, very different control shape. ReAct is reactive - no plan until step N+1 is chosen, model picks next action each turn. Plan-Execute is deliberate - whole plan drawn before any leaf runs, plan tree is visible up front. Use ReAct when the next step truly depends on what just happened. Use Plan-Execute when you can name all the steps up front and just want them ordered, parallelized, or audited.
summary: Contrasts ReAct's iterative step-by-step decisions against Plan-Execute's full plan tree written up front before execution.

text: A plan is a tree. The root names the overall goal. Each leaf names a concrete tool call with the data it needs. Example: ticket T4 where a customer wants to cancel and get a refund. Root: Resolve T4: Cancel + Refund. Leaves: 1. lookup_customer (to get customer_id), 2. lookup_subscription (to get invoice_id), 3. cancel (subscription), 4. process_refund (business_rule error -> escalate). Key idea: every leaf names a tool and the data it will need. The plan is structured output, not free text. The model emits four leaf objects, each with a tool name, an argument template, and a note about which earlier leaf supplies each argument. The fourth leaf flags a known business_rule snag the planner anticipates.
summary: Shows that a plan is a tree of structured tool-call leaves, illustrated by a four-leaf cancel-and-refund ticket plan.

text: Once the plan is set, a thin executor walks the leaves in order. Each leaf calls its tool, captures the result, and feeds the result into the next leaf's arguments. The model only re-enters the loop when something the plan did not anticipate happens. Example sweep: 1. lookup_customer -> customer_id: c-9924 (Ok). 2. lookup_subscription -> invoice_id: inv-7741 (Ok). 3. cancel -> status: cancelled (Ok). 4. process_refund -> Error: business_rule (Err). Executor adapts and appends Step 5: escalate_human (reason: business_rule, Done). When plans break, executor catches the business_rule error and appends Step 5: escalate_human. The executor did not throw the entire plan away. Leaves 1, 2, and 3 already succeeded - their results are kept. Only the failed leaf triggers a replan, and the replan appends one new leaf instead of rebuilding the tree from scratch.
summary: Walks the four-leaf plan in order, shows leaf 4 failing with a business_rule error, and the executor adapting by appending an escalate_human leaf.

text: Reflection sits on top of any loop. After the model writes a draft, the same model (or a separate critic call) grades it. If the grade is low, the model revises. It is a cheap, model-only feedback loop that catches confidently wrong answers before they ship. Steps: (1) Model writes draft answer - first pass, free text or structured output, may be confidently wrong. (2) Model (or critic) scores the draft 0-10 with reasoning - grade covers accuracy, format, completeness, tone; reasoning spells out the misses. (3) If score < 7, model revises and re-evaluates - revision uses the critique as a diff list; re-score decides whether to stop or loop again. Safety valve: one revision cap typical, more triggers escalation. Critic prompt sketch: Grade the draft 0-10 on accuracy, format, completeness. Return JSON: { "score": int, "reasoning": str, "fix_list": [str] }. If score < 7, orchestrator runs the model again with the fix_list pasted into the prompt. Second draft re-scored. Two failed revisions in a row escalate_human. Cost is one extra model call per draft, plus another full call if a revision is needed. Win: catches math errors, missing fields, tone mismatches. Bound revisions so reflection cannot spiral into an infinite revise loop.
summary: Introduces reflection as a draft-grade-revise feedback loop with a 0-10 critic, fix list, and bounded retries to escalation.

text: Four shapes, one decision matrix. Plot the task on two axes: how complex is the task, and how much do you need to audit the reasoning. The right loop falls out of the cell you land in. Matrix - Simple Task / Low Audit: Plain Tool-Use (cheap single-call flow). Simple Task / High Audit: ReAct (simple task, visible reasoning). Complex Task / Low Audit: Plan-Execute (complex plan, light audit). Complex Task / High Audit: Plan-Execute + Reflection (complex plan, heavy audit, self-check). Decision shortcut: Single tool, trusted output -> Plain Tool-Use. Few steps, need visible reasoning -> ReAct. Many steps, order known -> Plan-Execute. High-stakes answer, must self-check -> Plan-Execute + Reflection. Loops are not a ladder where bigger is always better. Each extra structure costs tokens, latency, and review surface. Pick the smallest shape that handles the task and graduate only when the task forces you to.
summary: A 2x2 task-complexity by auditability matrix that maps to Plain Tool-Use, ReAct, Plan-Execute, or Plan-Execute+Reflection.

text: Diagram for max-iters cap: Horizontal counter from one to ten showing iteration count with the tenth iteration highlighted as the forced termination point. Yellow cells numbered 1 through 9 connected by arrows show the iteration counter ticking forward. The 10th cell is red with a STOP badge above it and a bold X mark, indicating the loop is force-terminated when the counter reaches the cap. Caption: 'Iteration Counter - Force-Terminate At Iter 10'.
summary: Visual of the iteration counter incrementing up to 10 where the loop is force-terminated.

text: Diagram for budget cap: Horizontal budget bar filling from zero to one hundred percent with markers at thirty sixty and one hundred percent showing iteration cost consumption stopping at budget exhaustion. The bar is green from 0-30%, amber from 30-60%, red from 60-100%. Markers show $0.15 at 30%, $0.30 at 60%, and $0.50 - STOP at 100%. Caption: 'Budget Bar - Loop Terminates When Bar Hits 100%'.
summary: Visual of a budget bar with markers showing cost consumption across iterations until the cap stops the loop at $0.50.

text: Diagram for escalation flow: Fail-safe escalation flow from any non-success termination to user message and escalate_human call with partial transcript. Three stacked boxes connected by downward arrows. Step 1 (red): Loop terminates - any non-success stop (max-iters / budget / halt). Step 2 (amber): Agent emits final message to user - 'I wasn't able to fully resolve this. A human agent will follow up.' Step 3 (cyan): Call escalate_human with partial transcript - hands off the full chain of reasoning so the human can resume. Caption: 'Fail-Safe Pattern - Triggered By ANY Non-Success Termination'.
summary: Visual of the three-step fail-safe escalation flow from loop termination to user message to escalate_human call.

text: Loop termination in agent runtimes. Four reasons an agent loop ends: success (model emits final answer with no tool_use block, stop_reason end_turn), max-iters hard cap (typically 10-20 iterations to prevent runaway loops), token/cost budget exhaustion (dollar cap like $0.50 per run, safer than iteration count because per-iter cost varies 10x), and explicit stop (tool returns halt:true flag, or user cancels). Only success skips escalation. All non-success terminations must trigger the fail-safe pattern: emit a user-visible message explaining partial completion, then call escalate_human with the full partial transcript so a human can resume with complete reasoning context. Production rule: no silent failures.
summary: Chapter-level overview of the four agent loop termination conditions and the mandatory fail-safe escalation pattern for non-success exits.

text:
Four Ways A Loop Ends. An agent loop is a process that must stop. There are exactly four reasons it does. Three of them are non-success: the loop ran out of room, ran out of money, or was told to halt. Knowing which one fired tells you what to do next.

Four stop conditions:
1. SUCCESS - Model emits final answer.
2. MAX-ITERS - Hit the cap (e.g., 10).
3. BUDGET - Token / cost exhausted.
4. EXPLICIT STOP - Tool returns a 'halt' signal, or user cancels.

One of these four conditions fires on every agent run. Every production agent must detect each one and respond differently. The remaining sub-steps walk through detection and the fail-safe pattern.
summary: Introduces the four mutually exclusive stop conditions for an agent loop: success, max-iters, budget, and explicit stop.

text:
Success: The Model Stops Calling Tools. The happy path is also the subtlest. The model never says 'I'm done' out loud. It just stops asking for tools. The runtime watches the most recent response and detects the absence of any tool_use block.

Iteration N-1: Model emits tool_use + text. Still working. Runtime runs the tool, feeds the result back, loops again.

Iteration N: Model emits ONLY text (no tool_use). That's success - the model decided no more tools needed. Stop reason from the API is 'end_turn' instead of 'tool_use'. The final text is the answer to the user.

This is the only stop condition that does not require an escalation message. Everything else is partial completion and the user needs to be told what happened.
summary: Explains how the runtime detects agent success by absence of tool_use blocks and an end_turn stop reason.

text:
Hard Cap: 10-20 Iterations. The max iter cap (also called max-iters) is the dumbest safety net but also the most important. A counter ticks up every loop pass. When it hits the cap, the runtime force-terminates the loop without asking the model.

Production rule: 10-20 typical max-iter cap. Higher = pay-to-think; lower = miss answers.

The cap protects against runaway loops where the model can't make progress and keeps re-trying the same tool. Without a cap a single buggy ticket can bill thousands of dollars in an hour.
summary: Describes the max-iters hard cap, typically 10 to 20, that force-terminates an agent loop to prevent runaway cost.

text:
Token / Cost Budget. Iteration count is a proxy for cost; the real number is dollars. A budget cap measures the total tokens (or dollars) spent across all iterations and stops the loop when the wallet is empty. Sample budget: $0.50 per agent run.

Budget consumption stages:
- 30%: Iter 1-2 consumed $0.15
- 60%: Iter 3-4 consumed $0.30
- 100%: Budget exhausted - STOP at $0.50

Budget is the SAFER cap than iterations - real cost can vary 10x per iteration. One iteration that triggers a thinking burst can spend 10k tokens; the next might spend 500. Iteration count doesn't see that. A dollar budget does.
summary: Explains the token/dollar budget cap as a safer termination condition than iteration count because per-iter cost varies 10x.

text:
Tools Can Say Stop. Some tools are terminal by design. An escalate_human tool, for example, hands the conversation to a human and there is nothing left for the agent to do. The tool result includes a halt flag, and the runtime respects it.

Tool result shape with halt signal:
```
{
  "tool": "escalate_human",
  "result": { "ticket_id": "T4", "human_assigned": true },
  "halt": true
}
```

The loop respects this signal and terminates cleanly. Similar pattern: a user cancellation injects a halt event.

Explicit stops are the cleanest non-success case. The model doesn't get confused, the runtime doesn't guess, and there is a clear handoff record in the transcript.
summary: Explains explicit stop signals where tools return halt:true (or users cancel) and the runtime terminates cleanly.

text:
When The Loop Gives Up, Escalate. Any non-success termination (max-iters, budget, or explicit halt that wasn't a successful resolution) must trigger the same fail-safe pattern. The user gets a clear message, and a human gets the transcript to pick up where the agent stopped.

Fail-safe pattern (3 steps):
1. Loop terminates - any non-success stop (max-iters / budget / halt).
2. Agent emits final message to user - "I wasn't able to fully resolve this. A human agent will follow up."
3. Call escalate_human with partial transcript - hands off the full chain of reasoning so the human can resume.

Production rule: No silent failures. Every non-success termination produces a user-visible message AND an escalate_human call with the full partial transcript. Silent failures are how agent bugs hide in production for weeks.

The transcript is the load-bearing artifact: it carries the agent's reasoning, every tool call, every result, so the human picking up the ticket has full context. No re-litigating from scratch.
summary: Defines the mandatory three-step fail-safe escalation pattern triggered by any non-success agent termination.

text: Diagram: Memory taxonomy tree with Agent Memory as root at the top. Root branches into Short-Term and Long-Term nodes. Short-Term has one child: Working (labeled Scratchpad, this chapter). Long-Term has three children: Episodic (Events), Semantic (Facts), Procedural (Skills), labeled 26.9 through 26.11. The tree visualizes how one memory concept divides into two layers and then four concrete leaves.
summary: Describes the SVG memory taxonomy tree diagram with the root, two branches, and four labeled leaves for chapter navigation.

text: Agent memory splits into two layers: short-term working memory inside the active context window (read/written each loop turn, discarded when the session ends) and long-term persistent memory in an external store (survives across sessions, loaded on demand). Long-term memory subdivides into three kinds - episodic (past events with timestamps), semantic (facts as key-value or vector), and procedural (skills, recipes, cached workflows). The full taxonomy is one root, two branches, four leaves (Working, Episodic, Semantic, Procedural). A T2 ticket snapshot shows each layer holding different content for the same task. Four layers exist because each has different shapes, retrieval patterns, update rules, and lifespans.
summary: Whole-chapter recap: two memory layers, long-term splits into episodic/semantic/procedural, four leaves total, illustrated with a ticket snapshot.

text: An agent has memory in two very different places. Short-term memory (Working Memory) lives inside the active context window of the current conversation - the model reads and writes it every loop turn, and it is discarded when the session ends. Long-term memory (Persistent Memory) lives outside that context window in an external store, persists across sessions and conversations, and gets loaded back in on demand. Short = this conversation. Long = everything before.
summary: Introduces the two-layer split of agent memory: short-term working memory inside the context window versus long-term persistent memory in an external store.

text: Long-term memory is not one thing. It separates by what kind of information it holds. Three sub-types each answer a different question. Episodic: past events with timestamps and content, e.g., Past Ticket #4521: Failed Password Reset Escalated For MFA Bug. Semantic: facts stored as key-value or vector, e.g., Alice = Pro Tier, Signed Up 2024-08, Prefers Email. Procedural: skills, recipes, cached workflows, e.g., Refund > $200 -> Escalate To Human. The shape: events (when), facts (what), skills (how).
summary: Splits long-term memory into three sub-types - episodic for events, semantic for facts, procedural for skills - each with a concrete example.

text: Agent memory taxonomy tree. One root, two branches, four leaves. Root: Agent Memory. Branches: Short-Term and Long-Term. Short-Term has one leaf: Working (Scratchpad, covered in 26.8). Long-Term has three leaves: Episodic (Events), Semantic (Facts), Procedural (Skills). Each leaf becomes its own chapter - working memory next, then episodic, semantic, procedural through 26.11, with summary and context-management closing the section.
summary: Presents the full memory taxonomy diagram - root agent memory, two branches, four leaves - that maps the remaining memory chapters.

text: Concrete memory snapshot for ticket T2 (password reset + email change for Alice). Working: Customer alice@example.com / c-9924. Issue: password reset + email changed. Next: change_email then reset_password. Episodic: 2026-03-04: Past ticket #4521 for failed password reset; escalated due to legacy MFA bug. Semantic: Alice = Pro tier, signed up 2024-08, prefers email contact, USD billing. Procedural: Refund > $200 -> escalate_human. Always confirm identity before change_email. The customer ID c-9924 and refund cap $200 reappear across the memory chapters - the agent loads them from memory instead of re-deriving each turn.
summary: Walks a concrete T2 ticket snapshot showing what content each of the four memory layers holds for the same task.

text: Why have four layers and not one? Each holds a different shape of information with different retrieval patterns, update rules, and lifespans. Working memory holds the current task state across loop iterations. Episodic memory gives the agent recall of specific past events. Semantic memory provides stable customer facts without re-asking. Procedural memory caches learned routines so the agent does not re-derive them every time. Each layer earns its place.
summary: Explains why agents need four distinct memory layers - each one solves a different problem with different retrieval, update, and lifespan rules.

text: Timeline of working memory: created at task start, updated through N iterations, discarded when task ends, with a promotion arrow pointing to long-term store before discard. Four stops along a horizontal red line: Task Start (Created), Iter N (Updated), Promote (To Long-Term), Task End (Discarded). The final stop is filled solid red to indicate discard. A purple arrow rises from the Promote stop up to a Long-Term Store label, showing the only path for information to survive past task termination.
summary: Diagram showing four-stage timeline from task start through promotion to discard, with promotion arrow to long-term store.

text: Working memory is the in-context scratchpad an agent reads on REASON and updates on OBSERVE every loop turn. It is a structured object with five fields - customer_context, current_goal, completed_steps, next_step, constraints - that anchors the running task. Across iterations of ticket T2 the scratchpad fills in customer info, appends completed tool calls, and ends with next_step null to signal task completion. When the task ends the scratchpad is discarded, so anything worth keeping must be promoted to long-term memory first. Compared to long-term memory (external vector DB or Postgres, millions of facts, persistent), working memory is in-context, a few hundred tokens, model-written, ephemeral. Agents need both, with promotion as the bridge.
summary: Chapter overview: scratchpad shape, per-iteration updates, discard-at-end lifecycle, and contrast with long-term memory.

text: Working memory is the in-context scratchpad - a note pad the model keeps. The agent reads it on every REASON step and updates it after every OBSERVE step. It lives inside the prompt context window itself, not in any external store. The loop around the note pad has four steps: 1. REASON: the model reads the scratchpad to decide the next tool. 2. ACT: the tool runs. 3. OBSERVE: the result comes back and the scratchpad is updated. 4. LOOP: back to step 1 with the updated note pad. The scratchpad is the agent's short-term reasoning trace. Lose it, and the agent has to re-derive what it was doing from scratch.
summary: Working memory is an in-context scratchpad read on REASON and updated on OBSERVE in every loop iteration.

text:
What goes in the scratchpad. The scratchpad is not free-form text. It is a structured object with stable field names so the model can read and write specific slots. Five fields anchor the running task. Working Memory (Shape): {
  "customer_context": "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro",
  "current_goal": "Reset password (email also changed)",
  "completed_steps": ["lookup_customer", "change_email"],
  "next_step": "reset_password",
  "constraints": ["refund cap $200"]
}. customer_context answers who, current_goal answers what, completed_steps answers what so far, next_step answers what next, and constraints keep the agent within policy. Same five fields appear in 28.6 observability traces.
summary: Working memory is a structured object with five fields: customer_context, current_goal, completed_steps, next_step, and constraints.

text: Updated every iteration. Watch the scratchpad evolve across four loop turns of ticket T2 (Alice: password reset + email change). Each row is the state right after the iteration's OBSERVE step. Iteration 1: customer_context: "" (not yet looked up), completed_steps: [], next_step: "lookup_customer". Iteration 2: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer"], next_step: "change_email". Iteration 3: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email"], next_step: "reset_password". Iteration 4: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email", "reset_password"], next_step: null (task complete). By iteration 4, next_step is null and the task is done. The scratchpad terminated cleanly, which is how loop termination (26.6) detects success.
summary: Across four iterations the T2 scratchpad fills customer_context, appends completed_steps, and ends with next_step null signalling termination.

text: Working memory dies when the task ends. When the loop terminates, the scratchpad is discarded. Anything worth keeping must be promoted to long-term memory BEFORE that discard. Otherwise it is gone. Timeline: Task Start (Created), Iter N (Updated), Promote (To Long-Term), Task End (Discarded). A promotion arrow points up from the timeline to the Long-Term Store before discard. "Resolved by email reset link" is a working memory entry. After promotion to episodic memory, the next session can recall it. Skip the promote, and the next session asks Alice the same question from scratch.
summary: Scratchpad is discarded at task end; valuable facts must be promoted to long-term memory before termination or lost forever.

text: Working vs Long-Term. Side-by-side: what is different between the in-context scratchpad and the external long-term store. Different lifespan, different capacity, different writer. Lives In: Working = Prompt Context Window, Long-Term = External Store (Vector DB / Postgres). Written By: Working = Model On Each Loop Turn, Long-Term = Explicit Promotion Logic. Capacity: Working = A Few Hundred Tokens, Long-Term = Millions Of Facts / Events. Lifespan: Working = Discarded When Task Ends, Long-Term = Persistent Across Sessions. Working memory is fast, cheap, and ephemeral. Long-term memory is slower, more expensive, but persistent. Agents need both - and the bridge between them is the promote step.
summary: Working memory lives in context (hundreds of tokens, model-written, ephemeral); long-term lives in external stores (millions of facts, promotion-written, persistent).

text: Vertical timeline of four past events for Alice the customer, each with a date and a short summary, with the current ticket marked at the bottom. Dates and labels: 2026-03-04 Failed Password Reset (escalated due to legacy MFA bug); 2026-04-12 Slow Dashboard (complained about loading time; resolved with cache fix); 2026-05-01 Invoice Request (requested May invoice; sent successfully); 2026-05-15 Password + Email Reset T2 (current ticket).
summary: Vertical timeline diagram shows Alice's four past events ending with the current ticket.

text: Two stage flow: stage one embeds each event and stores it in the vector database; stage two embeds a query and runs ANN search to retrieve the top-k matching past events. Stage 1 boxes: Event ('Failed Password Reset') -> Embed ([1024 floats]) -> Vector DB. Stage 2 boxes: Query ('What past issues?') -> Embed (same encoder) -> ANN Search (top-K events). The same encoder is used for both events and queries.
summary: Two-stage diagram shows how events are embedded into a vector DB and queries retrieve top-K via ANN search.

text: Episodic memory gives agents access to specific past events with timestamps and summaries. Events are embedded and stored in a vector database; queries are embedded with the same encoder and matched via ANN search to retrieve top-K. Before the agent reasons on a new ticket, the host fetches the top-3 past episodes and injects them into the system prompt - the same retrieval-and-inject pattern ChatGPT Memory uses. Entries are structured records (id, customer_id, timestamp, summary, tools_used, outcome, embedding) - summaries for the model, embeddings for search, tools/outcome for analytics. A pruning policy drops old resolved events, keeps escalations, summarizes duplicates, and honors compliance deletes.
summary: Episodic memory stores past events as embeddings, retrieves top-K via ANN, injects them into the prompt, and prunes for quality and compliance.

text: Episodic memory holds specific past events. Each event has a timestamp and a short description of what happened. Example: four episodes the agent has on file for Alice the customer. 2026-03-04 Failed Password Reset - escalated due to legacy MFA bug. 2026-04-12 Slow Dashboard - complained about loading time; resolved with cache fix. 2026-05-01 Invoice Request - requested May invoice; sent successfully. 2026-05-15 Password + Email Reset (T2) - current ticket. Episodes answer 'what happened, when?'. They are NOT the customer's stable facts (that is semantic memory) and they are NOT how-to recipes (that is procedural memory).
summary: Episodic memory stores time-stamped past events for a customer, distinct from semantic facts or procedural recipes.

text: Episodic memory lives in a vector database. Each event becomes an embedding; a search query becomes an embedding; an ANN index returns the most similar past events. Stage 1: Event ('Failed Password Reset') -> Embed ([1024 floats]) -> Vector DB. Stage 2: Query ('What past issues?') -> Embed (same encoder) -> ANN Search (top-K events). Vector storage substrate is the IVF and HNSW layer covered earlier. Used as the layer below episodic memory: write once, search by similarity from then on.
summary: Past events are stored and retrieved using embeddings and ANN search over a vector database.

text: When a new conversation starts, the agent retrieves relevant past episodes BEFORE reasoning about the current ticket. The host system fetches the top-3 events and injects them into the system prompt as context. Step 1: User message arrives - T2: 'I forgot my password and my email changed.'. Step 2: Build recall query - Query = 'current ticket summary + customer_id c-9924'. Step 3: Retrieve top-3 episodes - ANN search returns 3 most similar past events. Step 4: Inject into system prompt - episodes added to the prompt as context block. Step 5: Agent reasons with past context - past MFA-bug episode informs current approach. ChatGPT Memory works similarly: the host fetches relevant facts on each new conversation and injects them. The model does not 'remember' - the host system gives it the appearance of memory through retrieval-and-inject.
summary: Five-step recall flow injects retrieved past episodes into the system prompt before the agent reasons.

text: Each episode entry is a structured record. The agent does not store raw transcripts - it stores summaries + the tools used + the outcome + the embedding for retrieval. Episodic Memory Entry: { "id": "ev-7821", "customer_id": "c-9924", "timestamp": "2026-03-04T14:22:00Z", "summary": "Failed password reset escalated due to legacy MFA bug.", "tools_used": ["reset_password", "escalate_human"], "outcome": "human_resolved", "embedding": "[1024 floats]" }. summary is what the model reads. embedding is what ANN search uses. tools_used and outcome let analytics ask 'which past escalations involved MFA bugs?'.
summary: An episodic entry is a structured JSON record with id, timestamp, summary, tools used, outcome, and embedding.

text: Memory has to forget. Unbounded growth wrecks retrieval quality. Pruning policy decides what to keep and what to drop. Four rules cover most cases. Age > 12 months AND outcome = resolved cleanly -> Drop. Age > 12 months AND outcome = escalated -> Keep (rare patterns valuable). Aggregated similar events -> Summarize into one parent and drop children. Manual customer request -> Delete on demand (compliance). Compliance-driven deletion is non-negotiable. A customer can request their entire episodic log be wiped, and the agent must honor it within whatever window the regulation requires.
summary: A four-rule pruning policy keeps memory useful and honors compliance-driven deletion requests.

text: This chapter Semantic Memory - Learned Facts. Covers: (1) semantic memory holds stable 'currently true' facts, distinct from time-stamped episodic events; (2) the customer profile card shape with typed fields like customer_id, tier, preferred_contact, billing_currency, and a nested preferences bag; (3) two storage substrates - key-value/Postgres for typed exact-match fields and vector store for free-text similarity facts, used together in production; (4) profile growth from Day 1 to Day 30 to Day 90 via confirmed observations only, never single-message guesses; (5) the 6-months test for what counts as a fact: stable preferences, identity, constraints go in; transient mood, one-time requests, and guesses stay out.
summary: Chapter overview: semantic memory stores long-lived facts in structured plus vector stores, growing via confirmed observations and gated by the 6-months test.

text: Semantic memory holds stable facts about a customer. Different from episodic memory: an episode is 'this happened on this date'; a fact is 'this is currently true'. Episodic fact (time-stamped event): On 2026-03-04, Alice opened a ticket about a password reset. Semantic fact (stable state): Alice prefers email contact. Same person, different memory layer. Episodic answers 'what happened?', semantic answers 'what is true?'. Both are needed; they live in different stores with different update rules.
summary: Semantic memory stores stable current-truth facts about a user, distinct from time-stamped episodic events.
Semantic memory is shaped as a customer profile card with typed fields plus a nested preferences bag.

text: Key-Value Or Vector. Two storage substrates serve different fact shapes. Most production agents use BOTH: structured for the spine, vector for the long-tail freetext. Structured (Key-Value / Postgres): use when fields are typed, predictable, queried by exact match. Example: customer_id -> profile lookup. Vector (Embedding + Similarity): use when facts are free-text, queried by meaning. Example: 'User has a dairy allergy' embedded for retrieval. The spine (typed profile) is small and exact. The long-tail (free-form preferences and constraints) is large and fuzzy. Use the right tool for each shape.
summary: Semantic memory uses key-value storage for typed exact-match fields plus vector storage for free-text similarity retrieval.

text: How The Profile Fills Up. Semantic memory grows over time. Alice's profile at signup has 4 fields. By day 30 it has 5. By day 90 it has nested preferences. Growth comes from confirmed observations. Day 1 (Signup): customer_id: c-9924, tier: Pro, signed_up: 2024-08, primary_email: alice@example.com. Day 30: (all Day-1 fields) + preferred_contact: email, learned from 'please email me at...'. Day 90: (all Day-30 fields) + preferences.skip_onboarding_emails: true, learned from user opt-out click. Profile fills only on CONFIRMED observations. A guess from one ambiguous message does not get written. A repeated preference observed across sessions does.
summary: The profile grows incrementally from 4 to 5 to nested fields as the agent confirms observations across days.

text: What Counts As A Fact? Rule of thumb: if it would still be true in 6 months, write it to semantic memory. Otherwise leave it in the working scratchpad or skip it entirely. Write to semantic: stable preferences (always email), confirmed identity (Alice is account owner), constraints (dairy allergy). Do not write: transient mood ('frustrated today'), one-time requests ('send a copy this once'), guesses (uncertain inferences). 6-Months Test: would knowing this still help six months from now? If yes, write it. If no, it belongs in working memory or the chat history and should expire naturally.
summary: The 6-months test decides what to write: stable preferences, identity, constraints go in; transient moods, one-offs, and guesses stay out.

text: Recipe retrieval flow diagram: new ticket text is embedded, ANN search runs against the recipe library trigger embeddings, and the top-one match is surfaced for the agent to use as scaffolding. Five-stage horizontal pipeline labeled: New Ticket (free text), Embed (1024 floats), ANN Search (vs library), Top-1 (or no match), Use Recipe (scaffolding). Arrows connect each stage left-to-right showing the data flow from raw ticket text to a usable retrieved recipe.
summary: Diagram of the five-stage pipeline for retrieving a matching recipe from procedural memory.

text: Procedural memory stores how-to skills (recipes) external to the system prompt. Each recipe has a name, trigger_embedding, ordered steps, success_rate, and uses count. The agent retrieves recipes by embedding the new ticket and running ANN search against trigger embeddings; top-1 match becomes scaffolding, otherwise the agent reasons from first principles. Recipes differ from prompted instructions by living externally, being retrieved on demand to save tokens, and being updatable from outcomes (falling success_rate triggers rewrite). Use prompting for universal rules and procedural memory for task-specific learnable workflows.
summary: Procedural memory holds external, retrievable, learnable how-to recipes that complement the system prompt.

text: Procedural memory encodes skills - HOW to do something - not facts about the world. Semantic fact example: 'Refund cap is $200.' Procedural skill example: 'When a refund > $200 is requested, look up customer tier first. Enterprise customers self-approve up to $500. Otherwise call escalate_human with urgency=Medium.' Facts encode WHAT is true. Procedures encode WHAT TO DO. The agent uses both: fact says '$200 cap'; procedure says 'if cap exceeded, escalate'.
summary: Procedural memory stores how-to skills/recipes, distinct from semantic memory which stores facts.

text: The recipe library is a small set of named workflows the customer-support agent reuses. Each recipe has a trigger condition and an ordered step list. Library examples: 'Handle Refund Request' (trigger: customer asks for refund, 3 steps); 'Resolve Password Issue' (trigger: login/password error, 4 steps); 'Escalate Billing Dispute' (trigger: disputed invoice or charge, 2 steps); 'Resolve Sync Failure' (trigger: data out-of-sync across devices, 5 steps). Four recipes cover common customer-support workflows. Each one used to be a prompt-engineering exercise; once it stabilized, it got moved into procedural memory.
summary: A recipe library holds named workflows with trigger conditions and step lists that the agent reuses.

text: Recipe retrieval pipeline: A new ticket gets embedded; ANN search runs against the recipe library's trigger_embedding field; the top-1 match is surfaced (or 'no match' if similarity is below threshold). Five stages: New Ticket (free text) -> Embed (1024 floats) -> ANN Search (vs library) -> Top-1 (or no match) -> Use Recipe (scaffolding). The agent uses the retrieved recipe as a scaffold, then adapts to the specifics of the current ticket. If no recipe matches, the agent falls back to first-principles reasoning - and the run becomes a candidate to mine for a new recipe.
summary: New tickets are embedded and matched against recipe trigger embeddings via ANN search; top-1 recipe becomes scaffolding.

text:
A recipe is a structured record. Trigger embedding for retrieval; steps for execution; success_rate and uses for analytics and self-improvement. Recipe shape:
{
  "id": "recipe-refund",
  "name": "Handle refund request",
  "trigger_embedding": "[1024 floats]",
  "steps": [
    "lookup_customer to get customer_id and tier",
    "if tier == Enterprise: auto-approve up to $500; else $200",
    "lookup_subscription for invoice_id",
    "process_refund(invoice_id, reason); if business_rule error: escalate_human"
  ],
  "success_rate": 0.92,
  "uses": 187
}
success_rate = 0.92 means 92% of times this recipe ran, it produced a clean outcome. uses = 187 says the agent has run this recipe 187 times. These numbers drive improvement: a falling success_rate triggers a rewrite review.
summary: A recipe record has id, name, trigger_embedding, steps, success_rate, and uses fields; metrics drive self-improvement.

text: Why not just prompt the recipe every time? Prompting and procedural memory both encode 'how to do X'. They differ in where the recipe lives, what it costs, and whether it can learn from outcomes. Comparison: Where it lives - Prompting: inside the system prompt; Procedural: external store, retrieved on demand. Token cost - Prompting: every conversation pays the prompt tokens; Procedural: only retrieved when relevant. Can learn? - Prompting: static, cannot update from outcomes; Procedural: updatable, success_rate drives rewrite. Best for - Prompting: universal rules (tone, format); Procedural: task-specific recipes. Prompting stays great for universal rules - tone, format, safety constraints. Procedural memory wins for task-specific recipes that change with real-world outcomes. Use both, deliberately.
summary: Prompting vs procedural memory compared across location, token cost, learnability, and best fit; use both deliberately.

text: Horizontal context window bar at 8k tokens with turn-by-turn message stacking. The bar runs from turn 1 to turn 100. The first 30 turns fill the bar (8k tokens used), and a vertical red marker at turn 30 labels the bar as Full. The rest of the conversation past turn 30 cannot fit.
summary: Diagram showing an 8k context window bar that fills completely by turn 30 of a conversation.

text: Rolling summary technique in three stages. Stage 1: the conversation reaches 50% capacity on the bar. Stage 2: the oldest half is summarized into a small purple summary block that replaces the raw turns. Stage 3: total capacity drops back to 25%, with the summary block plus a smaller raw section, so the conversation can continue.
summary: Diagram of the three-stage rolling summary cycle: fill to 50%, compress oldest half into a summary block, drop back to 25%.

text: Hierarchical summary tree. Top: a single meta-summary box covering the whole conversation. Middle: three per-batch summary boxes labeled Batch Summary 1, 2, and 3. Bottom: raw turn batches as small boxes (T1, T3, T5, T7 etc.) connected upward to their batch summary. Leaves are raw turn batches of 10 turns each, mid is per-batch summary, top is the meta-summary.
summary: Diagram of a three-level hierarchical summary tree from raw turn leaves through per-batch summaries to a single meta-summary.

text: Four threshold bands across a context window utilization bar. 25% band green: Do Nothing - plenty of room. 50% band yellow: Compress Half - summarize oldest 25%. 75% band orange: Aggressive - summarize older half plus start hybrid retrieval. 90% band red: Panic - drop everything except summary plus last 5 turns plus top retrieved.
summary: Diagram of the four-band threshold policy showing escalating compression actions at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 90% context utilization.

text: Context window management for agent loops: at 8k tokens and ~250 tokens per turn, conversations fill by turn 30. Use rolling summary at 50% capacity (summarize oldest half into 2 paragraphs), hierarchical summarization for week-long chats (batch leaves, per-batch mids, one meta-summary), and pick what stays in context using recency, relevance, or hybrid (top-K recent + top-K relevant - the production default). Trigger compression at fixed thresholds (25/50/75/90% bands with escalating actions including a panic mode). Memory layers feed a seven-step prompt assembly: system prompt, memory block, summary, recent turns, retrieved turns, working memory, current user message.
summary: Summarizes the chapter's tools for keeping long agent conversations inside a fixed token budget through summarization, turn selection, threshold-based compression, and structured prompt assembly.

text: Long conversations are not free. With an 8k token context window and roughly 250 tokens per turn, the window fills by turn 30. Past that, something has to give. The agent must compress old turns, drop them, or both. The rest of this chapter is about how to manage that ceiling.
summary: Introduces the token-budget problem: at 8k context and ~250 tokens per turn, the window fills around turn 30 and old turns must be compressed or dropped.

text: The rolling-summary technique: when the window hits 50% capacity, summarize the oldest half into a 2-paragraph block. The summary replaces the raw turns. Capacity drops to 25%. Repeat every 50%. The model itself usually generates the summary (a 2-paragraph LLM call per compression cycle). The summary lives in the conversation history at the spot where the raw turns used to be. The summary itself grows slowly as older summaries are re-compressed.
summary: Explains the rolling-summary technique: at 50% capacity, summarize the oldest half into 2 paragraphs that replace the raw turns, dropping usage back to 25%.

text: For week-long conversations, flat rolling summary degrades. The hierarchical approach organizes raw turns into batches (leaves), per-batch summaries (mid), and a meta-summary of the whole conversation (top). Hierarchical wins for week-long conversations because the meta-summary stays small while you can still zoom into specific batches when needed. Leaves are raw turn batches (10 turns each), mid is per-batch summary, top is meta-summary.
summary: Describes hierarchical summarization: turns grouped into batches with per-batch summaries and one top meta-summary, enabling week-long conversations with zoom-in capability.

text: After summarization, what stays in the prompt? Three strategies, each with a different tradeoff between continuity and topical fit. Recency keeps the most-recent N turns raw and drops a summary of older turns; good for continuity-heavy conversations. Relevance keeps turns most similar to the current query and drops a summary of less-relevant turns; good for long-range topical recall. Hybrid keeps top-K recent plus top-K relevant and drops a summary of everything else; best default in practice. Hybrid wins in production because most conversations have both continuity needs (the user said "this" earlier) and topical recall needs (the user asked a similar thing 80 turns ago).
summary: Compares recency, relevance, and hybrid strategies for selecting which turns remain in context after summarization, with hybrid as the production default.

text: Production agents trigger compression at fixed thresholds, not on demand. Four bands cover the production rule: at 25% utilization do nothing (plenty of room); at 50% compress half (summarize oldest 25%); at 75% go aggressive (summarize older half and start hybrid retrieval); at 90% panic (drop everything except summary plus last 5 turns plus top retrieved). Threshold-based compression is production-friendly because the cost is predictable. On-demand compression can hide behind a single user turn that suddenly explodes the window.
summary: Lays out the four-threshold production rule for triggering context compression at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 90% utilization with escalating actions.

text: Memory plus context-engineering decide what the model sees on EVERY turn. The prompt-assembly stack ties memory layers together. The assembly order: 1. System Prompt (static plus recipe from procedural memory); 2. Memory Block (retrieved episodes plus profile card from semantic); 3. Conversation Summary (rolling or hierarchical summary of old turns); 4. Recent Turns (last N raw turns - recency); 5. Retrieved Turns (top-K relevant turns from older history - hybrid); 6. Working Memory (current scratchpad state); 7. Current User Message (what the user just said). Every layer comes from somewhere. Memory sources combined with an assembly stack and summarization keep the whole prompt under the token budget.
summary: Closes the chapter by mapping memory sources into a seven-layer prompt assembly order from system prompt through current user message.

text: Diagram: single agent box labeled 'One Agent - 5 Jobs On One System Prompt' surrounded by five red job boxes labeled Retrieve, Classify, Refund, Troubleshoot, Escalate. Visualizes the crowded role assignment that creates a performance ceiling for one agent.
summary: Diagram of one agent surrounded by five competing jobs illustrating role overload.

text: Diagram: dispatcher at top fans out to three parallel agents - Agent A (lookup_customer, 1.2s), Agent B (lookup_subscription, 1.4s), Agent C (search_kb, 1.6s). Shows three independent sub-tasks running concurrently with wall-clock equal to the slowest one rather than the sum.
summary: Diagram of a dispatcher fanning out to three parallel worker agents with per-agent latencies.

text: Diagram: planner agent at top decomposes the task and dispatches via arrows to two worker agents below - Worker 1 (Lookup Customer) and Worker 2 (Process Refund). Two-layer planner-worker architecture where workers execute sub-tasks and return results.
summary: Two-layer planner-worker architecture diagram with one planner dispatching to two workers.

text: Single agents hit a ceiling when overloaded with roles. Multi-agent helps in three cases: specialization (one agent per role with smaller tool sets and a triage router), parallelism (independent sub-tasks run concurrently so wall-clock is max not sum, e.g., 1.6s vs 4.2s), and planner/worker layering (decomposition separated from execution). Avoid multi-agent for single-step tasks, context-continuity tasks, latency-critical loops, and small dev teams. Decision rule: multi-agent IF specialization OR parallelism OR planner/worker fits; otherwise single agent.
summary: Multi-agent wins on specialization, parallelism, or planner/worker fit; otherwise stay single-agent.

text: A single agent loaded with all customer-support roles - retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, escalate - has a crowded system prompt. Each role pulls the prompt in a different direction, creating a performance ceiling. Each new role added to the system prompt costs precision on every other role. At some point, splitting roles into separate agents beats stuffing them into one.
summary: One agent with too many roles hits a performance ceiling because each role degrades the others.

text: Replace the crowded single agent with 3 focused agents. Each has a tighter system prompt, a smaller tool set, a clearer goal. Specialists: Billing Specialist (tools: lookup_subscription, process_refund; handles refunds, invoices, tier changes), Troubleshooting Specialist (tools: search_kb, lookup_customer; diagnoses errors, outages, sync failures), Triage Router (tools: lookup_customer; classifies each ticket and routes to specialist). Each specialist has 1-2 tools, not the full 8-tool inventory. Smaller surface = fewer wrong-tool errors. The triage router picks which specialist a ticket goes to.
summary: Specialization splits one crowded agent into focused specialists with smaller tool sets and a triage router.

text: Some sub-tasks have no data dependencies. Run them on separate agents concurrently. The wall-clock cost is max(sub-task latency), not the sum. Dispatcher fans out to Agent A (lookup_customer, 1.2s), Agent B (lookup_subscription, 1.4s), Agent C (search_kb, 1.6s). Sequential = 1.2 + 1.4 + 1.6 = 4.2s. Parallel = max(1.2, 1.4, 1.6) = 1.6s. Almost 3x faster. The win compounds as you add more independent sub-tasks.
summary: Independent sub-tasks run in parallel across agents so wall-clock equals the slowest, not the sum.

text: Some agents are good at decomposing tasks; others are good at executing single steps. Separating these roles produces a 2-layer setup. A Planner Agent at the top decomposes the task. Worker agents below execute single sub-tasks (e.g., Worker 1: Lookup Customer, Worker 2: Process Refund). The planner does NOT execute steps. The workers do NOT decompose. Each agent stays on its layer. This separation makes both layers more reliable than one mixed agent.
summary: Planner-worker splits decomposition from execution into two layers, each more reliable than a mixed agent.

text: Multi-agent costs something. More LLM calls, more state to track, more places for failure. Four anti-patterns where one agent is the right answer: (1) Single-Step Tasks - just call the tool; adding a second agent doubles latency for zero gain. (2) Context Continuity Tasks - if the same context carries through every step, hand-off loses information. (3) Latency-Critical Loops - each agent adds 1-2 seconds of reasoning; a 100ms SLA loop cannot afford this. (4) Small Dev Teams - multi-agent failures are hard to debug; a 2-person team will drown. Decision Rule: multi-agent IF specialization OR parallelism OR planner/worker fits the workload. Otherwise single agent.
summary: Four anti-patterns where multi-agent loses, plus a decision rule that picks multi-agent only for specialization, parallelism, or planner/worker fit.

text: Diagram: hub-and-spoke topology. An orchestrator box at the top center labeled 'Orchestrator - Plans + Aggregates' has bidirectional double-headed arrows connecting it to three worker boxes at the bottom (Worker 1, Worker 2, Worker 3), each labeled 'One Sub-Task'. There are no edges between workers - all communication flows through the orchestrator. The visual encodes that workers stay simple while the orchestrator owns coordination cost, preventing peer-to-peer chatter and shared-state corruption.
summary: Hub-and-spoke diagram showing one orchestrator connected by two-way arrows to three isolated workers.

text: Diagram: single worker execution flow. Left box shows the input sub-task description ('Lookup customer tier for c-9924'). A right arrow flows into the middle box, the Worker Agent, which runs an internal loop: Reason -> Act -> Observe, within its tool set only. A right arrow then flows to the right box showing the structured result returned to the orchestrator: { tier: "Pro", usage: "heavy" }. The diagram makes concrete that each worker is a self-contained agent loop that consumes one sub-task and emits a structured payload.
summary: Diagram showing a single worker taking a sub-task input, running an internal ReAct loop, and returning a structured result to the orchestrator.

text: The orchestrator-worker pattern is the most common production multi-agent shape: a central orchestrator plans, dispatches, and aggregates while N workers each handle one sub-task in isolation. Topology is hub-and-spoke - workers communicate only with the orchestrator. The orchestrator runs three phases (Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate) as separate LLM calls. Workers run their own Reason-Act-Observe loops within their tool set and return structured results; chaining between workers is the orchestrator's job, which keeps parallel dispatch safe. Total latency equals max(worker time) plus plan plus aggregate, not the sum. Aggregation has three patterns: concatenation (simple, verbose), voting (best for classification and hallucination reduction), and synthesis (the production default for natural-language answers).
summary: Summarizes the orchestrator-worker pattern: hub-and-spoke topology, three orchestrator phases, isolated workers, parallel latency, and three aggregation strategies.

text: The orchestrator-worker pattern is the most common production multi-agent shape. A central orchestrator plans and aggregates; workers each handle one sub-task. Hub-and-spoke topology: workers only talk to the orchestrator, never to each other. The orchestrator owns coordination cost; workers stay simple. No edges between workers means no peer-to-peer chatter and no shared-state corruption.
summary: Introduces the orchestrator-worker hub-and-spoke topology where one planner coordinates N isolated workers.

text: The orchestrator runs three phases per task, each a separate LLM call with a distinct system prompt - the same agent loops the model through three different mindsets. Plan: break the incoming task into 3-5 sub-tasks each workable in isolation. Dispatch: send each sub-task to its assigned worker agent. Aggregate: when workers return, merge results into a single coherent answer. The 3 phases give the orchestrator 3 separate minds, each simpler than a single do-everything prompt.
summary: Explains the orchestrator's three-phase loop of Plan, Dispatch, and Aggregate using separate LLM calls with distinct prompts.

text: Each worker takes one sub-task description, runs its own agent loop within its tool set, and returns a structured result. Example: Sub-Task 'Lookup customer tier for c-9924' -> Worker Agent runs internal Reason -> Act -> Observe loop within its tool set only -> Structured Result { tier: "Pro", usage: "heavy" }. Workers do not talk to each other. If Worker A's result feeds Worker B's input, the orchestrator does that chaining. This is what makes parallel dispatch safe.
summary: Shows how a single worker consumes one sub-task, runs an internal ReAct loop, and returns a structured result.

text: Trace for ticket T3 ('Dashboard slow + 500 errors'): Step 1 Orchestrator receives T3 and plans 3 sub-tasks: A) search_kb for 'dashboard slow'; B) search_kb for '500 errors'; C) lookup_customer for usage tier. Step 2 Worker A returns top-3 knowledge base articles on dashboard performance. Step 3 Worker B returns top-3 knowledge base articles on 500 errors. Step 4 Worker C returns Alice's tier (Pro) and usage stats (heavy dashboard user). Step 5 Orchestrator aggregates, composes a single answer combining knowledge base hits and customer context, recommends dashboard optimization, and escalates 500 errors to engineering. Workers A, B, C ran in parallel - steps 2-4 happened roughly at the same time. Total latency = max(worker time) + plan + aggregate, not sum.
summary: Walks through a concrete five-step T3 ticket trace showing parallel worker dispatch and final aggregation with latency formula.

text: Aggregation - merging worker outputs into a single answer - has three common patterns. Concatenation: stitch all worker outputs together in order; simple but verbose; best for quick prototypes and outputs already well-sectioned. Voting: workers each propose and orchestrator picks majority; good for classification; best for categorical outputs and hallucination reduction. Synthesis: orchestrator reads all worker outputs and writes a single coherent answer; most common, most expensive; best as the production default for natural-language answers. Synthesis is the production default; voting wins when workers are doing classification; concatenation is fine for structured outputs that need no rephrasing.
summary: Compares concatenation, voting, and synthesis as the three aggregation strategies for combining worker outputs.

text: Three-level agent hierarchy diagram: at the top, a single green Supervisor box. It connects down with edges to three middle-level Sub-Supervisor boxes positioned at x=120, 280, 440 in the viewBox. Each Sub-Supervisor connects down to two Leaf specialist boxes offset by -40 and +40 from the parent's x. Edges are planar with no crossings. Caption: Top = Supervisor. Mid = Sub-Supervisors. Leaves = Specialists (Tools).
summary: Diagram showing the three-level supervisor / sub-supervisor / leaf tree with planar parent-child edges.

text: Hierarchical multi-agent is a tree of supervisors delegating to sub-supervisors and leaf specialists, with three levels as the sweet spot. Each supervisor at every level runs the same five-step loop: Receive, Plan, Route Down, Aggregate, Return Up, with routing as the new responsibility versus orchestrator-worker. Pick hierarchical when the domain has genuine sub-specialties (billing, troubleshooting); flatten when depth exceeds 3. A concrete customer-support tree has Support Supervisor over Billing, Troubleshooting, and Escalation sub-supervisors with narrow-tool leaves. Failures bubble up: a leaf escalates to its supervisor, which may retry or escalate further, eventually reaching the Escalation Supervisor that calls escalate_human - the safety net that prevents silent stalls.
summary: Chapter overview of hierarchical multi-agent: tree topology, per-supervisor five-step loop, decision rule vs orchestrator-worker, support-agent example, and bottom-up escalation safety net.

text: Hierarchical multi-agent is a tree with multiple levels of delegation. A top supervisor delegates to sub-supervisors which delegate to leaf specialists. Each leaf has 1-2 narrow tools. This is different from orchestrator-worker which has only a single level of workers. Three levels is the practical sweet spot. Two levels is just orchestrator-worker renamed. Four levels usually signals over-engineering.
summary: Hierarchical agents form a multi-level tree of supervisors delegating down to leaf specialists, with three levels as the sweet spot.

text: At each level a supervisor does five things: Receive a sub-task from its parent. Plan whether a single child handles it or break it into smaller sub-tasks. Route Down by picking the child agent(s) best matched to the sub-task. Aggregate by waiting for children and combining their results. Return Up the aggregated result to the parent supervisor. Every supervisor performs the same five-step loop at its own level. Routing is the new responsibility versus orchestrator-worker - a supervisor must know which child agent fits which sub-task type.
summary: Each supervisor runs a five-step loop - Receive, Plan, Route Down, Aggregate, Return Up - with routing as the new responsibility.

text: Choosing hierarchical over orchestrator-worker is a domain shape question. Pick Orchestrator-Worker when workers are peer specialists at one level, example: 3 knowledge base search workers run in parallel. Pick Hierarchical when the domain has clear sub-domains, example: billing has refund / invoice / tier-change as sub-areas. Avoid Hierarchical when tree depth > 3 levels - usually means over-engineering; flatten the design. Most production customer-support agents fit hierarchical because billing and troubleshooting are genuinely different sub-domains with different tools and policies.
summary: Pick hierarchical when the domain has real sub-specialties; pick orchestrator-worker for peer workers; flatten anything past three levels.

text: The customer-support agent fits hierarchical cleanly. Root: Support Supervisor - all-tickets entry point that classifies and routes. Sub-supervisors: Billing Supervisor with leaves Refund Specialist, Invoice Specialist, Tier-Change Specialist. Troubleshooting Supervisor with leaves Database Specialist, Network Specialist. Escalation Supervisor with leaf Human Hand-Off (escalate_human). Each leaf specialist uses 1-3 tools from the canonical 8-tool inventory: refund specialists use process_refund + lookup_subscription, network specialists use search_kb, escalation calls escalate_human.
summary: Concrete support tree: Support Supervisor routes to Billing, Troubleshooting, Escalation sub-supervisors, each with specialist leaves using narrow tool sets.

text: A leaf specialist that cannot finish escalates up to its supervisor. The supervisor decides: retry with a different leaf, or escalate to its own parent. Eventually a top-level escalation reaches the Escalation Supervisor which calls escalate_human. Worked trace: Refund Specialist hits business_rule error on a $250 refund and escalates up. Billing Supervisor considers Tier-Change Leaf, rules it out, escalates up. Support Supervisor routes to Escalation Supervisor. Escalation Supervisor calls escalate_human with full trace. Escalation up the tree is the safety net; without it a leaf failure becomes a silent stall.
summary: Failures bubble up the tree - a refund leaf error escalates through billing and root supervisors until the Escalation Supervisor calls escalate_human.

text: Hand-off ring topology diagram with four agents arranged in a circle: Triage (top), Billing (right), Escalation (bottom), Troubleshooting (left). Each adjacent pair is connected by bidirectional arrows showing hand-off can flow either direction. A diagonal dashed cross-edge runs between Triage and Troubleshooting, showing a secondary non-adjacent transfer path. There is no central parent node - all four agents are peers at the same level. Visualizes the ring/general-graph topology where any agent can transfer to any other subject to its handoffs list.
summary: Describes the ring topology SVG with four peer agents connected bidirectionally plus one diagonal cross-edge.

text: Hand-Offs introduces the Swarm pattern where an agent finishes its part and returns the next agent, switching control directly without a central orchestrator. Each agent declares a handoffs list of allowed transfer targets. When hand-off happens, a package travels along: full conversation history, working memory snapshot, and hand-off reason - so the receiving agent never starts from scratch. A worked T4 trace shows triage to billing to escalation across three agents on one ticket. Topology is a ring or general graph (peer agents) rather than a tree (nested parent-child domains).
summary: Summarizes the Swarm hand-off pattern: agents return the next agent, carry a context package, and form ring rather than tree topologies.

text: The Swarm pattern (now in the OpenAI Agents SDK) replaces an orchestrator routing sub-tasks with agents that finish their part and RETURN the next agent. The runtime switches control directly with no central coordinator. Contrast: Orchestrator-Worker / Delegation style has central control - the orchestrator picks who handles next, waits for the result, then picks again. Hand-Off / Swarm style is decentralized - Agent A returns 'next = agent B' and the loop switches control directly. Hand-off is decentralized; orchestration is centralized. Both ship to production - the pick depends on whether you want a single brain or peer-to-peer routing.
summary: Introduces the Swarm hand-off pattern where agents return the next agent instead of routing through a central orchestrator.
Shows the agent definition shape: name, instructions, and a handoffs list declaring which agents it can transfer control to.

text: When agent A hands off to agent B, B does not start fresh. It receives a hand-off package with everything it needs to pick up cleanly. The package contains three parts: (1) Full Conversation History - all turns since session start; (2) Working Memory Snapshot - current scratchpad state at hand-off time; (3) Hand-Off Reason - why the sender decided to hand off (e.g. 'Classified as billing'). The receiving agent reads the entire package before its first reasoning step. No re-litigation from scratch. The conversation history plus working memory plus reason is the contract.
summary: Lists the three pieces of the hand-off package - full conversation history, working memory snapshot, and hand-off reason - that travel with every transfer.

text: Trace for ticket T4 = 'Cancel my subscription + refund last invoice.' Watch the hand-off ring move control across three agents. Step 1: Triage Agent receives T4 ('Cancel subscription + refund last invoice'). Step 2: Triage Agent classifies as billing, returns hand-off to billing_agent, loop switches. Step 3: Billing Agent receives full conversation history, processes cancel. Step 4: Billing Agent finds refund > $200; business_rule error. Returns hand-off to escalation_agent. Step 5: Escalation Agent receives full trace, calls escalate_human with cancel-and-refund context. Three agents, three hand-offs, one ticket. Each agent did its specialty. The hand-off package carried full context so the escalation_agent did not need to ask 'what happened before'.
summary: Walks through a concrete five-step trace where ticket T4 moves Triage to Billing to Escalation via hand-offs.

text: Hand-off topology is a ring (or general graph) - any agent can transfer to any other, subject to the handoffs list. This is different from tree topology, which has fixed parent-child relationships. Tree topology fits when domains are nested (billing has refund/invoice as sub-areas). Ring topology fits when domains are equal peers (triage, billing, troubleshooting, escalation are at the same level, no nesting). Choose ring when agents are peers; choose tree when domains decompose hierarchically.
summary: Contrasts ring vs tree topology and explains ring fits peer agents while tree fits nested hierarchical domains.

text: Two-agent pair diagram where the worker agent on the left produces a draft answer and the critic agent on the right reads the draft and the original question, then scores or rewrites it. Worker box is cyan, critic box is red, with a draft arrow flowing left to right between them.
summary: Diagram showing worker agent producing a draft and critic agent reviewing and scoring it.

text: Circular flow diagram with four stations: worker drafts, critic scores 0-10, conditional revise if below 7, critic re-scores, with a loop arrow back to the worker until score is high enough or retry cap is reached. All four boxes are teal and arrows go clockwise.
summary: Diagram of the bounded draft-score-revise-rescore loop with retry cap.

text: Three-agent debate diagram: Agent Pro on the top left in green argues FOR; Agent Con on the top right in red argues AGAINST; Judge Agent at the bottom center in purple reads both arguments and picks one. Arrows flow from Pro and Con down to the Judge.
summary: Diagram of three-agent debate setup with Pro, Con, and Judge agent arrangement.

text: This chapter introduces critic and debate as multi-agent quality patterns. A critic is a separate skeptical agent that reviews a worker's draft for isolation that self-critique can't give. The critic-revise loop is bounded by a score threshold and retry cap. Debate uses three agents - Pro, Con, Judge - for contested high-stakes decisions. A refund-agent example shows a policy critic catching a 30-day rule and saving $60. Decision rubric: use critic for high-stakes mutations (+1 LLM call), use debate for contested decisions (+3 calls), skip both for low-stakes lookups.
summary: Overview of critic, critic-revise loop, debate, refund example, and when to apply each multi-agent quality pattern.

text: Critic is reflection-as-multi-agent: instead of one agent self-critiquing, a separate critic agent reviews the worker's draft. Same idea, more isolation - the critic does not share working memory with the worker. The critic has a different system prompt - it is told to be SKEPTICAL of the worker's draft. The separation makes it less likely the model just rubber-stamps its own earlier output. A second agent checks the first: worker agent produces the draft answer for the customer, critic agent reads draft plus question and scores or rewrites.
summary: Introduces the critic pattern where a separate skeptical agent reviews a worker agent's draft instead of self-critique.

text: The critic-revise loop is bounded. Worker drafts; critic scores 0-10 with reasoning; if below threshold, worker revises with critic's feedback; critic re-scores. Loop until score is high enough OR the retry cap is hit. Circular flow with four stations: worker drafts, critic scores 0-10, conditional revise if below 7, critic re-scores, loop back to worker. The retry cap matters. Without it, a worker that can't please the critic loops forever - a cost-runaway failure mode.
summary: Explains the bounded draft-critique-revise loop with scoring threshold and retry cap to prevent infinite loops.

text: Debate is a 3-agent variant. Agent Pro argues for a position; Agent Con argues against; Judge agent reads both arguments and the original question and picks. Useful when the answer is contested or high-stakes enough to be worth two views. Debate triples the LLM bill vs single-agent. Use it sparingly - high-stakes contested decisions where you genuinely want two voices, not noise-reduction on a routine task.
summary: Explains the three-agent debate pattern with Pro, Con, and Judge agents for contested high-stakes decisions.

text: Concrete example: a refund agent makes a draft decision; a policy critic catches that the invoice is >30 days old; the refund gets revised down. Trace: Step 1 Customer requests refund for invoice #INV-9924, $180. Step 2 Refund Agent says 'Approved, processing $180 refund now.' Step 3 Policy Critic says 'Hold. Check if invoice was paid >30 days ago. If yes, partial refund only (60% policy for >30 days).' Step 4 Refund Agent (Revised) says 'Confirmed >30 days. Approved for $120 (60% policy).'
