(1) Sandboxing: every tool runs in a cage (container/VM/process isolation) that allowlists filesystem reads and outbound network so a compromised tool cannot reach the agent runtime, host OS, secrets, or other customers. (2) Capability scope: per-role tool sets - triage reads only, billing mutates with hard caps like refund cap $200, escalation hands off to humans only. The runtime checks agent identity against the tool registry. (3) Audit logging: every tool call written append-only, signed, immutable, with timestamp/agent_id/customer_id/tool/input/result/consent, kept 7 years for SOC 2 financial compliance, feeding incident response, audits, and anomaly detection. (4) Rate limits in the runtime (not the agent): cheap reads generous, mutations tight (process_refund 5/hr), escalations moderate (10/hr); over-limit returns tool_error. (5) Human consent prompts for high-stakes mutations - the agent waits for Approve/Deny.
summary: Production agents are secured with five stacked layers: sandboxing, per-role capability scope, immutable audit logs, runtime rate limits, and human consent prompts.

text: Tools run inside a sandbox boundary. Inside the cage: the tool process, its allowed filesystem reads, and its allowed outbound network destinations. Outside the cage: the agent runtime itself, the host OS, customer data unrelated to this session, the secrets store, other customers, internal APIs. If a tool is compromised, the cage limits the damage. The cage is a runtime contract enforced by the platform (container, VM, process isolation). The tool author declares what it needs; the runtime grants only that. Compromised tools cannot escape what they were never given.
summary: Tools execute in a sandbox so a compromised tool cannot reach the agent, host OS, secrets, or other customer data.

text: Capability scope is per-role. Different agents get different tool sets. Triage Agent (yellow): search_kb, lookup_customer - read-only, no mutations. Billing Specialist (orange): read tools + process_refund (cap $200) + change_subscription - mutation allowed with hard cap. Escalation Agent (red): escalate_human + send_email - hands off to humans only. Even if any one agent is compromised, the blast radius is bounded. This is defense in depth at the capability layer. Capability tokens travel with the agent identity. The runtime verifies the agent's role against the tool registry before executing any tool call. No role, no call.
summary: Each agent role gets only the tools it needs, so a compromise of one agent cannot trigger mutations outside its scope.
Every tool call is recorded in an immutable, signed audit log retained for years to support incident response, compliance, and anomaly detection.

text: Even within scope, rate-limit each tool. Cheap reads can be generous. Expensive mutations need tight caps. Escalations need a moderate cap so spam-escalation doesn't overwhelm the human queue. Hitting the limit forces a hard stop or re-routes to a different agent. Examples: search_kb - 100/agent/hour (cheap read); lookup_customer - 50/agent/hour (cheap read); process_refund - 5/agent/hour (cost containment); escalate_human - 10/agent/hour (human-wall pressure). Rate limits live in the runtime, not in the agent. The model cannot raise them by asking. When the limit is hit, the runtime returns a tool_error result so the agent can adapt: escalate to human, queue for later, or refuse politely.
summary: Per-tool rate limits enforced by the runtime cap how often each tool can be called, with tighter limits on costly mutations and human escalations.

text:
For any tool that mutates state above a threshold, the runtime asks for human consent before executing. The agent waits. The human approves or denies. The decision goes into the audit log alongside the request. Example consent prompt UX mockup:

The agent wants to call:
  process_refund

  invoice_id : INV-9924
  amount     : $150
  reason     : "customer requested"

Allow this action?

  [ Approve ]   [ Deny ]   [ Always Allow For This Customer ]

Consent prompts close the production-hardening loop: sandboxing limits damage, capability scope limits reach, audit logs preserve the record, rate limits cap volume, and consent prompts keep a human in the loop for the highest-stakes calls. Five layers, one safe agent.
summary: High-stakes tool calls trigger a human consent prompt that pauses the agent until approved, completing five layers of agent production hardening.

text: Diagram: LangGraph state machine for a customer-support agent. Four nodes (Classify, Lookup, Refund, Respond) connected by directed edges in a horizontal flow. A shared State Object ribbon runs beneath the nodes holding { ticket, customer, intent, lookup_result, refund_id, resolution }. Each node reads and writes that state as the runtime walks edges.
summary: Diagram showing a four-node LangGraph customer-support agent with a shared state object flowing through Classify, Lookup, Refund, and Respond.

text: Diagram: Conditional edges from the classify node. After Classify writes state.intent, the router branches into Billing_Handler (uses refund and invoice tools) when intent is billing, Troubleshooting_Handler (uses knowledge-base tools) when intent is troubleshooting, or Escalation_Handler (hands off to human) when intent is escalation. A pill below labeled "Router Reads State.Intent" represents the routing function.
summary: Diagram showing conditional edges from Classify branching to billing, troubleshooting, or escalation handlers based on state.intent.

text: Diagram: Checkpoint and resume sequence in three steps. Step 1 (green): agent runs three nodes, hits a long-running tool (async approval request), working memory built up. Step 2 (cyan): checkpoint state, persist to store, graph pauses, waits for result. Step 3 (purple): tool result arrives hours or days later, graph resumes from the checkpoint. Footer: crucial for async, long-running, and human-in-the-loop workflows.
summary: Diagram of the three-step checkpoint-and-resume sequence: run to long-running tool, persist state and pause, resume later from the checkpoint.

text: LangGraph models agents as state machines on a directed graph. Nodes are step functions, edges are transitions (optionally conditional), and a shared state object threads through every node. Three primitives: add_node, add_edge, state schema. Conditional edges replace switch statements by routing on state values like intent. Checkpointing persists state at every node boundary so async, long-running, or human-in-the-loop tool calls can pause and resume without losing context. Fits agents that are multi-step graphs where state matters; overkill for simple one-shot tool use.
summary: LangGraph turns multi-step agents into checkpointable state machines on a directed graph with nodes, conditional edges, and shared state.

text: LangGraph treats an agent as a state machine on top of a graph. Each node is a step (classify, lookup, refund, respond). Each edge is a transition. A shared state object threads through the graph and every node reads and writes it. The graph is the program. The state is the variables. The runtime walks edges for you. Example customer-support agent: Classify -> Lookup -> Refund -> Respond, with a shared state object holding { ticket, customer, intent, lookup_result, refund_id, resolution }.
summary: LangGraph models an agent as a state machine where nodes are steps, edges are transitions, and a shared state object flows through every node.

text:
LangGraph exposes three primitives: add_node registers a step function, add_edge wires steps together (optionally with a condition), and a state schema declares what every node can read and write. Pattern:

graph.add_node("classify", classify_fn)
graph.add_node("billing_handler", billing_fn)
graph.add_node("respond", respond_fn)

graph.add_edge("classify", "billing_handler", condition=is_billing)
graph.add_edge("billing_handler", "respond")

state = { ticket: ..., customer: ..., resolution: ... }

add_node registers a Python function. add_edge wires it. The condition argument on the edge picks the next node from state. The state object is the only thing passed between nodes.
summary: LangGraph's three core primitives are add_node, add_edge with optional conditions, and a state schema shared across nodes.

text: Conditional edges are the routing primitive. After classify writes state.intent, conditional edges fork into billing_handler, troubleshooting_handler, or escalation_handler. The router function reads state.intent and returns the next node name. Billing_Handler uses refund and invoice tools. Troubleshooting_Handler uses knowledge-base tools. Escalation_Handler hands off to a human. The conditional edge replaces a switch statement. Routing logic lives on the edge, not buried inside the next node. The graph stays readable.
summary: Conditional edges branch based on state, letting a router function pick the next node from values like state.intent.

text: LangGraph checkpoints state at every node boundary by default. If a tool call is long-running, async, or needs human approval, the graph pauses, persists state, and resumes from the checkpoint when the result arrives. No re-running earlier nodes. No rebuilt context. Sequence: Step 1 the agent runs nodes and hits a long-running tool (async approval request) with working memory built up. Step 2 checkpoint state, persist to store, graph pauses, waits for result. Step 3 tool result arrives hours or days later, graph resumes from the checkpoint. Without checkpoints, async tool calls force you to either block the agent (kills throughput) or rebuild the conversation from scratch (loses context). Checkpointing makes long-running flows feel like one continuous run.
summary: LangGraph checkpoints state at every node boundary so async or human-in-the-loop tool calls can pause and resume without losing context.

text: LangGraph fits when your agent looks like a directed graph and state is the star of the show. Signals that map to LangGraph: you can describe the flow as a directed graph (nodes are steps, edges are transitions, state is the payload); state needs to survive across LLM / tool calls (working memory belongs in one object that threads through every node); you want visualizable, debuggable flow control (the graph renders, you can point at the failing edge); you can pay a small abstraction tax for structure (a few extra lines of setup in exchange for predictable runs). Avoid LangGraph when: simple one-shot tool use, a single classify-and-respond call, you just need to call the API directly without graph machinery. The cost is the structure tax. The payoff is visualizable, checkpointable, debuggable agent flows. Worth it when your agent has more than two steps.
summary: Use LangGraph when an agent is a multi-step directed graph with shared state; skip it for one-shot tool use.

text: CrewAI and AutoGen are two multi-agent frameworks built on different abstractions. CrewAI is role-based: each Agent has a role, goal, backstory, and tools, and a Crew object delegates tasks to the best-fit agent. AutoGen is conversational: AssistantAgents and a UserProxyAgent talk in a GroupChat, with a GroupChatManager choosing the next speaker. A ticket T4 refund example runs cleanly through both - CrewAI hands a task from triage to billing; AutoGen routes messages through a moderator. Pick CrewAI when you can list distinct roles with clear goals, pick AutoGen when agents need free-form conversation like debate or review, and skip both when one agent plus tools is enough. Framework choice matters less than agent design quality.
summary: Chapter overview of CrewAI vs AutoGen abstractions, code shapes, traces, and a decision rule for which to use.

text: CrewAI and AutoGen tackle the same problem (multiple agents working together) with two different abstractions. CrewAI is role-based: each agent has a role, goal, backstory, and tools. AutoGen is conversational: agents talk to each other through a manager that routes messages. CrewAI primitive is Role-Based with fields Role + Goal + Backstory + Tools; runtime is a Crew running tasks against agents; feel is job descriptions then hire. AutoGen primitive is Conversational with fields System Message + Speaker/Listener; runtime is a Manager routing messages between agents; feel is group chat with a moderator. Same problem, two different abstractions. The pick depends on whether your mental model is team of specialists or group chat with a moderator.
summary: Introduces CrewAI (role-based) and AutoGen (conversational) as two abstractions for multi-agent systems.

text:
A CrewAI agent looks like a job description. Role names the position. Goal states the outcome. Tools is the kit. The Crew object ties agents to tasks and runs them. The runtime tries to match each task to the best-fit agent. Example code:

triage_agent = Agent(
  role="Support Triage Specialist",
  goal="Classify the ticket and route to the right specialist.",
  tools=[classify_intent]
)
billing_agent = Agent(
  role="Billing Specialist",
  goal="Resolve billing-related requests including refunds and invoices.",
  tools=[lookup_customer, lookup_subscription, process_refund, escalate_human]
)
crew = Crew(agents=[triage_agent, billing_agent], tasks=[handle_ticket_task])

The role and goal fields are not cosmetic. The runtime feeds them into the agent's system prompt and uses them to pick which agent to delegate a task to. Backstory is optional flavor that shapes voice.
summary: Shows the CrewAI Agent/Crew code shape with role, goal, tools, and how the runtime uses role/goal for delegation.

text:
AutoGen models agents as participants in a chat room. Each AssistantAgent has a name and system_message. A UserProxyAgent stands in for the human or upstream system. A GroupChatManager moderates: it sees every message and picks who speaks next. Example code:

triage = AssistantAgent(
  name="triage",
  system_message="You classify support tickets and tag the right handler."
)
billing = AssistantAgent(
  name="billing",
  system_message="You handle billing requests: refunds, invoices, subscriptions."
)
user_proxy = UserProxyAgent(name="user_proxy")

manager = GroupChatManager(
  groupchat=GroupChat(agents=[triage, billing, user_proxy])
)

The manager is the orchestration core. It reads message context to choose the next speaker. This makes back-and-forth conversation natural (debate, review, brainstorm) but adds a routing layer you have to tune.
summary: Shows the AutoGen AssistantAgent / UserProxyAgent / GroupChatManager code shape and how the manager routes messages.

text:
Ticket T4 (cancel and refund) runs through both frameworks. Same outcome. Different orchestration shape. CrewAI hands tasks. AutoGen routes messages.

CrewAI Trace - Ticket T4:
Step 1: Triage Agent classifies ticket T4 -> Intent = Billing.
Step 2: Triage Agent hands the task to Billing Agent.
Step 3: Billing Agent runs lookup_customer(c-9924) -> Pro tier, in good standing.
Step 4: Billing Agent runs process_refund(INV-9924, $150).
Step 5: Billing Agent calls escalate_human because refund > auto-approve threshold.

AutoGen Trace - Ticket T4:
Step 1: user_proxy posts ticket T4 to group chat.
Step 2: Triage speaks: classify -> Intent = Billing, tag @Billing.
Step 3: Billing joins: lookup_customer(c-9924), process_refund(INV-9924, $150).
Step 4: Escalation joins: approve the refund (manager routes message).
Step 5: Triage closes: replies to user_proxy with resolution.

CrewAI feels like the triage agent passes a baton to the billing agent. AutoGen feels like they discuss it in chat with a moderator. Both arrive at the same resolution. Pick the one that matches how you describe the work in English.
summary: Compares CrewAI baton-pass vs AutoGen group-chat traces for the same refund ticket T4.

text:
Framework choice matters less than agent design quality. A well-designed CrewAI agent beats a poorly-designed AutoGen agent every time. Pick whichever abstraction makes your team faster.

When to pick what:
Pick CrewAI when you can list distinct roles with clear goals. Example: Customer Support Engineer, Researcher, Writer, Reviewer.
Pick AutoGen when agents need free-form conversation. Example: Debate, Brainstorm, Multi-Reviewer Code Review.
Skip both when you have one agent + tools (no second agent needed). Example: Most customer-support bots live here.

Pick the abstraction your team can reason about fastest and ship a working agent before debating the perfect runtime.
summary: Decision rule: pick CrewAI for clear roles, AutoGen for free-form conversation, skip both for single-agent + tools.

text: This chapter compares two vendor-native agent SDKs: Claude Agent SDK (loop primitive, built around the Claude API) and OpenAI Agents (hand-off primitive, successor to Swarm, built around OpenAI tool-calling). Claude SDK fits single agents with deep tool use; OpenAI Agents fits multi-role workflows with routing. Both ship native vendor features (prompt caching, MCP, Assistants API, built-in function tools) but carry high lock-in and low portability. Use them when committed to one vendor for 12-18+ months and the abstractions match your agent shape; avoid for multi-vendor strategies or when you need LangGraph-style checkpointing.
summary: Vendor-native SDKs (Claude Agent SDK loop, OpenAI Agents hand-off) trade portability for native vendor features.

text: When the model vendor ships the framework. Vendor-native SDKs are tightly integrated with one model provider. Claude Agent SDK is built around the Claude API and exposes a loop primitive. OpenAI Agents (the successor to Swarm) is built around OpenAI tool-calling and exposes a hand-off primitive. Both ship native support for that vendor's other features. Claude Agent SDK: accent cyan, Loop Primitive, API: Run_Agent_Loop With System Prompt + Tools, Native: Prompt Cache, Structured Output, MCP, Fits: Single Agent Doing Deep Tool Use. OpenAI Agents: accent purple, Hand-Off Primitive, API: Agent.Handoffs = [Other_Agent, ...], Native: Assistants API, Built-In Function Tools, Fits: Multi-Role Workflows With Routing. Both vendors ship the abstractions they think their model is best at. Claude invests in long iterative loops with deep tool use. OpenAI invests in multi-agent hand-off chains. Pick the one whose abstractions match your agent shape.
summary: Introduces the two vendor SDKs and contrasts their primitives, native features, and ideal use cases.

text:
Loop Primitive. The Claude Agent SDK exposes AgentLoop: a configured loop that takes a system prompt, a tool inventory, an iteration cap, and an on-message callback. Inside the loop, the SDK runs reason-act-observe for you and returns the final message once the model stops asking for tools. Framework Pattern (Shape) - Claude Agent SDK:

loop = AgentLoop(
  system_prompt=SYSTEM,
  tools=[lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human],
  max_iterations=10,
  on_message=log_message
)
result = loop.run(user_message="I want a refund for INV-9924")

The loop is the abstraction. You hand the SDK the model, tools, and a cap, then call run. Reason-act-observe lives inside the SDK. You never write the while loop yourself.
summary: Shows AgentLoop code shape: configure system prompt, tools, iteration cap, then call run; SDK hides reason-act-observe.

text:
Hand-Off Primitive. OpenAI Agents (the successor to Swarm) exposes hand-offs. Each agent declares which other agents it can hand off to. The Runner walks the chain: each agent either resolves the request or returns the next agent. The chain terminates when an agent returns a final response. Framework Pattern (Shape) - OpenAI Agents:

triage = Agent(
  name="triage",
  instructions="Classify and route.",
  handoffs=[billing_agent, troubleshooting_agent]
)
result = Runner.run_sync(triage, "Refund my last invoice")

The handoffs field is the routing graph. The Runner reads each agent's response to decide if it is a final answer or a hand-off to the next agent. No loop on your side. Just declare who can pass the baton to whom.
summary: Shows OpenAI Agents hand-off code: each Agent declares handoffs list, Runner walks the chain until final response.

text: Two Primitives, Two Mental Models. Loop versus hand-off is the headline difference. Lock-in and portability are the production trade-offs you carry forever. Moving to another vendor means rewriting the agent harness, not just swapping a model. Comparison table: Core Primitive - Claude: Loop (Single Agent, Multi-Iteration); OpenAI: Hand-Off (Multi-Agent Chain). Best Fit - Claude: Stand-Alone Agents With Deep Tool Use; OpenAI: Multi-Role Workflows With Routing. Lock-In - Claude: High - Bound To Claude API + Abstractions; OpenAI: High - Bound To OpenAI API + Abstractions. Portability - Claude: Low - Switching Vendor Means Rewriting The Loop; OpenAI: Low - Switching Vendor Means Rewriting The Hand-Off Chain. High lock-in is not automatic disqualification. If you are sure about the vendor for the next 18 months, lock-in is the price of native features. If you are not sure, the price is rewriting the agent harness later.
summary: Compares Claude SDK vs OpenAI Agents across primitive, fit, lock-in, and portability; both high lock-in, low portability.

text: Use The Vendor SDK When... Vendor SDKs are the right pick when the vendor relationship is long-term and its abstractions fit your shape. Avoid them when you need multi-vendor support or features the SDK does not ship (LangGraph-style checkpointing, for example). Fit signals: (1) Committed To One Vendor Long-Term - You picked Claude or OpenAI and are not switching for 12+ months. (2) Want The Vendor's Latest Features Day One - Prompt caching, new tool-calling modes, built-in memory land faster here. (3) Vendor Abstractions Match Your Agent Shape - Single-agent loop -> Claude SDK. Multi-role hand-off -> OpenAI Agents. Avoid Vendor SDK When: A multi-vendor strategy is required, you anticipate switching models, or you need framework features the vendor does not ship (e.g. LangGraph-style checkpointing across long-running calls). Lock-in is a deliberate trade. You buy speed and native features. You pay in portability. Make the trade once, eyes open, before you have 50 agents in production.
summary: Decision rules: pick vendor SDK for long-term commitment, day-one features, matching abstractions; avoid for multi-vendor or missing features like checkpointing.

text: Decision tree for staying custom. Five leaves: three say YES (high-volume traffic over 10K per second, tight latency budget P95 under 2 seconds, multi-vendor strategy required) and two say NO (one-off prototype shipping this week, team of fewer than 3 engineers).
summary: SVG decision tree with five leaves for choosing custom vs framework based on traffic, latency, vendor strategy, prototype urgency, and team size.

text: Hybrid layer diagram. Stack of four layers showing what teams typically buy from a framework (scaffolding, schema validation, basic retries) and what they build custom (LLM adapter for vendor swap, observability that integrates with the monitoring stack, domain-specific tool gating).
summary: SVG layered stack diagram contrasting framework-bought layers (scaffolding, schema, retries) with custom-built layers (LLM adapter, observability, tool gating).

text: Going custom (no-framework) on agents is a deliberate trade. The three real reasons are control over every behavior, stripping the 50-200ms per-iteration framework overhead, and avoiding LLM vendor lock-in via a custom adapter. The minimum-viable loop is about 11 lines: history list, max_iter for-loop, tool dispatch via a TOOLS dict, terminate on no-tool-call or max iterations. Going custom means you own the must-builds (observability, retries with exponential backoff per error class, eval harness) and decide when to add the can-skips (checkpointing, multi-agent hand-off). The decision tree: go custom for traffic over 10K/sec, P95 under 2s, or multi-vendor portability; stay on a framework for one-off prototypes and teams under 3 engineers. The dominant production answer is hybrid: buy scaffolding, schema validation, and basic retries from a framework, then build the vendor-agnostic LLM adapter, observability hooks, and domain-specific tool gating yourself.
summary: Complete picture of when and how to build a no-framework agent: reasons, minimal loop, must-build features, decision signals, and the hybrid default.

text: Three reasons to roll your own agent instead of using a framework: (1) Control - you want exact control of every behavior with no framework magic, because frameworks hide decisions like when to retry, when to truncate, and what to cache. (2) Cost - frameworks add 50-200ms overhead per iteration, which at high-volume traffic equals millions of dollars per year at scale. (3) Avoid vendor lock-in - you anticipate moving across LLM providers, and a custom LLM adapter lets you swap Claude for OpenAI for Llama without rewrites. Custom is not a default. Custom is what you reach for when one of these three reasons applies and you can afford the engineering cost. Otherwise: pick a framework and ship.
summary: The three legitimate production reasons to build a no-framework agent: control, cost overhead, and avoiding vendor lock-in.
A complete reason-act-observe agent loop fits in roughly 11 lines of Python pseudocode using a history list, a max_iter loop, and a TOOLS dict.

text: Frameworks ship a lot more than the loop. When you go custom, every framework feature is on your build list. Some are non-negotiable for production; others can be deferred. Must build: (1) Observability (spans, traces, metrics) - without traces you cannot debug production failures. (2) Retries with exponential backoff per tool error class - transient network errors are real, permanent business-rule errors are not retryable. (3) Eval harness integration - without evals you ship regressions; see LLM-as-judge and trace evals chapters. Can skip: (1) Checkpointing for long-running / human-in-the-loop - skip if every run is sub-second, build only if you need async resumes. (2) Hand-off between agents - skip if you have one agent, build only if you have multi-agent workflows. 'Must build' is the floor for a production custom agent. 'Can skip' is the ceiling you can defer until traffic justifies it. Misjudge the floor and you ship a fragile agent that breaks the first time a flaky tool surfaces.
summary: When going custom you inherit the framework's feature list: observability, retries with backoff, and eval harness are must-build, while checkpointing and multi-agent hand-off can be deferred.

text: Decision tree titled 'Custom Or Framework?' with a root labeled 'Five Signals' branching to five leaves. YES - CUSTOM leaves: high-volume traffic over 10K per second (framework overhead becomes the bottleneck); tight latency budget P95 under 2 seconds (strip every wrapper, inline hot paths); multi-vendor strategy required (vendor-agnostic adapter eases migration). NO - USE FRAMEWORK leaves: one-off prototype, ship this week (ship fast, rebuild later if it sticks); team of fewer than 3 engineers (cannot maintain custom harness plus features). Footer: default to framework unless a specific reason pushes you custom. One-off prototypes and small teams are the most common framework cases. Production scale and vendor portability are the most common go-custom cases.
summary: Five-leaf decision tree picking custom vs framework based on traffic, latency, vendor strategy, prototype urgency, and team size.

text: Hybrid stack diagram showing the four layers most production teams use. Buy: scaffolding, schema validation, basic retries - pip install a framework for the boring 80 percent. Build: LLM adapter for vendor swap - wrap Claude / OpenAI / local behind a single call interface. Build: observability stack - plug into your existing monitoring stack (OpenTelemetry, Datadog, Honeycomb). Build: domain-specific tool gating - gate refund tools, PII tools, and customer-specific permissions in your layer. Footer: production reality is that most teams run on top of a framework and replace hot layers. Hybrid is the smart default once you have one production agent and a working team. Buy what the framework does well. Build what differentiates you. Skip the rebuild-from-scratch trap.
summary: The hybrid 'build some, buy some' stack: buy scaffolding/schema/retries from a framework and custom-build the LLM adapter, observability hooks, and domain-specific tool gating.

text: Nine-layer agent decision stack diagram. From top (strategy) to bottom (operations): 1. Approach (24.5), 2. Loop Pattern (26.1 to 26.5), 3. Memory Layers (26.7 to 26.12), 4. Multi-Agent (27.1 to 27.5), 5. Tools (25.1 to 25.5), 6. Protocols (25.6 to 25.11), 7. Eval (28.1 to 28.5), 8. Production (28.6 to 28.11), 9. Framework (28.12 to 28.15). Each layer is a colored row labeled with its number, name, decision question (e.g. Prompt / Tune / RAG / Agent?, Workflow / ReAct / Plan-Execute / Reflection?, MCP / A2A / Bespoke?, LangGraph / CrewAI / Vendor SDK / Custom?), and a chapter reference. Strategy label sits at the top, Operations label at the bottom.
summary: Diagram of the nine-layer agent decision stack from strategy top to operations bottom, with each layer's decision question and source chapter range.

text: This chapter The Complete Agent Decision Framework. Synthesizes the entire section into a nine-layer top-down decision stack: 1 Approach, 2 Loop Pattern, 3 Memory Layers, 4 Multi-Agent, 5 Tools, 6 Protocols, 7 Eval, 8 Production, 9 Framework. Each layer references its source chapters. Walks the stack end to end on a fresh use case (Internal IT-Support Agent, 500-person company, password/software/VPN/hardware tickets, $500 escalation, SSO + ticketing integration). Picks: Agent + Workflow-with-agent-step + Working/Episodic/Semantic memory + Orchestrator-Worker + 8 scoped tools + MCP (no A2A) + 4-axis eval with LLM-as-judge + OTel/LangSmith/prompt cache/guardrails/injection defense + LangGraph. Closes with three capabilities: Decide, Diagnose, Defend.
summary: Chapter-level summary covering the nine-layer agent decision stack applied to an internal IT-support agent use case with concrete picks for every layer.

text: Designing a production agent is nine decisions, top to bottom. Strategy at the top (what approach? what loop?). Operations at the bottom (which framework? which observability stack?). Each layer is a chapter you have already worked through. The stack is the synthesis. The full decision stack: 1. Approach (Prompt / Tune / RAG / Agent? - 24.5). 2. Loop Pattern (Workflow / ReAct / Plan-Execute / Reflection? - 26.1-26.5). 3. Memory Layers (Working + Which Long-Term? - 26.7-26.12). 4. Multi-Agent (Single / Orchestrator / Supervisor / Hand-Off? - 27.1-27.5). 5. Tools (Which Tools? What Scope? - 25.1-25.5). 6. Protocols (MCP / A2A / Bespoke? - 25.6-25.11). 7. Eval (Dimensions + Judge + Traces? - 28.1-28.5). 8. Production (Observability + Cost + Guardrails + Injection + Security? - 28.6-28.11). 9. Framework (LangGraph / CrewAI / Vendor SDK / Custom? - 28.12-28.15). Nine layers. Each chapter answers one. Any agent that ships needs a concrete pick at every layer, with a reason that traces back to the chapter that taught it.
summary: Introduces the nine-layer agent decision stack from strategy (approach, loop, memory) to operations (eval, production, framework) with source chapters.

text: Design an agent for a new use case: Internal IT-Support Agent. Use case: serve a 500-person company. Handle employee tickets across: Password Resets, Software Install Requests, VPN Issues, Hardware Orders. Constraints: Hardware orders over $500 must escalate to the IT Manager. Must integrate with internal SSO and the ticketing system. This is a different shape than customer support: internal users, smaller blast radius, dollar threshold for escalation, integration with internal systems.
summary: Sets up the internal IT-support agent use case (500-person company, four ticket types, $500 escalation threshold, SSO integration) to walk through the decision stack.

text: Top three layers set the agent's shape. Layer 1 Approach: pick Agent. Why: mutating actions (password reset, hardware order) need tool calls plus a loop. Pure prompting or RAG cannot take actions. Layer 2 Loop Pattern: pick Workflow with an agent step. Why: most tickets are deterministic (classify, route, handle). The handler itself is an agent loop with tool use. Pure ReAct is overkill. Layer 3 Memory Layers: pick Working + Episodic + Semantic. Why: Working = current ticket. Episodic = past employee tickets (90-day window). Semantic = employee profile (department, hardware history, software stack). Approach is agent because work mutates state. Loop is workflow-with-agent-step because routes are deterministic and only the handler is an agent. Memory uses all three because employees have stable profiles and recurring patterns.
summary: Walks layers 1-3 for the IT agent: pick Agent approach, workflow-with-agent-step loop, and all three memory layers (working, episodic, semantic) with reasoning.

text: Multi-agent shape and tool inventory both flow from the work decomposition. Layer 4 Multi-Agent: pick Orchestrator-Worker. Why: triage classifier routes to specialist handlers (password / software / hardware). Hand-off when a handler needs another specialist's tools. Layer 5 Tools + Capability Scope: pick 8 internal tools, scoped per handler. Why: password handler gets reset_password and audit tools. Hardware handler gets order_hardware bounded by $500 cap. Capability scope is per-role. Orchestrator-Worker is the right multi-agent shape because routes are well-defined and bounded. Capability scope per handler keeps the password agent from ever calling order_hardware - bounded blast radius even under jailbreak attempts.
summary: Walks layers 4-5: orchestrator-worker multi-agent with triage to specialists, and 8 tools scoped per handler so password handler cannot call order_hardware.

text: Protocols decide how the agent connects to the rest of the company's systems. Eval strategy decides how you know the agent works before it ships and stays working after. Layer 6 Protocols: pick MCP for SSO + Ticketing, no A2A. Why: MCP servers wrap SSO and the ticketing system. The IT team can swap backends without touching the agent. No A2A because this is a single-team agent, not a cross-org mesh. Layer 7 Eval Strategy: pick 4-axis eval + LLM-as-judge + trace evals. Why: eval axes: Correctness, Latency, Cost, Safety. LLM-as-judge on resolutions. Trace evals on every refund or escalation step. Eval set: 50 golden + 20 adversarial + growing regression set. MCP gives clean separation between agent and IT-system internals. Eval set sizes are small enough to ship this quarter and growable as production traffic exposes new failure modes.
summary: Walks layers 6-7: MCP wrapping SSO/ticketing (no A2A), and 4-axis eval (correctness/latency/cost/safety) with LLM-as-judge, trace evals, 50 golden + 20 adversarial set.

text: The last two layers turn the design into a shippable system. Layer 8 Production Hardening: pick OTel + LangSmith + Prompt Cache + Guardrails + Injection Defense. Why: OTel + LangSmith for observability. Prompt cache on the long system prompt. Latency target P95 < 5s. Hardware-order guardrail enforces the $500 cap. Input layer runs prompt-injection defense per 28.10. Layer 9 Framework: pick LangGraph. Why: the workflow-with-agent-step shape maps cleanly to LangGraph nodes and edges. Conditional edges handle routing. Checkpoints cover async manager-approval steps for hardware orders. LangGraph wins because the workflow-with-agent-step shape is exactly a graph: classify node, route node, handler node, escalation node, respond node. Conditional edges handle routing. Checkpoints handle hardware-order manager-approval async waits.
summary: Walks layers 8-9: production hardening with OTel/LangSmith/prompt cache/guardrails/injection defense (P95 < 5s), and LangGraph framework with conditional edges and checkpoints.

text: Section 13 walked from the anatomy of a single LLM call to a complete production agent decision framework. What you have now is not opinions. It is three concrete capabilities: Decide - every layer above has a concrete decision in front of you, not a fog. Diagnose - when the agent fails in production, you know which layer to investigate. Defend - in a design review, you argue your choices from mechanics, not feel. Section 13 done. The production agent is yours to ship. Every layer above traces back to a chapter you have worked through. The decision stack is the map. The chapters are the terrain. You can walk it.
summary: Closes the section by naming three capabilities the framework gives - Decide, Diagnose, Defend - and frames the decision stack as map to the chapter terrain.
